name: Betania Patmos
classification_notes: Betania Patmos is a calligraphic display script with flowing, connected letterforms designed for decorative and religious publishing contexts. The design has a handwritten warmth with formal calligraphic structure.
summary: A flowing calligraphic script designed primarily for decorative use in religious and devotional publishing.
description: Betania Patmos features connected, pen-influenced letterforms that give it warmth and a handcrafted feel. Its primary application is in religious or inspirational publishing where a softer, calligraphic voice is appropriate. The single Regular weight and narrow scope of application limit its broader design utility.

name: Betania Patmos GDL
classification_notes: Betania Patmos GDL is a variant of the Betania Patmos script with glyph differentiation for language-specific needs, sharing the same flowing calligraphic construction. It extends coverage for particular orthographic requirements.
summary: A language-variant of Betania Patmos offering adjusted glyphs for specific orthographic requirements.
description: Betania Patmos GDL shares the soft, pen-drawn aesthetic of the base Betania Patmos family while providing glyph-level adjustments for particular script or language needs. Like its sibling, it is best suited to devotional and inspirational publishing contexts. Its highly specialised function means it has limited general-purpose application.

name: Betania Patmos In
classification_notes: Betania Patmos In is an inline variant of the Betania Patmos calligraphic script, featuring interior stroke treatments that add visual interest for display purposes. The inline effect enhances its decorative character.
summary: An inline-treated variant of Betania Patmos that adds decorative stroke detail for display-focused applications.
description: Betania Patmos In introduces inline stroke detailing to the base calligraphic script, increasing its visual complexity and decorative appeal. This treatment makes it most effective at large sizes in poster or title contexts within religious or devotional design. Its single-weight, specialised nature restricts it to a narrow set of use cases.

name: Betania Patmos In GDL
classification_notes: Betania Patmos In GDL combines the inline decorative treatment of Betania Patmos In with the language-specific glyph adjustments of the GDL variants. It is the most specialised member of the family.
summary: The inline, language-adjusted variant of Betania Patmos, combining decorative stroke detail with orthographic specificity.
description: Betania Patmos In GDL sits at the intersection of the family's inline treatment and its language-specific glyph set, serving a very particular publishing niche. The decorative inline strokes read best at large sizes, consistent with its display-only positioning. This is a font for a defined audience with specialised typographic requirements rather than a general-purpose tool.

name: Beth Ellen
classification_notes: Beth Ellen is a casual, personal handwriting script with an intimate, unpolished character that mimics real pen-on-paper writing. The letterforms are irregular and loosely formed, emphasising authenticity over polish.
summary: A personal, intimate handwriting script that prioritises authentic irregularity over formal calligraphic structure.
description: Beth Ellen captures the unself-conscious quality of everyday handwriting, with naturally varying letterforms that feel genuinely personal rather than artificially polished. It suits informal correspondence-inspired design, greeting cards, and editorial contexts where warmth and personality are valued. Its single weight and casual nature mean it functions best as an accent face rather than a workhorse.

name: Bevan
classification_notes: Bevan is a bold, high-contrast display serif based on Victorian-era wood type and poster lettering. Its heavy strokes and pronounced serifs make it suited to large-scale headline use.
summary: A muscular Victorian-inspired display serif built for maximum impact at headline sizes.
description: Bevan draws on the tradition of nineteenth-century wood type poster lettering, delivering thick strokes, strong contrast, and sturdy slab-like serifs. It works well for posters, editorial headlines, and branding that needs historical weight and authority. Its support for Latin Extended and Vietnamese broadens its utility across multilingual projects.

name: BhuTuka Expanded One
classification_notes: BhuTuka Expanded One is a wide, single-weight display serif supporting both Gurmukhi and Latin scripts with a sturdy, upright construction. The expanded proportions give it a monumental, poster-ready character.
summary: A wide, sturdy display serif notable for its rare dual support of Gurmukhi and Latin scripts.
description: BhuTuka Expanded One fills a genuine gap by offering a bold, expanded display face that handles Gurmukhi script alongside Latin, making it particularly valuable for South Asian publishing and branding contexts. Its broad letterforms and confident weight read well at large sizes on posters and headlines. The single-weight offering limits versatility, but within its niche it is a purposeful and well-considered design.

name: Big Shoulders
classification_notes: Big Shoulders is a tall, narrow display sans-serif with strong industrial influences and a full nine-weight range. Its compressed proportions and upright geometry give it a distinctly utilitarian, American gothic character.
summary: A tall, compressed industrial display sans with a wide weight range and unmistakable American gothic character.
description: Big Shoulders takes cues from the condensed gothic lettering found on Chicago transit signage and early twentieth-century American commercial typography. The full weight spectrum from Thin to Black gives designers considerable expressive range while maintaining a coherent visual identity. It excels in headlines, posters, packaging, and branding where a bold, utilitarian edge is required.

name: Big Shoulders Inline
classification_notes: Big Shoulders Inline shares the same compressed industrial structure as Big Shoulders but adds inline strokes through the letterforms, introducing a decorative, engraved quality. The inline treatment enhances its vintage and signage appeal.
summary: The inline variant of Big Shoulders adds an engraved, decorative layer to its compressed American gothic framework.
description: Big Shoulders Inline retains every structural quality of the core family whilst introducing inline cuts that echo letterpress printing, engraved signage, and nineteenth-century display type. This makes it well suited to retro-flavoured branding, poster work, and packaging where a touch of craft or period detail is desirable. Like its siblings, it covers Latin Extended and Vietnamese, maintaining broad multilingual reach.

name: Big Shoulders Stencil
classification_notes: Big Shoulders Stencil applies stencil cuts to the compressed industrial sans structure of the Big Shoulders family, creating deliberate breaks in strokes for a utilitarian, military-inflected aesthetic. The full nine-weight range preserves the family's expressive range.
summary: A stencil-cut companion to Big Shoulders that channels military and industrial signage with its deliberate stroke breaks.
description: Big Shoulders Stencil introduces the characteristic gaps of stencil lettering into the family's tall, compressed gothic framework, lending it a rugged, utilitarian edge that suits military-inspired branding, packaging, and editorial headlines. The nine weights from Thin to Black mean the stencil effect can read as either delicate and fine or bold and imposing depending on context. It is a coherent and well-executed extension of the broader Big Shoulders system.

name: Bigelow Rules
classification_notes: Bigelow Rules is a single-weight decorative display face constructed from ruled lines and geometric ornamental elements, producing a retro, hand-lettered circus aesthetic. It is a highly specialised typeface with very limited functional range.
summary: A ruled-line novelty display face evoking circus posters and vintage fairground lettering.
description: Bigelow Rules constructs its letterforms from parallel rules and ornamental fills, giving it the feel of a nineteenth-century circus or carnival broadsheet. Its appeal is firmly decorative and highly contextual — it works in poster art, themed event branding, and retro packaging where the specific aesthetic is the point. Beyond these narrow applications it offers little versatility.

name: Bigshot One
classification_notes: Bigshot One is a single-weight display serif with heavy, bulbous letterforms and a playful, almost cartoonish character. The exaggerated stroke weight and compact proportions give it a loud, attention-grabbing personality.
summary: A single-weight display serif with heavy, cartoon-inflected letterforms built purely for visual impact.
description: Bigshot One leans into exaggeration, with thickened strokes and compact forms that recall comic book lettering and novelty signage. It is a one-note typeface whose single weight and limited script support restrict it to very specific decorative headline and poster contexts. Within those constraints it is bold and distinctive, but it will not suit nuanced or extended typographic work.

name: Bilbo
classification_notes: Bilbo is a lightweight formal script inspired by copperplate and italic calligraphic traditions, with flowing connecting strokes and an elegant, refined character. Its delicacy makes it suited to formal stationery rather than display headlines.
summary: A refined calligraphic script with flowing copperplate influences suited to formal and elegant applications.
description: Bilbo draws on the tradition of pen-drawn formal scripts, offering graceful, connected letterforms with a light touch that reads as sophisticated rather than decorative. It suits wedding stationery, packaging for luxury goods, feminine branding, and editorial contexts where elegance and craft are priorities. Its support for Latin Extended and Vietnamese adds practical multilingual range for a script of its type.

name: Bilbo Swash Caps
classification_notes: An ornate swash caps script with elaborate calligraphic flourishes and high contrast strokes. Designed primarily for decorative headline use with its uppercase swash characters as the centrepiece.
summary: A highly ornamental swash caps script built for decorative headlines and formal display settings.
description: Bilbo Swash Caps leans heavily on calligraphic tradition with its elaborate flourished uppercase letters and flowing lowercase. It suits wedding stationery, formal invitations, and luxury branding where maximum decorative impact is required. The single weight limits versatility, making it a one-role typeface.

name: BioRhyme
classification_notes: A contemporary slab serif with wide proportions, low contrast, and sturdy slab serifs that give it a bold editorial personality. The generous x-height and open spacing make it legible at text sizes despite its display character.
summary: A wide, characterful slab serif with genuine editorial presence and a broad weight range.
description: BioRhyme's robust slab serifs and wide proportions give it a confident, slightly retro editorial feel that works well in magazine layouts and feature headlines. Its extensive weight range from ExtraLight to ExtraBold provides real flexibility for typographic hierarchy. It is less suited to long-form body text but excels in display and mid-size editorial contexts.

name: BioRhyme Expanded
classification_notes: The expanded companion to BioRhyme, with dramatically wide letterforms and pronounced slab serifs that amplify its editorial and display qualities. Best suited to short, impactful text at large sizes.
summary: An aggressively wide slab serif designed to command attention at headline and poster scales.
description: BioRhyme Expanded pushes the proportions of the original family to their logical extreme, producing letterforms that dominate the horizontal axis with authority. It is a natural choice for bold editorial headlines, posters, and packaging where typographic drama is the goal. The limited weight range and extreme width make it unsuitable for body copy or tight spaces.

name: Birthstone
classification_notes: A flowing, upright-to-slightly-cursive formal script with smooth, continuous stroke connections and moderate contrast. It draws on copperplate and penmanship traditions without being rigidly historical.
summary: A refined formal script with smooth connections and a graceful pen-drawn quality.
description: Birthstone captures the elegance of traditional penmanship with a contemporary lightness, making it appropriate for high-end branding, wedding stationery, and cosmetic packaging. Its clean stroke transitions and balanced proportions keep it legible at moderate display sizes. The single Regular weight keeps it focused on decorative headline use.

name: Birthstone Bounce
classification_notes: A bouncy, informal handwritten script with irregular baseline movement and a lively rhythm that distinguishes it from its more formal sibling. The two-weight range adds a small degree of typographic flexibility.
summary: A lively bouncing script with an informal handwritten energy that suits casual branding and display use.
description: Birthstone Bounce introduces deliberate baseline variation and a more relaxed pen movement compared to Birthstone, giving it a spontaneous, approachable character. It works well for children's products, informal packaging, social media graphics, and any context requiring a handmade feel at display size. The addition of a Medium weight offers a modest range for visual contrast.

name: Biryani
classification_notes: A humanist sans-serif with Devanagari support, featuring open apertures and gentle stroke modulation that aligns the Latin and Devanagari scripts harmoniously. Designed for multilingual use across digital and print media.
summary: A practical humanist sans with strong Devanagari and Latin support, built for multilingual publishing.
description: Biryani's primary distinction is its thoughtful integration of Devanagari and Latin scripts, with matching optical weight and spacing conventions across both. The generous weight range from ExtraLight to Black makes it a versatile workhorse for editorial, UI, and screen applications. Its open, clean forms ensure reliable legibility at small sizes in both scripts.

name: Bitcount
classification_notes: A conceptual display typeface built from a dot-matrix or pixel grid structure, where letterforms are constructed from circular or square modules. The varied weights are achieved by altering the density and fill of the grid rather than stroke width.
summary: A modular pixel-grid typeface that translates dot-matrix aesthetics into a refined contemporary display family.
description: Bitcount is a systematic exploration of letterform construction through a fixed circular-dot grid, where weight variation emerges from how densely the grid is populated rather than from stroke manipulation. This gives it a distinctive retro-digital character rooted in LED and dot-matrix display traditions. It is best deployed in contexts that celebrate its modular logic — technology branding, editorial design, and expressive headline work.

name: Bitcount Grid Double
classification_notes: A variant of the Bitcount system using a double grid structure, creating letterforms with greater perceived resolution and a slightly more refined dot-matrix appearance. The double grid adds visual density and a distinctive hatched quality to heavier weights.
summary: A double-grid variant of Bitcount that produces denser, more intricate pixel-based letterforms with a hatched visual texture.
description: Bitcount Grid Double uses two overlapping dot grids to build its characters, resulting in a more complex visual structure than the single-grid version and a greater sense of tonal richness at larger sizes. The heavier weights in particular develop a dense, almost mechanical texture that makes them striking for poster and screen display use. Like the rest of the Bitcount family, its modular DNA makes it ideal for technology, editorial, and design-forward branding contexts.

name: Bitcount Grid Double Ink
classification_notes: Constructed from a double-cell grid of dot or pixel units, giving each letterform a structured, bitmap-like appearance with additional visual density. The 'Ink' variant adds filled or bleed effects that simulate ink spread within the grid.
summary: A double-grid pixel typeface with an ink-bleed variant that pushes retrofuturist bitmap aesthetics into refined typographic territory.
description: Bitcount Grid Double Ink uses a double-cell dot matrix structure to create letterforms that feel simultaneously digital and tactile, with the ink effect adding organic warmth to an otherwise mechanical system. The wide weight range makes it surprisingly versatile across headline, poster, and branding applications. It suits tech-adjacent brands, editorial projects, and any context where retro digital culture needs to be invoked with craft.

name: Bitcount Grid Single
classification_notes: Built on a single-cell grid of discrete dot units, producing clean, legible bitmap-style letterforms with a systematic, almost monospaced visual rhythm. Each character is constructed with strict grid fidelity, emphasising structure over expression.
summary: A single-cell grid pixel typeface that distils the bitmap aesthetic to its cleanest, most systematic form.
description: Bitcount Grid Single renders each letterform as a precise arrangement of dots on a strict single-unit grid, delivering a crisp and unambiguous digital character. The result is highly readable at display sizes and carries strong associations with early computing and screen culture. It works well for tech branding, editorial headlines, and any design language rooted in digital history.

name: Bitcount Grid Single Ink
classification_notes: Combines the strict single-cell grid structure with an ink-bleed treatment that softens dot edges and creates the impression of letterpress or screen-printed output. The tension between geometric precision and organic spread gives it a distinctive dual character.
summary: A single-grid pixel typeface whose ink variant introduces organic warmth into an otherwise rigidly systematic construction.
description: Bitcount Grid Single Ink takes the clean dot-grid framework of its base counterpart and layers on an ink-spread effect, blurring boundaries between dots to evoke printed or stamped reproduction. This makes it feel more tactile and less purely digital than the standard Grid Single, opening it up to uses in packaging, print branding, and handcrafted editorial contexts. The broad weight range ensures it performs from delicate thin settings through to heavy display use.

name: Bitcount Ink
classification_notes: A standalone ink-effect variant within the Bitcount family that applies bleed and spread treatments to pixel-constructed letterforms without the explicit grid overlay, producing a softer and more expressive bitmap aesthetic. Dot structures bleed into one another, simulating physical printing imperfections.
summary: Bitcount Ink strips back the rigid grid to foreground the tactile ink-bleed effect, producing the most organic and expressive member of the Bitcount family.
description: Without the hard grid lines of its sibling variants, Bitcount Ink lets the ink-spread effect dominate, giving letterforms a warm, almost handmade quality despite their fundamentally digital construction. The smearing and blending of dot units evokes risograph printing, rubber stamps, or aged screen output. It is best suited to poster work, expressive branding, and editorial projects where character and texture matter more than precision.

name: Bitcount Prop Double
classification_notes: A proportionally spaced variant of the Bitcount system using a double-cell dot structure, allowing letterforms to occupy natural widths rather than fixed monospaced slots. The double-cell construction adds visual weight and complexity to each character.
summary: A proportionally spaced, double-dot-cell pixel typeface that brings natural spacing rhythm to the structured Bitcount system.
description: Bitcount Prop Double departs from the fixed grid of the Grid variants by allowing each character to take its natural proportional width, producing text that sits more comfortably in flowing headline and display settings. The double-cell dot construction adds density and presence, making it feel more substantial than single-cell alternatives. It suits display typography where pixel aesthetics are desired without the rigid fixed-width feel of a monospaced system.

name: Bitcount Prop Double Ink
classification_notes: Merges the proportional spacing of the Prop family with a double-cell dot construction and an ink-bleed effect, combining typographic naturalness with tactile, printed warmth. The interplay between structured dots and organic spread gives it the richest texture in the proportional subfamily.
summary: Proportional spacing and a double-cell ink-bleed effect combine to make Bitcount Prop Double Ink the most textured and expressive proportional variant in the family.
description: By layering ink-spread onto the already dense double-cell dot structure and freeing characters to their natural proportions, this variant achieves a richly physical quality unusual in digitally constructed typefaces. Dots bleed and merge in ways that recall screen printing or letterpress on textured stock, while proportional spacing keeps it comfortable for headline runs. It excels in branding, poster design, and editorial work where warmth and character are as important as legibility.

name: Bitcount Prop Single
classification_notes: A proportionally spaced, single-cell dot-grid typeface offering clean bitmap letterforms with natural character widths. The single-cell structure keeps forms light and open, while proportional spacing aids readability in display text settings.
summary: The cleanest and most readable of the proportional Bitcount variants, using a single-cell dot structure with natural character spacing.
description: Bitcount Prop Single applies the minimal single-cell grid logic to a proportionally spaced system, producing pixel letterforms that are open, airy, and highly legible at headline sizes. The absence of ink effects keeps it crisp and precise, making it the most versatile variant for contexts where the pixel aesthetic must remain clear and unambiguous. It is well suited to tech-focused branding, data visualisation headings, and screen-forward editorial design.

name: Bitcount Prop Single Ink
classification_notes: Combines proportional spacing and single-cell dot construction with an ink-bleed treatment, softening the pixel grid into something that feels more printed than rendered. It occupies the middle ground between the rigid digital feel of the clean variants and the heavier organic texture of the double-cell ink versions.
summary: Proportional single-cell dots meet ink-bleed softness in a variant that balances pixel clarity with organic, printed warmth.
description: Bitcount Prop Single Ink takes the open, readable structure of Prop Single and introduces an ink-spread effect that gently merges dot edges, lending the typeface a handcrafted, printed quality without sacrificing legibility. This makes it more approachable and warmer than its purely geometric counterparts, well suited to contexts where the retro-digital aesthetic needs a human touch. It works particularly well in branding, packaging, and editorial headlines where personality is essential.

name: Bitcount Single
classification_notes: Bitcount Single renders letterforms using a single-weight dot grid, mimicking LED or pixel matrix displays with a deliberate lo-fi aesthetic. Each character is constructed from discrete circular or square nodes arranged on a fixed bitmap-style grid.
summary: A dot-matrix display typeface built on a strict pixel grid, evoking vintage LED signage and early computer screens.
description: Bitcount Single uses a single-strand construction across all weights, achieving variation through dot size and spacing rather than stroke contrast. It suits retro-tech branding, digital art installations, and display headlines where a machine-made aesthetic is desirable. The extensive weight range makes it versatile despite its highly specific personality.

name: Bitcount Single Ink
classification_notes: Bitcount Single Ink shares the dot-grid matrix structure of Bitcount Single but applies an ink-trap or filled-node treatment that gives letters a slightly denser, more printed quality. The construction remains modular and pixel-derived throughout.
summary: The ink-filled counterpart to Bitcount Single, adding a richer, denser texture to the same dot-matrix letterforms.
description: Bitcount Single Ink takes the same pixel-grid scaffolding as its sibling but fills nodes more fully, producing a heavier visual impression that reads better at smaller display sizes. It works well in contexts where the lo-fi digital aesthetic needs a little more weight and presence, such as packaging, posters, or motion graphics. The pairing of both Bitcount variants allows for layered or contrasting typographic effects.

name: Bitter
classification_notes: Bitter is a contemporary slab serif designed specifically for sustained reading on screen, featuring sturdy serifs, open apertures, and generous x-height. Its letterforms draw on transitional proportions while optimising for pixel rendering across devices.
summary: A screen-optimised slab serif with the stamina for long-form reading and the character to hold its own in editorial settings.
description: Bitter was conceived from the outset for digital reading environments, making deliberate choices around stroke weight and serif structure to maintain clarity on low-resolution displays. Its Cyrillic support broadens its utility considerably for multilingual publishing projects. The variable font axis makes it particularly adaptable across responsive web layouts.

name: Black And White Picture
classification_notes: Black And White Picture is a Korean and Latin display face with a high-contrast, brush-influenced stroke treatment that creates a strong graphic, almost photographic black-and-white impact. Its single weight limits application to purely decorative headline use.
summary: A striking single-weight display typeface with extreme contrast designed to make Korean and Latin headlines command attention.
description: Black And White Picture pushes stroke contrast to dramatic extremes, creating letterforms that feel closer to graphic illustration than conventional typography. It is suited to editorial covers, poster headlines, and fashion-adjacent contexts where maximum visual impact matters more than extended readability. Its single-weight constraint makes it a specialist tool rather than a versatile workhorse.

name: Black Han Sans
classification_notes: Black Han Sans is a bold, single-weight Korean sans-serif with strong, uniform strokes and tight spacing engineered for maximum impact in headlines and titles. Its Latin characters echo the same heavy, condensed sans aesthetic as the Hangul.
summary: A heavy, punchy Korean sans-serif built for headline work where density and impact are the primary requirements.
description: Black Han Sans delivers a single, uncompromising weight that prioritises visual force over typographic flexibility. Its Hangul letterforms are tight and robust, sitting harmoniously alongside similarly weighted Latin text. It is well suited to poster design, app interfaces, and branding contexts targeting Korean-speaking audiences.

name: Black Ops One
classification_notes: Black Ops One features a chunky, stencil-inflected construction with military and industrial connotations, using flattened terminals and deliberate ink-trap cuts to reinforce its mechanical character. It operates convincingly at large display sizes only.
summary: A bold stencil-inflected display face with strong military and industrial overtones suited to game, action, and tech branding.
description: Black Ops One draws on military stencilling traditions, delivering letterforms that feel stamped rather than drawn. Its single bold weight makes it a focused headline instrument for game titles, action campaigns, and technical or tactical branding. The Cyrillic Extended support adds useful range for multilingual projects in the same genre.

name: Blaka
classification_notes: Blaka is a bold Arabic and Latin display typeface inspired by classical Kufi script traditions, rendered with thick, angular strokes that share visual kinship with Western blackletter forms. Its Latin companion is designed to harmonise with the assertive weight and geometric angularity of the Arabic.
summary: A bold, angular Arabic display typeface rooted in Kufi traditions, with a Latin companion that echoes its blackletter-like intensity.
description: Blaka brings the dramatic weight and geometric rigidity of classical Kufi calligraphy into a contemporary display setting, creating letterforms of considerable visual authority. Its affinity with blackletter aesthetics makes it compelling for cross-cultural poster and headline work where both scripts must carry equal typographic weight. It is a single-weight display face best reserved for titles and short bursts of text.

name: Blaka Hollow
classification_notes: Blaka Hollow is the inline or outlined variant of Blaka, retaining the same angular Kufi-inspired Arabic and blackletter-adjacent Latin structures but rendering strokes as open outlines rather than solid fills. This treatment significantly reduces apparent weight while preserving the dramatic formal qualities of the original.
summary: The hollow outline counterpart to Blaka, trading solid mass for an open, graphic quality that suits large-scale poster and display work.
description: Blaka Hollow preserves every angular detail of its sibling while inverting the relationship between stroke and space, producing a lighter yet equally dramatic effect. At large sizes the outlined forms create an almost architectural quality, making them effective for event posters, editorial spreads, and decorative overlays. Like Blaka, it is a specialist display face unsuited to body or UI text.

name: Blaka Ink
classification_notes: Blaka Ink combines Arabic Kufic blackletter-style forms with Latin counterparts, rendered with a distinctive ink-trap and rough-edged aesthetic. The design bridges Arabic and Latin scripts within a bold, decorative display tradition.
summary: A striking Arabic-Latin display face that fuses Kufic blackletter construction with an inky, textured character.
description: Blaka Ink brings a raw, hand-pressed quality to its bold angular forms, making it feel both ancient and contemporary. It is best suited to display contexts where cultural duality or bold decorative presence is required. Posters, editorial headers, and branding with a strong visual identity are natural applications.

name: Blinker
classification_notes: Blinker is a clean geometric sans-serif with a wide, open feel and relatively uniform stroke weight across its broad weight range. Its generous x-height and open apertures give it strong legibility at both display and text sizes.
summary: A versatile geometric sans-serif with a wide stance and a generous weight range suited to both headlines and body copy.
description: Blinker's clean construction and open letterforms make it a reliable workhorse for digital interfaces and print layouts alike. Its eight weights provide considerable flexibility, allowing a single typeface to carry an entire design system. It works particularly well in tech, editorial, and corporate branding contexts.

name: Bodoni Moda
classification_notes: Bodoni Moda is a contemporary revival of the classical Bodoni model, featuring extreme contrast between thick and thin strokes, flat unbracketed serifs, and a pronounced vertical axis. The design includes optical size variants and a variable font axis, making it more practically usable than many strict Bodoni revivals.
summary: A refined and highly usable contemporary revival of the Bodoni didone tradition, with optical sizes and variable font support.
description: Bodoni Moda captures the sharp elegance of the classical Bodoni model while addressing its notorious legibility limitations through optical size adjustments. The extreme stroke contrast lends itself naturally to luxury fashion, editorial covers, and high-end branding. Its variable font axis adds modern versatility rarely found in traditional revivals.

name: Bodoni Moda SC
classification_notes: Bodoni Moda SC is the small caps companion to Bodoni Moda, sharing the same high-contrast didone characteristics but with natively drawn small capitals throughout. This makes it better suited to formal typographic settings where small caps are required for running text or titles.
summary: The small caps variant of Bodoni Moda, offering natively drawn small capitals for refined typographic layouts.
description: Bodoni Moda SC provides properly proportioned small capitals rather than scaled-down full caps, preserving the optical balance expected in quality typography. It is ideal for book design, formal publications, and luxury editorial work where classical typographic conventions are valued. Paired with its sibling, it enables sophisticated multi-level hierarchies.

name: Bokor
classification_notes: Bokor is a decorative Khmer display face with bold, organic letterforms that have a hand-crafted, almost brush-like quality. Its Latin companion shares the same expressive weight and vernacular character.
summary: A bold Khmer display typeface with an expressive, hand-drawn quality suited to vernacular signage and poster work.
description: Bokor draws on the visual culture of Cambodian hand-lettering, giving it an authenticity that distinguishes it from more neutral Khmer text faces. Its strong, decorative forms make it most effective at large sizes in headlines, posters, and cultural branding contexts. The Latin companion maintains the same energy, ensuring visual cohesion across scripts.

name: Boldonse
classification_notes: Boldonse is a heavy, high-impact display serif with thick slab-like forms, exaggerated proportions, and a distinctly retro advertising feel. Its single Regular weight is optimised entirely for large-scale display use.
summary: A heavy retro display serif built for maximum impact on posters and advertising layouts.
description: Boldonse channels mid-twentieth-century commercial typography with its blunt, oversized forms and confident weight. It is a single-purpose display face that performs best at large sizes in poster, packaging, and headline contexts. Its limited weight offering means it suits projects where one strong typographic statement is the goal.

name: Bona Nova
classification_notes: Bona Nova is a humanist serif with moderate contrast, bracketed serifs, and a warmth derived from calligraphic origins. Its extensive multilingual support across Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts is a notable strength for scholarly and multilingual publishing.
summary: A warm humanist serif with broad multilingual support across Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin, designed for scholarly and editorial text.
description: Bona Nova balances classical serif warmth with practical text legibility, making it well suited to long-form reading in books, academic journals, and multilingual publications. Its Cyrillic and Greek character sets are carefully drawn rather than mechanically derived, giving the font credibility for serious typographic work across scripts. The regular and bold weights cover most text hierarchy needs capably.

name: Bona Nova SC
classification_notes: Bona Nova SC is the small caps companion to Bona Nova, maintaining the same humanist serif character with natively designed small capitals across its multilingual range. This variant is particularly valuable for formal academic and book typography requiring proper small cap treatment.
summary: The small caps companion to Bona Nova, extending its multilingual humanist serif quality to formal typographic applications requiring true small capitals.
description: Bona Nova SC provides natively drawn small caps across its Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek ranges, making it a serious tool for book designers and academic publishers. Proper small capitals preserve optical weight and proportion in a way that scaled full caps cannot, and their availability across multiple scripts is relatively rare. Paired with Bona Nova, it enables complete and sophisticated typographic systems for multilingual documents.

name: Bonbon
classification_notes: Bonbon is an ornate, heavily decorative script with exaggerated swashes and flourishes typical of Victorian-era lettering. Its extreme ornamentation makes it unsuitable for text use and limits it to very large display sizes.
summary: An extravagantly ornate display script laden with Victorian flourishes and swash letterforms.
description: Bonbon leans hard into decorative excess, with looping ascenders, sweeping tails, and elaborate connecting strokes that demand attention at large sizes. It suits novelty branding, greeting card headlines, or event invitations where maximum decorative impact is the goal. Legibility suffers quickly at smaller sizes, making it strictly a one-line showpiece.

name: Bonheur Royale
classification_notes: Bonheur Royale is an elegant, high-contrast formal script inspired by traditional copperplate calligraphy, with refined hairline upstrokes and weighty downstrokes. Its extended Latin and Vietnamese support broadens its appeal for multilingual luxury applications.
summary: A refined formal script drawing on copperplate tradition, delivering understated elegance with broad multilingual support.
description: Bonheur Royale captures the disciplined grace of copperplate penmanship, making it well suited to wedding stationery, luxury packaging, and high-end branding. Its Vietnamese character support sets it apart from many comparable scripts on Google Fonts. The single-weight offering limits versatility, but within its lane it performs with genuine class.

name: Boogaloo
classification_notes: Boogaloo is a casual, slightly condensed inline-style display sans with a retro hand-painted quality and rounded stroke endings. Its informality and personality make it immediately recognisable but unsuitable for serious typographic contexts.
summary: A fun, retro-flavoured display sans with hand-painted informality and a distinctly 1960s personality.
description: Boogaloo's chunky letterforms and slightly uneven strokes give it the feel of vintage poster lettering or children's book titles. It works well for food and beverage branding, casual event posters, and any context that calls for approachable, unpretentious energy. One weight only keeps it simple, and its single-style nature means it tires quickly across extended typographic systems.

name: Borel
classification_notes: Borel is designed specifically to model clear, legible French cursive handwriting as taught in schools, with careful attention to letterform consistency and natural pen rhythm. Unusually, it includes mathematical symbol support alongside extended Latin coverage.
summary: A pedagogically grounded cursive script modelled on French school handwriting, with the rare addition of maths symbol support.
description: Borel occupies an unusual niche, designed to faithfully replicate the style of cursive taught in French primary schools, making it genuinely useful for educational materials and children's publishing. Its inclusion of mathematical characters extends its reach into technical or scientific educational contexts. The legibility at text sizes is better than most script fonts, though its informal cursive character keeps it away from professional body copy.

name: Bowlby One
classification_notes: Bowlby One is a bold, heavy display typeface with wide, rounded letterforms and a strong geometric influence, designed for maximum impact at large sizes. It carries a friendly yet assertive character suited to bold headline applications.
summary: A heavy, rounded display face built for bold headlines that need presence without aggression.
description: Bowlby One delivers substantial visual weight through its broad, rounded letterforms and confident stroke widths, making it effective for posters, app branding, and punchy editorial headlines. Its friendly roundness softens what could otherwise be an intimidatingly bold presence. Available in a single weight, it functions best as a headline-only workhorse rather than part of a wider typographic system.

name: Bowlby One SC
classification_notes: Bowlby One SC is a small capitals variant of Bowlby One, extending its coverage to Latin Extended scripts and replacing lowercase with true small caps throughout. This gives it a more formal, monumental quality while retaining the original's bold rounded character.
summary: The small caps companion to Bowlby One, adding a monumental register and extended Latin script support to the same bold rounded design.
description: Bowlby One SC swaps the lowercase for well-proportioned small capitals, lending the design a more architectural, signage-ready quality that suits titling, logos, and display contexts requiring uniformity of height. Extended Latin support makes it more internationally versatile than its sibling. Like the original, it is a single-weight display-only typeface, best deployed sparingly for maximum effect.

name: Bpmf Huninn
classification_notes: Bpmf Huninn is a Traditional Chinese sans-serif designed with particular attention to the Bopomofo (注音符號) phonetic system used in Taiwan, offering clean, screen-optimised strokes for educational and interface use. Its Latin companion is harmonised in weight and proportion with the Chinese glyphs.
summary: A Taiwanese Traditional Chinese sans-serif with strong Bopomofo support, designed for readable screen and educational use.
description: Bpmf Huninn addresses the specific typographic needs of Taiwan's educational system, where Bopomofo annotations are essential for early readers and language learners. Its clean, upright stroke structure makes it practical for interfaces, apps, and digital publishing in Traditional Chinese. The Latin glyphs are thoughtfully matched to the CJK character weight, producing cohesive multilingual layouts.

name: Bpmf Iansui
classification_notes: Bpmf Iansui is a Traditional Chinese script-style typeface that emulates the informal, handwritten quality of everyday Chinese writing while retaining Bopomofo phonetic annotation support. It presents a warmer, more casual alternative to the structured sans-serif of Bpmf Huninn.
summary: A handwritten-style Traditional Chinese font with Bopomofo support, bringing warmth and informality to CJK typography.
description: Bpmf Iansui mimics the natural rhythm and slight irregularity of handwritten Chinese characters, making it appealing for children's educational materials, casual publishing, and branding that wants a personal, approachable feel. Its Bopomofo annotation capability keeps it functional within Taiwanese educational contexts where phonetic guidance is required. The script character distinguishes it clearly from utilitarian CJK system fonts, though this limits its use in formal or long-form text settings.

name: Bpmf Zihi Kai Std
classification_notes: A Traditional Chinese kaishu (regular script) inspired sans-serif designed for legibility across CJK and Latin characters. The strokes reflect calligraphic kai script conventions while remaining functional at text sizes.
summary: A Traditional Chinese typeface rooted in kaishu calligraphic structure, bridging CJK and Latin scripts with quiet authority.
description: Bpmf Zihi Kai Std draws on the classical kai script tradition to produce a typeface that feels grounded and culturally authentic. Its Latin characters are designed to harmonise with the Chinese glyphs rather than clash. Best suited to educational materials, traditional publishing, and any project requiring dignified bilingual Chinese–Latin typesetting.

name: Braah One
classification_notes: A single-weight sans-serif supporting Gurmukhi and Latin scripts, with clean, open letterforms that prioritise legibility. The design maintains consistent stroke weight and a modern, neutral character.
summary: A clean, single-weight sans-serif that gives Gurmukhi and Latin scripts a confident shared voice.
description: Braah One is designed for headlines and display use, offering a crisp, modern feel across both Gurmukhi and Latin character sets. Its uniform stroke weight and open apertures keep it highly readable even at large sizes. It suits branding, editorial headers, and digital interfaces where multilingual South Asian and Latin text must coexist gracefully.

name: Brawler
classification_notes: A sturdy text serif with moderate contrast and robust slab-like serifs, designed explicitly for long-form reading. Its chunky construction gives it a workhorse quality reminiscent of newspaper typography.
summary: A dependable text serif built for extended reading, with robust proportions that hold up well in print and on screen.
description: Brawler takes cues from traditional newspaper and book typefaces, prioritising legibility and endurance over stylistic flourish. Its sturdy serifs and moderate stroke contrast ensure that it performs reliably across a range of rendering environments. It is a solid choice for long-form editorial content, blogs, and any setting where reading comfort is paramount.

name: Bree Serif
classification_notes: Bree Serif features a distinctive italic-influenced upright construction, with angled stroke entries and an informal warmth that sets it apart from conventional text serifs. It carries a single weight, making it best suited to display and headline use.
summary: Bree Serif brings an upright italic sensibility to the serif genre, giving headlines a warm, personable energy.
description: Originally designed for RTÉ, Bree Serif's defining trait is its italic-derived letterforms — angled entry strokes and open curves lend it an approachable, slightly informal character unusual for a serif. Available in a single weight, it excels as a display and headline face rather than a text workhorse. It suits branding, advertising, and editorial contexts where personality and legibility need to coexist.

name: Bricolage Grotesque
classification_notes: Bricolage Grotesque is a contemporary grotesque with intentional irregularities and optical quirks that give it a handcrafted, characterful quality distinct from sanitised neo-grotesques. It spans a wide weight range and supports variable font technology.
summary: A characterful grotesque that embraces deliberate imperfection, giving it warmth and individuality within a modern sans-serif framework.
description: Bricolage Grotesque draws on the tradition of vernacular and hand-painted lettering to inject irregularity into an otherwise structured grotesque skeleton. Its wide weight range — from ExtraLight to ExtraBold — and variable font support make it versatile across scales. It is ideally suited to editorial design, brand identities, and digital interfaces that want a human edge without sacrificing clarity.

name: Bruno Ace
classification_notes: Bruno Ace is a techno-influenced display face with angular, geometric letterforms and a futuristic character. Its single weight and stylised construction make it a purely display-oriented type choice.
summary: A sharp, angular display face with a science-fiction edge, built for maximum impact at large sizes.
description: Bruno Ace takes geometric construction to a stylised extreme, with letterforms that suggest technological precision and forward momentum. It works best as a single-weight display face for posters, gaming, tech branding, and screen titles. Its unconventional character limits its versatility but makes it immediately distinctive in the right context.

name: Bruno Ace SC
classification_notes: Bruno Ace SC is the small capitals variant of Bruno Ace, sharing its angular, futuristic geometry but applying it uniformly across the character set in small caps form. It carries the same single-weight, display-only limitations.
summary: The small caps companion to Bruno Ace, delivering the same techno-geometric aesthetic in an all-caps typographic register.
description: Bruno Ace SC applies the angular, futuristic letterforms of Bruno Ace to a small capitals-based design, creating a slightly more uniform and controlled display voice. It suits logotypes, titles, and interface labels within tech, gaming, and sci-fi contexts. Like its sibling, it is a single-purpose display face with a narrow creative range.

name: Brygada 1918
designer: Mateusz Machalski, Borys Kosmynka, Joanna Chyżyńska, Ania Wieluńska
classification_notes: Brygada 1918 is a revival of a pre-war Polish typeface originally designed for the 1918 Polish independence period, featuring high contrast, sharp serifs, and a dignified old-style character. It supports Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts with broad weight coverage.
summary: A high-contrast serif revival steeped in early twentieth-century Polish typographic heritage, now extended across Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts.
description: Brygada 1918 draws directly from typefaces used in interwar Poland, preserving their sharp, elegant contrast and authoritative presence. Its revival for the Google Fonts library extends the original design across three major scripts, making it a rare historically grounded choice with genuine multilingual breadth. It excels in editorial headers, cultural institutions, book covers, and any context demanding typographic weight and heritage.

name: Bubblegum Sans
classification_notes: Rounded, bubbly letterforms with thick strokes and a casual, cheerful character. Evokes children's lettering and confectionery branding.
summary: A bouncy, rounded display typeface with an unabashedly cheerful personality suited to children's content and playful branding.
description: Bubblegum Sans delivers thick, softly rounded letterforms that feel like they belong on a sweet shop window or a children's book cover. Its exaggerated curves and casual rhythm make it instantly approachable. Best reserved for short headlines, logos, and playful editorial contexts where warmth and fun are the priority.

name: Bubbler One
classification_notes: An extremely thin, airy single-weight sans with wide, open letterforms and minimal stroke contrast. The spacious tracking gives it a light, almost ethereal quality.
summary: A single-weight geometric sans of notable lightness and openness, lending itself to airy, elegant headline treatments.
description: Bubbler One operates at an unusually restrained weight, with wide proportions and generous spacing that give text a floating, barely-there quality. It works well in large display settings where its delicacy can be appreciated, particularly in editorial or fashion contexts. Its single-weight limitation makes it unsuitable for body text or any context requiring typographic hierarchy.

name: Buda
classification_notes: An unusual, highly stylised light-weight display face with organic, almost hand-drawn curves and eccentric letterforms. Its singular character resists easy categorisation.
summary: A peculiar and expressive light display face with organic curves that feels hand-crafted and genuinely idiosyncratic.
description: Buda occupies a curious niche as a light display typeface whose letterforms carry an almost illustrative, hand-drawn quality. Its eccentric details make it memorable but limit its versatility to very specific tonal contexts. It suits boutique branding, artistic posters, or editorial headlines where distinctiveness is prized over legibility at small sizes.

name: Buenard
classification_notes: A warm humanist serif with moderate contrast and slightly bracketed serifs, offering good readability across body and display sizes. The range of weights adds useful versatility.
summary: A reliable humanist serif with a warm personality and a solid weight range, well suited to both body text and mid-sized headlines.
description: Buenard draws on humanist serif traditions to produce a typeface that feels approachable without sacrificing typographic rigour. Its moderate stroke contrast and well-proportioned letterforms hold up comfortably at text sizes, making it a dependable choice for editorial work, longer-form web content, and branding that needs a grounded, readable serif. The inclusion of SemiBold and Bold weights gives designers useful tools for establishing hierarchy.

name: Bungee
classification_notes: A bold, condensed, vertically oriented display typeface inspired by signpainting and urban wayfinding. Strong geometric structure with flat-topped, compressed letterforms designed for stacking and vertical setting.
summary: A striking display grotesque inspired by urban signpainting, built for vertical text, stacked lettering, and bold environmental applications.
description: Bungee was designed by David Jonathan Ross with signage and architectural lettering firmly in mind, featuring a compressed, flat-topped structure that performs brilliantly when set vertically or stacked in columns. Its industrial confidence makes it an excellent choice for wayfinding systems, poster headlines, and branding that needs authority and presence. The broader Bungee family extends its versatility with inline, outline, shade, and hairline variants that reward layering.

name: Bungee Hairline
classification_notes: The hairline variant of Bungee, retaining all structural characteristics of the original but replacing filled strokes with ultra-thin outlines. Produces a delicate, almost ghostly version of the bold parent.
summary: The ultra-thin outline member of the Bungee family, offering an ethereal counterpoint to its bold siblings for layered or high-impact typographic work.
description: Bungee Hairline preserves every structural feature of the original Bungee while reducing its forms to the thinnest possible outlines, creating an airy, engraved quality that contrasts strikingly with the filled variants. It is most effective when layered with other Bungee styles or used at very large sizes where its delicate strokes register clearly. Standalone use at headline sizes can appear insubstantial, so careful sizing and contrast management are essential.

name: Bungee Inline
classification_notes: An inline variant of Bungee featuring a white stripe cut through the letterform strokes to simulate dimensionality and a classic sign-painting effect. Bold and decorative in character.
summary: The inline variant of Bungee brings classic sign-painter dimensionality to the family, with a white channel cut through each stroke for instant visual pop.
description: Bungee Inline applies a traditional inline treatment to the compressed grotesque forms of the Bungee family, splitting each stroke with a central highlight that evokes vintage signage and mid-century display lettering. The effect is bold and decorative, lending itself to posters, event graphics, and packaging where a retro-inflected sense of craft is desirable. Like all Bungee variants, it shines at large sizes and in vertical or stacked compositions.

name: Bungee Outline
classification_notes: An outline-only variant of Bungee where letterforms are rendered as hollow, unstroked contours. Produces a strong graphic silhouette with none of the filled weight of the primary typeface.
summary: The outline member of the Bungee family strips letterforms back to their bare contours, offering a graphic, open alternative for layered display work.
description: Bungee Outline renders the family's compressed grotesque forms as pure outlines, creating an open, airy effect that works particularly well when layered over colour fields or beneath the filled Bungee Regular. The absence of internal fill makes it well suited to packaging, poster design, and typographic compositions where transparency and depth are desirable. On its own it reads as light and graphic; combined with other Bungee styles it contributes to richly dimensional typographic arrangements.

name: Bungee Shade
classification_notes: Bungee Shade is a vertical inline display typeface derived from the Bungee family, featuring a bold shadow or shade layer that creates a strong three-dimensional effect. The letterforms are rooted in American signage and architectural lettering traditions.
summary: A bold inline display face with a dramatic shade layer that channels classic American signage lettering.
description: Bungee Shade takes the architectural, vertically-oriented letterforms of the Bungee family and adds a pronounced shadow layer, giving text an unmistakable depth and presence. It is best suited to large-scale headline and poster work where maximum visual impact is the goal. Its retro-signage character makes it a strong choice for branding projects seeking a bold, Americana-inflected identity.

name: Bungee Spice
classification_notes: Bungee Spice is a chromatic variable font from the Bungee family, designed to be rendered in multiple colours using OpenType COLR/CPAL technology, producing a layered, polychrome effect. Its bold, compact letterforms are rooted in American urban signage.
summary: A chromatic display typeface that leverages colour font technology to deliver vivid, multi-layered lettering straight from the file.
description: Bungee Spice extends the Bungee family into colour font territory, using the COLR/CPAL specification to render layered hues without any manual separation of artwork. The result is immediate, eye-catching text that recalls hand-painted fairground and circus lettering. It is a specialist tool for digital-first posters, social media graphics, and any project where flamboyant colour typography is the centrepiece.

name: Bungee Tint
classification_notes: Bungee Tint is a layered display variant in the Bungee family featuring a hairline inline cut through the solid letterforms, creating a distinctive tinted or semi-filled appearance. It sits between the fully solid Bungee and the more elaborate Shade and Spice variants.
summary: A tinted inline variant of Bungee that adds a hairline inner stroke to its bold signage letterforms for a lighter, layered effect.
description: Bungee Tint uses a fine inline cut through its chunky, vertically-oriented capitals to create a visual distinction between the outline and the interior of each letter. This gives it a slightly lighter feel than the fully filled Bungee while retaining the family's urban, architectural character. It works well in layered typographic compositions alongside other Bungee variants, and is suited to headline and poster contexts where nuanced contrast is welcome.

name: Butcherman
classification_notes: Butcherman is a decorative display face inspired by Victorian circus and horror-genre lettering, featuring elaborately ornamented capitals with carved or engraved-style details. The letterforms are heavy and theatrical, evoking a gothic fairground aesthetic.
summary: A theatrical Victorian-horror display face dripping with ornamental detail and gothic fairground atmosphere.
description: Butcherman draws on the richly decorated woodtype and engraved letterforms of nineteenth-century circus and horror ephemera, packing its capitals with internal hatching, serifs, and embellishments. Its applications are firmly in the realm of novelty and theming — Halloween branding, horror-genre titles, carnival-themed events, and similarly atmospheric contexts. Outside those niches it offers very little versatility, functioning as a pure one-trick showpiece.

name: Butterfly Kids
classification_notes: Butterfly Kids is a loose, childlike script typeface with irregular, hand-drawn letterforms that mimic a child's handwriting, featuring uneven baselines and rounded strokes. It has a deliberately naive and playful quality.
summary: A wobbly, childlike handwriting script designed to evoke innocence and spontaneous playfulness.
description: Butterfly Kids mimics the unpredictable, endearing quality of a young child's handwriting, with letterforms that bounce off the baseline and vary in stroke weight and angle. Its warmth and informality make it appropriate for children's book covers, greeting cards, educational materials, and playful branding aimed at young audiences. Legibility is limited at small sizes, so it functions almost exclusively as a display and headline face.

name: Bytesized
classification_notes: Bytesized is a pixel-grid-inspired sans-serif designed to evoke the aesthetic of early bitmap screen fonts, with geometric, modular letterforms built on a coarse grid. It occupies the niche between retro digital display and contemporary screen culture.
summary: A pixel-art-inspired display sans that channels the charm of early computer and video game typography.
description: Bytesized constructs its letterforms on a visible grid logic, giving the impression of enlarged bitmap fonts from vintage home computers and arcade games. It appeals to projects steeped in retro gaming culture, tech nostalgia, and lo-fi digital aesthetics. Practical use is largely confined to headlines, logos, and short display bursts rather than sustained reading.

name: Cabin
classification_notes: Cabin is a humanist sans-serif with subtle geometric influences, featuring open apertures, moderate contrast, and letterforms that balance warmth with professional neutrality. It draws on the tradition of faces like Gill Sans and Johnston.
summary: A dependable humanist sans with enough warmth to feel approachable and enough neutrality to function as a reliable workhorse.
description: Cabin sits comfortably in the humanist sans tradition, offering open counters and a slightly informal personality that suits both screen interfaces and print body text. Its four weights provide a practical range for establishing typographic hierarchy without needing to reach for supplementary typefaces. It is a solid, unshowy choice for editorial design, app interfaces, and corporate communications that want friendliness without sacrificing professionalism.

name: Cabin Condensed
classification_notes: Cabin Condensed is the narrowed variant of the Cabin family, retaining the humanist warmth and open apertures of the parent typeface while occupying significantly less horizontal space. It shares the same four-weight range as Cabin.
summary: The condensed companion to Cabin, offering the same humanist character in a narrower format suited to space-constrained settings.
description: Cabin Condensed preserves the approachable, open quality of the Cabin family while reducing the horizontal footprint of each character, making it well suited to narrow columns, navigation labels, and headline settings where space is at a premium. Its four weights give it sufficient range for hierarchical typesetting within a condensed grid. It pairs naturally with the regular-width Cabin for a coherent, flexible typographic system.

name: Cabin Sketch
classification_notes: Cabin Sketch renders the letterforms of the Cabin sans-serif in a hand-drawn, pencil-sketch style with visible stroke lines and open counters. The effect is casual and illustrative rather than typographic in a traditional sense.
summary: A hand-drawn sketch interpretation of Cabin that trades polish for a casual, illustrative personality.
description: Cabin Sketch applies a pencil-stroke texture to the familiar Cabin sans-serif structure, giving it a rough, handcrafted quality that suits informal branding, posters, and editorial headers. Its legibility holds at large sizes but deteriorates quickly at small scales. Best reserved for short display settings where the sketched texture reads as a deliberate stylistic choice.

name: Cactus Classical Serif
classification_notes: Cactus Classical Serif is a high-contrast serif designed primarily for Traditional Chinese text, drawing on classical brushstroke proportions with Cyrillic and Latin companions. The Latin component follows oldstyle serif conventions with moderate contrast and bracketed serifs.
summary: A classically inspired serif built first for Traditional Chinese, with harmonious Cyrillic and Latin support.
description: Cactus Classical Serif prioritises the structural demands of Traditional Chinese typography, borrowing calligraphic contrast and stroke rhythm from historical brush forms. Its Latin and Cyrillic companions are designed to sit comfortably alongside Chinese text without dominating or clashing. This makes it particularly suited to bilingual publishing, academic texts, and long-form reading environments.

name: Caesar Dressing
classification_notes: Caesar Dressing is a heavily stylised display face evoking ancient Roman stone-carved letterforms, with thick serifs, pronounced wedge terminals, and decorative internal detailing. The overall effect is theatrical and period-specific.
summary: A theatrical Roman-inspired display face that leans hard into ancient stone-carving aesthetics.
description: Caesar Dressing borrows from classical Roman inscriptional lettering but amplifies the drama with bold proportions and ornamental flourishes, making it unmistakably a display novelty. It works well for themed restaurant branding, historical event posters, or costume packaging where period atmosphere is the goal. Its usability is strictly limited to large, short-form settings.

name: Cagliostro
classification_notes: Cagliostro is a single-weight geometric sans-serif with clean, open forms and even stroke widths that suggest mid-century European influences. It has a calm, unassuming character with moderate letter spacing.
summary: A quiet, single-weight geometric sans with mid-century European sensibility and clean open forms.
description: Cagliostro offers a restrained geometric personality that works comfortably for logotypes, short headlines, and light interface applications. The lack of weight variants limits its typographic range considerably, making it more useful as an accent or display face than a workhorse text family. Its even tone and open apertures give it a friendly approachability without veering into novelty territory.

name: Cairo
classification_notes: Cairo is a contemporary humanist sans-serif designed for Arabic and Latin scripts, with carefully harmonised proportions between the two writing systems. The Arabic component follows Naskh conventions while the Latin is clean and modern.
summary: A well-crafted bilingual humanist sans that achieves genuine harmony between Arabic and Latin scripts.
description: Cairo is one of the more successful attempts at a unified Arabic–Latin type family on Google Fonts, with both scripts sharing similar optical weight and proportional logic. Its broad weight range from ExtraLight to Black makes it a genuinely versatile workhorse for digital products, editorial layouts, and branding that must function across Arabic and Latin-script languages. The clean, neutral forms ensure it does not call undue attention to itself in text settings.

name: Cairo Play
classification_notes: Cairo Play extends the Cairo family as a variable font, offering continuous interpolation across the weight axis with the same dual Arabic–Latin design philosophy. The variable format adds flexibility for responsive and animated digital use.
summary: The variable-font companion to Cairo, offering continuous weight control across both Arabic and Latin scripts.
description: Cairo Play delivers all the design qualities of Cairo in a variable format, giving designers fine-grained control over weight for responsive web typography, fluid interfaces, and animation. The Arabic and Latin scripts remain harmonised across the full variable axis, which is a technically impressive achievement. It is an excellent choice for digital products serving multilingual Arabic and Latin-script audiences.

name: Cal Sans
classification_notes: Cal Sans is a single-weight geometric sans-serif with wide, bold proportions and rounded terminals, clearly influenced by the Cal.com brand identity from which it originates. It has strong optical weight and an open, confident character.
summary: A bold, wide geometric sans originally created for the Cal.com brand, released as an open-source display face.
description: Cal Sans was designed as a bespoke brand typeface before being made freely available, which gives it a more considered personality than many single-weight novelty faces. Its wide, confident proportions and geometric clarity make it well suited to software product branding, startup identity work, and large-scale headlines. The single weight limits its utility, but within its intended role as a display and headline face it performs with distinction.

name: Caladea
classification_notes: Caladea is a metric-compatible alternative to Cambria, sharing its proportions and general transitional serif structure with moderate contrast and sturdy serifs optimised for screen rendering. It was designed specifically to substitute Cambria in open-source environments.
summary: A screen-optimised transitional serif designed as a metric-compatible open-source alternative to Cambria.
description: Caladea was created to give LibreOffice and open-source document users a high-quality drop-in replacement for Microsoft's Cambria, sharing the same character widths and spacing. Its moderate contrast, sturdy serifs, and careful hinting make it a reliable choice for long-form document typography and body text on screen. While it lacks the refinement of premium text faces, it is a solid, dependable workhorse for practical publishing needs.

name: Calistoga
classification_notes: Calistoga is a chunky, ink-trap-influenced display serif with rounded terminals and a warm, approachable character. Its letterforms draw on early twentieth-century American wood type with a modern sensibility.
summary: A warm, chunky display serif with wood-type energy and ink-trap detailing that gives it both personality and historical depth.
description: Calistoga draws on early American display type traditions, delivering bold, rounded letterforms that feel at once nostalgic and contemporary. It suits headlines, posters, and branding projects that need character without resorting to outright novelty. Its Latin Extended and Vietnamese coverage makes it a more versatile option than many display faces of its kind.

name: Calligraffitti
classification_notes: Calligraffitti mimics informal hand-lettering with an irregular, graffiti-influenced baseline and loose stroke rhythm. It reads as spontaneous and urban rather than polished or calligraphic.
summary: A rough, graffiti-styled casual script that trades elegance for urban spontaneity.
description: Calligraffitti captures the energy of marker-written street lettering, with deliberately uneven strokes and an informal rhythm that signals youth and irreverence. It works best at large sizes for short bursts of text — event posters, social graphics, or anything requiring a handmade edge. Its limited Latin-only coverage and single weight restrict its versatility considerably.

name: Cambay
classification_notes: Cambay is a humanist sans-serif designed specifically to support both Devanagari and Latin scripts in harmonious combination, with open apertures and a legible, text-optimised structure. Its forms are clean and functional without being cold.
summary: A practical humanist sans built for bilingual Devanagari and Latin typesetting, with solid legibility at text sizes.
description: Cambay was designed with the specific challenge of harmonising two visually distinct script systems, making it a thoughtful choice for publications, interfaces, and documents serving Hindi and English readers simultaneously. Its open letterforms and moderate contrast hold up well in body text across both scripts. The available Regular and Bold weights cover basic typographic hierarchy, though the family would benefit from more weights.

name: Cambo
classification_notes: Cambo is a sturdy transitional serif with moderate contrast, bracketed serifs, and an upright, somewhat formal disposition. It has a classical structure but slightly heavier strokes that aid screen legibility.
summary: A dependable single-weight transitional serif with classical proportions suited to long-form reading.
description: Cambo sits comfortably in the transitional tradition, with well-balanced letterforms and moderate contrast that make it a reliable choice for editorial and book-style typesetting. Its single Regular weight limits its utility for complex typographic systems, but within that constraint it performs consistently at both text and small display sizes. Latin Extended support broadens its reach across European languages.

name: Candal
classification_notes: Candal is a single-weight, bold geometric sans with tight spacing and strong, uniform stroke widths that give it a confident, signage-ready presence. Its forms are simple and highly legible at distance.
summary: A bold, single-weight geometric sans built for impact at large sizes and signage applications.
description: Candal's heavy, even strokes and open geometry make it well suited to headlines, wayfinding, and branding contexts where clarity at a distance is essential. Its single weight is a limitation for complex typographic systems, but within that role it delivers consistent, confident letterforms. The Latin-only coverage keeps it focused on Western European applications.

name: Cantarell
classification_notes: Cantarell is a humanist sans-serif originally developed as the default interface typeface for the GNOME desktop environment, with clean, open forms optimised for screen rendering at small sizes. Its design balances friendliness with functional neutrality.
summary: Designed as the GNOME desktop's default UI font, Cantarell is a clean, screen-optimised humanist sans that performs reliably at small sizes.
description: Cantarell was purpose-built for digital interfaces, making it an honest choice for web and application typography where clarity at small sizes is paramount. Its humanist construction gives it warmth without sacrificing the neutrality needed in UI contexts. Available in Regular and Bold, it covers essential weight contrast for interface hierarchies, though its range is modest compared to more comprehensive system typefaces.

name: Cantata One
classification_notes: Cantata One is a single-weight serif with strong contrast, wedge-shaped serifs, and an expressive, slightly calligraphic structure that gives it a distinctive editorial quality. It reads as confident and somewhat formal.
summary: A high-contrast, wedge-serif display face with calligraphic roots and strong editorial presence.
description: Cantata One's pronounced thick-thin contrast and wedge serifs give it a robust, structured personality suited to editorial headlines, book covers, and display contexts that call for something more characterful than a neutral text serif. Its single weight means it functions best as a headline or accent face rather than a workhorse body font. Latin Extended support gives it reasonable coverage across European languages.

name: Cantora One
classification_notes: Cantora One is a rounded, single-weight sans-serif with softened terminals and a friendly, informal disposition that sits somewhere between geometric and humanist traditions. Its even stroke weight and open forms give it a welcoming, approachable quality.
summary: A soft, rounded single-weight sans with an approachable, informal character suited to friendly branding and display use.
description: Cantora One's rounded terminals and even stroke weight make it a natural fit for children's content, casual branding, food packaging, and any context that benefits from a warm, unpretentious tone. Its single weight limits its use in complex typographic systems, but its clean geometry works well at display sizes. Latin Extended support extends its usability across a range of European languages.

name: Caprasimo
classification_notes: Caprasimo is a bold, chunky display serif with exaggerated proportions and a playful, retro character. Its thick strokes and rounded details give it a friendly yet impactful personality suited to headline use.
summary: A chunky, characterful display serif with retro warmth and bold presence.
description: Caprasimo brings a nostalgic, almost hand-lettered energy to display typography through its exaggerated proportions and generous stroke weight. It works well for logotypes, packaging, and editorial headlines where a friendly yet confident tone is required. Its single Regular weight limits versatility but makes its intent refreshingly clear.

name: Capriola
classification_notes: Capriola is a humanist sans-serif with subtly rounded terminals that lend it a warm, approachable quality without tipping into overt playfulness. Its balanced proportions make it functional across both display and text sizes.
summary: A softly rounded humanist sans that balances friendliness with quiet professionalism.
description: Capriola's gently rounded stroke endings give it an accessible, contemporary feel that suits digital interfaces, editorial design, and brand communication where warmth is valued. It performs reliably at body sizes on screen, maintaining legibility without feeling sterile. The single-weight offering restricts its range but its even rhythm makes it a solid choice for clean, approachable layouts.

name: Caramel
classification_notes: Caramel is an elegant, high-contrast display script with flowing cursive strokes and refined connecting letterforms inspired by traditional calligraphy. Its decorative swashes and delicate thin strokes give it a luxury aesthetic.
summary: An elegant high-contrast cursive script with luxury calligraphic flair.
description: Caramel draws on classical penmanship traditions, offering flowing connections and refined stroke contrast that read as sophisticated and upmarket. It suits wedding stationery, premium packaging, fashion branding, and editorial accents where a sense of handcrafted elegance is desired. As with most display scripts, it is best reserved for short headlines and logotypes rather than extended text.

name: Carattere
classification_notes: Carattere is a formal upright script with strong calligraphic structure and pronounced stroke contrast, sitting closer to copperplate traditions than casual brush lettering. Its precise letterforms give it a polished, ceremonial quality.
summary: A refined upright calligraphic script with copperplate-influenced precision and elegance.
description: Carattere takes its cues from formal penmanship, delivering high stroke contrast and disciplined letterforms that feel ceremonial and authoritative. It is well suited to luxury branding, invitations, certificates, and editorial display contexts where a traditional yet polished script is called for. Its upright stance distinguishes it from more typical slanted scripts, lending an unusual formality.

name: Cardo
classification_notes: Cardo is a scholarly humanist serif designed for classical and multilingual academic publishing, with careful attention to Greek, Latin, and extended Unicode character sets. Its modestly high contrast and open apertures prioritise readability in dense scholarly text.
summary: A scholarly humanist serif built for multilingual academic and classical text setting.
