description: Cardo was designed specifically to meet the needs of classicists, biblical scholars, and linguists, with extensive support for ancient scripts, polytonic Greek, and a wide range of diacritics. Its clean, legible forms hold up well in long-form body text, particularly in academic journals, critical editions, and digital humanities projects. It is one of the more principled and purposeful text faces available on Google Fonts.

name: Carlito
classification_notes: Carlito is a metrically compatible open-source replacement for Calibri, sharing its spacing and proportions while offering Cyrillic and Greek script support. Its humanist structure and neutral tone make it a reliable text and UI workhorse.
summary: A metrically Calibri-compatible humanist sans with broad multilingual script support.
description: Carlito was developed as a drop-in metric substitute for Microsoft's Calibri, making it particularly useful in open-source office environments and document workflows where layout compatibility is essential. Its humanist proportions and clean rendering ensure reliable legibility in body text at screen sizes, and its Cyrillic and Greek coverage extends its usefulness across multilingual projects. It is a pragmatic rather than inspired choice, but it fulfils its function effectively.

name: Carme
classification_notes: Carme is a restrained humanist sans-serif with classical proportions and understated character, designed with editorial and text use in mind. Its even stroke weight and open forms support comfortable reading at body sizes.
summary: A quietly classical humanist sans suited to understated editorial and body text work.
description: Carme offers clean, well-proportioned letterforms with a humanist warmth that avoids the coldness of geometric or grotesque styles. Its single weight limits its typographic range, but within that constraint it functions well for body text in editorial, digital, and web contexts. It is an unshowy choice that rewards considered use in refined, text-led design.

name: Carrois Gothic
classification_notes: Carrois Gothic is a compact, screen-optimised sans-serif with gothic influences, designed for clarity at small sizes in digital interfaces. Its slightly condensed proportions and consistent stroke weight make it space-efficient without sacrificing legibility.
summary: A compact screen-optimised gothic sans designed for efficient, legible digital use.
description: Carrois Gothic was built with on-screen legibility firmly in mind, offering consistent stroke weights and tight but readable spacing that suit interface design and small-scale digital typography. Its gothic character gives it a neutral, workmanlike quality that fits dashboards, navigation, and functional UI contexts without drawing attention to itself. The single Regular weight keeps it simple, though it limits its versatility in more complex typographic hierarchies.

name: Carrois Gothic SC
classification_notes: A condensed grotesque sans-serif designed exclusively in small capitals, with even stroke weights and compact letterforms. The small caps construction gives it a formal, architectural quality at small sizes.
summary: A single-weight grotesque designed entirely in small capitals, giving it a distinctive formal rigidity.
description: Carrois Gothic SC offers a compact, all-small-caps grotesque that suits branding, editorial headers, and UI labels where a restrained, uniform appearance is required. Its even stroke weight and consistent rhythm make it legible at a range of sizes. The lack of a lowercase restricts its versatility, making it best deployed as a titling or accent face.

name: Carter One
classification_notes: A bold, chunky display serif with pronounced slab-like qualities and strong contrast, evoking mid-century American commercial lettering. The letterforms are assertive and tightly spaced, designed for impact at large sizes.
summary: A punchy, high-impact display serif with strong mid-century American commercial lettering roots.
description: Carter One is built for headlines and poster work where boldness and personality are paramount. Its thick strokes and confident proportions recall signage and advertising type from the 1950s and 60s, lending warmth and authority to branding and packaging projects. Available only in a single regular weight, it is a one-purpose face used best at large display sizes.

name: Cascadia Code
classification_notes: A monospaced coding font developed by Microsoft featuring optional programming ligatures and a clean, humanist-influenced design. It supports Arabic, Cyrillic, and braille scripts alongside a broad Latin character set.
summary: Microsoft's well-crafted coding typeface with optional ligatures and broad script support, designed for developer environments.
description: Cascadia Code was created specifically for use in terminals and code editors, with programming ligatures that combine common character sequences into single glyphs for improved readability. Its humanist proportions soften the rigidity typical of monospaced fonts, reducing eye strain during extended sessions. Support for Arabic, Cyrillic, and braille makes it one of the more internationally capable coding fonts available.

name: Cascadia Mono
classification_notes: The dedicated monospaced variant of Cascadia Code, sharing its humanist design language and multilingual support but without programming ligatures. Intended for contexts where ligatures are unsupported or undesired.
summary: The ligature-free companion to Cascadia Code, offering the same clean humanist design for coding environments that do not support ligatures.
description: Cascadia Mono provides the same robust character set and legible proportions as Cascadia Code but strips out the programming ligatures, making it suitable for terminals and editors where ligature rendering is problematic. Its consistent stroke weight and open apertures aid readability in small sizes and on lower-resolution screens. Multilingual support across Arabic, Cyrillic, and braille gives it practical reach beyond Latin-only environments.

name: Castoro
classification_notes: A refined oldstyle serif with moderate contrast and calligraphic origins, drawing on Italian Renaissance typeface traditions. Its proportions and spacing are calibrated for comfortable long-form reading.
summary: An elegantly proportioned oldstyle serif rooted in Italian Renaissance tradition, suited to refined editorial and book typography.
description: Castoro balances classical calligraphic forms with contemporary refinement, making it a strong choice for book interiors, literary magazines, and long-form editorial design. Its moderate contrast and well-considered spacing maintain readability across a range of text sizes. The companion titling variant, Castoro Titling, extends its usefulness into display applications.

name: Castoro Titling
classification_notes: A display-weight titling cut of the Castoro serif family, with refined contrast and letterforms optimised for use at large sizes. Sparing spacing and high optical precision distinguish it from the text-weight sibling.
summary: A high-refinement titling serif designed for large-scale typographic applications with crisp classical proportions.
description: Castoro Titling takes the design language of Castoro and optimises it for headlines, posters, and editorial display use, where tighter spacing and greater optical precision come into their own. The elevated contrast between thick and thin strokes gives it a distinguished, fashion-forward presence suited to luxury branding and cultural publishing. It performs best when used generously in size and not set for continuous reading.

name: Catamaran
classification_notes: A versatile humanist sans-serif with a large weight range from Thin to Black, designed to support Tamil alongside Latin and Latin Extended scripts. Its open, friendly forms and upright design make it suitable for both text and interface use.
summary: A broad-range humanist sans with native Tamil support, offering reliable legibility from body text to bold headlines.
description: Catamaran's nine-weight range provides designers with considerable flexibility for typographic hierarchies across digital and print contexts. Its humanist proportions and open counters ensure strong readability at small sizes, while the heavier weights carry sufficient weight for impactful headlines. The inclusion of Tamil script alongside Latin characters makes it a practical choice for multilingual South Asian publishing and interface design.

name: Caudex
classification_notes: A sturdy, upright serif with roots in classical Greek typography, designed to support Greek, Greek Extended, and Latin scripts on an equal footing. The design is functional and unpretentious, prioritising readability over stylistic flourish.
summary: A dependable classical serif built with Greek script as a primary concern, offering solid multilingual text performance.
description: Caudex is particularly valuable for academic and literary projects requiring high-quality Greek text setting alongside Latin, as both scripts receive equal typographic attention. Its Regular and Bold weights cover essential typographic hierarchy needs for body text and subheadings without distraction. The unassuming design prioritises utility, making it a practical workhorse for scholarly publications, classical studies, and multilingual documents.

name: Cause
classification_notes: Cause is a flowing, upright-leaning script with a wide weight range from Thin to Black, giving it unusual versatility for a script face. Its letterforms are smooth and contemporary, suited to branding applications.
summary: A contemporary script with an unusually broad weight range that stretches from whisper-thin to heavy black.
description: Cause spans nine weights, making it one of the more versatile script typefaces on Google Fonts. Its clean, flowing letterforms work well across branding, packaging, and headline contexts where a script needs to carry both elegance and impact. The extended Latin character set broadens its appeal for multilingual design work.

name: Caveat
classification_notes: Caveat mimics casual, everyday handwriting with natural variation and slight irregularity in its letterforms. It reads as genuinely personal rather than stylised, and its Cyrillic support broadens its usefulness considerably.
summary: A relaxed, casual handwriting script that feels genuinely personal and unpretentious.
description: Caveat captures the loose, unguarded quality of everyday handwriting without tipping into caricature. Available in four weights with Cyrillic support, it is a practical choice for annotation-style design, editorial accents, and informal branding. Its naturalness keeps it from feeling gimmicky, which is rarer in the genre than it should be.

name: Caveat Brush
classification_notes: Caveat Brush adds a rougher, brush-textured quality to the base Caveat handwriting style, giving it more tactile character. Available only in Regular weight, it is more of a display companion than a workhorse.
summary: The brush-textured companion to Caveat, trading smoothness for a rougher, more handmade quality.
description: Caveat Brush applies a visible brush texture to the familiar Caveat letterforms, making it feel more tactile and lo-fi. With only a single weight available, its use is necessarily limited to display and decorative contexts such as posters, short headlines, or informal branding. It pairs naturally with its sibling Caveat for mixed-register compositions.

name: Cedarville Cursive
classification_notes: Cedarville Cursive is a clean, upright cursive with a schoolbook quality, drawn with even stroke weights and simple, unflamboyant letterforms. It reads as friendly and legible rather than ornate.
summary: A plain, schoolbook-style cursive that prioritises legibility over decorative flourish.
description: Cedarville Cursive draws on the clean, upright cursive tradition taught in American primary schools, resulting in a typeface that feels familiar and approachable. Its even stroke weight and lack of ornamentation make it more readable than many script faces at small sizes. It suits children's content, educational materials, and informal personal branding.

name: Ceviche One
classification_notes: Ceviche One is a bold, decorative display face with chunky slab-like serifs and strong inline detailing that gives it a retro Latin American flavour. It is firmly a headline-only typeface.
summary: A chunky, retro-inflected display face with inline detailing and a distinctly Latin American poster feel.
description: Ceviche One draws on the bold decorative lettering traditions of mid-century Latin American signage and poster design. Its thick forms and incised inline strokes give it a strong graphic personality suited to short headlines, event posters, and packaging where visual impact is the priority. There is only a single weight, which limits its flexibility but reinforces its character.

name: Chakra Petch
classification_notes: Chakra Petch is a technical, angular sans-serif with a slightly futuristic character drawn from Thai script construction principles. It supports both Latin and Thai, with a clean, mechanical quality throughout.
summary: An angular, technical sans-serif with a futuristic edge, built with Thai and Latin support in a unified design.
description: Chakra Petch takes its structural cues from Thai letterform construction while delivering a strikingly modern, near-mechanical appearance in both scripts. The result is a typeface that feels at home in technology branding, gaming interfaces, and editorial design where a hard-edged, forward-looking aesthetic is required. Its range of six weights gives it genuine versatility across display and UI contexts.

name: Changa
classification_notes: Changa is a sans-serif designed to harmonise Arabic and Latin scripts with a shared geometric sensibility and consistent weight range. The Arabic is set in a Naskh-influenced style that sits comfortably alongside the clean Latin.
summary: A genuinely bilingual Arabic–Latin sans-serif that gives both scripts equal typographic weight and coherence.
description: Changa was designed from the outset to work across Arabic and Latin text without either script feeling like an afterthought. Its geometric, slightly condensed forms carry well from display use down to body text, and the seven-weight range makes it a practical workhorse for bilingual publishing and branding. Eduardo Tunni's careful calibration of proportions means switching between scripts within a layout feels natural rather than jarring.

name: Changa One
classification_notes: Changa One is a bold, condensed display face with italicised stress and strong, assertive proportions. Its single-weight offering and overtly punchy character limit it to headline and poster use.
summary: A bold, condensed display face with an aggressive italic angle built directly into its upright forms.
description: Changa One compresses strong, assertive letterforms into a narrow display package with a distinctive forward-leaning energy even in its Roman setting. It is built for impact at large sizes — headlines, posters, and high-visibility branding — and its single weight keeps it honest about that purpose. Designed by Eduardo Tunni as a companion to Changa, it shares the family's clean structural logic while pushing into more expressly display territory.

name: Chango
classification_notes: Chango is a heavy, ultra-bold condensed display typeface with strong ink traps and tight spacing designed for maximum impact at large sizes. Its letterforms are robust and slightly rounded, giving it an energetic, muscular character.
summary: A single-weight ultra-bold display typeface built entirely for loud, large-scale headlines.
description: Chango delivers raw typographic weight with its heavy strokes and condensed proportions, making it ideal for posters, banners, and impactful web headlines. Its ink traps and sturdy construction ensure it holds up visually even at very large sizes. There is no subtlety here — it is a typeface that demands attention.

name: Charis SIL
classification_notes: Charis SIL is a Unicode-compliant humanist serif designed primarily for linguistic and scholarly use, offering extensive character coverage including phonetic symbols and diacritics. Its design is warm and readable, drawing from Bitstream Charter in spirit.
summary: A scholarly humanist serif with exceptional multilingual coverage, built for linguists and long-form reading.
description: Charis SIL was developed by SIL International to support the world's languages, including extensive Latin, Cyrillic, and phonetic characters. Its humanist proportions and generous x-height make it a comfortable reading typeface for academic papers, bibles, and multilingual publications. It is a pragmatic workhorse rather than a fashionable choice, valued for reliability and coverage above all.

name: Charm
classification_notes: Charm is a Thai-Latin display script with a delicate, calligraphic quality and slightly decorative terminals that give it a refined, elegant feel. The Latin characters complement the flowing Thai glyphs with a harmonious pen-inspired construction.
summary: A graceful bilingual display script pairing elegant Thai glyphs with complementary calligraphic Latin letterforms.
description: Charm bridges Thai and Latin scripts with a consistent calligraphic sensibility, making it well suited to packaging, greeting cards, and decorative headings that require bilingual text. Its delicate strokes and slightly ornamental flourishes lend a feminine, refined quality to designs. It works best at larger sizes where its fine details can be appreciated.

name: Charmonman
classification_notes: Charmonman is a Thai-Latin script typeface with a more informal, handwritten quality than Charm, featuring looping connective strokes and a casual rhythm that reads as friendly rather than formal. Its Latin characters share the same relaxed, pen-drawn sensibility.
summary: A casual, looping bilingual script that brings a warm, handwritten personality to Thai and Latin text alike.
description: Charmonman suits informal branding, social media graphics, and decorative display contexts where a natural, hand-crafted feel is desired across Thai and Latin scripts. Its flowing loops and relaxed construction feel approachable rather than precious, giving it broader appeal than more formal scripts. It is best reserved for display use rather than body copy.

name: Chathura
classification_notes: Chathura is a Telugu-Latin sans-serif with a wide weight range from Thin to ExtraBold, designed to support the complex stroke structures of Telugu script alongside clean Latin letterforms. Its design is open and functional, prioritising legibility across both scripts.
summary: A functional Telugu-Latin sans-serif offering an unusually wide weight range for both scripts.
description: Chathura is a practical choice for any project requiring consistent typographic treatment across Telugu and Latin text, from websites to print publications. Its five-weight range gives designers meaningful flexibility for hierarchy and emphasis. The letterforms are clean and unpretentious, prioritising readability over stylistic flourish.

name: Chau Philomene One
classification_notes: Chau Philomene One is a single-weight condensed geometric sans with a distinctive constructed quality and uniform stroke contrast, giving it a somewhat mechanical, utilitarian character. Its tight proportions and clean geometry suit display use at headline sizes.
summary: A compact, geometric condensed sans with a utilitarian precision suited to space-efficient headlines.
description: Chau Philomene One's tight letter spacing and consistent geometry make it a practical choice for headlines where horizontal space is limited. Its single weight limits versatility but the condensed design compensates with visual efficiency. It suits editorial headers, posters, and functional display contexts rather than body text.

name: Chela One
classification_notes: Chela One is a bold, high-contrast display typeface with pronounced thick-thin transitions and flared terminals that give it a vintage Latin American flavour. Its exuberant proportions and decorative sensibility make it suitable for festive or expressive headline use.
summary: A high-contrast display serif with a festive, Latin American flair built entirely for expressive headlines.
description: Chela One draws on the tradition of ornate Latin American display lettering, with exaggerated stroke contrast and decorative details that command attention at poster scale. It works well on event flyers, branding with a retro or festive character, and any headline context that calls for personality over restraint. Its single weight keeps it firmly in the category of a specialised display tool.

name: Chelsea Market
classification_notes: Chelsea Market is a quirky, hand-crafted display typeface with irregular stroke widths, textured edges, and an artisanal quality that evokes hand-painted signage and market lettering. Its deliberately imperfect construction gives it immediate character and warmth.
summary: A textured, hand-painted display typeface with the warmth and imperfection of artisanal market lettering.
description: Chelsea Market is named after the New York food market and captures the handmade, eclectic spirit of independent traders and craft producers. Its irregular strokes and rough edges make it a strong choice for food packaging, artisan branding, café signage, and poster work that needs an authentic, unpretentious feel. It is a single-weight novelty typeface, but an effective and charming one within its niche.

name: Chenla
classification_notes: Chenla is a decorative Khmer display typeface with expressive, stylised letterforms suited to headlines and cultural contexts. Its design draws on traditional Khmer calligraphic forms while adding a modern decorative flair.
summary: A decorative Khmer display face that brings expressive calligraphic energy to headlines and cultural design work.
description: Chenla is designed specifically for the Khmer script, making it a rare resource for designers working with Cambodian language content. Its stylised, ornate forms are best suited to display sizes where their decorative character can be appreciated. It works well for posters, cultural publications, and headline applications requiring a distinctly Khmer aesthetic.

name: Cherish
classification_notes: Cherish is an elaborate decorative script with highly ornate swashes, flourishes, and connecting letterforms reminiscent of Victorian and Spencerian penmanship. The letterforms are delicate and romantic, suited to formal or luxury branding.
summary: An ornate, flourish-heavy script that channels Victorian penmanship for romantic and luxury design applications.
description: Cherish features sweeping swashes and intricate letterform connections that make it an unambiguously decorative choice. It suits wedding stationery, boutique branding, packaging, and any context where an air of elegance or romance is required. Its Vietnamese and Latin Extended support broadens its appeal for multilingual luxury projects.

name: Cherry Bomb One
classification_notes: Cherry Bomb One is a bold, exuberant display typeface with chunky, rounded letterforms and a playful hand-lettered quality, supporting both Japanese and Latin scripts. The design has a strong personality reminiscent of manga titling and fun pop-culture graphics.
summary: A boisterous, chunky display face with manga-influenced energy, covering both Japanese and Latin scripts.
description: Cherry Bomb One's rounded, heavily stylised letterforms give it an unmistakable personality rooted in Japanese pop culture and comic aesthetics. Its dual-script support makes it particularly valuable for designers working across Japanese and Latin-language markets. Best deployed at large sizes for headlines, posters, packaging, or any context that calls for unapologetic visual impact.

name: Cherry Cream Soda
classification_notes: Cherry Cream Soda is a single-weight retro display typeface with inline detailing and a mid-century American soda-fountain aesthetic. Its decorative inline strokes give it a cheerful, nostalgic character suited to playful branding.
summary: A cheerful retro display face with inline detailing that evokes mid-century American soda-fountain signage.
description: Cherry Cream Soda's inline stroke treatment and bouncy letterforms make it immediately evocative of 1950s American diner and confectionery graphics. It is a single-weight font, which limits its versatility, but within its niche it delivers reliable retro charm. Ideal for food and beverage branding, novelty packaging, and editorial contexts requiring a playful vintage tone.

name: Cherry Swash
classification_notes: Cherry Swash is a decorative serif display typeface featuring ornate swashed terminals and high-contrast strokes that give it a vintage, elegant quality. Available in Regular and Bold, it has enough weight range for limited hierarchical use.
summary: An ornate swash serif with high-contrast strokes and a vintage elegance suited to headline and branding work.
description: Cherry Swash pairs decorative swashed terminals with classical serif structure, giving it a personality that sits between formal and festive. The inclusion of a Bold weight provides modest flexibility for titling hierarchies. It works well for editorial headers, boutique packaging, and any brand identity that wants to reference heritage and craftsmanship with a touch of flair.

name: Chewy
classification_notes: Chewy is a loose, hand-drawn rounded display typeface with informal, bubbly letterforms and generous spacing. Its single weight has a strong cartoonish character that makes it instantly friendly and approachable.
summary: A bubbly, hand-drawn rounded display face with an irresistibly cheerful and cartoonish personality.
description: Chewy's soft, inflated letterforms and casual rhythm make it one of the more recognisable playful display fonts in the Google Fonts library. It suits children's branding, game interfaces, food packaging, and any context where warmth and approachability are paramount. Being a single weight does limit its functional range, but within display applications it performs with consistent charm.

name: Chicle
classification_notes: Chicle is a distinctive display typeface with organic, slightly condensed letterforms and a subtle Art Nouveau influence in its flowing, naturalistic curves. Its unusual character sits somewhere between a serif and a decorative display face.
summary: An organically structured display serif with Art Nouveau undertones and gently idiosyncratic letterforms.
description: Chicle occupies an unusual space in display typography, blending classical serif structure with the kind of flowing, nature-inspired curves associated with early twentieth-century decorative arts. Its single weight limits its use to headline and titling contexts, but its distinctive character makes it memorable. Well suited to editorial headers, artisanal branding, and packaging projects seeking something with genuine typographic personality.

name: Chilanka
classification_notes: Chilanka is a handwritten informal script designed for the Malayalam script, with natural, flowing letterforms that mimic casual pen-on-paper writing. It also supports Latin, making it a practical choice for bilingual content.
summary: A warm, handwritten Malayalam script face with a natural, pen-written quality and Latin companion support.
description: Chilanka is one of relatively few typefaces designed with genuine care for the Malayalam script, presenting its characters with an approachable, handwritten informality. The Latin glyphs are designed to harmonise with the Malayalam, making it functional for bilingual Indian-language content. It suits editorial, educational, and cultural contexts where a human, unpretentious tone is desired.

name: Chiron GoRound TC
classification_notes: A rounded sans-serif designed for Traditional Chinese with soft, approachable terminals. The rounded stroke endings give it a friendly, modern character across an extensive CJK glyph set.
summary: A rounded Traditional Chinese sans-serif with a warm, contemporary feel suited to digital interfaces and multilingual publishing.
description: Chiron GoRound TC covers Traditional Chinese, Cyrillic, and Cyrillic Extended scripts with consistent rounded terminals that soften its appearance without sacrificing legibility. Its eight weights make it adaptable across display and text sizes. It suits apps, websites, and editorial work requiring harmonious mixed-script typography.

name: Chiron Hei HK
classification_notes: A clean, neutral sans-serif optimised for Traditional Chinese (Hong Kong standard) with a contemporary structure influenced by modern CJK type design. Stroke contrast is minimal and legibility at small sizes is a clear priority.
summary: A versatile Traditional Chinese sans-serif built for clarity and consistency across both CJK and Latin scripts.
description: Chiron Hei HK takes its cues from the Hei (黑體) genre of CJK type, delivering uniform stroke weights and open forms optimised for screen reading. Its eight weights provide strong typographic hierarchy in both Traditional Chinese and Cyrillic contexts. It is well suited to long-form digital publishing, UI design, and any project demanding reliable multilingual support.

name: Chiron Sung HK
classification_notes: A serif typeface in the Ming (明體/宋體) tradition, characterised by high-contrast strokes with strong horizontal serifs and sharp diagonal stress. Designed for Hong Kong Traditional Chinese standards with accompanying Cyrillic support.
summary: A Ming-style Traditional Chinese serif bringing the elegance of classical CJK book type to contemporary digital publishing.
description: Chiron Sung HK follows the Song/Ming serif convention, with the characteristic high-contrast horizontal and vertical strokes and bracketed serifs familiar from Chinese printing traditions. Its eight weights extend its usefulness from refined body text to prominent headlines. It is an excellent choice for editorial, literary, and cultural publishing in Traditional Chinese.

name: Chivo
classification_notes: Chivo is a sturdy grotesque with a slightly quirky character, featuring subtle ink-trap-influenced details and confident stroke widths. It reads as contemporary and purposeful rather than strictly neutral.
summary: A confident grotesque sans with a distinctive edge that works equally well for editorial body text and assertive headlines.
description: Chivo's slightly irregular grotesque character sets it apart from purely neutral workhorses, giving it personality without sacrificing versatility. Its wide weight range, from Thin to Black, makes it a capable single-family solution for complex editorial and branding hierarchies. It pairs naturally with its companion Chivo Mono for technical or editorial projects requiring mixed type.

name: Chivo Mono
classification_notes: The monospace companion to Chivo, maintaining the parent family's grotesque personality within fixed-width constraints. It shares Chivo's slightly characterful stroke forms while meeting the demands of code and tabular settings.
summary: Chivo's fixed-width counterpart, carrying the family's distinctive grotesque personality into code and technical typesetting.
description: Chivo Mono extends the Chivo family into monospace territory, preserving the original's character while satisfying the functional demands of coding environments and tabular data. Its nine weights give it unusual flexibility for a monospace face, from delicate UI use to bold editorial accent. It works seamlessly alongside Chivo for projects mixing prose and code.

name: Chocolate Classical Sans
classification_notes: Chocolate Classical Sans is a single-weight Traditional Chinese humanist sans with a warm, calligraphic underpinning. Its stroke modulation and slightly organic forms reflect classical Chinese brush-writing influences adapted for digital use.
summary: A warmly humanist Traditional Chinese sans-serif with subtle calligraphic roots that distinguish it from colder geometric CJK faces.
description: Chocolate Classical Sans draws on classical Chinese calligraphy traditions to introduce gentle stroke modulation into a broadly sans-serif framework, giving it more warmth than the typical Hei-style face. Its Latin characters are harmoniously drawn to sit well alongside the Chinese glyphs. Available only in Regular weight, it is best suited to body text and editorial contexts where warmth matters more than typographic range.

name: Chokokutai
classification_notes: Chokokutai is a bold, expressive Japanese display face with thick, rounded, cartoon-like strokes evoking hand-painted signage and manga lettering. Its single weight is unambiguously decorative.
summary: A chunky, exuberant Japanese display face channelling the energy of hand-painted manga and retro signage lettering.
description: Chokokutai's thick, rounded strokes and playful construction make it immediately eye-catching for headlines, posters, and branding with a Japanese cultural flavour. Its single Regular weight is intentionally bold, positioning it firmly as a display-only tool. It suits youth branding, game titles, entertainment graphics, and any context demanding high visual energy.

name: Chonburi
classification_notes: Chonburi is a high-contrast display typeface designed for both Latin and Thai scripts, featuring dramatic thick-thin strokes that give it a bold, poster-worthy presence. Its design bridges traditional Thai letterform conventions with a modern display sensibility.
summary: A high-contrast Thai–Latin display face with dramatic stroke variation built for bold headlines and poster work.
description: Chonburi was designed to bring the same graphic authority to Thai typography that high-contrast Latin display faces enjoy, making it one of the more distinctive Thai typefaces available freely. Its strong thick-thin contrast and single-weight structure make it purpose-built for large-scale headline use. It is particularly effective for bilingual Thai–English posters, editorial covers, and cultural branding.

name: Cinzel
classification_notes: Cinzel draws directly from ancient Roman inscriptional lettering, featuring high contrast strokes, sharp serifs, and capitals-only design rooted in classical proportion. The letterforms are elegant and authoritative, evoking stone-cut epigraphy.
summary: A stately all-caps serif rooted in Roman inscriptional tradition, carrying genuine gravitas across a wide weight range.
description: Cinzel is built on the proportions and stroke contrast of classical Latin epigraphy, making it immediately imposing without feeling theatrical. Its six weights from Regular to Black give designers flexibility for both refined editorial headers and bold display work. It suits luxury branding, film titles, book covers, and any context demanding a sense of history and permanence.

name: Cinzel Decorative
classification_notes: Cinzel Decorative adds elaborate inline and ornamental detail to the base Cinzel letterforms, amplifying the classical Roman aesthetic into full decorative display territory. Available only in three weights, it is unambiguously a titling face.
summary: An ornate, inline-decorated companion to Cinzel that pushes Roman classicism into unapologetically lavish display territory.
description: Cinzel Decorative applies intricate inline detailing to the already imposing Cinzel capitals, dramatically narrowing its range of appropriate use but sharpening its impact within that range. It is best reserved for single-word or short-phrase settings where the ornamentation can breathe — think luxury event invitations, award ceremonies, or high-end packaging. Pairing it with plain Cinzel for subheadings creates a coherent classical hierarchy.

name: Clicker Script
classification_notes: Clicker Script is a flowing, high-contrast connecting script with sweeping ascenders and descenders characteristic of formal penmanship tradition. The letterforms are smooth and legible at larger sizes but lack the nuance of premium calligraphic typefaces.
summary: A clean, flowing formal script with confident stroke contrast, suited to elegant headline and branding applications.
description: Clicker Script sits comfortably in the tradition of formal connecting scripts used for invitations, logotypes, and premium product packaging. Its generous letterforms and clear connections keep it readable at display sizes, though it should never be set small. It works well paired with neutral sans-serifs in branding contexts where a touch of handcrafted elegance is needed without veering into excessive ornamentation.

name: Climate Crisis
classification_notes: Climate Crisis is a variable font designed to animate the physical effects of climate change, with letterforms that literally melt or distort as the variable axis is adjusted. It is a concept-driven, activist display typeface with a single strong visual message.
summary: A variable display typeface whose letterforms visibly melt along their axis, functioning as both typography and environmental statement.
description: Climate Crisis uses its variable font technology not for optical refinement but for direct visual metaphor — the typeface literally melts as the axis moves, evoking rising temperatures and glacial collapse. This makes it highly specific in application, most effective in editorial, activist, and awareness-campaign contexts where the concept reinforces the message. Outside those scenarios it reads as a novelty, but within them it is genuinely purposeful and striking.

name: Coda
classification_notes: Coda features an unusual inline and textured aesthetic that combines elements of geometric construction with decorative detailing, giving it a distinctive retro-inflected character. The ExtraBold weight amplifies the decorative quality considerably.
summary: A distinctive display face with retro decorative detailing that sets it apart from straightforward geometric sans designs.
description: Coda occupies an interesting niche between clean geometric construction and ornamental display typography, with details that suggest mid-century design influence without direct pastiche. Its two weights — Regular and ExtraBold — offer a reasonable range for headline and titling work, particularly in cultural, music, or lifestyle contexts. It is too characterful for body or UI use and works best in short, large-scale settings where its personality can register fully.

name: Codystar
classification_notes: Codystar is an ultra-light, star-patterned novelty display face with extremely thin strokes and decorative geometric ornamental fills that give it a distinctly retro, neon-signage quality. Its two weights are both extremely display-specific.
summary: An ultra-thin novelty display typeface with star-fill detailing that evokes vintage neon signage and retro Americana.
description: Codystar's defining feature is its decorative star-shaped counter fills and hairline-thin strokes, which combine to create an immediately recognisable retro-neon aesthetic. It is unabashedly a one-trick face, best deployed in large-scale display contexts — posters, signage, or event branding — where the novelty reads as intentional style rather than limitation. At small sizes or in running text it becomes entirely illegible.

name: Coiny
classification_notes: Coiny is a chunky, rounded display sans with inflated, bubbly letterforms and a single weight that gives it a playful, child-friendly character. Its Tamil script support makes it notably more versatile in multilingual South Asian contexts.
summary: A buoyant, rounded display sans with genuine multilingual ambition, supporting both Latin and Tamil scripts in a single playful weight.
description: Coiny's inflated, pillowy letterforms give it an immediately approachable and light-hearted personality well suited to children's content, food packaging, and casual branding. Its Tamil support is a meaningful differentiator, making it a rare choice for bilingual display work spanning South Asian and Western markets. The single weight limits typographic hierarchy, but within its intended register it delivers consistent warmth and energy.

name: Combo
classification_notes: Combo is a bold, hand-painted inline display typeface with a casual brush quality combined with a chunky serif structure, evoking mid-century commercial sign painting. The inline white stroke through the letterforms is its defining visual feature.
summary: A hand-painted inline display serif with strong mid-century sign-painting energy, built for bold, characterful headline use.
description: Combo draws directly from the tradition of American commercial lettering, with its chunky slab-adjacent serifs, casual brush irregularity, and distinctive inline white stroke giving it an authentic retro warmth. It suits branding, packaging, and poster work where a hand-crafted, nostalgic feel is the goal — think barbecue restaurants, craft beverages, or vintage-revival editorial. Its single weight keeps it firmly in the display-only camp with no pretension to versatility.

name: Comfortaa
classification_notes: Comfortaa features perfectly circular letter forms with uniformly rounded terminals, giving it a distinctly soft, geometric character. The design is highly consistent across its weight range and supports Cyrillic and Greek scripts.
summary: A uniformly rounded geometric sans with a soft, approachable character that works well for branding and digital interfaces.
description: Comfortaa's circular geometry and rounded terminals give it an immediately warm and modern feel. It performs well in display sizes for app branding, children's content, and lifestyle products. The wide weight range from Light to Bold makes it versatile despite its decorative origins.

name: Comforter
classification_notes: Comforter is a flowing, high-contrast calligraphic script with generous loops and elegant, connected letterforms. It has a formal, celebratory quality suited to display use only.
summary: An elegant, flowing calligraphic script with high contrast and a refined celebratory feel.
description: Comforter draws on formal penmanship traditions, with its sweeping strokes and connected letterforms lending it a luxurious, occasion-appropriate quality. It suits wedding stationery, greeting cards, and premium branding at large sizes. Single-weight and highly expressive, it is strictly a display face.

name: Comforter Brush
classification_notes: Comforter Brush shares the same flowing structure as Comforter but introduces visible brush texture, giving it a more casual, handmade energy. The irregular ink edges add warmth without sacrificing legibility at display sizes.
summary: A textured brush take on the Comforter script, trading formal polish for lively, handcrafted energy.
description: Comforter Brush retains the generous loops and connected strokes of its companion face but layers in visible brush texture for a more tactile feel. It suits craft branding, editorial headlines, and packaging where a human, hand-lettered quality is desired. Like Comforter, it functions exclusively as a display face at larger sizes.

name: Comic Neue
classification_notes: Comic Neue is a redesign of Comic Sans that regularises its proportions and spacing while retaining the informal, handwritten character. It is significantly more refined and typographically considered than its source material.
summary: A cleaned-up, more typographically considered reworking of Comic Sans that retains informality without the notoriety.
description: Comic Neue addresses the spacing and consistency problems of Comic Sans while keeping the casual, approachable tone that made its predecessor so widely used. It works for children's educational material, informal digital interfaces, and accessible reading contexts. Three weights provide modest flexibility for a face of this character.

name: Comic Relief
classification_notes: Comic Relief is a serif display face with softened, rounded forms and a gentle, storybook quality that references hand-drawn lettering. It supports Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts across two weights.
summary: A soft, rounded serif display face with a storybook warmth suited to children's content and informal branding.
description: Comic Relief combines serif structure with rounded terminals and subtly hand-drawn proportions, giving it a distinctive charm at large sizes. It is well suited to children's books, educational interfaces, and playful branding projects. Multilingual support across Latin, Greek, and Cyrillic broadens its appeal considerably.

name: Coming Soon
classification_notes: Coming Soon imitates informal, quickly written handwriting with uneven baselines and loose letterforms. It is a low-fidelity, novelty script with limited typographic application.
summary: A casual, rough handwriting simulation with a throwaway charm and very limited serious typographic use.
description: Coming Soon mimics the look of hurried, informal handwriting with inconsistent stroke weights and loose letterforms. It occupies a narrow niche, occasionally useful for mockups, informal signage, or deliberately low-fi digital contexts. As a single-weight Latin-only face, its versatility is extremely limited.

name: Comme
classification_notes: Comme is a clean, humanist sans-serif with open apertures and a neutral but warm character across a wide nine-weight range. It draws on Japanese design sensibilities applied to Latin and extended Latin scripts.
summary: A clean, neutral humanist sans with a broad weight range that covers body text and UI applications with quiet competence.
description: Comme offers a wide weight range from Thin to Black with consistent humanist proportions and open letterforms that aid legibility at smaller sizes. It is well suited to digital interfaces, editorial body text, and corporate communications where clarity is the priority. The Latin Extended script support makes it a practical choice for multilingual projects.

name: Commissioner
classification_notes: Commissioner is a variable humanist sans with an unusual design axis that shifts letterforms from upright grotesque to flared, almost roman proportions. This makes it unusually versatile across both display and text applications.
summary: A variable humanist sans that transitions from neutral grotesque to subtly flared roman, giving it remarkable range across contexts.
description: Commissioner's defining feature is its parametric design axis controlling the degree of flare on terminals, allowing it to shift from a clean grotesque to a more characterful, almost serifed form. This versatility makes it excellent for editorial design, branding systems, and long-form digital text. Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek support make it a strong choice for multilingual typographic projects.

name: Concert One
classification_notes: A rounded, slightly condensed display sans with heavy weight and uniform stroke contrast. Exaggerated rounded terminals give it a friendly, energetic character suitable for headlines.
summary: A chunky, rounded display sans that brings warmth and punch to headlines.
description: Concert One's bold rounded forms and tight spacing make it a reliable choice for posters, app branding, and editorial headers where energy is needed. Its single weight limits versatility, but within its niche it performs confidently. Best deployed at large sizes where the rounded details read clearly.

name: Condiment
classification_notes: A loosely connected casual script with an informal, hand-lettered quality. Letterforms are relaxed and slightly irregular, evoking quick brushwork rather than calligraphic precision.
summary: A loose, informal script with a breezy hand-lettered charm.
description: Condiment suits packaging, greeting cards, and informal branding where a casual human touch is desired. Its irregular rhythm keeps it feeling spontaneous rather than stiff. Use sparingly at display sizes; it struggles at small settings.

name: Content
classification_notes: Designed specifically for the Khmer script with regular and bold weights, offering practical coverage for Cambodian-language digital content. Forms follow conventional Khmer typographic conventions with clean execution.
summary: A clean, functional Khmer typeface providing solid display and body coverage for Cambodian-language content.
description: Content addresses the limited availability of quality Khmer fonts on the web, offering two weights suitable for both headlines and body text. Its straightforward construction prioritises legibility on screen over stylistic flourish. A practical workhorse for Khmer-language publishing.

name: Contrail One
classification_notes: A single-weight display sans with a distinctive slanted, aerodynamic construction that suggests motion. Strokes have an italic-like lean baked into the upright letterforms.
summary: An aerodynamic display sans with a built-in forward lean that radiates speed and energy.
description: Contrail One's motion-inspired design makes it well suited to sports, gaming, and event branding where dynamism is the goal. The single weight restricts its range, but the personality is strong and immediate. Works best at large sizes where the angled construction reads as intentional rather than quirky.

name: Convergence
classification_notes: A lightweight humanist sans with open apertures and gentle stroke variation. The design feels understated and approachable, prioritising readability over strong stylistic identity.
summary: A quiet, readable humanist sans that serves body text and UI applications without drawing attention to itself.
description: Convergence's open forms and modest weight make it a serviceable choice for digital interfaces and running text where legibility is the priority. It lacks the refinement and range of more established humanist sans families, limiting its appeal for premium work. Nonetheless it functions reliably for everyday web use.

name: Cookie
classification_notes: A flowing, high-contrast calligraphic script with elegant curves and strong thick-to-thin modulation. The letterforms are formal enough to suggest refinement while remaining legible as a headline face.
summary: A graceful calligraphic script with strong contrast and a refined, decorative feel.
description: Cookie suits wedding stationery, beauty branding, and editorial headlines where elegance is the primary requirement. The high stroke contrast adds visual richness at display sizes, though it limits use at small settings. A go-to script for projects requiring classic femininity without excessive ornamentation.

name: Copse
classification_notes: A single-weight humanist serif with moderate contrast and organic, pen-influenced letterforms. Bracketed serifs and open counters give it warmth and approachability for reading contexts.
summary: A warm humanist serif with organic forms that reads comfortably at text sizes.
description: Copse is a sensible choice for editorial body text on screen, where its open counters and pen-influenced construction aid legibility. The absence of multiple weights restricts its use in complex typographic hierarchies. It occupies a modest but functional position among free web serifs.

name: Coral Pixels
classification_notes: A pixel-grid display typeface that deliberately references early digital and arcade aesthetics. Letterforms are constructed on a visible modular grid, giving a retro low-resolution appearance.
summary: A retro pixel font that channels early video game aesthetics with deliberate lo-fi charm.
description: Coral Pixels suits gaming projects, retro-themed branding, and digital art contexts where the pixel grid is part of the visual language. Its appeal is inherently niche and novelty-driven, making it a one-trick face with limited versatility. Best used sparingly as a headline or accent element rather than extended text.

name: Corben
classification_notes: Corben features chunky, rounded serifs with a playful cartoon-like quality and strong stroke contrast. Its exaggerated proportions and robust letterforms make it unsuitable for body text but eye-catching at large sizes.
summary: A chunky, rounded display serif with a cartoon-like character that works best as a headline or logotype face.
description: Corben's exaggerated, almost bubbly serifs and bold stroke contrast give it an approachable, retro-inflected personality. It suits children's publishing, casual branding, and any headline context where warmth and visual punch are priorities. Its limited weight range keeps it firmly in display territory.

name: Corinthia
classification_notes: Corinthia is a high-contrast, calligraphic script with tall, elegant letterforms and generous ascenders and descenders. Its formal, copperplate-influenced style gives it a refined, ceremonial quality.
summary: An elegant, high-contrast calligraphic script with tall letterforms suited to formal and luxury contexts.
description: Corinthia draws on copperplate and formal penmanship traditions, delivering sweeping ascenders and sharp hairline strokes against bold downstrokes. It is well suited to wedding stationery, luxury packaging, editorial headlines, and fashion branding where refinement is essential. Its bold weight adds versatility without sacrificing its formal character.

name: Cormorant
classification_notes: Cormorant is an extremely high-contrast, Garamond-inspired serif designed for dramatic display use, with very fine hairlines that would break down at small sizes. It includes multiple stylistic variants as separate font families.
summary: A breathtakingly high-contrast Garamond revival crafted for dramatic display use and luxury editorial work.
description: Designed by Christian Thalmann, Cormorant pushes contrast to an extreme, resulting in hairlines so delicate they demand large sizes to survive. Its Garamond heritage lends it classical authority, while the exaggerated proportions feel entirely contemporary and at home in fashion editorial and high-end branding. The family spans multiple optical variants, making it one of the most comprehensive display serif offerings on Google Fonts.

name: Cormorant Garamond
classification_notes: Cormorant Garamond is a faithful, high-contrast revival of the classic Garamond model with sharply cut serifs and elegant proportions optimised for display sizes. It shares its DNA with the broader Cormorant family but is the most traditionally text-like of the variants.
summary: The most classically Garamond of the Cormorant variants, offering timeless old-style elegance at display sizes.
description: Cormorant Garamond distils the warmth and refinement of the sixteenth-century Garamond models into a modern, high-contrast interpretation best used at headline and display scales. Its fine hairlines and cupped serifs give editorial layouts and luxury branding a sense of historical gravitas. It pairs beautifully with neutral grotesque sans-serifs for contemporary editorial contrast.

name: Cormorant Infant
classification_notes: Cormorant Infant is a variant of the Cormorant family featuring slightly reduced contrast and more conventional, upright proportions, making it marginally more legible at smaller sizes than the core Cormorant. It retains the family's characteristic elegance.
summary: A slightly softened Cormorant variant that nudges the family's extreme contrast toward greater text legibility.
description: Cormorant Infant reduces some of the hairline fragility found in the base family, making it a touch more practical for smaller display sizes and sub-heading use. It retains the graceful, classical quality of the Cormorant system while offering a marginally more robust option for designers who need versatility. It remains a display-first typeface and should not be used for extended body copy.

name: Cormorant SC
classification_notes: Cormorant SC is the small capitals variant of the Cormorant family, with true-drawn small caps that maintain the family's extreme high contrast and elegant proportions. It is designed for titling, monograms, and refined display settings.
summary: The small caps variant of Cormorant, bringing true-drawn small capitals and classical typographic refinement to display settings.
description: Cormorant SC provides true small capitals rather than scaled-down full caps, preserving the stroke weight and elegance that the Cormorant family is known for. It is particularly effective for titling, chapter headings, monograms, and luxury branding contexts where typographic sophistication matters. Used in combination with the other Cormorant variants, it enables nuanced typographic hierarchies with a consistent visual language.

name: Cormorant Unicase
classification_notes: Cormorant Unicase blends uppercase and lowercase letterforms at a unified x-height, creating an unusual mixed-case aesthetic that is distinctive and experimental. It is a display-only face best used sparingly for its novelty.
summary: An experimental unicase variant of Cormorant that merges upper and lowercase forms at a unified height for a striking, unconventional effect.
description: Cormorant Unicase draws on the unicase tradition to combine uppercase and lowercase letterforms within a single shared cap height, producing text that reads as simultaneously formal and avant-garde. This makes it most effective for short display strings, logotypes, and editorial headlines where typographic surprise is an asset. It is not suited to sustained reading and demands careful, considered use to avoid visual confusion.

name: Cormorant Upright
classification_notes: Cormorant Upright removes the italic axis entirely, presenting the Cormorant design in a strictly upright roman posture across all weights. This gives it a slightly more neutral, architectural quality compared to the other Cormorant variants.
summary: A strictly upright roman variant of Cormorant that trades the family's italic warmth for a more architectural, neutral presence.
description: Cormorant Upright offers the same high-contrast Garamond-derived elegance as the rest of the family but eschews any italic or oblique forms, resulting in a more structured, upright texture. This makes it appealing for architectural, legal, or fashion contexts where a classical typeface with a cooler, more formal tone is desirable. Its expanded language support, including Vietnamese, gives it broader reach than some of the other Cormorant variants.

name: Cossette Texte
classification_notes: Cossette Texte is a clean humanist sans-serif designed for comfortable reading at smaller sizes, with open apertures and balanced stroke contrast. Its Regular and Bold weights provide a functional pairing for editorial body text.
summary: A straightforward humanist sans built for legibility in extended reading at text sizes.
description: Cossette Texte pairs with its display counterpart Cossette Titre as part of a coordinated type system. Its open forms and measured proportions make it well-suited to editorial layouts, digital interfaces, and any context demanding sustained readability. The two-weight offering covers most practical text hierarchy needs.

name: Cossette Titre
classification_notes: Cossette Titre shares the same humanist foundations as Cossette Texte but is optimised for larger display sizes, with slightly tighter spacing and more pronounced character in its forms. It functions as the headline companion within the Cossette type system.
summary: The display-focused half of the Cossette family, designed to headline alongside Cossette Texte.
description: Cossette Titre is intended for titles, headings, and prominent UI labels where slightly more personality is welcome without sacrificing the system's overall coherence. Used together with Cossette Texte, the pair offers a cohesive type system for editorial, branding, and screen applications. The Regular and Bold weights keep the range practical rather than ornamental.

name: Courgette
classification_notes: Courgette is a single-weight italic brush script with flowing, connected letterforms and moderate stroke contrast that evokes casual handwritten signage. Its forms are legible without being overly formal, sitting comfortably between display cursive and informal handwriting.
summary: A flowing, casual brush script with enough charm for branding work and enough legibility to function as a display headline.
description: Courgette's connected, slightly bouncy letterforms lend warmth to food packaging, café menus, greeting cards, and lifestyle branding. It reads well at display sizes but loses clarity quickly at small sizes, so it is best reserved for headlines and logotypes. The extended Latin character set broadens its appeal across Western European markets.

name: Courier Prime
classification_notes: Courier Prime is a refined revival of the classic Courier typewriter face, with improved spacing, hinting, and screen rendering over the original. It retains the familiar slab-serif monospaced feel while being genuinely pleasant to read on screen.
summary: A carefully modernised take on Courier that finally makes the typewriter classic comfortable for screen use and screenwriting.
description: Courier Prime was developed specifically for screenplay formatting software, addressing the shortcomings of ageing Courier implementations with improved metrics and hinting. It works equally well as a coding font, terminal typeface, or editorial accent for anyone seeking that deliberate typewriter aesthetic. The Regular and Bold weights, along with italic variants, make it more versatile than most Courier derivatives.

name: Cousine
classification_notes: Cousine is a metric-compatible alternative to Courier, designed by Steve Matteson as part of the Chrome OS core fonts, with support for Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek scripts. It takes a cleaner, more neutral approach than traditional typewriter-style monospaced faces.
summary: A metric-compatible monospaced workhorse designed for screen and coding use, with broad multilingual script support.
description: Cousine was built to be interchangeable with Courier New in terms of metrics, easing document compatibility across systems while offering superior screen rendering. Its support for Cyrillic and Greek makes it a practical choice for multilingual coding environments and technical documentation. Clean, unfussy, and reliable — it is not exciting, but it does the job well.

name: Coustard
classification_notes: Coustard is a sturdy, high-contrast slab serif with pronounced thick-thin variation uncommon in typical slab designs, giving it a more editorial and declarative character. The Black weight in particular has genuine visual weight for poster and display applications.
summary: A high-contrast slab serif with real backbone, most at home in editorial headlines and poster work.
description: Coustard's unusual combination of slab serifs with pronounced stroke contrast gives it a hybrid personality that sits between a traditional slab and a more classical serif. The Black weight is its strongest asset, delivering commanding presence for magazine covers, posters, and display advertising. The Regular is serviceable for subheadings but the family truly earns its keep at large sizes.

name: Covered By Your Grace
classification_notes: Covered By Your Grace is a loose, informal handwritten script that mimics the relaxed pen lettering of everyday handwriting, with inconsistent baseline and casual stroke variation. It is expressive and personal in character rather than polished or calligraphic.
summary: A warm, unpretentious handwriting script that captures the spontaneous feel of a personal note.
description: Covered By Your Grace suits informal greeting card designs, personal branding, and social media graphics where authenticity and approachability matter more than precision. Its deliberately imperfect letterforms and relaxed baseline give it genuine personality, though it is unsuitable for body text or formal applications. Best used sparingly at display sizes to preserve its handmade charm.

name: Crafty Girls
classification_notes: Crafty Girls is a whimsical, childlike handwriting font with irregular letterforms, mixed cap heights, and a distinctly playful, arts-and-crafts aesthetic. It reads more as a novelty display face than a functional script.
summary: A quirky, childlike handwriting script with strong novelty appeal and a deliberately crafted imperfection.
description: Crafty Girls is designed to evoke the kind of cheerful, slightly wobbly lettering found on hand-made greeting cards and children's craft projects. It has a very specific personality that works for playful branding, children's content, and informal invitations, but its narrow use case makes it a one-trick font. Limited to a single weight and Latin only, it offers little flexibility beyond its niche.

name: Creepster
classification_notes: Creepster uses jagged, dripping letterforms with exaggerated ink-trap-like cuts to evoke a classic horror aesthetic. The design draws heavily from pulp horror poster lettering of the mid-twentieth century.
summary: A campy, horror-themed display face built entirely for spooky seasonal and novelty applications.
description: Creepster leans fully into the Halloween aesthetic with dripping, irregular strokes and a deliberately unsettling character. It works well for event posters, game titles, and novelty branding where a horror or gothic tone is needed. Its single weight and narrow stylistic range make it a one-trick font, but it performs that trick confidently.

name: Crete Round
classification_notes: Crete Round softens traditional oldstyle serif structures with gently rounded stroke terminals, giving it an approachable warmth unusual in the serif category. The proportions are sturdy and book-like, suited to text setting at moderate sizes.
summary: A warmly rounded oldstyle serif that balances classical structure with a softened, contemporary finish.
description: Crete Round takes the sturdy proportions of a traditional book serif and rounds off the terminals to produce something more inviting and modern. It reads well in body text and works comfortably for editorial layouts, small-run publications, and branding that wants approachable sophistication. The single weight limits flexibility, but the design is polished and distinctive.

name: Crimson Pro
classification_notes: Crimson Pro is a refined, high-quality text serif rooted in the oldstyle tradition, with modulated stroke contrast and well-drawn italics that reward close reading. Its extensive weight range and variable font support make it one of the more capable literary serifs on Google Fonts.
summary: A serious, well-crafted oldstyle serif with a full weight range designed unambiguously for demanding long-form text work.
description: Crimson Pro refines and extends the earlier Crimson Text with improved spacing, enhanced diacritics, and variable font axes, making it a genuinely versatile tool for book designers and editorial teams. Its humanist proportions and calligraphic origins give the italics particular elegance, and the eight-weight range covers everything from delicate captions to assertive headings. Vietnamese and extended Latin support broaden its usefulness considerably.

name: Crimson Text
classification_notes: Crimson Text is an oldstyle text serif inspired by the work of designers such as Aldus Manutius and Jan Tschichold, with classical proportions and moderate stroke contrast suited to extended reading. It predates Crimson Pro and has a slightly rougher finish, though it remains a competent text face.
summary: A classically inspired oldstyle serif drawn for comfortable long-form reading, rooted in Renaissance type traditions.
description: Crimson Text draws on the rich tradition of Renaissance oldstyle typefaces to produce a readable, characterful serif well suited to academic papers, literary publications, and editorial layouts. Its moderate contrast and generous x-height aid legibility at body sizes, and the inclusion of bold and semibold weights provides basic hierarchy options. Where Crimson Pro has since surpassed it technically, Crimson Text remains a reliable and widely used choice.

name: Croissant One
classification_notes: Croissant One features a single weight with an ornate inline treatment applied to thick strokes, producing a high-contrast decorative letterform with a distinctly retro flavour. The soft, rounded overall structure contrasts with the intricate internal detailing.
summary: A decorative inline display face with a retro character best suited to headline and poster work.
description: Croissant One applies a classic inline stripe to rounded, slab-adjacent letterforms, creating a festive, period-feeling display face. It suits event posters, packaging, and branding projects that want a vintage boulangerie or fair-ground aesthetic. Its single weight and highly specific personality limit broader application.

name: Crushed
classification_notes: Crushed is an extremely condensed, heavy display face with a blocky, compressed structure reminiscent of tight-set sports and tabloid typography. Letter spacing is tight and the overall effect is dense and impactful.
summary: An aggressively condensed heavy display face designed for maximum typographic impact in minimal horizontal space.
description: Crushed packs bold, tightly compressed letterforms into a single confrontational weight ideal for headlines, sports graphics, and poster work. Its extreme condensation allows large text to occupy small horizontal footprints without losing punch, making it useful for banner and signage contexts. The design is functional within its narrow brief but offers little versatility beyond that role.

name: Cuprum
classification_notes: Cuprum is a compact, slightly condensed humanist sans-serif with a no-nonsense, technical character and support for Cyrillic scripts. Stroke contrast is minimal and the overall texture is clean and efficient, reading well at both text and UI sizes.
summary: A compact, utilitarian humanist sans with strong Cyrillic support and an efficient, technical character.
description: Cuprum sits in a useful middle ground between neutral grotesque and expressive humanist sans, with a slightly condensed build that makes economical use of horizontal space. Its Cyrillic Extended coverage makes it a practical choice for multilingual projects spanning Latin and Slavic scripts. The four-weight range is sufficient for interface work, editorial hierarchies, and branding with a technical or functional tone.

name: Cute Font
classification_notes: Cute Font is a Korean-primary display face with large, rounded, almost bubble-like letterforms designed for a youthful and playful aesthetic. Latin characters are included but clearly secondary to the Hangul design.
summary: A bubbly, rounded Korean display face with an overtly cute and youthful personality.
description: Cute Font prioritises warmth and approachability above all else, with swollen, rounded strokes in both its Hangul and Latin characters that read as cheerful and child-friendly. It suits app interfaces aimed at young audiences, casual social content, and packaging for playful consumer products. The single weight and highly specific tone make it unsuitable for anything requiring formality or versatility.

name: Cutive
classification_notes: Cutive is inspired by early typewriter typefaces, featuring monospaced-like proportions and slab serifs with a mechanical, utilitarian character. The letterforms have an even, slightly worn quality reminiscent of vintage typewritten text.
summary: A typewriter-inspired serif with slab details and mechanical proportions that evokes the charm of vintage typed documents.
description: Cutive draws directly from the aesthetic of classic typewriter fonts, bringing a nostalgic, analogue quality to digital text. Its even stroke weight and slab serifs make it well-suited to editorial projects, literary publications, and any design seeking a retro or journalistic tone. It works best at display sizes where its typewriter personality can be fully appreciated.

name: Cutive Mono
classification_notes: Cutive Mono is the monospaced companion to Cutive, sharing the same typewriter-inspired slab serif design with strictly equal character widths. The mechanical aesthetic is heightened by the fixed-pitch spacing that directly references typewriter output.
summary: A monospaced typewriter serif that pairs the nostalgic charm of Cutive with the functional demands of fixed-pitch typography.
description: Cutive Mono extends the Cutive family into monospace territory, making it practical for code display, terminal aesthetics, and editorial contexts where a retro typewritten feel is desired. Its slab serifs and even stroke weight give it warmth that distinguishes it from colder technical monospaces. It suits editorial layouts, creative coding portfolios, and projects with a vintage-tech or literary sensibility.

name: DM Mono
classification_notes: DM Mono is a clean, geometric monospaced typeface with open apertures and a rational construction that prioritises legibility at small sizes. Its three weights provide modest typographic range within a restrained, modern design system.
summary: A precise, geometric monospace designed as part of the DM type system, offering clarity and calm authority in technical contexts.
description: DM Mono was developed by Colophon Foundry as the monospaced member of Google's DM type family, intended for use alongside DM Sans and DM Serif. Its open letterforms and generous x-height make it highly legible in code editors, documentation, and UI components. The family's cohesion with its sans and serif counterparts makes it particularly valuable in product design systems that require typographic consistency across varied roles.

name: DM Sans
classification_notes: DM Sans is a low-contrast geometric sans-serif with optically adjusted letterforms designed for strong legibility at small sizes on digital screens. Its wide range of nine weights from Thin to Black gives it considerable versatility across interface and editorial applications.
summary: A versatile geometric sans with carefully optically corrected letterforms, built from the ground up for digital screen legibility.
description: DM Sans was commissioned by Google and designed by Colophon Foundry as a core typeface for Google's marketing and product communications. Its geometric structure is tempered by subtle humanist adjustments that prevent the sterility sometimes found in pure geometric designs. With nine weights and strong Latin Extended coverage, it functions equally well as a UI typeface, editorial body font, or branding typeface across both print and screen.

name: DM Serif Display
classification_notes: DM Serif Display features pronounced stroke contrast and delicate hairline serifs optimised for use at large sizes, where its elegant tension between thick and thin elements can be fully appreciated. It draws from classical serif models but is interpreted with a contemporary sharpness.
summary: An elegant high-contrast display serif designed for large-scale typesetting, with sharp hairlines and classical proportions reinterpreted for contemporary use.
description: DM Serif Display is the expressive counterpart to DM Serif Text, sharing the same design DNA but optimised specifically for headlines, posters, and editorial display settings. Its pronounced contrast between thick strokes and razor-thin hairlines creates a sophisticated, fashion-forward character. It pairs naturally with DM Sans in editorial layouts and brand identities that seek a refined, modern-classical voice.

name: DM Serif Text
classification_notes: DM Serif Text is a moderate-contrast serif designed for sustained reading at text sizes, with sturdier hairlines and slightly adjusted proportions compared to its display sibling. It maintains an elegant, classical structure while remaining practical for body-copy usage.
summary: A refined text-optimised serif that brings classical elegance to body copy, designed as the readable counterpart to DM Serif Display.
description: DM Serif Text shares the design principles of DM Serif Display but is engineered for comfortable reading at smaller sizes, with modestly increased stroke weights on its fine elements to withstand rendering at text scales. Its classical proportions and moderate contrast give it a dignified, editorial quality suited to long-form digital publishing, editorial design, and branded content. It completes the DM family as a cohesive typographic system spanning display, text, sans, and mono styles.

name: Dai Banna SIL
classification_notes: Dai Banna SIL is a specialised serif typeface developed to support the New Tai Lue script alongside Latin, designed with scholarly and literacy-focused applications in mind. The design follows SIL's careful approach to script support, prioritising readability and cultural appropriateness for minority language communities.
summary: A scholarly serif typeface developed by SIL International to provide proper typographic support for the New Tai Lue script alongside Latin.
description: Dai Banna SIL is produced by SIL International as part of their commitment to providing high-quality typefaces for under-resourced scripts and minority language communities. It supports the New Tai Lue script used by the Tai Lue people primarily in Yunnan, China, and Southeast Asia, offering a rare combination of linguistic and typographic rigour. With five weights from Light to Bold, it provides genuine flexibility for publications, educational materials, and digital platforms serving New Tai Lue-speaking communities.

name: Damion
classification_notes: Damion is a flowing, informal brush script with energetic, loosely connected letterforms that convey spontaneity and movement. Its single regular weight is drawn with broad, confident strokes that reference marker or large-brush lettering traditions.
summary: An energetic brush script with sweeping, confident strokes that brings informal warmth and movement to display typography.
description: Damion captures the loose, gestural quality of marker or large-brush lettering, making it well-suited to branding, packaging, poster design, and any context requiring a casual yet dynamic handwritten feel. Its flowing connections between letters create a sense of momentum and personality that more formal scripts lack. It works best at large display sizes and pairs effectively with neutral or geometric sans-serifs for contrast in brand and editorial compositions.

name: Dancing Script
classification_notes: A lively, informal handwriting script with natural bounce and variation in letterform size and baseline. The strokes have a pen-drawn quality with moderate contrast and flowing connections between letters.
summary: Dancing Script is one of the most widely used casual handwriting scripts on the web, offering natural rhythm and warmth across four weights.
description: Its lively, bouncing baseline and organic letterforms make it feel genuinely handwritten rather than mechanical. It works well for wedding stationery, greeting cards, and informal branding where a personal, approachable tone is needed. The variable weight range gives designers reasonable flexibility for headlines and accent text.

name: Danfo
