classification_notes: Instrument Serif is a high-contrast, elegant serif with a classical spirit and a modern editorial sensibility. Available only in Regular weight, it relies on refined proportions and sharp detailing rather than weight variation.
summary: An elegant, high-contrast serif designed for refined editorial and fashion-adjacent applications.
description: Instrument Serif carries a quietly luxurious tone, with sharp serifs and generous contrast between thick and thin strokes that reward large display sizes. Its single-weight offering makes it best suited to headline and display roles rather than long-form text. It works particularly well alongside Instrument Sans in branded or editorial contexts.

name: Intel One Mono
classification_notes: Intel One Mono is a purpose-built coding typeface with a humanist influence, designed for clarity and reduced eye strain during extended coding sessions. Its letterforms are carefully disambiguated to avoid common character confusion in code contexts.
summary: Intel's purpose-built coding font, designed with humanist clarity and rigorous legibility for developers.
description: Intel One Mono was developed in collaboration with Frere-Jones Type to address the specific demands of software development environments, prioritising the disambiguation of easily confused characters such as 0, O, l, and 1. Its humanist details soften the mechanical quality common to monospaces, reducing fatigue during extended use. The five-weight range makes it adaptable across IDEs, terminals, and technical documentation.

name: Inter
classification_notes: Inter is a highly refined neo-grotesque designed specifically for screen readability, with a tall x-height, open apertures, and carefully tuned metrics across a comprehensive weight range. It has become one of the most widely adopted UI typefaces of its era.
summary: The definitive screen-optimised grotesque sans, built for UI work and now ubiquitous across digital products.
description: Inter was conceived from the ground up for computer screens, with a tall x-height and open letterforms that maintain clarity at small sizes and across low-resolution displays. Its neutral, clean character makes it equally at home in interface design, dashboards, and body copy. Extensive multilingual support including Cyrillic and Greek, combined with variable font technology, has made it a go-to workhorse for global digital products.

name: Inter Tight
classification_notes: Inter Tight is a narrower companion to Inter, sharing the same underlying design principles but with tighter spacing and condensed proportions suited to space-constrained layouts. It retains the original's screen legibility credentials while offering greater typographic density.
summary: Inter's condensed sibling, offering the same screen-ready clarity in a narrower, more space-efficient form.
description: Inter Tight mirrors the trusted legibility of Inter while compressing the horizontal footprint, making it well suited to data-dense interfaces, navigation elements, and headlines where economy of space matters. The full nine-weight range matches the original, giving designers consistent flexibility. It operates best in digital contexts where its screen-optimised origins remain an advantage.

name: Iosevka Charon
classification_notes: Iosevka Charon is a highly customised variant of the Iosevka typeface family, featuring narrower proportions and a utilitarian, code-focused aesthetic. Its extensive character set including Armenian and Braille reflects the open-source project's broad technical ambitions.
summary: A narrow, technically rigorous monospace drawn from the Iosevka project, built for coding and terminal use.
description: Iosevka Charon inherits the slender, space-efficient proportions that define the Iosevka family, making it particularly useful in split-screen coding environments and terminal applications where horizontal space is at a premium. Its inclusion of Armenian and Braille scripts marks it out as a broader multilingual technical resource. The four weights from Light to Bold provide a reasonable range for documentation and interface use alongside raw code.

name: Iosevka Charon Mono
classification_notes: Iosevka Charon Mono is the strictly monospaced variant within the Charon subfamily, enforcing uniform character widths compared to the quasi-proportional default. It shares the same condensed, utilitarian character and broad script coverage.
summary: The strictly monospaced cut of Iosevka Charon, maintaining uniform character widths for coding and terminal contexts.
description: Iosevka Charon Mono enforces consistent glyph widths throughout, making it the more orthodox choice for coding environments that demand true monospacing for alignment and readability. It carries the same narrow proportions and broad script support as Iosevka Charon, including Armenian and Braille. The four-weight range covers most practical needs for developers and technical writers.

name: Irish Grover
classification_notes: Irish Grover is a bold, informal display typeface with rough, hand-lettered characteristics and a distinctly playful, retro personality. It is a single-weight novelty font with limited practical application beyond decorative headline use.
summary: A rough, jovial display typeface with strong retro charm and limited but committed personality.
description: Irish Grover channels the spirit of hand-painted signage and vintage poster lettering, with irregular stroke edges and an exuberant bounce that makes it immediately expressive. Its single weight and display-only nature restrict it to headlines, posters, and novelty branding where character outweighs versatility. Best deployed sparingly where a warm, informal, retro tone is the primary requirement.

name: Island Moments
classification_notes: A flowing, casual brush script with loose, expressive letterforms and natural ink-like strokes. Designed for decorative headline use rather than extended reading.
summary: A loose, romantic brush script best suited to decorative headlines and lifestyle branding.
description: Island Moments carries a warm, carefree energy through its fluid, hand-lettered strokes. Its generous Latin Extended and Vietnamese coverage makes it useful for multilingual display contexts. Best deployed sparingly for wedding stationery, travel branding, or social media graphics.

name: Istok Web
classification_notes: A clean humanist sans-serif with open apertures and balanced proportions, designed with screen legibility in mind. Cyrillic support makes it a practical choice for multilingual digital projects.
summary: A no-nonsense humanist sans with solid Cyrillic support, built for screen readability.
description: Istok Web is a practical workhorse designed to perform reliably at small sizes on screen. Its humanist skeleton ensures warmth and legibility across body copy and interface text. The inclusion of Cyrillic and Cyrillic Extended scripts makes it a dependable choice for Russian and Eastern European digital publishing.

name: Italiana
classification_notes: Despite being classified as sans-serif in the catalogue, Italiana is a high-contrast serif with elegant, fashion-forward letterforms reminiscent of Italian didone traditions. Extremely fine hairlines contrast with bold strokes for a luxurious aesthetic.
summary: A high-contrast didone-style serif dripping with Italian fashion-house elegance.
description: Italiana draws directly from the dramatic contrast ratios of classic Italian display typography, pairing razor-thin hairlines against bold main strokes. It is best deployed at large sizes where its refinement can be fully appreciated — think luxury brand identities, editorial covers, and high-end packaging. Its single regular weight limits versatility but sharpens its focus as a pure display face.

name: Italianno
classification_notes: A refined, upright-leaning formal script with elegant, calligraphic letterforms and fine hairline contrast. Echoes Italian copperplate penmanship traditions.
summary: A graceful formal script evoking copperplate penmanship with an Italian classical sensibility.
description: Italianno channels traditional calligraphic craft through its fine strokes and flowing, connected letterforms. It is well suited to formal invitations, luxury packaging, and branding contexts where a sense of heritage and refinement is required. Latin Extended and Vietnamese coverage extends its reach across multilingual display applications.

name: Itim
classification_notes: A friendly, handwritten-style script with rounded, informal letterforms and consistent stroke weight. Designed with Thai script support, making it distinctive among casual Latin scripts on Google Fonts.
summary: A cheerful, rounded handwriting script with the notable advantage of Thai script support.
description: Itim's informal, pen-drawn character gives it a warm and approachable personality suited to children's content, casual branding, and social media. Its consistent stroke weight and rounded terminals keep it highly legible even at modest sizes. The inclusion of Thai alongside Latin makes it a genuinely useful choice for bilingual Thai–English design projects.

name: Jacquard 12
classification_notes: A low-resolution pixel/bitmap-style display typeface that emulates 12×12 grid dot-matrix or embroidery chart aesthetics. Each letterform is constructed from a rigid modular grid.
summary: A rigid 12-unit pixel grid typeface that embraces lo-fi bitmap and embroidery chart aesthetics.
description: Jacquard 12 reduces letterforms to their most elemental pixel-by-pixel construction, referencing both early digital displays and textile charting. Its Math script support is an unusual inclusion that hints at technical and diagrammatic applications. Best used for display headlines, game interfaces, or projects where a deliberate retro-digital aesthetic is the point.

name: Jacquard 12 Charted
classification_notes: A variant of Jacquard 12 featuring charted or outlined pixel construction, creating a grid-dot aesthetic that more explicitly references cross-stitch and embroidery pattern charts.
summary: The charted companion to Jacquard 12, rendering letterforms as explicit embroidery-chart dot grids.
description: Jacquard 12 Charted takes the pixel-grid concept of its sibling and exposes the underlying chart structure, so each 'pixel' reads as a marked grid cell rather than a filled square. This makes the textile and craft reference more overt and gives headlines a distinctly open, airy texture. It is a niche novelty face but effective for projects leaning into craft, gaming, or retro-digital visual culture.

name: Jacquard 24
classification_notes: A higher-resolution pixel typeface built on a 24-unit grid, offering noticeably smoother diagonal approximations and greater letterform detail than its 12-unit sibling.
summary: A 24-unit pixel grid typeface that brings greater resolution and refinement to the Jacquard bitmap family.
description: Jacquard 24 doubles the grid resolution of Jacquard 12, allowing for more convincing curves and diagonal strokes while retaining the unmistakable pixel-construction aesthetic. The additional detail makes it slightly more legible at mid-sizes whilst preserving the retro-digital character. It suits display headlines, poster work, and screen-culture branding where a polished but still explicitly pixelated look is desired.

name: Jacquard 24 Charted
classification_notes: Constructed from a grid of small squares mimicking jacquard loom punch-card patterns, giving it a distinctly pixelated, textile-inspired appearance. The 'Charted' variant adds a visible grid overlay that emphasises the woven structure.
summary: A pixel-grid typeface that simulates jacquard weaving patterns, making every letter look like it was charted for a loom.
description: Jacquard 24 Charted renders letterforms as dot-matrix grids, evoking the punch-card charts used in textile weaving. The charted variant overlays the underlying grid, reinforcing the craft and technical aesthetic. Best suited to display headings, textile branding, and retro-digital editorial work.

name: Jacquarda Bastarda 9
classification_notes: A bastarda-style blackletter rendered at an extremely coarse pixel resolution of 9 units, merging medieval calligraphic tradition with raw low-resolution bitmap aesthetics. The collision of historical letterform and digital degradation is the defining tension of the design.
summary: A bastarda blackletter reduced to a brutally coarse 9-pixel grid, fusing medieval script with lo-fi digital texture.
description: Jacquarda Bastarda 9 takes the flowing diagonal strokes of bastarda calligraphy and rebuilds them at a 9-unit pixel resolution, producing chunky, deliberately crude letterforms. The result is simultaneously archaic and digital, making it ideal for music poster work, horror-adjacent branding, or any context demanding dramatic, genre-bending display type. Its math script support is an unusual inclusion that broadens its expressive range.

name: Jacquarda Bastarda 9 Charted
classification_notes: Identical in concept to Jacquarda Bastarda 9 but with a visible grid chart overlay, making the pixel construction explicit and adding a graph-paper or weave-chart visual quality to the blackletter forms.
summary: The charted companion to Jacquarda Bastarda 9, exposing the underlying pixel grid to amplify the typeface's textile-digital concept.
description: Jacquarda Bastarda 9 Charted layers a visible square grid over the coarse blackletter forms, making the construction process part of the visual statement. This variant leans even harder into the craft-pattern aesthetic, evoking cross-stitch charts or weaving diagrams. Most useful for projects where the grid itself is a meaningful design element rather than an incidental feature.

name: Jacques Francois
classification_notes: A refined French-style transitional serif with moderate contrast, bracketed serifs, and elegant proportions inspired by 18th-century French typography. The letterforms are clean and bookish without being stiff.
summary: An elegant French-inflected transitional serif that brings 18th-century typographic refinement to contemporary editorial and branding work.
description: Jacques François captures the restrained sophistication of classical French book typography, with well-judged contrast and graceful stroke endings. It performs well at text sizes for editorial body copy and equally holds its own in headline settings where a refined, literary tone is needed. Its name and character make it a natural choice for fashion, cultural, and heritage branding.

name: Jacques Francois Shadow
classification_notes: A decorative companion to Jacques François that adds a hard drop shadow built directly into the glyph outlines, creating a vintage chromatic type effect from a single font file. The shadow is offset diagonally, giving strong three-dimensional presence.
summary: Jacques François Shadow bakes a vintage drop-shadow effect directly into the font, delivering instant chromatic poster drama in a single weight.
description: By encoding the shadow as part of the glyph itself, this typeface allows designers to achieve a classic layered-type look without compositing or duplication in their layout tool. The effect reads immediately as 19th-century display printing, making it well suited to posters, event graphics, and heritage-styled packaging. It is inherently a one-trick font, but it executes that trick with charm and period accuracy.

name: Jaini
classification_notes: A decorative Devanagari display typeface with rounded, almost toy-like strokes and a friendly, expressive character informed by traditional hand-lettering. The Latin companion follows the same rounded, playful logic.
summary: A warm, rounded display typeface designed for Devanagari that brings a joyful, hand-crafted sensibility to both its Indic and Latin glyphs.
description: Jaini draws on vernacular hand-lettering traditions to produce Devanagari letterforms that feel approachable and celebratory rather than formal. Its softened strokes and generous curves make it suitable for children's publishing, festive packaging, and cultural branding projects that require authentic Devanagari display type with personality. The Latin characters are thoughtfully matched in weight and mood.

name: Jaini Purva
classification_notes: A companion to Jaini described as 'Purva' (earlier or eastern), sharing a similar rounded and expressive display aesthetic but with subtly different stroke construction and cultural reference points within Devanagari lettering traditions.
summary: Jaini Purva is a rounded Devanagari display face that offers a slightly distinct hand-lettered variation on the Jaini aesthetic.
description: Where Jaini presents one interpretation of expressive Devanagari display lettering, Jaini Purva provides an alternate take — the two are designed as a complementary pair for projects requiring typographic variety within a consistent visual language. It shares the rounded, celebratory tone of its sibling and is equally at home in festive, cultural, or children's contexts. Together the two faces offer useful flexibility for Devanagari-first design work.

name: Jaldi
classification_notes: A clean, functional humanist sans-serif designed primarily for Devanagari text use, with open apertures and modest stroke contrast that aid legibility at small sizes. The Latin is harmonised in weight and rhythm with the Devanagari component.
summary: A practical, legible humanist sans designed for Devanagari body text, with a Latin companion that sits comfortably alongside it.
description: Jaldi prioritises readability across digital and print applications for Devanagari script, with open letterforms and clear differentiation between similar characters. Its two-weight family — Regular and Bold — keeps things simple but covers the essential typographic hierarchy for body-copy settings. It is a dependable workhorse choice for multilingual publishing, app interfaces, and documents requiring reliable Devanagari text type.

name: Jaro
classification_notes: Jaro features soft, rounded terminals and a compact, nearly monolinear construction with a tall x-height. Its single weight carries a friendly, approachable character suited to display sizes.
summary: A rounded, compact sans-serif with a warm geometric personality that works well at display sizes.
description: Jaro's uniform stroke weight and softly rounded terminals give it an immediately approachable feel that sits comfortably in branding, app interfaces, and headline contexts. Its extended Latin and Vietnamese support broadens its utility across multilingual projects. The single weight limits its typographic range, but within that constraint it performs with consistency and charm.

name: Jersey 10
classification_notes: Jersey 10 is a bitmap-inspired pixel font constructed on a coarse 10-unit grid, faithfully replicating the aesthetic of early low-resolution digital displays. Letterforms are blocky and strictly orthogonal.
summary: A coarse 10-pixel grid font that channels the raw aesthetic of early arcade and home-computer typography.
description: Jersey 10 reproduces the chunky, limited character of 10-pixel bitmap lettering, making it an immediately recognisable reference to early video game and computing culture. It is best deployed at large sizes where the pixel grid reads clearly, in gaming interfaces, retro-themed branding, or nostalgic editorial work. At small sizes the legibility collapses, so its application is firmly in the display register.

name: Jersey 10 Charted
classification_notes: Jersey 10 Charted shares the 10-unit pixel grid of Jersey 10 but includes visible grid or chart-style detailing within the letterforms, adding a structured, graph-paper quality. It is a decorative variant of the base Jersey 10 design.
summary: A charted variant of Jersey 10 that overlays a visible grid structure onto the pixel letterforms for an extra layer of retro-technical character.
description: Jersey 10 Charted extends the pixel-grid concept by making the underlying construction grid visible within each glyph, evoking graph paper, circuit-board diagrams, and early computer printouts. This additional texture makes it even more niche than its base counterpart, restricting it almost entirely to decorative headline and poster use. It suits projects where the visible grid motif reinforces a technical or retro-computing narrative.

name: Jersey 15
classification_notes: Jersey 15 is built on a 15-unit pixel grid, offering slightly more resolution and letter detail than Jersey 10 while retaining the characteristic blocky bitmap aesthetic. Stroke construction remains strictly rectilinear.
summary: A mid-resolution pixel font on a 15-unit grid that balances retro bitmap character with marginally improved legibility over the coarser Jersey 10.
description: Jersey 15 occupies the middle ground in the Jersey family, offering more letterform definition than the 10-pixel variant while still reading unmistakably as a pixel font. The added resolution allows for slightly more nuanced diagonal hints and punctuation, making it a touch more versatile for retro-styled interfaces or editorial use. Like its siblings, it is a one-register display face with limited typographic flexibility.

name: Jersey 15 Charted
classification_notes: Jersey 15 Charted applies visible grid detailing to the 15-unit pixel base, combining a slightly finer bitmap resolution with the graph-paper overlay treatment seen across the Charted variants. The result is a more detailed decorative pixel face.
summary: A charted variant of Jersey 15 that introduces a visible construction grid into moderately resolved pixel letterforms.
description: Jersey 15 Charted inherits the slightly improved resolution of Jersey 15 and layers the signature grid-overlay treatment on top, producing letterforms that read as simultaneously technical and nostalgic. The combination suits retro-computing aesthetics, game-jam projects, and any context where visible pixel structure is itself the design statement. It remains firmly a decorative display face with no practical body-text application.

name: Jersey 20
classification_notes: Jersey 20 uses a 20-unit pixel grid, providing noticeably more letterform detail and smoother apparent curves than the coarser Jersey variants. It begins to approach the legibility threshold where pixel fonts can function in subheading contexts.
summary: A 20-pixel grid font that offers the most readable entry in the standard Jersey series without sacrificing its retro bitmap identity.
description: Jersey 20's finer grid resolution allows for more convincing letter shapes, with diagonal strokes and rounded characters appearing more refined than in the 10 or 15 variants. This makes it the most practically useful of the standard Jersey fonts, suitable for game UI text, retro-themed subheadings, and digital signage where the pixel aesthetic is intentional. It still belongs unambiguously to the display category and should not be set small.

name: Jersey 20 Charted
classification_notes: Jersey 20 Charted applies the visible grid-overlay treatment to the 20-unit pixel base, combining relatively detailed letterforms with the graph-paper aesthetic that characterises all Charted variants. It is the most resolved of the Charted sub-series.
summary: The most resolved of the Charted pixel variants, combining a 20-unit grid with visible construction lines for a detailed retro-technical effect.
description: Jersey 20 Charted achieves the greatest letterform clarity in the Charted sub-series, as the 20-unit resolution gives the internal grid overlay room to read legibly rather than reducing glyphs to noise. It suits projects that want a technical, engineered quality alongside retro-computing nostalgia — think game credits, sci-fi interfaces, or data-art installations. Despite its relative refinement, it remains a single-purpose decorative display face.

name: Jersey 25
classification_notes: Jersey 25 is the finest-resolution member of the Jersey family, built on a 25-unit pixel grid that produces comparatively smooth and well-proportioned letterforms. At this resolution the pixel construction becomes a stylistic choice rather than an obvious constraint.
summary: The highest-resolution Jersey variant, where a 25-unit pixel grid yields letterforms refined enough to read clearly while still carrying unmistakable bitmap character.
description: Jersey 25 sits at the top of the family's resolution hierarchy, and its finer grid allows for convincing curves, clean diagonals, and improved overall legibility compared to its siblings. It is the most versatile Jersey font, capable of handling headline text in gaming contexts, retro-themed editorial work, and digital displays where the pixel aesthetic is a deliberate design choice. Even so, it remains a display face — its personality is too specific for neutral body-text use.

name: Jersey 25 Charted
classification_notes: A chunky pixel-grid typeface built on a 25-pixel charter grid, giving it a distinctly retro video-game aesthetic. The letterforms are blocky and highly legible at large sizes despite their pixelated construction.
summary: A bold pixel-grid display face with strong retro video-game energy.
description: Jersey 25 Charted draws directly from the visual language of early arcade and console gaming, with each glyph constructed on a rigid pixel matrix. It works best at large sizes for headlines, posters, and game interfaces where its chunky, nostalgic character can dominate. It has limited utility at body sizes but excels as a statement display face.

name: JetBrains Mono
classification_notes: Designed specifically for developers, JetBrains Mono features increased x-height, wide apertures, and ligature support for common coding sequences. Its letterforms are optimised to reduce eye strain during prolonged reading of code.
summary: A purpose-built coding typeface from JetBrains that prioritises legibility and developer ergonomics.
description: JetBrains Mono was engineered from the ground up with software developers in mind, offering a generous range of weights from Thin to ExtraBold and broad script support including Cyrillic and Greek. Its distinctive features include programming ligatures and carefully tuned spacing that makes dense code easier to parse at a glance. It is one of the most polished and well-considered coding fonts available on Google Fonts.

name: Jim Nightshade
classification_notes: Jim Nightshade is an energetic brush script with dramatic stroke contrast and a strong calligraphic rhythm. The letterforms lean into an expressive, hand-painted quality with vigorous connections between characters.
summary: A vigorous brush script with bold stroke contrast and a lively, hand-painted character.
description: Jim Nightshade channels the energy of mid-century commercial lettering, with thick-to-thin transitions and fluid connections that give it real typographic presence. It suits display applications such as poster headlines, artisan branding, and editorial pull quotes where an expressive, human touch is wanted. Being a single-weight script, its range of use is naturally limited.

name: Joan
classification_notes: Joan is a refined, high-contrast serif with a distinctly editorial sensibility, featuring delicate hairline serifs and elegant proportions. Its restrained design sits comfortably between classical tradition and modern minimalism.
summary: An elegant high-contrast serif with a cool, editorial confidence suited to fashion and culture publishing.
description: Joan's sharp hairline serifs and refined proportions make it well suited to magazine mastheads, editorial layouts, and luxury brand identities where typographic sophistication matters. It carries an understated modernity that works especially well in large headline settings. Its delicate contrast means care should be taken at small sizes and on low-resolution screens.

name: Jockey One
classification_notes: Jockey One is a sturdy, condensed display sans-serif with a strong vertical emphasis and relatively uniform stroke weight. The compact proportions and bold presence make it well suited to space-constrained headline use.
summary: A robust condensed sans-serif built for punchy, space-efficient headlines.
description: Jockey One packs considerable typographic impact into a narrow footprint, making it a practical choice for banners, signage, and headline typography where horizontal space is at a premium. Its single regular weight limits its versatility in longer hierarchies, but within its niche it is clean and dependable. The slightly sporty, confident tone makes it a natural fit for events, sports branding, and editorial contexts.

name: Jolly Lodger
classification_notes: Jolly Lodger is a novelty display font with an openwork or inline decorative construction, giving letters a playful, circus-poster quality. The single weight and highly ornamental nature limit its application to specific thematic contexts.
summary: A circus-inspired inline display face dripping with Victorian fairground charm.
description: Jolly Lodger leans heavily on the aesthetic of late-19th-century decorative wood type, with its openwork letterforms evoking carnival posters and music-hall signage. It is a one-trick font, but within that trick it is committed and well executed, working best at large sizes for themed events, retro branding, or novelty packaging. Versatility is not the point here — atmosphere is.

name: Jomhuria
classification_notes: Jomhuria combines Latin letterforms with Arabic script characteristics, featuring high contrast and a somewhat decorative display quality. The design draws on calligraphic tradition while adapting it to a Latin structure.
summary: A high-contrast display serif with calligraphic roots bridging Latin and Arabic typographic traditions.
description: Jomhuria is designed with multilingual contexts in mind, harmonising Latin display letterforms with Arabic-influenced stroke qualities for cultural and editorial work that spans both scripts. Its dramatic contrast and confident presence make it effective for headlines and poster work. The single-weight offering keeps it firmly in display territory.

name: Jomolhari
classification_notes: Jomolhari is a careful digital revival of traditional Tibetan script, designed to support both Tibetan and Latin text with complementary proportions. The Latin component is a restrained old-style serif intended to harmonise with the Tibetan glyphs.
summary: A thoughtfully crafted Tibetan and Latin typeface that brings traditional Tibetan script into high-quality digital typography.
description: Jomolhari is an essential resource for publishers, academics, and cultural organisations working with Tibetan-language content, offering careful attention to the conventions of traditional Uchen script. The accompanying Latin is a modest old-style serif designed to complement rather than compete with the Tibetan glyphs in mixed-script documents. It is a specialist tool, but within its domain it is one of the most reliable options available on Google Fonts.

name: Josefin Sans
classification_notes: Josefin Sans draws on 1920s–30s geometric sans traditions, with even stroke widths, near-perfect circular forms, and distinctively tall, elegant proportions. The thin weights in particular have a strong Art Deco flavour.
summary: A refined geometric sans with strong Art Deco roots and elegant proportions that work particularly well at large sizes.
description: Josefin Sans channels the geometric type design of the early twentieth century, delivering clean, symmetrical letterforms with a decidedly vintage sensibility. Its tall x-height and uniform stroke weight make it a natural choice for fashion branding, editorial headlines, and luxury packaging. The wide weight range and variable font support give designers considerable flexibility.

name: Josefin Slab
classification_notes: Josefin Slab pairs the same geometric, Art Deco-influenced skeleton as its sans companion with sturdy slab serifs of uniform thickness. The result is a characterful display slab with a strongly retro personality.
summary: The slab serif companion to Josefin Sans, sharing its geometric Art Deco skeleton with the addition of uniform slab serifs.
description: Josefin Slab applies a consistent slab serif treatment to the same underlying geometric structure as Josefin Sans, making the two typefaces highly compatible for mixed-face typographic work. Its retro character suits editorial projects, poster design, and branding that needs a period flavour without feeling pastiche. Text sizes are serviceable but the design is most at home in display contexts.

name: Jost
classification_notes: Jost is a contemporary geometric sans modelled closely on the early twentieth-century German grotesque tradition, with optically corrected proportions and a large Cyrillic character set. It reads cleanly at both display and text sizes.
summary: A clean, versatile geometric sans with a strong Bauhaus influence and broad multilingual support including Cyrillic.
description: Jost takes clear inspiration from Futura and other Bauhaus-era geometric grotesques, but introduces subtle optical corrections that improve readability at smaller sizes. Its large weight range from Thin to Black makes it adaptable across branding, interface design, and editorial work. The inclusion of Cyrillic alongside Latin extended characters gives it genuine utility for multilingual projects.

name: Joti One
classification_notes: Joti One is a novelty display face featuring exaggerated, bubbly letterforms with informal ink-trap-like detailing and heavy weight. It has a distinctly playful, juvenile aesthetic with limited application range.
summary: A chunky, bubbly novelty display face with an exuberant and distinctly juvenile character.
description: Joti One leans fully into its playful personality, offering heavy, rounded letterforms that work for children's media, fun posters, or informal display contexts. Its limited single-weight offering and narrow use case make it a one-trick typeface that is difficult to deploy with much typographic sophistication. Best used sparingly and at large sizes where its exuberance is a feature rather than a liability.

name: Jua
classification_notes: Jua is a Korean-Latin display sans with softly rounded terminals and a friendly, informal weight. Designed primarily for Korean web use, it has a compact structure optimised for screen rendering.
summary: A rounded, friendly sans designed primarily for Korean web typography, with a warm and approachable character.
description: Jua delivers a warm, informal aesthetic suited to Korean-language digital content, social media graphics, and casual interface text. Its rounded terminals and even weight give it an approachable personality that works well for children's content, informal branding, and app interfaces targeting Korean-speaking audiences. The Latin companion is functional but secondary to the Korean design.

name: Judson
classification_notes: Judson is a practical humanist text serif with moderate contrast, open apertures, and sturdy serifs intended to maximise legibility for extended reading on screen. Its design is unpretentious and workhorse in character.
summary: A sturdy humanist text serif designed with screen readability in mind, offering reliable performance in body copy settings.
description: Judson prioritises legibility over stylistic flourish, making it a dependable choice for long-form reading on digital platforms. Its open apertures, moderate stroke contrast, and well-considered spacing ensure it holds up in running text across a range of screen sizes and resolutions. With only Regular and Bold weights on offer, it is best suited to projects where a workhorse serif is needed rather than typographic variety.

name: Julee
classification_notes: Julee is a loose, informal handwritten script with irregular baseline behaviour and a relaxed, spontaneous character. The letterforms are simple and sketchlike rather than calligraphic in the traditional sense.
summary: A casual, handwritten script with a loose and spontaneous quality suited to informal display use.
description: Julee mimics the feel of quick, unpolished handwriting, giving it a natural, unpretentious character that suits informal invitations, greeting cards, and casual branding. Its irregularity lends charm at display sizes but makes it unsuitable for body text or any context requiring legibility under pressure. The single weight limits its typographic versatility considerably.

name: Julius Sans One
classification_notes: Julius Sans One is a spaced, all-caps-optimised geometric sans with elegantly thin strokes and a strong Art Deco sensibility. It is designed almost exclusively for headline and titling use with generous letter spacing.
summary: An elegantly spaced geometric sans conceived almost entirely for all-caps titling, with a refined Art Deco character.
description: Julius Sans One is built around wide tracking and thin, uniform strokes that give it a luxurious, high-fashion sensibility reminiscent of 1920s titling. It is nearly unusable at text sizes or in mixed case, but in all-caps headline settings it delivers instant elegance for fashion, beauty, and premium branding work. Its single-weight limitation is part of its focused, purposeful design philosophy.

name: Junge
classification_notes: Junge is a refined, low-contrast serif with a classical humanist structure and clean, open letterforms. Its elegant proportions give it a literary quality suited to formal editorial use.
summary: A quietly elegant humanist serif with classical proportions and a restrained, literary character.
description: Junge carries the measured refinement of traditional book typefaces without feeling archaic, making it well suited to long-form editorial and literary publishing. Its single Regular weight limits versatility, but within that constraint it performs reliably for body text and subheadings. It suits contexts where understated sophistication is preferred over typographic variety.

name: Jura
classification_notes: Jura is a low-contrast geometric sans with distinctive angular details and a slightly technical, futuristic character. Its letterforms have an engineered precision that sets it apart from warmer humanist alternatives.
summary: A precise geometric sans with angular detailing and a cool, technical personality across a broad range of scripts.
description: Jura's engineered aesthetic makes it a natural fit for interfaces, dashboards, and technology-oriented branding where a clinical clarity is an asset. Its support for Cyrillic Extended and Greek scripts makes it genuinely useful for multilingual projects. The range of weights from Light to Bold provides reasonable flexibility for hierarchical typographic systems.

name: Just Another Hand
classification_notes: Just Another Hand is a loose, informal all-caps-style handwritten script with an unaffected, sketchy quality. Its simplicity reads as spontaneous rather than designed.
summary: A breezy, unpretentious handwritten script that trades polish for genuine informal charm.
description: Just Another Hand suits casual signage, social media graphics, and personal project branding where an authentic handmade feel is more important than elegance. Its single weight and limited Latin coverage make it a one-role typeface, best deployed at display sizes for short phrases or labels. It lacks the refinement for editorial or commercial brand use.

name: Just Me Again Down Here
classification_notes: Just Me Again Down Here is a whimsical, bouncy handwritten script with irregular baseline alignment and a self-consciously quirky personality. The letterforms feel drawn quickly with a fine-tipped pen.
summary: A quirky, bouncy handwritten script with deliberate irregularity and an overtly personal character.
description: The uneven baseline rhythm and informal letter construction give this typeface a diary-like intimacy, making it popular for greeting card graphics, craft projects, and lighthearted social content. It functions best at large display sizes where its personality is legible; at small sizes the irregular forms become harder to read. Its novelty is its main asset, and it offers little outside that specific register.

name: K2D
classification_notes: K2D is a clean, low-contrast geometric sans designed with strong Latin and Thai script support, featuring harmonious proportions across both writing systems. Its extensive weight range and open forms give it genuine typographic versatility.
summary: A well-constructed geometric sans with comprehensive Thai and Latin support across a generous weight range.
description: K2D is notable for the care taken to balance Latin and Thai letterforms, making it one of the more reliable choices for bilingual Thai–English design work. Its clean, neutral geometry means it functions equally well in interfaces, print editorial, and brand communications. The full weight range from Thin to ExtraBold gives designers ample room to build strong typographic hierarchies.

name: Kablammo
classification_notes: Kablammo is an exuberant variable display typeface with inflated, bubbly letterforms and an energetic, cartoonish quality. Its forms draw from comic and pop-art lettering traditions.
summary: An unapologetically loud bubble-letter display face built for maximum visual impact and cartoon energy.
description: Kablammo thrives in contexts demanding immediate attention — event posters, youth branding, social media visuals, and packaging where bold personality outweighs typographic convention. Its variable axis allows some control over the exaggerated forms. Cyrillic support broadens its usable range for multilingual campaigns, though its application is firmly limited to display use.

name: Kadwa
classification_notes: Kadwa is a sturdy, upright serif designed primarily for Devanagari with a complementary Latin companion, offering a reliable workhorse quality for bilingual South Asian publishing. Its forms are clear and functional rather than ornate.
summary: A dependable bilingual serif that bridges Devanagari and Latin scripts with consistent, readable letterforms.
description: Kadwa is designed to serve practical publishing and document needs in Hindi and other Devanagari-script languages, with Latin glyphs that harmonise without calling attention to themselves. Its two-weight range — Regular and Bold — is modest but sufficient for straightforward editorial hierarchies. It is a solid utilitarian choice for institutional, governmental, or educational typesetting requiring bilingual Devanagari–Latin support.

name: Kaisei Decol
classification_notes: Kaisei Decol is a decorative Japanese serif (Mincho-style) with deliberate stylistic embellishments that give it a literary and expressive character beyond standard text serifs. Its Latin glyphs are designed to harmonise with the Japanese type.
summary: A characterful decorative Mincho serif that brings expressive literary warmth to Japanese and Latin typography.
description: Kaisei Decol occupies a deliberate middle ground between functional text serif and decorative display face, making it suitable for Japanese editorial design, book covers, cultural branding, and headline typography. Its stylistic details — slightly accentuated stroke contrast and refined terminals — give printed and digital work a sophisticated, considered quality. The inclusion of Cyrillic alongside Japanese and Latin makes it an unusually broad multilingual offering for a display-leaning serif.

name: Kaisei HarunoUmi
classification_notes: A Japanese Mincho-style serif designed for calm, readable body text across CJK and Latin scripts. Features moderate contrast and traditional stroke endings suited to long-form reading.
summary: A composed Japanese Mincho serif that balances traditional CJK aesthetics with reliable Latin and Cyrillic support.
description: Kaisei HarunoUmi draws on classical Mincho proportions, offering restrained stroke contrast and sturdy serifs that hold up well at smaller sizes. Its multilingual range makes it a practical choice for Japanese publishing, academic documents, and editorial work that spans multiple scripts. The name evokes the spring sea — unhurried and expansive — which reflects its measured, readable character.

name: Kaisei Opti
classification_notes: An optically adjusted Mincho serif with slightly more open spacing and refined stroke endings compared to its Kaisei siblings, lending it a lighter, more refined texture on screen.
summary: A refined Mincho serif optimised for optical clarity, offering an elegant reading experience across Japanese, Cyrillic, and Latin text.
description: Kaisei Opti takes the Mincho tradition and applies optical sizing principles, resulting in a typeface that feels more open and airy than closely related cuts. It suits editorial layouts, digital publishing, and any context where Japanese and Latin text must coexist gracefully. The lighter overall texture distinguishes it within the Kaisei family for use at smaller display or comfortable body sizes.

name: Kaisei Tokumin
classification_notes: The workhorse member of the Kaisei family, offering the widest weight range including ExtraBold, making it suitable for both body copy and prominent headlines within a unified Japanese–Latin typographic system.
summary: The most versatile Kaisei serif, spanning thin to ExtraBold for a fully cohesive Japanese–Latin typographic system.
description: Kaisei Tokumin extends the Mincho framework with an ExtraBold weight not found in its siblings, giving designers the typographic range needed to build complete hierarchies in a single family. It performs reliably at body sizes and gains authority as weights increase, making it well suited to newspaper-style layouts, digital magazines, and long-form Japanese web content. Cyrillic support broadens its international applicability further.

name: Kalam
classification_notes: A casual, pen-written script with informal letterforms that convey a natural handwritten quality across both Devanagari and Latin. Stroke variation is moderate and approachable rather than calligraphic.
summary: A warm, casual handwritten script that bridges Devanagari and Latin with a consistent pen-on-paper personality.
description: Kalam was designed to bring an authentic handwritten feel to both Latin and Devanagari scripts, a pairing that demands careful attention to stroke rhythm and cultural authenticity. Its Light to Bold range allows it to function in children's educational materials, informal branding, greeting cards, and social media graphics. The typeface avoids the stiffness common to digitised handwriting, maintaining a believable spontaneity across glyphs.

name: Kalnia
classification_notes: A high-contrast display serif with elegant, elongated proportions and fine hairlines that suggest luxury fashion typography. The extensive weight range from Thin to Bold supports diverse hierarchical use.
summary: An elegant high-contrast display serif with razor-fine hairlines and fashionable proportions suited to luxury branding and editorial headlines.
description: Kalnia pairs dramatic stroke contrast with tall, refined letterforms that immediately register as premium and contemporary. It includes mathematical symbol support alongside extended Latin, making it a surprisingly versatile choice beyond pure display work. Designers will find it most at home in fashion lookbooks, high-end packaging, magazine covers, and brand identities where sophistication is paramount.

name: Kalnia Glaze
classification_notes: A glazed variant of Kalnia featuring an inline or layered decorative treatment that enhances its display character, intended exclusively for headline and poster use at large sizes.
summary: Kalnia Glaze adds a decorative inline treatment to its parent design, amplifying its luxury display presence for posters and editorial splash pages.
description: Where Kalnia already carries confident contrast, the Glaze variant layers an ornamental effect onto the letterforms that rewards large-scale use but becomes impractical at smaller sizes. This makes it a deliberate one-context tool: striking at display sizes and uncompromising in its decorative intent. It is best deployed for headline moments in fashion campaigns, event posters, and premium packaging where spectacle is the goal.

name: Kameron
classification_notes: A sturdy, low-contrast slab serif with squared, unbracketed serifs and a robust construction that prioritises legibility and strong texture across weights.
summary: A dependable slab serif with squared serifs and solid construction that delivers authority at headline sizes and in branding contexts.
description: Kameron takes its cues from nineteenth-century slab serif traditions, presenting unbracketed serifs and low stroke contrast that give it a no-nonsense, industrial confidence. It works particularly well for editorial headlines, packaging typography, and logotype work where a typeface needs to command attention without resorting to decorative excess. The weight range from Regular to Bold provides enough variation for simple typographic hierarchies.

name: Kanchenjunga
classification_notes: A clean humanist sans-serif with support for the Kirat Rai script alongside Latin, representing a notable contribution to digital typography for an underserved writing system.
summary: A clean humanist sans that pairs Latin with Kirat Rai script support, making it a rare and valuable resource for Kiranti-language digital publishing.
description: Kanchenjunga is notable primarily for its Kirat Rai script coverage, serving a community with limited high-quality digital typeface options. The Latin component follows humanist sans conventions with open apertures and even spacing, making the combined family functional for bilingual interfaces and documents. Its four weights give designers enough range for basic typographic hierarchy in both scripts.

name: Kanit
classification_notes: Kanit is a loopless sans-serif designed to harmonise Latin and Thai scripts, with slightly condensed proportions and strong geometric underpinnings. The letterforms share a uniform stroke weight and a contemporary, slightly industrial character.
summary: A sleek, condensed geometric sans that bridges Latin and Thai scripts with confident, modern proportions.
description: Kanit's slightly compressed geometry makes it a strong choice for headlines, packaging, and branding projects that need a contemporary edge. Its extensive weight range from Thin to Black gives designers considerable flexibility across both display and text applications. The Thai and Latin scripts are carefully matched in weight and optical size, making it genuinely useful for bilingual Thai–English communication.

name: Kantumruy Pro
classification_notes: Kantumruy Pro is a clean, humanist sans-serif designed primarily to serve the Khmer script, with Latin support that follows the same open, legible principles. Letterforms are carefully constructed for clarity at small sizes on screen.
summary: A purposeful humanist sans built for the Khmer script, offering excellent legibility across digital and print contexts.
description: Kantumruy Pro addresses a genuine typographic need by providing a well-spaced, optically balanced Khmer typeface with matching Latin. Its open apertures and consistent stroke modulation make it reliable for body text in apps, websites, and editorial layouts serving Khmer-speaking audiences. The weight range from Thin to Bold gives it enough versatility for hierarchy without sacrificing its core legibility.

name: Kapakana
classification_notes: Kapakana is a decorative script typeface drawing on Japanese brush calligraphy traditions, blending Latin letterforms into the same expressive, flowing aesthetic. The design is highly stylised and primarily suited to decorative and display purposes.
summary: An expressive brush-influenced script that fuses Japanese calligraphic tradition with Latin letterforms into a single decorative system.
description: Kapakana is a niche display typeface intended for contexts where a Japanese aesthetic is central to the visual identity — packaging, posters, branding for food, culture, or lifestyle products. Its fluid, brush-like strokes create a sense of handcrafted energy, though its highly stylised nature limits legibility at small sizes. Best used sparingly as a headline or logo element rather than for extended text.

name: Karantina
classification_notes: Karantina is a display typeface designed to support both Hebrew and Latin scripts with a distinctive, hand-drawn quality and loosely stencilled aesthetic. The letterforms are irregular and playful, clearly optimised for decorative rather than functional use.
summary: A quirky, hand-drawn display face that pairs Hebrew and Latin in an irregular, stencil-inflected style.
description: Karantina's roughened, loosely geometric letterforms give it a raw, street-art quality that suits posters, event graphics, and branding projects seeking an edgy or DIY aesthetic. Its Hebrew and Latin integration is a genuine asset for bilingual Israeli or international design work. The limited three-weight range and narrow stylistic scope make it a one-trick typeface best deployed with restraint.

name: Karla
classification_notes: Karla is a clean, slightly condensed humanist sans-serif with friendly, open letterforms and a subtle warmth that distinguishes it from colder grotesques. It was designed for screen legibility and performs well across a wide range of sizes.
summary: A dependable humanist sans with warm, open letterforms that works reliably as a body text and UI typeface.
description: Karla sits comfortably in the workhorse category — it is not flashy, but it is consistently legible and pleasant to read on screen across its full weight range. Its slight condensing gives it good economy in layouts without sacrificing readability, making it well-suited to app interfaces, dashboards, and long-form editorial on web. The extensive weight options allow for clear typographic hierarchy in both display and text roles.

name: Karla Tamil Inclined
classification_notes: Karla Tamil Inclined is the oblique companion to Karla Tamil Upright, offering a slanted variant for emphasis within Tamil-language typography. The design follows humanist sans principles adapted carefully to Tamil script conventions.
summary: The inclined companion to Karla Tamil Upright, providing an oblique option for Tamil-language text with clean humanist construction.
description: Karla Tamil Inclined is a functional supplementary face designed to work in tandem with its upright sibling, offering a means of emphasis in Tamil typographic contexts where italic styles are not traditionally part of the script's conventions. Its clean lines and modest weight range make it suitable for body text in digital publications and apps. The two-weight offering is minimal, limiting its versatility in complex typographic systems.

name: Karla Tamil Upright
classification_notes: Karla Tamil Upright is a humanist sans-serif designed to bring the same clean, legible character of the original Karla to the Tamil script, with matching Latin support. Letterforms are upright, open, and optimised for screen reading.
summary: A clean humanist sans tailored for Tamil script, extending the Karla design system to serve Tamil-language digital publishing.
description: Karla Tamil Upright offers Tamil-speaking audiences a reliable, screen-optimised typeface with the open, legible character associated with the broader Karla family. Its design bridges Tamil and Latin letterforms without compromising the integrity of either script, making it a sensible choice for bilingual Tamil–English web and app projects. The limited weight range of Regular and Bold may restrict its use in applications requiring finer typographic hierarchy.

name: Karma
classification_notes: Karma is a humanist serif designed to harmonise Devanagari and Latin scripts within a single, cohesive typographic system, with moderate contrast and sturdy, readable letterforms. The design prioritises legibility and warmth over stylistic flourish.
summary: A warm humanist serif that thoughtfully unifies Devanagari and Latin scripts for bilingual body text applications.
description: Karma addresses the practical challenge of setting Devanagari and Latin together in continuous text by designing both scripts to share a compatible visual rhythm and weight. Its moderate stroke contrast and open letterforms make it a dependable choice for editorial content, digital publications, and websites serving Hindi or Marathi audiences alongside English. The five-weight range offers enough flexibility for establishing a clear typographic hierarchy in longer documents.

name: Katibeh
classification_notes: Katibeh is a Naskh-influenced Arabic display typeface with a compact, upright structure suited to headlines. The Latin companion shares a similar dense, decorative quality.
summary: A compact Arabic display face with Naskh roots, designed to make a bold impression at large sizes.
description: Katibeh draws on the Naskh calligraphic tradition while pushing towards a more upright, display-oriented construction. It is well suited to headlines, posters, and branding projects that require Arabic and Latin scripts to coexist. Its dense letterforms work best at larger sizes where the detail reads clearly.

name: Kaushan Script
classification_notes: Kaushan Script mimics casual brush lettering with lively, confident strokes and natural variation in stroke weight. Its connected letterforms give it an energetic, hand-painted quality.
summary: A lively brush script with confident strokes and natural energy, ideal for expressive headlines and branding.
description: Kaushan Script captures the spontaneity of brush lettering through varied stroke weights and fluid connections between letters. It works particularly well for logotypes, poster headlines, and packaging where a hand-crafted character is desired. Its bold, assured style means it commands attention without feeling overly formal.

name: Kavivanar
classification_notes: Kavivanar is a Tamil and Latin handwriting-style typeface with a gentle, informal rhythm and simple letterforms. The Tamil script integration is a relatively rare feature among script typefaces.
summary: A friendly handwritten script covering both Tamil and Latin, offering an informal warmth that is uncommon in multilingual type.
description: Kavivanar pairs a casual Latin handwriting style with a Tamil script counterpart, making it a distinctive choice for bilingual projects. Its simple, unpretentious letterforms lend themselves to informal signage, children's materials, and light editorial use. The Tamil coverage makes it genuinely useful for designers working across South Asian and Western contexts.

name: Kavoon
classification_notes: Kavoon is a quirky, rounded display face with letterforms that blend serif and decorative influences in an unusual, characterful way. Its uneven stroke modulation gives it a hand-drawn novelty feel.
summary: A quirky display typeface with an unusual blend of rounded forms and decorative character, best deployed sparingly.
description: Kavoon occupies an eccentric niche, combining rounded, slightly chunky letterforms with idiosyncratic details that give it a retro novelty quality. It suits display contexts such as poster headlines, children's branding, or themed packaging where personality is the priority. Its single weight limits its versatility, so it works best as an accent rather than a workhorse.

name: Kay Pho Du
classification_notes: Kay Pho Du is a serif typeface designed primarily for the Kayah Li script, a minority script of Myanmar, with a complementary Latin. Its multiple weights make it one of the more fully developed typefaces for this script on Google Fonts.
summary: A well-developed serif covering the rare Kayah Li script alongside Latin, offering genuine typographic range for an underserved writing system.
description: Kay Pho Du provides meaningful weight coverage from Regular to Bold, making it suitable for running text as well as display use in Kayah Li, a script spoken by communities in Myanmar and Thailand. This level of development is rare for a minority script typeface and gives designers real flexibility for bilingual publishing and educational materials. The Latin companion is functional and unobtrusive, sitting comfortably alongside the Kayah Li forms.

name: Kdam Thmor Pro
classification_notes: Kdam Thmor Pro is a geometric-influenced Khmer sans-serif with strong, clear forms suited to both display and text use. The Latin is a clean, neutral companion that does not overpower the Khmer.
summary: A strong, geometric Khmer sans-serif with clear, confident letterforms that work across display and text applications.
description: Kdam Thmor Pro brings a modern, structured clarity to Khmer script, which has historically been underserved by high-quality digital typefaces. Its geometric sensibility gives it a contemporary feel suited to branding, editorial, and screen use in Cambodian contexts. The accompanying Latin is clean and restrained, ensuring harmonious bilingual setting.

name: Keania One
classification_notes: Keania One is a bold, wide display typeface with thick, rounded letterforms and a strong retro-graphic character. Its single heavy weight signals it is built purely for impact at large sizes.
summary: A thick, wide display face with a bold retro personality that commands attention on posters and signage.
description: Keania One is built around heavy, rounded strokes and a generous width that give it unmistakable visual weight. It suits poster design, bold headlines, and signage where presence matters more than subtlety. The single-weight offering limits its scope, but within that constraint it delivers a clear, confident aesthetic.

name: Kedebideri
classification_notes: Kedebideri is a sans-serif typeface designed for the Beria Erfe script, a recently created writing system for the Boro language of northeast India, with a substantial six-weight range. As one of very few typefaces supporting this script, it fulfils a rare and practical typographic need.
summary: A clean, well-weighted sans-serif providing essential typographic support for the Beria Erfe script, a newly created writing system for the Boro language.
description: Kedebideri stands out for covering the Beria Erfe script across six weights, from Regular to Black, giving designers and publishers genuine flexibility when working with Boro-language content. This degree of weight coverage is a meaningful contribution to a script with almost no prior digital type support. The Latin forms are straightforward and neutral, making bilingual composition workable without visual conflict.

name: Kelly Slab
classification_notes: Kelly Slab is a robust Egyptian-style slab serif with sturdy bracketed serifs and even stroke contrast, designed for display use. Its Cyrillic support broadens its utility across multilingual settings.
summary: A sturdy, characterful slab serif with strong display presence and solid Cyrillic support.
description: Kelly Slab draws on the Egyptian slab serif tradition, offering thick, confident strokes and well-bracketed serifs that hold up well at large sizes. Its multilingual range makes it a practical choice for display headlines across Latin and Cyrillic scripts. Best deployed on posters, packaging, or editorial headers where a vintage-inflected robustness is desired.

name: Kenia
classification_notes: Kenia is a highly ornamental display typeface with an intricate, woven or stencil-like aesthetic that draws on decorative lettering traditions. It is strictly a novelty face with very limited versatility.
summary: An ornate, intricately patterned display face suited to one-off decorative applications.
description: Kenia features elaborate internal detailing and a heavily stylised structure that gives it a distinctly craft or folk-art quality. Its extreme decorative nature limits it almost entirely to headlines, posters, or branding moments where spectacle is the sole objective. Legibility at small sizes is negligible, and its single weight restricts flexibility considerably.

name: Khand
classification_notes: Khand is a clean, low-contrast humanist sans designed to serve both Devanagari and Latin scripts with consistent rhythm and optical balance. Its upright, compact letterforms make it efficient at text and display sizes alike.
summary: A well-balanced multi-script humanist sans that handles Devanagari and Latin with equal composure.
description: Khand was developed with careful attention to cross-script harmony, ensuring that its Devanagari and Latin characters share consistent proportions and spacing. Its range of five weights gives it genuine flexibility for editorial layouts, app interfaces, and brand identity systems. The compact, upright design keeps text economical without sacrificing readability.

name: Khmer
classification_notes: Khmer is a functional sans-serif typeface designed specifically to support the Khmer script, providing readable letterforms for a script with complex above- and below-baseline stacking. It is a utility face rather than a design statement.
summary: A plain, workhorse Khmer-script sans built for legible everyday text rendering.
description: Khmer addresses the fundamental need for clean, screen-ready coverage of the Khmer script, which requires careful handling of its stacked diacritics and complex glyph relationships. As a single-weight face its applications are relatively narrow, suiting body text in web and app contexts where simplicity and legibility are paramount. It is best treated as a utility font rather than a typeface with strong aesthetic intent.

name: Khula
classification_notes: Khula is a large-family humanist sans with a generous x-height and open apertures, designed for comfortable text reading across both Devanagari and Latin. Its range from Light to ExtraBold gives it genuine typographic range.
summary: A readable, multi-weight humanist sans spanning Devanagari and Latin with reliable warmth.
description: Khula was built to serve long-form reading in bilingual contexts, with letterforms that remain open and friendly across its full weight range. The generous x-height and carefully tuned spacing make it particularly effective for body text on screen or in print. Its five weights offer enough contrast for typographic hierarchy within a single-family system.

name: Kings
classification_notes: Kings is a formal, high-flourish display script with sweeping swashes and tall, elegant letterforms reminiscent of 19th-century engrossing scripts. It is designed for large-size display use only.
summary: A grand, flourish-heavy display script with the theatrical poise of Victorian engraving.
description: Kings revels in its extravagant swashes and high ascenders, channelling the tradition of copperplate engrossing into a digital typeface made for bold typographic moments. It suits luxury branding, wedding stationery, editorial headers, and fashion contexts where opulence and formality are assets. Its Vietnamese support adds practical value beyond the expected Latin range.

name: Kirang Haerang
classification_notes: Kirang Haerang is a playful, hand-drawn Korean display font with irregular, expressive strokes that give it a casual, youthful energy. Its Latin companions share the same informal spirit.
summary: A bubbly, hand-drawn Korean display font brimming with youthful, informal energy.
description: Kirang Haerang embraces deliberate irregularity, with letterforms that feel handcrafted and spontaneous rather than mechanically precise. It is best suited to youth-oriented branding, social media graphics, posters, and headlines where a relaxed, approachable personality is the goal. Its single weight limits versatility, but within its niche it delivers a distinctive and cheerful presence.

name: Kite One
classification_notes: Kite One is a quiet, legible humanist sans with slightly rounded terminals and an even, comfortable stroke weight suited to text and UI contexts. Its design is understated and functional rather than expressive.
summary: A gentle, unassuming humanist sans with rounded touches that keeps body text comfortable and unpretentious.
description: Kite One sits in the tradition of friendly, approachable humanist sans-serifs, with slightly softened terminals that take the edge off without veering into overt roundedness. It performs best in interfaces, web body text, and lightweight editorial settings where legibility and neutrality are more important than personality. Its single weight is a practical limitation, but within that constraint it is a reliable and clean choice.

name: Kiwi Maru
classification_notes: Kiwi Maru features soft, rounded serifs designed to harmonise Latin and Japanese scripts, giving it a gentle, approachable character. The strokes have a consistent, friendly warmth suited to informal or child-friendly contexts.
summary: A soft, rounded serif that bridges Japanese and Latin scripts with charm and consistency.
description: Kiwi Maru's rounded terminals and gentle stroke contrast make it immediately warm and approachable, standing out among CJK-compatible typefaces. It works well for children's publishing, lifestyle branding, and any context where a serif needs to feel welcoming rather than authoritative. Its Cyrillic and Japanese coverage makes it a practical choice for multilingual projects.

name: Klee One
classification_notes: Klee One is modelled on Japanese educational handwriting, with letterforms that blend script informality with print legibility across Latin, Cyrillic, and Japanese. The strokes carry subtle calligraphic movement while remaining highly readable at text sizes.
summary: A handwriting-inspired typeface drawn from Japanese educational script traditions, readable yet personal.
description: Klee One occupies the space between formal text type and casual handwriting, making it suitable for educational materials, personal blogs, and informal editorial work. Its Japanese heritage gives it a distinctive rhythm that carries through into its Latin and Cyrillic characters. The two weights offer enough range for simple typographic hierarchies without sacrificing its handmade warmth.

name: Knewave
classification_notes: Knewave mimics the look of a thick marker or brush applied quickly, with irregular edges and strong gestural energy. It is a single-weight novelty display face with limited range beyond headline or poster use.
summary: A chunky, rough-edged brush marker face built purely for bold, expressive display work.
description: Knewave replicates the spontaneous energy of a thick felt-tip or brush marker, with deliberately uneven outlines that give it a hand-done quality. It suits surf, skate, or street-culture branding, as well as informal posters and signage that need instant impact. With only one weight and limited character set, its application is narrow but confident within that scope.

name: KoHo
classification_notes: KoHo is a clean humanist sans-serif designed with strong Thai and Latin harmony, featuring open apertures and modulated stroke contrast. Its range of six weights makes it versatile for both text and display applications.
summary: A well-balanced humanist sans-serif with excellent Thai and Latin integration across a generous weight range.
description: KoHo was developed with careful attention to the structural demands of Thai script, resulting in letterforms that feel cohesive and considered across both writing systems. Its open counters and humanist proportions make it a comfortable reading face for body copy and UI work alike. The six-weight range from ExtraLight to Bold gives designers genuine flexibility for typographic hierarchy.

name: Kodchasan
classification_notes: Kodchasan has a more geometric and upright construction compared to its Thai-Latin peers, with consistent stroke weights and clean terminals. It supports Thai and Latin with a rational, modern aesthetic.
summary: A clean, geometrically influenced sans-serif offering solid Thai and Latin coverage for modern digital use.
description: Kodchasan applies a rational, near-monolinear approach to Thai and Latin letterforms, making it a reliable workhorse for interfaces and web content requiring bilingual support. Its evenness across weights gives it a neutral, professional tone without feeling cold. It handles body text comfortably and scales well into headline sizes without losing clarity.

name: Kode Mono
classification_notes: Kode Mono is a technically precise monospaced typeface with geometric construction and distinctive angular details that give it personality beyond the utilitarian norm. It covers a useful weight range from Regular to Bold.
summary: A characterful geometric monospace with enough personality to stand out in code editors and technical interfaces.
description: Kode Mono brings a considered geometric sensibility to the monospaced category, with subtle angular details that distinguish it from purely functional coding fonts. Its four weights make it adaptable for both source code display and technical UI elements such as labels and data tables. It is a strong option for developer tools, documentation sites, and any project that wants its monospace type to feel intentional.

name: Koh Santepheap
classification_notes: Koh Santepheap is a serif typeface designed primarily to support the Khmer script, with a harmonised Latin companion set sharing its stroke contrast and general proportions. Its wide weight range from Thin to Black gives it considerable versatility.
summary: A Khmer-first serif with a wide weight range that serves both text and display needs in multilingual settings.
description: Koh Santepheap addresses the relative scarcity of quality Khmer typefaces, offering a five-weight family that works from light editorial text through to heavy display headlines. The Latin characters are designed to sit naturally alongside the Khmer, avoiding the mismatched feeling common in many multilingual fonts. It is a valuable resource for publishers, NGOs, and digital platforms serving Khmer-speaking audiences.

name: Kolker Brush
classification_notes: Kolker Brush is a flowing, expressive brush script with thick-to-thin contrast and organic ink-like qualities, designed for large-scale decorative use. It is a single-weight display face with strong calligraphic character.
summary: A bold, fluid brush script with rich ink texture, built for expressive display and branding applications.
description: Kolker Brush channels the energy of a fully loaded brush swept across paper, with natural variation in stroke weight and slightly irregular edges that suggest authentic handcraft. It suits luxury branding, wedding stationery, editorial mastheads, and poster headlines where a sense of warmth and artisanal quality is needed. The single weight limits its typographic range, but within its intended display role it delivers genuine character.

name: Konkhmer Sleokchher
classification_notes: A decorative display typeface designed specifically for the Khmer script, featuring ornate, stylised letterforms with expressive flourishes. The Latin companion maintains a similarly decorative character.
summary: An ornate Khmer display typeface with bold decorative energy suited to headlines and cultural projects.
description: Konkhmer Sleokchher brings a strong decorative sensibility to Khmer typography, an area where expressive display options remain scarce. Its flourished forms make it well suited to posters, cultural events, and editorial headlines where visual impact is the priority. The Latin extension allows for mixed-script layouts without losing tonal consistency.

name: Kosugi
classification_notes: A clean, neutral Japanese gothic sans-serif designed for screen readability across CJK and Latin scripts. The letterforms are simple and utilitarian with minimal stylistic embellishment.
summary: A no-nonsense Japanese gothic sans designed for functional multilingual text rendering across digital interfaces.
description: Kosugi is a practical, workmanlike typeface covering Japanese, Cyrillic, and Latin scripts without aesthetic pretension. Its even stroke weight and open forms prioritise legibility at small sizes on screen, making it a sensible choice for UI, body text, and localised web applications. It lacks personality but compensates with broad utility.

name: Kosugi Maru
classification_notes: The rounded companion to Kosugi, sharing the same Japanese gothic structure but with softened terminals that add warmth and approachability. Stroke endings are gently curved rather than squared off.
summary: A softened, rounded Japanese gothic sans that brings warmth to multilingual digital typography.
description: Kosugi Maru takes the structural foundation of Kosugi and applies rounded terminals throughout, resulting in a noticeably friendlier tone. This makes it particularly well suited to consumer-facing apps, children's content, and informal digital products requiring Japanese and Latin support. The rounded treatment is consistent and deliberate rather than superficial.

name: Kotta One
classification_notes: A single-weight serif with refined proportions and moderate contrast, drawing on classical transitional models. Clean construction and elegant spacing give it a literary quality.
summary: A refined single-weight transitional serif with an understated elegance suited to editorial and literary contexts.
description: Kotta One presents clean, well-proportioned letterforms rooted in the transitional serif tradition, balancing classical structure with contemporary clarity. Its single weight limits versatility but what it offers is well executed, lending itself to magazine layouts, book covers, and long-form editorial text. The extended Latin coverage broadens its practical reach across European languages.

name: Koulen
classification_notes: A bold, upright Khmer display typeface with strong vertical stress and compact, blocky letterforms. The design prioritises visual weight and presence over decorative detail.
summary: A commanding upright Khmer display face built for bold headlines and high-impact typographic applications.
description: Koulen offers a forceful, upright presence that makes it immediately recognisable among Khmer typefaces. Its compact, heavy construction works well for signage, posters, and newspaper headlines where Khmer script needs to hold its own at large sizes. The Latin companion shares the same commanding weight, enabling cohesive bilingual display work.

name: Kranky
classification_notes: An irregular, hand-drawn display typeface with deliberately wobbly, uneven letterforms suggesting a rough, informal energy. Each character has an organic inconsistency that reads as playfully chaotic.
summary: A deliberately rough, wobbly display typeface that embraces imperfection for a chaotic, hand-drawn effect.
description: Kranky leans fully into lo-fi irregularity, with letterforms that appear hand-drawn with gleeful inconsistency. It suits Halloween graphics, quirky branding, and informal posters where polish would undermine the point. The single weight and narrow stylistic range limit its usefulness, firmly placing it in novelty territory.

name: Kreon
classification_notes: A humanist slab serif with low contrast, sturdy slab serifs, and open, legible letterforms designed to perform across both print and screen. The design blends slab structure with the warmth of humanist sans proportions.
