summary: A sturdy humanist slab serif with warm proportions and excellent text performance across print and screen.
description: Kreon occupies a distinctive niche by combining the visual solidity of slab serifs with the open, readable forms of humanist design, resulting in a typeface that feels approachable without sacrificing authority. Its five-weight range makes it genuinely versatile, covering body text, subheadings, and headlines within a single family. It works particularly well in editorial, educational, and long-form digital content where sustained readability is essential.

name: Kristi
classification_notes: A light, flowing cursive script with informal, handwritten character and thin, delicate strokes. The letterforms are connected with a natural, relaxed rhythm rather than formal calligraphic precision.
summary: A delicate, free-flowing cursive script with an effortlessly casual handwritten quality.
description: Kristi mimics the natural flow of informal cursive handwriting, with thin, lively strokes and a loose, unpretentious rhythm. It works well for greeting cards, wedding stationery, artisan branding, and any context calling for a personal, hand-touched feel. The very light weight limits its legibility at small sizes, so it is best reserved for decorative headlines and short display uses.

name: Krona One
classification_notes: Krona One features wide, near-monoline letterforms with strong geometric underpinning and a distinctly expanded horizontal proportion. Its single weight is bold and imposing, designed primarily for headline use.
summary: A wide, heavy geometric sans built for maximum impact at large sizes.
description: Krona One's expanded proportions and uniform stroke weight make it immediately commanding in headline and poster contexts. Its single Regular weight belies its visual strength — it reads as bold by virtue of its sheer width and solidity. Best deployed sparingly for titles, logotypes, and short-form display copy.

name: Krub
classification_notes: Krub is a clean, low-contrast humanist sans designed with careful attention to Thai script compatibility alongside Latin, resulting in balanced letterforms across both writing systems. Its range of weights makes it genuinely versatile.
summary: A practical, multilingual humanist sans that handles Thai and Latin with equal composure.
description: Krub was designed with Thai-Latin bilingual publishing in mind, ensuring consistent visual rhythm when the two scripts are mixed in running text. Its proportions are conventional enough to work as a UI typeface or editorial body font without drawing undue attention to itself. The full weight range makes it a dependable workhorse across digital and print environments.

name: Kufam
classification_notes: Kufam pairs a geometric Arabic design with a complementary Latin, both sharing similar optical weight and stroke contrast to achieve visual harmony in bilingual settings. The design is clean and contemporary with a neutral character.
summary: A geometric sans designed for seamless Arabic-Latin bilingual use across digital interfaces.
description: Kufam takes a rigorous approach to harmonising Arabic and Latin letterforms, making it a practical choice for publications, apps, and websites serving Arabic-speaking audiences. Its clean geometric structure keeps it feeling modern and unobtrusive at text sizes. The six-weight range provides sufficient flexibility for typographic hierarchy in both scripts.

name: Kulim Park
classification_notes: Kulim Park has a distinctive calligraphic influence visible in its letterform construction, with subtle inktraps and pen-derived angles giving it warmth unusual in sans-serifs. It skews towards editorial and refined branding applications.
summary: A refined humanist sans with calligraphic undertones that set it apart from generic grotesques.
description: Kulim Park draws on calligraphic tradition to introduce subtle stroke modulation and diagonal stress into what is otherwise a clean sans-serif structure. This gives it a quiet elegance well-suited to editorial design, cultural institutions, and premium branding. Its limited weight range keeps it focused rather than broadly utilitarian.

name: Kumar One
classification_notes: Kumar One is a bold display typeface rooted in Gujarati script tradition, with full-bodied letterforms that carry significant visual weight at display sizes. The Latin companion follows the same expressive, heavy character.
summary: A heavy Gujarati display face that brings South Asian typographic tradition into contemporary headline use.
description: Kumar One celebrates the visual richness of Gujarati script at large sizes, where its bold strokes and expressive curves make the strongest impression. The accompanying Latin glyphs share its confident weight, making it workable for bilingual headlines and posters. Its single weight limits it to display contexts, but within that remit it is distinctive and culturally grounded.

name: Kumar One Outline
classification_notes: Kumar One Outline is the inline/outline counterpart to Kumar One, retaining all its bold display proportions while replacing solid strokes with an open, outlined construction for decorative effect. It is inherently more specialised and limited in application.
summary: The outlined companion to Kumar One, adding decorative flair to the same bold Gujarati-Latin display structure.
description: Kumar One Outline strips the fill from its sibling's letterforms, producing an airy, graphic quality suited to poster work, packaging, and decorative headlines where a solid version might feel too heavy. It works most effectively at very large sizes where the outline detail remains legible and impactful. As a one-trick novelty variant, its range of application is deliberately narrow.

name: Kumbh Sans
classification_notes: Kumbh Sans is a rigorously geometric sans-serif with near-perfectly circular rounds and a neutral, Swiss-influenced character that makes it highly adaptable across interface and editorial contexts. Its nine-weight variable range is one of its strongest attributes.
summary: A neutral geometric sans with a broad weight range that covers everything from UI body copy to bold headlines.
description: Kumbh Sans draws on classical geometric sans conventions — circular bowls, consistent stroke width — while remaining legible enough at small sizes to serve as a UI or text typeface. Its nine weights, available as a variable font, give designers substantial typographic range within a single family. It is a dependable all-rounder without strong personality, which is precisely its appeal.

name: Kurale
classification_notes: Kurale is a multi-script serif designed to serve Latin, Cyrillic, and Devanagari simultaneously, with moderate contrast and conventional proportions that prioritise harmony across scripts over individual stylistic flourish. It reads comfortably at body text sizes.
summary: A quietly competent multi-script serif uniting Latin, Cyrillic, and Devanagari in a single cohesive family.
description: Kurale's primary achievement is the careful harmonisation of three distinct script traditions within a consistent typographic voice, making it well-suited to scholarly publications, multilingual books, and cultural projects. Its moderate stroke contrast and conventional serif structure keep it from feeling fashionable but ensure reliable readability. The single weight is a limitation, but within that constraint it performs honestly as a text typeface.

name: LINE Seed JP
classification_notes: LINE Seed JP is a clean, friendly sans-serif designed for digital interfaces, with harmonious coverage across Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, and Japanese scripts. Its consistent stroke weight and open apertures make it well-suited to screen reading at small sizes.
summary: A corporate digital sans built by LINE for seamless cross-script harmony across Japanese, Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek.
description: Designed by LINE Corporation for its own product ecosystem, LINE Seed JP prioritises legibility and visual consistency across a broad range of writing systems. Its friendly, open letterforms work well in mobile interfaces, messaging apps, and digital editorial contexts. The four-weight range gives designers enough flexibility for both body and headline use within a single type system.

name: LXGW Marker Gothic
classification_notes: LXGW Marker Gothic mimics the informal, pen-drawn quality of Japanese marker lettering, blending hand-drawn character with a sans-serif skeleton across CJK and Cyrillic scripts. Its single-weight offering suits casual, expressive applications rather than sustained reading.
summary: A marker-drawn Gothic that brings casual, hand-lettered energy to Chinese Traditional and Cyrillic scripts.
description: LXGW Marker Gothic draws on the aesthetic of Japanese marker-style Gothic typefaces, giving each glyph a slightly informal, hand-crafted texture. Its single Regular weight limits its versatility, making it best suited to short headlines, signage, or decorative display contexts. The broad script coverage, including Chinese Traditional and Cyrillic, is a genuine strength for multilingual creative projects.

name: LXGW WenKai Mono TC
classification_notes: LXGW WenKai Mono TC is a monospaced variant of the WenKai family, combining calligraphic-influenced CJK strokes with fixed-width Latin and Cyrillic glyphs. The result is an unusually warm, readable monospace that suits both coding and literary display.
summary: A calligraphically inspired monospace covering Chinese Traditional and Cyrillic with warmth rarely found in fixed-width type.
description: LXGW WenKai Mono TC inherits the brushy, humanist character of the WenKai family while conforming to fixed-width metrics suitable for code editors and terminal environments. Its three weights add modest flexibility, and the calligraphic detail in CJK glyphs distinguishes it sharply from utilitarian coding fonts. It works particularly well for developers working in Chinese-language environments who want personality alongside function.

name: LXGW WenKai TC
classification_notes: LXGW WenKai TC is inspired by traditional Chinese calligraphic brushwork, producing CJK glyphs with expressive, hand-written stroke variation alongside Latin and Cyrillic companions. The three-weight range and fluid stroke quality make it distinctive within the Chinese Traditional script space.
summary: A brush-calligraphy-inspired typeface bringing traditional Chinese handwriting warmth to digital Chinese Traditional text.
description: LXGW WenKai TC is modelled on the Kaiti style of Chinese calligraphy, characterised by flowing, brush-like strokes and a distinctly handwritten rhythm. It covers Chinese Traditional, Cyrillic, and Cyrillic Extended scripts, making it a rare option for multilingual contexts that need expressive East Asian letterforms. Best used for editorial settings, literary publications, and cultural projects where warmth and heritage are priorities.

name: La Belle Aurore
classification_notes: La Belle Aurore is a loose, handwritten script with an irregular, ink-pen quality that evokes personal correspondence and vintage love letters. Its single weight and exclusively Latin coverage focus its appeal squarely on decorative, romantic display use.
summary: A romantic, ink-pen handwriting script that evokes vintage French correspondence and personal letters.
description: La Belle Aurore captures the casual elegance of handwritten pen lettering, with natural variation in stroke weight and a pleasingly imperfect rhythm. Its name and character make it a natural fit for wedding stationery, perfume branding, café signage, and any project seeking a personal, nostalgic touch. At a single weight and Latin-only coverage, it is a purely decorative tool rather than a workhorse.

name: Labrada
classification_notes: Labrada is a refined transitional serif with moderate contrast and elegant proportions, offering an exceptionally wide weight range from Thin to Black. Its extended Latin and Vietnamese coverage, combined with nine weights, gives it strong versatility for editorial and branding work.
summary: A polished transitional serif with a sweeping nine-weight range that handles both refined body text and commanding display headlines.
description: Labrada's classical proportions and moderate stroke contrast place it firmly in the transitional serif tradition, drawing on the legacy of eighteenth-century type design. The nine-weight range — from Thin through to Black — makes it an unusually flexible choice for complete typographic systems, covering everything from delicate captions to bold poster headlines. Vietnamese and extended Latin support broaden its usefulness for international publishing and multilingual branding.

name: Lacquer
classification_notes: Lacquer is a bold, textured display typeface with a distinctly rough, industrial character, as though letters have been applied with a brush or roller and left with uneven ink coverage. Its single weight and Latin-only coverage make it a one-purpose display tool.
summary: A gritty, ink-textured display face with an industrial edge well suited to bold poster and packaging work.
description: Lacquer mimics the uneven, tactile quality of screen-printed or brush-applied lettering, giving it an authentically rough aesthetic that suits vintage-inspired branding, album artwork, and packaging. Its single bold weight means it works only at large sizes where the texture reads clearly, and it has no practical role in text or UI contexts. The name itself signals its character: slick and surface-level, but purposeful and confident at the right scale.

name: Laila
classification_notes: Laila is a humanist serif designed specifically for the Devanagari script alongside Latin companions, with careful attention to balancing the distinctive horizontal headline strokes of Devanagari with the weight and proportion of its Latin counterpart. Its five weights make it a credible text and editorial choice for multilingual Indian-language publishing.
summary: A thoughtfully crafted humanist serif that brings genuine typographic quality to Devanagari and Latin bilingual publishing.
description: Laila was designed with Devanagari as the primary script, giving it a solidity and authenticity in Hindi, Marathi, and Sanskrit contexts that many Latin-first fonts lack when extended to Indic scripts. Its five weights from Light to Bold provide a practical range for editorial, web, and book typography across bilingual documents. The Latin companion is well-matched in weight and spirit, making Laila a reliable workhorse for publishers and designers working across Indian and European languages.

name: Lakki Reddy
classification_notes: A casual, informal script designed for the Telugu script alongside Latin, with a flowing, hand-drawn quality. The letterforms have an unpretentious warmth suited to everyday vernacular use.
summary: A warm, informal script typeface bridging Telugu and Latin with a hand-drawn sensibility.
description: Lakki Reddy brings a relaxed, handwritten energy to multilingual settings where both Telugu and Latin scripts are required. Its casual forms make it well-suited to informal branding, greeting cards, and vernacular signage. The lack of multiple weights limits its versatility, but its charm is immediate and genuine.

name: Lalezar
classification_notes: A bold, high-impact display typeface supporting Arabic and Latin with strong, even stroke weights and confident letterforms. Designed for headlines and large-scale applications where presence is paramount.
summary: A powerfully bold display sans that commands attention across Arabic and Latin scripts at headline sizes.
description: Lalezar is built for impact, with dense, confident strokes that read clearly at large sizes in both Arabic and Latin. Its single-weight offering focuses its purpose squarely on headlines, posters, and display branding. The Arabic letterforms are particularly strong, making it a rare and valuable option for bilingual Middle Eastern and North African projects.

name: Lancelot
classification_notes: An ornate, calligraphic display typeface drawing on uncial and Insular manuscript traditions, with decorative swash terminals and high contrast strokes. It carries a distinctly medieval, literary character.
summary: An ornate, manuscript-inspired display typeface evoking the aesthetic of medieval Insular lettering.
description: Lancelot draws heavily from uncial and early medieval calligraphic traditions, making it a natural fit for fantasy branding, historical editorial projects, and book titling. Its decorative terminals and high-contrast strokes give it a refined ceremonial quality that distinguishes it from blunter blackletter alternatives. Best used sparingly at large sizes, where its intricate details can be fully appreciated.

name: Langar
classification_notes: A decorative display typeface supporting the Gurmukhi script alongside Latin, with rounded, expressive forms that carry a playful cultural character. Designed for large-scale display use in South Asian contexts.
summary: A cheerful, culturally rooted display typeface uniting Gurmukhi and Latin scripts with rounded, expressive forms.
description: Langar serves a niche but important role as a display typeface designed specifically to honour Gurmukhi script in a decorative register alongside Latin. Its rounded, open letterforms give it a friendly, inviting tone suited to food packaging, cultural events, and community branding. The single weight restricts its utility, but within display contexts it performs its cultural and visual role admirably.

name: Lateef
classification_notes: A high-quality Arabic typeface in the Nastaliq calligraphic tradition, featuring the characteristic diagonal baseline and flowing, cursive letterforms of classical Persian and Urdu typography. Its multiple weights make it unusually versatile for a Nastaliq design.
summary: A refined Nastaliq typeface offering rare multi-weight versatility for Arabic, Urdu, and Persian typography.
description: Lateef is one of the most fully developed Nastaliq typefaces available on Google Fonts, covering a wide weight range from ExtraLight to ExtraBold — an exceptional achievement in a notoriously complex script tradition. Its flowing, diagonal letterforms are rooted in classical Persian calligraphy, making it ideal for literary publishing, Urdu editorial work, and formal Arabic documents. The range of weights opens up typographic hierarchies that are rarely possible with Nastaliq typefaces.

name: Lato
classification_notes: A humanist sans-serif with subtle classical proportions, slight optical corrections borrowed from calligraphic tradition, and a warm yet clean character. Designed to balance the neutrality of a grotesque with the readability of a humanist.
summary: One of the most widely used humanist sans-serifs on the web, offering warmth and neutrality in equal measure.
description: Lato was designed with a dual character: the overall impression is geometric and clean, yet its letterforms carry subtle calligraphic traces that give it a human warmth few neutral sans-serifs achieve. Its extensive weight range makes it highly practical for complex typographic systems spanning body copy, UI, and headlines. Immensely popular in web design, it remains one of the most reliably readable and versatile typefaces available for screen.

name: Lavishly Yours
classification_notes: A highly expressive, flowing formal script with dramatic swash capitals and generous loops, designed for large-scale romantic and decorative applications. The letterforms are lush and extravagant, prioritising visual impact over readability at small sizes.
summary: A lavish, swash-heavy formal script built for romantic branding and decorative headlines.
description: Lavishly Yours lives up to its name with extravagant looping capitals and flowing cursive forms that feel unapologetically indulgent. It is well-suited to wedding stationery, perfume branding, lifestyle editorial, and any context demanding a sense of luxury and femininity. At small sizes legibility suffers considerably, but at poster and headline scale it delivers undeniable visual drama.

name: League Gothic
classification_notes: An extremely condensed, all-caps grotesque with tall, narrow proportions and uniform stroke weight, reviving the aesthetic of early twentieth-century American condensed wood type. The design is bold and architectural in its verticality.
summary: A strikingly tall condensed grotesque channelling the raw energy of vintage American wood-type printing.
description: League Gothic is one of the defining revival condensed grotesques in open-source typography, modelled on the classic Alternate Gothic typefaces of the early twentieth century. Its extreme vertical proportions make it a natural choice for headlines, posters, and editorial titles where maximum impact is needed in a narrow horizontal space. The typeface has been widely adopted in editorial design, music branding, and film titles, giving it a recognisable cultural presence.

name: League Script
classification_notes: A formal cursive script with thin, flowing strokes reminiscent of copperplate penmanship. Designed for decorative headline use with delicate letterforms and pronounced contrast.
summary: An elegant formal script with copperplate influence, suited to decorative headlines and ceremonial branding.
description: League Script draws on the tradition of copperplate penmanship, offering fine hairlines and flowing connecting strokes. It works best at large sizes where its delicate contrast can be appreciated. Suitable for wedding stationery, luxury branding, and editorial flourishes.

name: League Spartan
classification_notes: A bold geometric sans-serif with strong uppercase presence, closely related to Futura in its circular constructions and even stroke weight. The full weight range makes it versatile across display and text applications.
summary: A confident geometric sans with Futura-like rigour, delivering strong impact across headline and branding work.
description: League Spartan is built on circular geometric forms with near-uniform stroke width, giving it a clean, authoritative tone. Its wide weight range — from Thin to Black — makes it adaptable for everything from editorial headlines to logotype work. The extended Latin and Vietnamese support broadens its utility for international projects.

name: Leckerli One
classification_notes: A casual, rounded brush script with a friendly, hand-lettered quality. Letterforms are thick, bouncy, and informal, with natural ink-like variation suggesting a broad-nib brush.
summary: A warm, rounded brush script with a cheerful hand-lettered character well suited to informal branding.
description: Leckerli One captures the exuberance of casual brush lettering with its plump, connected forms and lively rhythm. It evokes mid-century display lettering with a contemporary friendliness. Best used for food packaging, children's branding, and informal poster headlines.

name: Ledger
classification_notes: A text-optimised serif with moderate contrast and sturdy construction designed for comfortable reading at body sizes. Cyrillic support extends its usefulness for multilingual publishing.
summary: A reliable text serif with solid construction and Cyrillic support, designed for legible long-form reading.
description: Ledger offers balanced proportions and moderate stroke contrast suited to body copy in print and digital editorial contexts. Its Cyrillic character set makes it a practical choice for Russian and Eastern European publishing projects. The single Regular weight limits its range but it performs dependably within its scope.

name: Lekton
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with geometric, almost modular letterforms inspired by Italian rationalist design. Stroke construction is angular and systematic, giving it a technical, architectural quality.
summary: A rationalist monospace with angular geometric forms, well suited to coding environments and technical documents.
description: Lekton is derived from type used at ECAL and reflects a structured, modular approach to letterform construction. Its consistent spacing and geometric clarity make it comfortable in code editors and terminal interfaces. The Regular and Bold weights cover most practical requirements for technical and programming use.

name: Lemon
classification_notes: A heavily stylised display face with chunky slab-like forms and pronounced decorative detail. Letterforms are bold and rigid, clearly intended for single-use headline impact rather than extended text.
summary: A thick, ornamental display face built for maximum visual impact at large poster and headline sizes.
description: Lemon is unashamedly one-trick, offering a single bold weight with decorative embellishments that demand large-scale use. It carries a retro quality reminiscent of Victorian-era display type and novelty wood type. Best reserved for poster headlines, packaging accents, and playful branding where a strong graphic statement is needed.

name: Lemonada
classification_notes: A rounded, friendly display typeface with Arabic and Latin support, designed with soft curves and a warm, open character. The variable weight range adds flexibility across display and short-text contexts.
summary: A warm rounded display face with genuine Arabic and Latin bilingual design, offering playful softness across a useful weight range.
description: Lemonada is notable for its careful bilingual construction, with Arabic and Latin letterforms sharing the same approachable, rounded character. Its soft terminals and generous curves lend themselves to friendly branding, packaging, and short editorial copy. The range from Light to Bold makes it adaptable without losing its distinctive warmth.

name: Lexend
classification_notes: Designed specifically to reduce visual noise and improve reading fluency, with carefully calibrated letter spacing and humanist proportions. Research-backed design choices distinguish it from standard UI sans-serifs.
summary: A research-driven humanist sans engineered to improve reading speed and reduce cognitive load on screen.
description: Lexend was developed by Thomas Jockin in collaboration with readability researchers, with letter spacing and form tuned to assist readers with dyslexia and reduce overall visual stress. Its open, humanist letterforms perform reliably across UI, body copy, and editorial contexts at all sizes. The full weight range from Thin to Black and broad Latin support make it a genuinely versatile workhorse for digital products.

name: Lexend Deca
classification_notes: Lexend Deca is the standard-width member of the Lexend family, designed with carefully adjusted letter spacing and open apertures to reduce visual stress and improve reading fluency. Its moderate proportions and clean stroke terminals make it highly legible at text sizes.
summary: A readability-focused humanist sans designed to reduce visual stress and support fluent reading.
description: Lexend Deca is the baseline width in the Lexend superfamily, developed from research into reading proficiency and visual crowding. Its open letterforms, generous spacing, and neutral character make it well suited to body text, educational materials, and accessible UI design. The full nine-weight range extends its usefulness from running text to interface hierarchies.

name: Lexend Exa
classification_notes: Lexend Exa sits one step wider than Deca in the Lexend series, with slightly increased letter width intended to further reduce inter-letter crowding. The additional width maintains the same clean, open construction shared across the family.
summary: A wider variant of the Lexend family, extending letter spacing to further ease reading for those who benefit from more open text.
description: Lexend Exa occupies a middle-wide position in the Lexend width spectrum, making it useful for readers who need slightly more lateral space between characters to process text comfortably. It retains the same neutral, approachable personality as Deca and suits educational platforms, accessibility-conscious web copy, and UI contexts where a touch more breathing room is desirable. Nine weights provide practical flexibility.

name: Lexend Giga
classification_notes: Lexend Giga pushes the width further still, producing notably expanded letterforms while preserving the family's consistent open-aperture humanist design philosophy. At display sizes it takes on an imposing, spacious quality.
summary: A generously wide humanist sans that doubles as a striking display face at larger sizes.
description: Lexend Giga's extended proportions were originally conceived for maximum letter-spacing accessibility, but the width lends the typeface an assertive presence when set large. It works well as a headline face for editorial or branding projects that want a clean, contemporary feel without resorting to condensed styles. Nine weights mean it can shift from bold display use to lighter, more refined applications.

name: Lexend Mega
classification_notes: Lexend Mega continues the progression toward wider letterforms, sitting between Giga and Peta in the family's width scale. The expansive set width amplifies the neutral, unadorned character of the underlying design.
summary: A very wide humanist sans that occupies the upper-middle of the Lexend width range, useful for bold headlines and accessible copy.
description: Lexend Mega's broad letterforms make it one of the more distinctively wide members of the family, giving headlines an expansive, confident feel. While its roots lie in readability research, in practice it serves branding and display contexts that call for a clean, open sans with strong horizontal presence. The full weight range keeps it versatile across different scales.

name: Lexend Peta
classification_notes: Lexend Peta reaches near the widest end of the Lexend spectrum, with letterforms substantially broader than conventional text faces. The design remains restrained and geometric in feel despite the extreme proportions.
summary: One of the widest entries in the Lexend family, pushing into genuinely expansive display territory while retaining humanist clarity.
description: Lexend Peta's extreme width places it firmly in display rather than text usage for most applications, making it a natural choice for posters, large-format headlines, and brand lockups that demand horizontal dominance. Its clean, undecorated construction keeps it from feeling novelty-driven despite the unusual proportions. All nine weights are available, allowing transitions from hair-thin to ultra-bold.

name: Lexend Tera
classification_notes: Lexend Tera occupies the second-widest position in the Lexend series, sharing the extreme proportions of Peta while maintaining the consistent open stroke construction found across the superfamily. Its very wide set makes it impractical for body text but powerful at display scale.
summary: An extreme-width humanist sans positioned near the top of the Lexend width scale, best suited to large-scale display use.
description: Lexend Tera's very broad letterforms are most effective in headlines, posters, and large digital displays where the expansive proportions read as deliberate and commanding rather than impractical. Like all Lexend widths, it benefits from the same carefully considered apertures and spacing rooted in the family's readability research. The nine available weights offer range, though heavier cuts are particularly striking at display sizes.

name: Lexend Zetta
classification_notes: Lexend Zetta is the widest member of the Lexend superfamily, representing the outermost point of the width-based readability system devised by Thomas Jockin. At this extreme, the letters are strikingly extended and best reserved for display applications.
summary: The widest typeface in the Lexend family, an extreme humanist sans built for maximum letter-spacing and commanding display use.
description: Lexend Zetta represents the logical endpoint of the family's width progression, with letterforms so broad they become a strong design statement in their own right. Originally conceived as the maximum-spacing option for readers with the greatest sensitivity to letter crowding, it finds a natural second life as an eye-catching display face for posters, covers, and brand moments. Its clean, neutral construction prevents the extreme width from tipping into novelty.

name: Libertinus Keyboard
classification_notes: Libertinus Keyboard is a single-weight symbol font depicting keyboard keys and input device glyphs, derived from the broader Libertinus open-source type project. It is highly specialised, serving documentation, technical writing, and UI annotation rather than general typographic use.
summary: A symbol font depicting keyboard keys and input glyphs, designed for technical documentation and UI annotation.
description: Libertinus Keyboard provides a set of pictographic glyphs representing keyboard keys, modifier symbols, and related input device icons, making it useful for software manuals, help documentation, and instructional interfaces. It is a single-weight font with no body text capability, functioning purely as a supplementary resource within a larger typographic system. Its open-source origin makes it freely available for both personal and commercial technical projects.

name: Libertinus Math
classification_notes: Designed specifically for mathematical typesetting, Libertinus Math provides comprehensive coverage of mathematical symbols and operators in a style consistent with the Libertinus Serif family. It follows the conventions of TeX-style math fonts with careful attention to spacing and glyph relationships.
summary: A dedicated mathematical font designed to work seamlessly within the Libertinus family for academic and scientific typesetting.
description: Libertinus Math provides the full range of symbols, operators, and special characters required for mathematical and scientific documents. It is built for use with modern TeX engines such as LuaLaTeX and XeLaTeX. Its primary audience is academics and researchers producing technical papers.

name: Libertinus Mono
classification_notes: Libertinus Mono is a monospaced companion to the Libertinus family, maintaining the classical serif character of the broader type system. It provides extended Latin coverage suitable for coding and technical documentation.
summary: A classical serif monospace typeface forming the coding companion to the wider Libertinus family.
description: Libertinus Mono carries the traditional, scholarly feel of its serif siblings into a fixed-width format, making it suitable for code listings within academic documents. Its extended Latin character set supports a broad range of European languages. It is best used for inline code or technical annotations rather than prolonged programming sessions.

name: Libertinus Sans
classification_notes: Libertinus Sans is a clean humanist sans-serif with broad multilingual support including Cyrillic and Greek scripts. It draws on the humanist tradition with open apertures and moderate stroke contrast.
summary: A restrained humanist sans-serif companion to Libertinus Serif, offering solid multilingual support across Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek.
description: Libertinus Sans provides a readable, neutral counterpart to the serif members of the Libertinus family, useful for headings or UI elements within scholarly or technical documents. Its support for Cyrillic and Greek makes it a practical choice for multilingual academic publishing. The design is functional rather than expressive, prioritising legibility across weights.

name: Libertinus Serif
classification_notes: Libertinus Serif is a revival of the well-regarded Linux Libertine typeface, refined for improved rendering and expanded script support. It features moderate stroke contrast, classical proportions, and broad multilingual coverage.
summary: A refined open-source serif revival offering classical proportions, strong multilingual support, and reliable performance in long-form text.
description: Libertinus Serif builds on the Linux Libertine legacy, correcting technical issues and expanding coverage to include Cyrillic and Greek scripts alongside Latin. It is well suited to academic papers, books, and formal editorial work where a trustworthy, traditional serif is required. Multiple weights make it a credible workhorse for continuous reading at body sizes.

name: Libertinus Serif Display
classification_notes: Libertinus Serif Display is a large-size optical variant of Libertinus Serif, with refined proportions and delicate detailing intended for headlines and titles. It shares the classical character of the text cut but is optimised for display rather than text setting.
summary: The display-optimised optical variant of Libertinus Serif, refined for headlines and large-scale typographic use.
description: Libertinus Serif Display carries the classical elegance of the Libertinus family into a dedicated display cut, with tighter spacing and more refined detailing suited to larger point sizes. It supports Cyrillic and Greek alongside Latin, extending its usefulness for multilingual publishing. It works best as a headline face paired with the regular text weight of Libertinus Serif.

name: Libre Barcode 128
classification_notes: Libre Barcode 128 renders characters as Code 128 barcodes, a one-dimensional symbology capable of encoding the full ASCII character set. It is a purely functional novelty face with no typographic application beyond barcode generation.
summary: A functional typeface that encodes typed characters as Code 128 barcodes, useful for labelling and packaging workflows.
description: Libre Barcode 128 converts standard text input into scannable Code 128 barcode strips, making it a practical tool for generating barcodes directly in design software without specialist applications. It is suited to product packaging, inventory labels, and logistics documents. Beyond its utilitarian function, it has no conventional typographic merit.

name: Libre Barcode 128 Text
classification_notes: Libre Barcode 128 Text extends the base barcode font by including a human-readable text interpretation below each barcode strip. This makes it more practical for real-world labelling where both machine and human readability are required.
summary: A Code 128 barcode font that pairs each encoded barcode with a legible text label beneath it.
description: Libre Barcode 128 Text adds a human-readable text line beneath the machine-scannable barcode, making it more immediately useful for product labels and shipping documentation. It is the more practical variant of the Libre Barcode 128 pair for most real-world scenarios. Like its companion, it serves a purely functional role with no broader typographic application.

name: Libre Barcode 39
classification_notes: Libre Barcode 39 renders characters as Code 39 barcodes, an older and simpler one-dimensional symbology that encodes uppercase letters, digits, and a small set of special characters. It is a purely functional novelty typeface.
summary: A typeface that renders text as Code 39 barcodes, covering uppercase alphanumeric characters for basic labelling needs.
description: Libre Barcode 39 uses the Code 39 symbology, which is widely supported by barcode scanners and suitable for straightforward inventory and identification labelling. Its character set is more limited than Code 128, making it best suited to simple alphanumeric data. Like the other Libre Barcode fonts, it is a utility tool rather than a typographic typeface.

name: Libre Barcode 39 Extended
classification_notes: A functional barcode typeface rendering Code 39 symbology with extended character set. Each character maps to a scannable barcode symbol rather than a conventional letterform.
summary: A purely functional barcode font rendering Code 39 extended symbols, useful for generating scannable barcodes directly in type.
description: Libre Barcode 39 Extended encodes the full extended Code 39 character set as scalable barcode glyphs, making it straightforward to produce barcodes without specialist software. It is suited to packaging, inventory labels, and technical documentation. As a novelty utility font, its application is entirely functional rather than aesthetic.

name: Libre Barcode 39 Extended Text
classification_notes: Combines Code 39 extended barcode symbols with human-readable text rendered beneath each barcode glyph. Provides both machine-readable and human-readable output in a single typeface.
summary: A Code 39 extended barcode font that pairs each symbol with a human-readable text label beneath, combining machine and human legibility in one.
description: Libre Barcode 39 Extended Text adds a printed character beneath each barcode glyph, allowing readers and scanners alike to interpret the encoded information. This makes it particularly practical for retail, warehousing, and logistics labelling where human verification is needed alongside scanning. It remains a strictly utilitarian tool with no decorative value.

name: Libre Barcode 39 Text
classification_notes: Renders the standard Code 39 barcode symbology with accompanying human-readable text. Covers the core Code 39 character set without the extended encoding of its sibling fonts.
summary: A standard Code 39 barcode font with human-readable text labels, offering a simple solution for generating scannable barcodes in print and digital contexts.
description: Libre Barcode 39 Text covers the basic Code 39 alphabet — uppercase letters, digits, and a handful of symbols — rendering each as a barcode with a legible character below. It suits straightforward labelling tasks in retail, asset tracking, and documentation. Like all fonts in this barcode family, it serves a technical purpose rather than a typographic one.

name: Libre Barcode EAN13 Text
classification_notes: Implements EAN-13 barcode symbology used widely in retail product identification. Glyphs encode digits into the standard 13-digit European Article Number format with human-readable numerals.
summary: An EAN-13 barcode font that generates retail-standard product barcodes with human-readable numerals, directly from typed digits.
description: Libre Barcode EAN13 Text produces the internationally recognised EAN-13 barcode format used on retail packaging worldwide, with numerals printed below for human verification. It is a practical tool for designers and developers producing product labels, packaging mock-ups, or print-ready artwork. Its usefulness is entirely functional, and it carries no independent typographic character.

name: Libre Baskerville
classification_notes: A libre revival of Baskerville optimised for screen legibility at body sizes, with slightly heavier strokes and more open spacing than traditional metal cuts. Balances classical proportions with contemporary digital readability.
summary: A screen-optimised revival of Baskerville that tempers the original's elegance with improved legibility for digital body text.
description: Libre Baskerville adapts the refined eighteenth-century Baskerville model for contemporary web and screen use, boosting stroke weight slightly and opening up spacing to perform reliably at small sizes. It is an excellent choice for long-form editorial content, academic publishing, and any project demanding a credible, readable serif. The extended Latin character support broadens its utility across European languages.

name: Libre Bodoni
classification_notes: A revival of the Bodoni model featuring high stroke contrast, flat unbracketed serifs, and vertical stress characteristic of neoclassical type. Designed for display and editorial use with optical sizing considerations.
summary: A libre Bodoni revival with the sharp contrast and crisp geometry of the neoclassical model, suited to high-end editorial and fashion contexts.
description: Libre Bodoni captures the dramatic thin-to-thick stroke contrast and razor-sharp serifs that define the Bodoni tradition, making it well suited to magazine headlines, fashion branding, and luxury editorial design. Its vertical axis and geometric rigour give it a formal, authoritative presence on the page. Extended Latin and Vietnamese support make it more broadly accessible than many comparable revival faces.

name: Libre Caslon Display
classification_notes: A revival of the Caslon model cut specifically for large display sizes, with refined stroke contrast and detail intended to read at headlines and beyond. Draws on the warmth and irregularity of the original William Caslon cuts.
summary: A display-sized Caslon revival that restores the warmth and optically calibrated detail of the original cuts at large sizes.
description: Libre Caslon Display is optimised for headlines and poster-scale use, where its finer details, subtle irregularities, and warm old-style character become fully apparent. It draws faithfully on the William Caslon tradition, lending projects a sense of historical authority and craft. It pairs naturally with Libre Caslon Text for a coherent, optically matched typographic system.

name: Libre Caslon Text
classification_notes: A text-optimised revival of Caslon with adjusted proportions and weight distribution for comfortable reading at body sizes. Retains the humanist warmth and slight irregularity of the Caslon tradition while prioritising clarity.
summary: A text-weight Caslon revival calibrated for readable body copy, bringing old-style warmth and trustworthiness to long-form setting.
description: Libre Caslon Text adapts the classic Caslon model for sustained reading at smaller sizes, with spacing and weight adjusted to maintain comfort across paragraphs of body copy. Its humanist letterforms lend editorial and literary projects a sense of tradition without feeling archaic. Used in tandem with Libre Caslon Display, it forms a well-considered optical size pairing for print and screen.

name: Libre Franklin
classification_notes: A revival of the classic Franklin Gothic family, offering a broad weight range from Thin to Black with strong grotesque characteristics. Features moderate contrast and sturdy, reliable letterforms suited to both text and display use.
summary: A versatile grotesque revival of Franklin Gothic with an impressive weight range and strong multilingual support.
description: Libre Franklin brings the authority of the classic American grotesque tradition to the open web, covering everything from fine editorial text to punchy headlines. Its nine weights make it exceptionally adaptable across branding, publishing, and UI contexts. Cyrillic and extended Latin support broaden its appeal for international projects.

name: Licorice
classification_notes: An ultra-thin, highly ornate formal script with dramatic hairline strokes and elaborate swash flourishes. Evokes 18th-century copperplate penmanship at an extreme, almost impractical, weight.
summary: An extremely delicate formal script that channels copperplate elegance at a hairline weight, designed purely for display use.
description: Licorice pushes formal script conventions to their thinnest possible extreme, producing something that reads more as drawn ornament than practical type. It suits luxury branding, wedding stationery, and editorial headlines where maximum delicacy is the point. Readability at small sizes is essentially nil, so it demands careful, restrained application.

name: Life Savers
classification_notes: A chunky, slab-influenced display face with rounded forms and a playful, informal character. The letterforms have a hand-crafted, slightly irregular quality that gives it warmth and approachability.
summary: A chunky, rounded display slab with a warm, hand-crafted quality that sits comfortably in playful or retro-inflected work.
description: Life Savers combines slab serif structure with rounded terminals and a loose, informal energy that keeps it from feeling too rigid. It works well for children's content, food and beverage branding, and casual display headlines where personality is more important than precision. The three available weights provide modest flexibility within its display-only remit.

name: Lilex
classification_notes: A clean, geometric monospaced typeface with a contemporary feel and broad script coverage including Cyrillic and Greek. Its range of weights from Thin to Bold makes it more flexible than most coding fonts.
summary: A clean geometric monospace with strong multilingual coverage and a useful weight range aimed squarely at coding and technical use.
description: Lilex offers the structured regularity expected of a coding font while bringing a slightly warmer geometric sensibility compared to strictly neutral alternatives. Its support for Cyrillic Extended and Greek makes it a practical choice for developers working across multiple scripts. The spread of weights from Thin to Bold is unusually generous for the monospace category.

name: Lilita One
classification_notes: A single-weight bold display sans with very tight spacing, thick strokes, and a compact, punchy presence. Designed for maximum impact at large sizes with minimal typographic subtlety.
summary: A single-weight heavy display sans built for maximum punch at large sizes, with no pretensions beyond bold impact.
description: Lilita One is a no-frills heavy display face that does exactly one thing — hits hard at headline scale. Its uniform weight and tight proportions make it effective for posters, app store graphics, and branding that needs to shout. The complete lack of weight variation limits its versatility, but within its narrow remit it performs reliably.

name: Lily Script One
classification_notes: A rounded, informal brush-style display script with a single regular weight and cheerful, bouncy letterforms. The connections between letters are smooth and casual rather than formally calligraphic.
summary: A bubbly, rounded display script with a casual, friendly energy suited to informal branding and headline use.
description: Lily Script One offers a cheerful informality that distinguishes it from more refined formal scripts, leaning into smooth, rounded shapes and relaxed connections. It suits children's brands, food packaging, and event graphics where approachability and warmth are the primary goals. Being a single-weight display face, it has no role in continuous text settings.

name: Limelight
classification_notes: A restrained Art Deco display face with geometric construction, elegant proportions, and stylised details that evoke the 1920s and 30s. The letterforms are upright, confident, and distinctly period in character.
summary: A clean Art Deco display face that captures 1920s geometric elegance without tipping into pastiche.
description: Limelight draws on the architectural letterforms of the Art Deco period, with carefully proportioned capitals and a distinct period flavour that is decorative without being overwrought. It suits entertainment branding, fashion editorial headlines, and any context calling for glamorous historical reference. A single weight limits its range, but within that constraint it is quietly authoritative.

name: Linden Hill
classification_notes: A revival of Frederic Goudy's Californian typeface, offering refined transitional serif characteristics with elegant proportions and moderate stroke contrast. The design has a bookish, literary quality rooted in early 20th-century American type design.
summary: A dignified transitional serif revival with literary credentials, well suited to long-form text and book-style editorial work.
description: Linden Hill is based on Goudy's Californian, bringing a measured elegance and old-school refinement to screen and print typography. Its moderate contrast and carefully drawn letterforms make it particularly effective for body text in books, journals, and long editorial features. The absence of a companion italic or bold weight is a practical limitation, but its Roman cut is genuinely accomplished.

name: Linefont
classification_notes: Linefont constructs letterforms entirely from repeated line segments or strokes, creating a distinctive grid- or signal-based aesthetic. It reads more as a visual system than a traditional typeface, with weight variation altering line density rather than stroke contrast.
summary: A modular display face that builds letterforms from repeated line elements, producing a distinctive techno-graphic effect across its wide weight range.
description: Linefont's construction principle — using lines rather than conventional strokes — gives it a strong visual identity suited to posters, album artwork, and tech-adjacent branding. Its broad weight range from Thin to Black allows significant tonal variation while maintaining the core concept. It is strictly a display instrument and should not be used for body text.

name: Lisu Bosa
classification_notes: Lisu Bosa is designed to support the Lisu script alongside Latin, with a warm humanist serif approach that ensures both scripts sit harmoniously together. Letterforms show moderate contrast and open apertures suited to extended reading.
summary: A humanist serif built specifically to serve the Lisu script alongside Latin, offering a rare and thoughtfully harmonised multilingual type solution.
description: Lisu Bosa fills a significant gap in multilingual type provision, giving Lisu-language publications access to a high-quality, weight-rich serif family. The humanist warmth of the design makes it comfortable for long-form reading in both scripts. With eight weights from ExtraLight to Black, it is versatile enough for editorial hierarchies as well as body text.

name: Liter
classification_notes: Liter is a straightforward single-weight grotesque sans with Cyrillic support, keeping a clean and neutral appearance without strong stylistic personality. Its simplicity and broad script coverage make it a functional choice for basic screen use.
summary: A plain, no-frills grotesque sans offering solid Cyrillic and Latin coverage in a single regular weight.
description: Liter makes no grand design statements, functioning instead as a clean and unobtrusive text face for interfaces and web copy. Its Cyrillic support gives it practical utility for Russian-language digital projects where neutrality is an asset. The single-weight offering limits its typographic flexibility considerably.

name: Literata
classification_notes: Literata was designed by TypeTogether for Google Play Books with extended on-screen reading in mind, featuring careful ink-trap details and optically adjusted letterforms across its range. It supports Cyrillic and Greek in addition to Latin, with refined contrast suited to small sizes on digital displays.
summary: Literata is a purpose-built digital reading serif from TypeTogether, refined specifically for comfortable long-form reading on screens across multiple scripts.
description: Commissioned by Google for Play Books, Literata brings optical sophistication to e-reading environments, with ink traps and carefully tuned spacing ensuring legibility at small sizes on varied displays. Its support for Cyrillic and Greek makes it a genuinely multilingual workhorse for publishers working across languages. The eight-weight range from ExtraLight to Black gives editorial designers ample room for typographic hierarchy.

name: Liu Jian Mao Cao
classification_notes: Liu Jian Mao Cao emulates the fast, expressive brushwork of Chinese cursive (caoshu) script, with fluid, abbreviated strokes that convey spontaneity and calligraphic energy. The Latin characters are complementary but clearly secondary to the Chinese design intent.
summary: A dynamic brush script rooted in the Chinese cursive caoshu tradition, capturing the speed and spontaneity of ink-on-paper calligraphy.
description: Liu Jian Mao Cao brings authentic Chinese brushwork to screen and print, making it valuable for projects requiring a calligraphic Chinese voice — packaging, editorial headers, cultural events, and artistic branding. The abbreviated, flowing strokes demand careful use at sufficient size to remain legible. It is emphatically a display face rather than a text setting tool.

name: Livvic
classification_notes: Livvic is a warm humanist sans-serif with slightly rounded terminals and open apertures that give it an approachable, contemporary character. It maintains good legibility across weights and shows influence from geometric construction tempered by humanist sensitivity.
summary: Livvic is an approachable humanist sans with just enough warmth and personality to distinguish itself from neutral grotesques, making it a reliable choice for branding and UI.
description: With open apertures, soft terminals, and a range from Thin to Black, Livvic works well across brand identity systems, app interfaces, and editorial contexts where a clean but not cold appearance is needed. Its Vietnamese support extends its practical reach for multilingual digital projects. The family is consistent and well-spaced, holding up reliably at both display and text sizes.

name: Lobster
classification_notes: Lobster is an exuberant connected script with bold, high-contrast strokes and elaborate ligatures, designed to command attention at display sizes. Its retro-American flavour draws on mid-twentieth century advertising lettering traditions.
summary: Lobster is one of the most recognisable display scripts on the web, delivering bold retro-American energy through its high-contrast connected letterforms and extensive ligature system.
description: Released in 2010, Lobster quickly became ubiquitous thanks to its confident personality and the novelty of its automatic ligature substitution, which keeps the script connected cleanly across letter combinations. It suits food and drink branding, vintage-style posters, and social media graphics where a loud, characterful headline is needed. Its very familiarity now makes it a double-edged choice — effective but widely seen.

name: Lobster Two
classification_notes: Lobster Two refines the original Lobster concept into a slightly more restrained, italic-style display script available in regular and bold weights, with the same connected letterforms and retro character. It reads as marginally more elegant than its predecessor while retaining the same visual warmth.
summary: Lobster Two tempers the exuberance of the original Lobster into a slightly more refined two-weight display script that retains the retro charm without quite as much visual noise.
description: Available in regular and bold, Lobster Two offers a touch more versatility than the original for projects needing a softer or a heavier option within the same aesthetic. It suits boutique branding, food packaging, and vintage-inspired editorial headers where a warm, hand-lettered quality is desirable. Like its predecessor, it is best used sparingly and at generous sizes.

name: Londrina Outline
classification_notes: Londrina Outline is the outlined variant of the Londrina family, featuring only the stroke contour of the letterforms with no fill, giving a light, decorative quality. The design retains the hand-drawn, informal character of the broader family.
summary: The hollow outline variant of Londrina, offering a light, hand-drawn decorative effect suited to casual display work.
description: Londrina Outline strips the letterforms back to their contours, producing an airy, informal display face with strong personality. It works well for posters, event branding, and headlines where a lighthearted, handcrafted feel is needed. Best used at large sizes where the outline detail registers clearly.

name: Londrina Shadow
classification_notes: Londrina Shadow presents the family's letterforms with an exaggerated drop shadow rendered as part of the glyph itself, creating a retro three-dimensional effect. The single-weight design is inherently decorative and limited in versatility.
summary: A novelty shadow variant of Londrina with a built-in retro drop shadow that gives the impression of depth at a single weight.
description: Londrina Shadow bakes a bold drop shadow directly into each glyph, evoking vintage signage and mid-century poster lettering. Its use cases are narrow — poster headlines, playful branding, and social graphics where the retro three-dimensional effect is the point. It functions as a one-trick companion to the broader Londrina family rather than a standalone workhorse.

name: Londrina Sketch
classification_notes: Londrina Sketch renders the letterforms with an incomplete, hand-sketched texture, as though the glyphs are roughed out in pencil rather than finished. This gives the face a deliberately unfinished, artistic quality.
summary: A sketch-textured variant of Londrina that mimics rough pencil lettering for an unfinished, artistic display effect.
description: Londrina Sketch applies a broken, sketchy texture to the Londrina letterforms, making each character appear hand-roughed rather than typeset. It suits creative projects, arts-and-crafts branding, and informal posters where a raw, gestural quality is desirable. Like the other decorative Londrina variants, it is a single-weight novelty face rather than a practical text tool.

name: Londrina Solid
classification_notes: Londrina Solid is the primary member of the Londrina family, a bold, hand-drawn sans-serif with informal, slightly irregular letterforms inspired by urban street lettering in São Paulo. The family spans from Thin to Black, giving it more range than its decorative siblings.
summary: A hand-drawn Brazilian street lettering-inspired sans-serif with genuine weight range and a confident, informal personality.
description: Londrina Solid draws on the vernacular lettering culture of São Paulo, producing letterforms that feel energetic and handmade without sacrificing legibility. Its four weights from Thin to Black make it the most versatile member of the Londrina family, suitable for headlines, posters, logos, and casual editorial display. The Black weight in particular has a commanding presence for large-format applications.

name: Long Cang
classification_notes: Long Cang is a Chinese simplified script typeface that mimics informal, rapid brushwork, with loose strokes and a casual handwritten quality. Its design bridges Chinese and Latin scripts with a consistent brushed aesthetic.
summary: A casual brush script covering Chinese Simplified and Latin, evoking quick, informal handwriting with genuine brushwork character.
description: Long Cang captures the spontaneous energy of everyday handwritten Chinese, with strokes that feel dashed off rather than carefully constructed. It extends to Latin characters with matching informality, making it useful for bilingual display contexts. Best suited to casual branding, social media graphics, and any project calling for an authentic handwritten East Asian flavour.

name: Lora
classification_notes: Lora is a well-crafted contemporary serif with moderate contrast and slightly calligraphic roots, designed specifically for comfortable on-screen reading at body sizes. Its letterforms balance classical elegance with digital legibility across Cyrillic and Latin scripts.
summary: A polished contemporary serif designed for screen reading, balancing moderate contrast and calligraphic warmth with excellent multilingual support.
description: Lora was purpose-built for digital body text, with letterforms that remain clear and inviting at small sizes across a wide range of screens. Its slight calligraphic influence gives it more personality than a purely neutral text face, making it equally at home in editorial, blogging, and literary contexts. Cyrillic and Latin coverage, combined with a variable font axis, make it one of the more reliable and complete serif options on Google Fonts.

name: Love Light
classification_notes: Love Light is an elegant, thin monoline script with sweeping ascenders and descenders, designed to convey romance and refinement at display sizes. The single lightweight design limits its versatility but suits luxury and wedding contexts well.
summary: A delicate thin monoline display script with flowing strokes suited to romantic, luxury, and wedding applications.
description: Love Light draws on copperplate and contemporary calligraphic traditions to produce an airy, refined script with generous loops and long flourished strokes. It is best used at large sizes where the fine weight and detail can be fully appreciated. Wedding stationery, boutique branding, and fashion packaging are its natural habitat.

name: Love Ya Like A Sister
classification_notes: Love Ya Like A Sister is a casual, comic-influenced hand-lettering face with chunky, irregular letterforms and a distinctly informal, friendly energy. The single weight and novelty character limit its practical range.
summary: A chunky, comic-influenced hand-lettering face with an exuberant, informal character suited to youthful and playful display use.
description: Love Ya Like A Sister channels the energy of casual hand-lettering and comic book typography, with irregular baseline jitter and rounded, imprecise strokes that feel genuinely personal. It suits children's content, informal branding, social media graphics, and any project where warmth and spontaneity are prioritised over refinement. Its single weight and limited Latin Extended coverage make it a specialist choice rather than a general-purpose display option.

name: Loved by the King
classification_notes: A decorative calligraphic script with an aged, handwritten quality evoking vintage correspondence and royal proclamations. Features dramatic flourishes and ink-trap-like details that suggest a quill-written origin.
summary: A theatrical calligraphic script that channels the grandeur of historical royal correspondence.
description: Loved by the King draws on the tradition of ornate penmanship with sweeping flourishes and an intentionally aged character. Its dramatic letterforms make it best suited to headlines, invitations, and branding projects seeking a sense of heritage or romance. It is not intended for body text and works at large display sizes only.

name: Lovers Quarrel
classification_notes: A delicate, high-contrast copperplate-influenced script with fine hairlines and sweeping connecting strokes. The thin weight and elegant curves give it a refined, feminine quality suited to formal occasions.
summary: An elegant copperplate-influenced script that brings refined femininity to formal display settings.
description: Lovers Quarrel features delicate hairline strokes and generous curves drawn from the copperplate engraving tradition, giving it an unmistakably romantic character. It suits wedding stationery, luxury branding, fashion editorial headlines, and packaging where a graceful, formal tone is required. Its Vietnamese support broadens its utility across multilingual projects.

name: Luckiest Guy
classification_notes: A bold, heavily outlined inline display face with an exaggerated retro American lettering style, featuring strong shadows and a playful, cartoon-like bounce. Each letter is inflated and chunky with a distinct fun-fair energy.
summary: A brash, inflated retro display face that captures the loud energy of vintage American signage and cartoon lettering.
description: Luckiest Guy takes clear inspiration from mid-century American advertising and fairground typography, with its heavily distressed inline shadow giving each letter a three-dimensional pop. It works best at large sizes for posters, game interfaces, children's branding, and any project demanding unapologetic visual impact. Its single weight limits versatility, but within its niche it is immediately effective.

name: Lugrasimo
classification_notes: A casual handwritten script with flowing, connected letterforms that simulate natural pen or brush writing. The stroke weight variation is moderate, giving it an approachable and informal character.
summary: A casual, flowing handwritten script with a warm and approachable personality suited to informal display use.
description: Lugrasimo mimics the natural rhythms of everyday cursive handwriting, with smooth connecting strokes and gentle weight contrast. It is best used in social media graphics, greeting cards, and lifestyle branding where a personal, hand-crafted feel is desired. Its single weight means it functions purely as a display or accent face rather than a text workhorse.

name: Lumanosimo
classification_notes: A smooth, connected handwritten script with slightly more refined stroke variation than a rough brush face, lending it a clean yet personal quality. The letterforms flow consistently with a neat, practiced hand aesthetic.
summary: A clean, flowing handwritten script that balances personal warmth with a tidy, practised appearance.
description: Lumanosimo offers smooth, well-connected cursive letterforms that feel handwritten without appearing rough or informal. This makes it a reasonable choice for boutique branding, product packaging, and social content where a human touch is valued alongside legibility. Like most single-weight scripts of its type, it is best reserved for short display strings rather than extended text.

name: Lunasima
classification_notes: A clean, friendly humanist sans-serif with open apertures and modest stroke contrast, designed with broad multilingual support across Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek. Its neutral character prioritises legibility over personality.
summary: A practical, legible humanist sans-serif built for multilingual screen and text use across Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek scripts.
description: Lunasima's open, humanist forms and generous x-height make it well-suited to interface design, web body text, and any context requiring clear readability across multiple writing systems. Its Cyrillic and Greek coverage makes it a dependable choice for European multilingual publishing and digital products. The two-weight range is modest but sufficient for basic text hierarchy.

name: Lusitana
classification_notes: A Portuguese-inspired serif with moderate stroke contrast and sturdy slab-influenced serifs that blend transitional structure with a robust, legible character. The design references Iberian typographic heritage with an accessible contemporary finish.
summary: A sturdy, readable transitional serif with Iberian character that performs reliably in editorial and body text settings.
description: Lusitana draws on the rich tradition of Portuguese and Iberian typography, combining moderate contrast and confident serifs with a warm, grounded personality. Its two weights make it practical for book design, long-form web editorial, and branding projects seeking a serif with cultural distinctiveness. It reads well at modest text sizes and holds its own in both print and screen contexts.

name: Lustria
classification_notes: A clean, contemporary transitional serif with moderate stroke contrast, sharp bracketed serifs, and a restrained elegance suited to extended reading. Its proportions are classical without being archaic, giving it broad editorial applicability.
summary: A clean, contemporary transitional serif that brings understated elegance to editorial and body text use.
description: Lustria offers well-balanced proportions and moderate contrast that make it at home in magazine layouts, long-form web articles, and corporate editorial design. Its single weight is a limitation for complex typographic hierarchies, but its refined letterforms hold up well in both display and text applications. It is a reliable, if unspectacular, choice for projects needing a polished serif without strong stylistic idiosyncrasies.

name: Luxurious Roman
classification_notes: A high-contrast, decorative Roman serif with exaggerated thick-thin stroke variation and classical proportions. The ornate detailing and sharp hairline serifs give it an opulent, ceremonial quality.
summary: A lavishly high-contrast display serif that channels classical Roman letterforms with an overtly luxurious sensibility.
description: Luxurious Roman leans into its name with dramatic stroke contrast and sharp, refined serifs that evoke gold-foil invitation cards and prestige branding. It is best deployed at large sizes where its hairline details can be fully appreciated. Use it for headlines, logotypes, and packaging where elegance and formality are the primary message.

name: Luxurious Script
classification_notes: A flowing, high-contrast calligraphic script with generous loops and a strong formal copperplate influence. Letterforms connect fluidly and lean with a consistent rightward slant.
summary: A formal copperplate-influenced display script with fluid connections and dramatic stroke contrast.
description: Luxurious Script draws on engraved copperplate traditions, offering sweeping ascenders, looping descenders, and the kind of hairline-to-thick contrast associated with wedding stationery and prestige packaging. It functions exclusively as a display face and is ill-suited to body copy or small sizes. Its character is best exploited in logotypes, social graphics, and ceremonial print work.

name: M PLUS 1
classification_notes: A clean, mechanically constructed Japanese-Latin sans-serif with near-uniform stroke width and open apertures. The design balances CJK legibility requirements with a modern, neutral Latin aesthetic.
summary: A versatile, neutrally styled geometric sans that handles Japanese and Latin scripts with equal poise across a broad weight range.
description: M PLUS 1 is a workhorse multilingual sans designed for digital interfaces and editorial use where Japanese and Latin text must coexist harmoniously. Its nine weights and variable font capability make it highly adaptable to typographic systems. The uniform stroke treatment and generous x-height ensure strong legibility from caption sizes up to large display settings.

name: M PLUS 1 Code
classification_notes: A fixed-width variant of the M PLUS 1 family that extends Japanese and Latin coding conventions into a single unified typeface. Stroke rhythm and character width are calibrated for code editor environments.
summary: A Japanese-Latin monospace designed specifically for coding environments where CJK and Latin characters must share a fixed grid.
description: M PLUS 1 Code solves the longstanding challenge of mixing Japanese and Latin glyphs in a code editor by fitting both within a consistent proportional system. The seven available weights give developers flexibility for different UI contexts, from lightweight editor themes to bold syntax highlighting. It is a practical, no-nonsense choice for any development tool or terminal that requires multilingual monospace support.

name: M PLUS 1p
classification_notes: An earlier iteration of the M PLUS family, offering broad multilingual support including Cyrillic and Greek alongside the standard Latin and Japanese coverage. Stroke construction is consistent and neutral, prioritising legibility over personality.
summary: A widely supported multilingual geometric sans covering Cyrillic and Greek scripts as well as Latin, making it a reliable choice for international projects.
description: M PLUS 1p is one of the earlier members of the M PLUS family and distinguishes itself with its expansive script coverage, including Cyrillic Extended and Greek. Its neutral, even-stroked design suits body text and interface copy across a wide range of digital contexts. The seven-weight range provides enough typographic flexibility for most editorial or product design systems.

name: M PLUS 2
classification_notes: A refined successor to M PLUS 1, with subtle humanist adjustments that soften the geometric rigidity of the original. Stroke terminals and spacing reflect greater optical sensitivity, improving readability in running text.
summary: A humanist-leaning revision of the M PLUS 1 design that trades strict geometry for improved text-setting warmth and readability.
description: M PLUS 2 refines the M PLUS 1 skeleton with slightly more humanist letterform decisions, making it marginally more comfortable for extended reading in both Japanese and Latin. It covers the same broad weight range and is available as a variable font, offering smooth interpolation across its axis. It suits editorial platforms, apps, and any context where a softened, approachable sans is preferred over a strictly mechanical one.

name: M PLUS Code Latin
classification_notes: A Latin-only fixed-width sans from the M PLUS family, designed with coding use cases in mind rather than CJK coverage. The letterforms are crisp, open, and built for on-screen rendering at small sizes.
summary: A Latin-focused coding monospace from the M PLUS family, optimised for on-screen legibility and available across a useful range of weights.
description: M PLUS Code Latin focuses exclusively on Latin and Latin Extended script, making it leaner and more targeted than its Japanese-inclusive sibling. Its open apertures, clearly differentiated glyphs, and consistent spacing make it well-suited to code editors, terminals, and technical documentation. The seven weights and variable font format allow developers to tailor the type weight to their specific interface or syntax-highlighting scheme.

name: M PLUS Rounded 1c
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with softened, rounded terminals applied uniformly to stroke endings, giving it a distinctly friendly and approachable character. The rounded treatment extends consistently across Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek scripts.
summary: A cheerful, rounded sans-serif from the M PLUS family that pairs broad multilingual coverage with a warm, approachable personality.
description: M PLUS Rounded 1c applies uniform rounded terminals to every stroke ending, distinguishing it clearly from the more neutral members of the M PLUS family. This softness makes it a natural fit for children's products, consumer apps, and any brand identity that wants to feel accessible rather than corporate. The seven weights and support for Cyrillic Extended and Greek give it meaningful reach for international projects with a friendly visual tone.

name: M PLUS U
classification_notes: M PLUS U is a clean, low-contrast geometric sans designed for Japanese and Latin scripts, with uniform stroke weights and open apertures that aid screen legibility. It covers a broad range of weights from Thin to Black, making it highly versatile across UI and editorial contexts.
summary: A versatile geometric sans-serif covering Japanese and Latin scripts across an extensive weight range.
description: M PLUS U combines clean geometric construction with broad multilingual support, making it a practical choice for interfaces and digital products requiring both Japanese and Latin text. Its uniform stroke weight and open forms maintain clarity at small sizes on screen. The wide weight range from Thin to Black gives designers considerable flexibility across headline and body settings.

name: Ma Shan Zheng
classification_notes: Ma Shan Zheng emulates the fluid, informal brushwork of Chinese regular script (楷書 kǎishū), with expressive stroke variation and visible calligraphic energy. Available only in Regular weight, it is primarily decorative and suited to display use.
summary: An expressive Chinese brush script that captures the energy of traditional kǎishū calligraphy.
description: Ma Shan Zheng draws directly from Chinese calligraphic tradition, with strokes that retain the spontaneous quality of hand-brushed letterforms. Its single Regular weight limits versatility, but makes it a strong choice for decorative headings, cultural branding, and editorial display work. It pairs naturally with similarly expressive Latin scripts.
