name: Macondo
classification_notes: Macondo is a decorative Latin display face with mild calligraphic influences and slightly informal proportions, drawing on colonial South American typographic traditions. Its single Regular weight and ornamental character restrict it firmly to headline and display applications.
summary: A decorative display serif with calligraphic warmth rooted in Latin American typographic heritage.
description: Macondo brings a distinctive, slightly rustic quality to display settings, with letterforms that feel handcrafted rather than mechanically precise. Its cultural references make it well suited to editorial projects, cultural branding, or any context requiring character and regional identity. The single weight limits its utility as a system font, keeping it firmly in the display category.

name: Macondo Swash Caps
classification_notes: Macondo Swash Caps is a companion to Macondo, adding swash capitals that heighten the calligraphic and decorative quality of the base design. The ornamental capitals make it even more specialised for headline and titling use.
summary: The swash capitals variant of Macondo, amplifying its decorative character for ornamental titling work.
description: Macondo Swash Caps layers flourished uppercase letterforms onto the base Macondo design, producing a typeface well suited to formal invitations, cultural posters, and decorative editorial headings. The swash forms add considerable visual drama but demand careful typesetting to avoid clutter. Like its companion, it is available only in Regular weight.

name: Mada
classification_notes: Mada is a clean, contemporary Arabic and Latin humanist sans-serif with open, legible forms and a generous weight range from ExtraLight to Black. Designed for screen and editorial use, it balances neutrality with a warm, approachable character.
summary: A refined humanist sans covering Arabic and Latin scripts with a broad, screen-ready weight range.
description: Mada was designed with Arabic readability at its core, producing an even typographic colour across both scripts that makes it effective for bilingual publications, interfaces, and editorial layouts. Its humanist construction avoids the coldness of strict geometric designs, lending warmth to body text while remaining sufficiently neutral for UI use. The eight-weight range gives designers strong flexibility across typographic hierarchies.

name: Madimi One
classification_notes: Madimi One is a friendly, rounded single-weight sans-serif with soft terminals and an informal, approachable tone. Its inclusion of mathematical character support broadens its utility beyond standard text applications.
summary: A cheerful rounded sans with soft terminals designed for friendly, approachable display and UI applications.
description: Madimi One's generous curves and rounded stroke endings give it an informal, warm quality that suits children's content, consumer apps, and casual branding. Its single weight limits compositional range but focuses the design's energy into one consistent voice. The addition of maths character support is a practical bonus for educational or scientific contexts.

name: Magra
classification_notes: Magra is a compact, even-weighted grotesque sans with clean, slightly angular forms and minimal stylistic flourish. Available in Regular and Bold only, it is a utilitarian face suited to simple UI and text applications.
summary: A compact, no-nonsense grotesque sans offering clean legibility in just two practical weights.
description: Magra takes a straightforward approach to grotesque sans design, delivering consistent, even letterforms without decorative distractions. The limited two-weight palette suits straightforward web and UI projects where typographic hierarchy can be achieved through size and spacing rather than weight variation. It performs reliably as a workhorse text face without distinguishing itself strongly in any particular direction.

name: Maiden Orange
classification_notes: Maiden Orange is a warm, single-weight transitional serif with moderate contrast and slightly informal proportions that give it a handcrafted quality. It is suited to editorial display and branding contexts rather than extended body text.
summary: A warm, characterful transitional serif with handcrafted undertones suited to editorial and display use.
description: Maiden Orange sits between formal classical serif and more expressive display design, offering enough typographic personality to work effectively in branding and headline settings. Its moderate stroke contrast and slightly uneven rhythm give it an approachable, artisanal quality that distinguishes it from colder, more mechanical serifs. The single Regular weight limits its utility in complex typographic systems but focuses its appeal for logo and headline use.

name: Maitree
classification_notes: Maitree is a serif typeface designed for both Latin and Thai scripts, featuring moderate contrast and open apertures that aid readability at smaller sizes. Its proportions are carefully balanced to harmonise across two very different writing systems.
summary: A dual-script serif designed to work gracefully across Latin and Thai with consistent optical weight.
description: Maitree offers a warm, readable texture well suited to long-form editorial and publishing work in both Thai and Latin contexts. Its range of six weights from ExtraLight to Bold gives it genuine versatility across headlines and body copy. It is a solid choice for bilingual publications, websites, and documents requiring typographic cohesion between the two scripts.

name: Major Mono Display
classification_notes: Major Mono Display is a monospaced typeface built on a strict geometric grid, with letterforms that alternate between uppercase and lowercase visual rhythm to create a distinctive optical pattern. Its mechanical regularity gives it a strong, almost coded aesthetic.
summary: A rigidly geometric monospace display face that turns typographic uniformity into a bold visual statement.
description: Major Mono Display leans into the constraints of monospacing as a design feature rather than a limitation, producing a strikingly even, grid-locked texture. It is best deployed at large sizes for headlines, posters, or digital art projects where its mechanical character can dominate. Its single Regular weight keeps it firmly in specialist display territory.

name: Mako
classification_notes: Mako is a clean, unpretentious humanist sans-serif with open letterforms and modest stroke contrast, designed primarily for comfortable on-screen reading. Its single Regular weight limits its range but its neutrality makes it an easy workhorse.
summary: A plain, unassuming humanist sans that prioritises legibility over personality.
description: Mako is a quietly functional typeface with little in the way of distinctive character, making it most useful for interfaces and body text where the type should stay out of the way. Its single weight restricts its application to lighter-weight settings, and it lacks the range needed for complex typographic hierarchies. It works adequately for small web projects and simple documents.

name: Mali
classification_notes: Mali is a casual handwritten script supporting both Latin and Thai, with a friendly, rounded informality that bridges the two scripts convincingly. Its six weights give it surprising versatility for a script typeface.
summary: A cheerful handwritten script that spans Latin and Thai with warmth and a well-considered weight range.
description: Mali captures an informal, pen-on-paper quality while remaining legible across its full weight range from ExtraLight to Bold. Its dual-script support makes it a rare find for bilingual Thai-Latin projects in branding, packaging, or children's publishing. The lighter weights work particularly well for body copy in informal contexts, while the bolder cuts suit headlines and logotypes.

name: Mallanna
classification_notes: Mallanna is a clean, upright sans-serif designed primarily for the Telugu script with accompanying Latin support, featuring even stroke widths and open counters suited to screen use. Its design is straightforward and functional rather than expressive.
summary: A functional Telugu and Latin sans-serif built for reliable legibility at everyday text sizes.
description: Mallanna addresses a practical need for clean, screen-ready Telugu typography with a single Regular weight that keeps things simple. Its open letterforms and consistent stroke weight make it a dependable choice for web content, interfaces, and documents in Telugu. The Latin component is serviceable rather than distinguished, serving primarily to complement the Telugu text.

name: Maname
classification_notes: Maname is a serif typeface designed to serve the Sinhala script alongside Latin, with harmonious proportions and a traditional editorial character. It is among the relatively few quality Sinhala fonts available for web use.
summary: A carefully drawn Sinhala-Latin serif that brings editorial credibility to an underserved script.
description: Maname fills a genuine gap in multilingual typography by offering a well-considered serif for Sinhala script alongside a compatible Latin. Its single Regular weight suits continuous body text in web publishing, news, and educational materials. The typeface is a valuable resource for anyone producing bilingual Sinhala-English content with typographic integrity.

name: Mandali
classification_notes: Mandali is a straightforward, low-contrast sans-serif designed for the Telugu script with basic Latin support, prioritising clarity and economy in its single Regular weight. Its design is modest and functional, with no stylistic embellishment.
summary: A no-frills Telugu sans-serif that does its job quietly and competently at text sizes.
description: Mandali is a utilitarian typeface whose sole purpose is legible Telugu and Latin text rendering on screen and in print. The single weight limits its usefulness for complex typographic hierarchies, but for straightforward body text in Telugu-language publishing or web content it is entirely adequate. It is best thought of as a reliable default rather than an expressive choice.

name: Manjari
classification_notes: Manjari is a refined, modern sans-serif with a distinctive flowing quality in its Malayalam letterforms, drawing on calligraphic tradition while maintaining clean, contemporary proportions. Its three weights and multilingual Latin Extended coverage give it genuine utility.
summary: A graceful Malayalam sans-serif that brings contemporary elegance to one of the world's most visually complex scripts.
description: Manjari stands out among Malayalam typefaces for its thoughtful balance between the script's inherent curvaceousness and the demands of modern screen typography. Designed by Shyam Krishnan, it is well suited to web publishing, app interfaces, and editorial design in Malayalam and bilingual Latin contexts. The Thin weight is particularly striking at display sizes, while Regular and Bold cover everyday text and heading needs.

name: Manrope
classification_notes: Manrope blends geometric and humanist traits, with clean open letterforms and consistent stroke width. Its optical adjustments across eight weights make it particularly legible at small sizes on screen.
summary: A refined geometric sans with humanist warmth, built for digital interfaces and extended reading.
description: Manrope sits comfortably between the precision of geometric sans-serifs and the approachability of humanist designs, making it versatile across UI, branding, and editorial contexts. Its generous x-height and open apertures maintain clarity at small sizes, while the range of weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold gives designers strong typographic hierarchy. Cyrillic and Greek support extends its usefulness across multilingual projects.

name: Mansalva
classification_notes: Mansalva is a relaxed, hand-drawn script with irregular baseline and informal letterforms that evoke handwritten notes. Its single weight limits versatility but reinforces its casual, personal character.
summary: A loose, informal handwritten script with genuine personality and a relaxed, personal feel.
description: Mansalva mimics the natural irregularity of casual handwriting, with slightly bouncy letterforms and uneven strokes that give it an authentic, unpretentious charm. It works well for branding, greeting cards, posters, and any project requiring a warm, personal touch. Its single weight means it functions purely as a display or accent typeface rather than a workhorse.

name: Manuale
classification_notes: Manuale is a bracketed serif with humanist proportions and calligraphic origins, offering good rhythm and readability across a wide weight range. Its sturdy construction makes it equally suited to text and display settings.
summary: A sturdy humanist serif with calligraphic roots that performs reliably across both text and display sizes.
description: Manuale draws on the tradition of Renaissance letterforms, with bracketed serifs and slightly calligraphic stress that give it warmth and readability. Its six weights from Light to ExtraBold make it a genuinely flexible family suitable for books, magazines, and web editorial work. Vietnamese support alongside Latin Extended coverage adds practical multilingual value.

name: Manufacturing Consent
classification_notes: Manufacturing Consent employs angular, blackletter-influenced display lettering with a political and confrontational aesthetic. Its single weight and narrow application make it a purely expressive, one-context typeface.
summary: A politically charged display face borrowing blackletter drama for confrontational poster and headline work.
description: Manufacturing Consent is a heavy, expressive display typeface built for impact rather than subtlety, with blackletter-inflected forms that carry a sense of urgency and provocation. Its use cases are narrow — poster art, protest graphics, zine headlines, and politically themed editorial — and it makes no pretence of versatility. Outside those specific contexts it is likely to feel overwrought.

name: Marcellus
classification_notes: Marcellus is a single-weight serif with elegantly flared terminals and high contrast, evoking inscriptional Roman letterforms. Its refined, lapidary quality positions it firmly as a display and titling face.
summary: An elegant, inscription-inspired serif with flared terminals and classical poise, suited to titles and headings.
description: Marcellus takes its cues from Roman stone-cut lettering, combining high stroke contrast with refined, slightly flared terminals that give headings a ceremonial gravitas. It is best employed at display sizes for editorial mastheads, luxury branding, book covers, and titling work where a classical tone is required. The single weight limits its use as a text face, but as a headline typeface it is quietly distinguished.

name: Marcellus SC
classification_notes: Marcellus SC is a companion to Marcellus that replaces lowercase letters with true small capitals, maintaining the same high-contrast, inscriptional quality. It is purpose-built for all-caps or small-caps titling applications.
summary: The small-caps companion to Marcellus, delivering classical Roman authority in an all-caps titling format.
description: Marcellus SC presents all its lowercase characters as true small capitals, making it ideal for subheadings, institutional titling, monograms, and formal display settings that demand consistent cap height throughout. The same elegant, lapidary character of Marcellus is preserved, with flared terminals and strong contrast lending an air of authority. It pairs naturally with its parent family but stands on its own for specialised typographic applications.

name: Marck Script
classification_notes: Marck Script is a connected casual script with a slightly rushed, informal handwritten quality and moderate slant. Its Cyrillic support is a notable practical feature that distinguishes it from comparable Latin-only scripts.
summary: A flowing casual script with convincing handwritten energy and useful Cyrillic support.
description: Marck Script reproduces the look of relaxed, connected handwriting with natural variation in stroke thickness and a comfortable forward lean. It suits informal branding, greeting cards, social media graphics, and display headlines where a personal, approachable tone is needed. Cyrillic script support is a practical bonus that broadens its appeal for multilingual Eastern European and Russian language projects.

name: Margarine
classification_notes: Margarine is a chunky, rounded display face with exaggerated blob-like terminals and a deliberately naive, retro feel. It is highly distinctive but narrow in application due to its novelty character.
summary: A boldly rounded, retro-flavoured display face with chunky, almost cartoonish letterforms built for immediate impact.
description: Margarine leans hard into a nostalgic, slightly kitsch aesthetic with its oversized rounded terminals and bubbly proportions that recall mid-century food packaging and candy branding. It works well for children's products, playful food and beverage branding, retro-themed posters, and display headlines where personality trumps refinement. Its single weight and novelty character mean it functions as an occasional accent rather than a typographic workhorse.

name: Marhey
classification_notes: Marhey is a contemporary Arabic display typeface with Latin companions, featuring expressive, calligraphically-influenced letterforms with dynamic stroke contrast. It bridges traditional Arabic typographic sensibility with a modern, playful energy.
summary: A lively Arabic-Latin display typeface that draws on calligraphic roots to deliver expressive, characterful headlines.
description: Marhey suits editorial and branding contexts where Arabic and Latin scripts must coexist with visual energy. Its calligraphic influences give it warmth and movement, making it well-suited for posters, packaging, and multilingual campaigns. The range of weights from Light to Bold offers reasonable flexibility for display hierarchies.

name: Markazi Text
classification_notes: Markazi Text is a refined text-optimised typeface designed for Arabic and Latin body copy, drawing on classical Arabic type conventions with modest stroke contrast suited to sustained reading. Its Latin companion is clean and unobtrusive.
summary: A classical, text-optimised Arabic and Latin serif built for comfortable reading at small sizes.
description: Markazi Text prioritises legibility in continuous text across both Arabic and Latin scripts, making it a dependable choice for bilingual publications, academic documents, and long-form digital content. Its modest contrast and well-considered spacing keep it readable on screen without sacrificing typographic character. This is a reliable workhorse for multilingual editorial work.

name: Marko One
classification_notes: Marko One is a sturdy single-weight serif with robust stroke contrast and slightly wedge-shaped serifs, giving it an assertive, editorial quality. Its construction suggests influence from transitional and humanist serif traditions.
summary: A confident, single-weight serif with strong contrast and an assertive editorial presence.
description: Marko One's robust construction makes it well-suited for headline and subheading use in editorial and branding contexts. Its single Regular weight limits versatility, but within that weight the letterforms carry conviction and work well at medium to large sizes. It can serve modest body text duties at comfortable sizes, though the weight restriction narrows its range.

name: Marmelad
classification_notes: Marmelad is a light, friendly humanist sans-serif with support for Cyrillic, offering soft, approachable letterforms with a slightly informal character. Its single weight and limited scope position it as a display or branding choice rather than a text workhorse.
summary: A soft, approachable humanist sans with Cyrillic support and a pleasantly informal character.
description: Marmelad's friendly, rounded quality lends itself to branding, packaging, and light editorial work where a warm but clean aesthetic is desired. Its Cyrillic support extends its usefulness across Russian-language projects without sacrificing visual consistency. The single Regular weight limits its typographic range, keeping it best suited to short-form display applications.

name: Martel
classification_notes: Martel is a multi-weight serif typeface designed with Devanagari and Latin scripts in harmony, offering a wide weight range from ExtraLight to Black suitable for both text and display use. Its letterforms carry moderate contrast and a measured, formal quality.
summary: A well-constructed multi-weight serif spanning Devanagari and Latin, covering everything from fine text to heavy display.
description: Martel's broad weight range makes it a genuinely versatile workhorse for Hindi and Latin typographic systems, serving body text at lighter weights and headline work at heavier ones. Its formal, measured construction suits publishing, academic, and institutional contexts where clarity and authority are valued. The coherent Devanagari and Latin pairing is particularly valuable for bilingual Indian publishing.

name: Martel Sans
classification_notes: Martel Sans is the sans-serif counterpart to Martel, sharing the same Devanagari and Latin dual-script design with a matching wide weight range. Its humanist construction and open apertures make it comfortable for both screen and print use.
summary: The sans-serif companion to Martel, offering a harmonious Devanagari and Latin system across an extensive weight range.
description: Martel Sans works seamlessly alongside its serif counterpart in bilingual typographic systems, making both together an unusually complete solution for Hindi and Latin content. Its humanist construction and generous apertures ensure legibility at text sizes on screen and in print. The wide weight range from ExtraLight to Black supports full typographic hierarchies in UI, editorial, and publishing contexts.

name: Martian Mono
classification_notes: Martian Mono is a distinctive monospaced typeface with a slightly condensed, almost mechanical aesthetic and strong vertical emphasis, designed with exceptional clarity for code and technical interfaces. It supports Cyrillic alongside Latin and spans a generous weight range.
summary: A precise, technically-flavoured monospace with Cyrillic support and a clean, characterful presence ideal for coding environments.
description: Martian Mono distinguishes itself from the crowded coding font market through its slightly alien, mechanical aesthetic that remains highly legible at small sizes. Its wide weight range from Thin to ExtraBold gives developers and designers unusual flexibility for terminal emulators, code editors, and technical documentation. Cyrillic support makes it a strong choice for Russian-speaking developers who want a quality coding typeface.

name: Marvel
classification_notes: Marvel is a clean, modestly geometric sans-serif with a neutral character and limited two-weight offering, positioned between a workhorse and a display face. Its simple, unadorned construction gives it a utilitarian quality without much typographic distinction.
summary: A clean, no-frills geometric sans that handles headline and light text duties without fuss.
description: Marvel's straightforward construction makes it serviceable for web projects requiring a neutral, geometric sans without strong personality. Its two-weight range — Regular and Bold — limits typographic hierarchy but keeps things simple for lightweight projects. It lacks the refinement or character of more distinguished geometric sans-serifs, placing it firmly in the functional rather than expressive category.

name: Matangi
classification_notes: Matangi is a multi-script humanist sans-serif designed to harmonise Devanagari and Latin letterforms with a warm, open character. The design balances optical consistency across both scripts with a range of weights suitable for extended text and display use.
summary: A warmly drawn humanist sans-serif with strong multi-script credentials, bridging Devanagari and Latin with genuine typographic care.
description: Matangi offers a substantial seven-weight range that makes it versatile across branding, editorial, and interface contexts. Its Devanagari support is thoughtfully integrated rather than an afterthought, making it a reliable choice for multilingual South Asian publishing and digital products. The open, humanist forms read cleanly at small sizes on screen.

name: Mate
classification_notes: Mate is a refined transitional serif with moderate contrast and sturdy serifs, drawing on classical book typography traditions. Its single Regular weight is optimised for comfortable long-form reading in print and on screen.
summary: A quietly elegant transitional serif built primarily for body text, with classical proportions and reliable readability.
description: Mate favours restraint over personality, making it a dependable workhorse for editorial and book-style layouts. The moderate stroke contrast and well-formed serifs give it a traditional bookish warmth without feeling archaic. Its single weight limits versatility, but within that constraint it performs consistently well for long-form reading.

name: Mate SC
classification_notes: Mate SC is the small capitals companion to Mate, providing true-cut small caps for the same transitional serif design. It is intended to complement the standard Mate for headings, captions, and typographic hierarchy rather than standalone body use.
summary: The dedicated small caps companion to Mate, offering true small capitals for refined typographic hierarchy.
description: Mate SC provides the small cap letterforms that the standard Mate family lacks, filling a specific typographic role in editorial and book design. True small caps integrate far more gracefully into running text or subheadings than digitally scaled capitals, making this a useful pairing with its sibling. Best deployed alongside Mate itself rather than used in isolation.

name: Matemasie
classification_notes: Matemasie is a single-weight expressive display sans-serif with playful, rounded letterforms and a distinctly informal character. The design leans towards decorative and branding use rather than extended reading.
summary: A cheerful, characterful display sans-serif with rounded forms that brings a playful energy to headlines and branding.
description: Matemasie occupies a niche as a single-weight display face with enough personality to anchor a brand identity or poster design. Its rounded, expressive letterforms make it particularly suitable for children's content, informal branding, and digital-native headline use. The lack of additional weights limits its flexibility, but its distinctive voice is its main selling point.

name: Material Icons
classification_notes: Material Icons is Google's standard icon font implementing Material Design iconography as ligature-accessible glyphs in a filled style. It is a utility font rather than a type design, intended purely for UI icon delivery via web fonts.
summary: Google's filled Material Design icon set delivered as a font, providing scalable UI icons for web and Android interfaces.
description: Material Icons uses font ligatures to map descriptive text names to icon glyphs, making them accessible and easy to implement in web projects. The filled style is the default variant, offering strong visual weight and high legibility at small sizes. It is a purely functional resource with no application outside of UI and system design contexts.

name: Material Icons Outlined
classification_notes: Material Icons Outlined is the outlined stroke variant of Google's Material Design icon font, offering lighter visual weight compared to the filled version. The open, stroke-based forms suit interfaces where subtlety and refinement are preferred.
summary: The outlined variant of Google's Material Icons, delivering lighter, stroke-based icons suited to clean, modern UI design.
description: Material Icons Outlined provides an alternative visual register to the standard filled icons, favouring a lighter and more refined aesthetic. The outlined forms work particularly well in dense interfaces where visual hierarchy benefits from tonal variation between icon states. Like all Material Icons variants, it functions solely as a UI utility resource.

name: Material Icons Round
classification_notes: Material Icons Round uses soft, rounded terminals and curves throughout its icon glyphs, producing a friendlier and more approachable visual character than the standard filled variant. It suits consumer-facing products and interfaces with a warmer aesthetic.
summary: A softer, rounded take on Google's Material Icons, offering a friendlier visual tone for consumer-facing digital interfaces.
description: Material Icons Round introduces rounded endpoints and curves to the Material Design icon vocabulary, making it well suited to apps and products where warmth and approachability are part of the brand language. The style pairs naturally with rounded sans-serif typefaces in UI layouts. Its utility is identical to other Material Icons variants — purely functional and screen-focused.

name: Material Icons Sharp
classification_notes: Material Icons Sharp employs hard, angular corners and sharp terminals throughout its glyphs, creating a more technical and precise visual character than the standard variant. It suits interfaces with a functional, industrial, or technical aesthetic.
summary: The angular, sharp-cornered variant of Google's Material Icons, projecting a crisp and technical character suited to functional interfaces.
description: Material Icons Sharp replaces the gentle curves of the standard Material Icons with hard right-angle corners, lending icons a more precise and utilitarian feel. This variant is a natural fit for dashboards, data tools, and technical products where a sharper aesthetic reinforces functional credibility. As with all Material Icons, its application is strictly limited to UI and system design.

name: Material Icons Two Tone
classification_notes: A symbol font rather than a typeface, rendering two-tone material design icons as glyphs using ligature-based encoding. Designed for use within Google's Material Design system with a dual-layer colour treatment.
summary: A specialist icon font delivering two-tone Material Design symbols as monospaced glyphs for digital interfaces.
description: Material Icons Two Tone is not a text typeface but a symbol set built for Google's Material Design ecosystem, providing icons with a distinctive two-tone fill style. It is intended exclusively for UI and app development contexts where consistent, scalable iconography is required. Its use is highly specific and it offers no typographic value beyond its icon delivery function.

name: Material Symbols
classification_notes: A variable symbol font offering Material Design icons across a broad weight range from Thin to Bold, enabling fine-grained control over stroke weight within a single variable font file. Part of Google's updated Material Symbols system.
summary: A variable icon font bringing the full Material Symbols library with extensive weight variation into a single adaptable file.
description: Material Symbols represents Google's evolution of its icon font offering, consolidating multiple styles into a variable font with axes for weight, fill, grade, and optical size. This makes it far more flexible than its predecessors for responsive and adaptive interface design. Like all icon fonts in this family, it serves UI development rather than typographic composition.

name: Material Symbols Outlined
classification_notes: The outlined variant of the Material Symbols variable icon font, featuring icons rendered with open stroke outlines rather than filled forms. Provides a lighter, more refined visual weight suitable for clean interface contexts.
summary: The outlined style of Google's Material Symbols variable icon font, offering clean stroke-based icons across a full weight range.
description: Material Symbols Outlined delivers Material Design icons in an unfilled, outlined style, giving interfaces a lighter and more open appearance compared to filled variants. As a variable font it supports weight, grade, and optical size axes, making it adaptable across different display contexts. Its application is strictly functional, serving app and web UI development.

name: Material Symbols Rounded
classification_notes: The rounded variant of the Material Symbols variable icon font, where icon terminals and corners are softened with rounded endings to produce a friendlier, warmer aesthetic. Shares the same variable axes as the broader Material Symbols family.
summary: Google's rounded-style Material Symbols icon font, lending interfaces a softer and more approachable character.
description: Material Symbols Rounded applies rounded terminals to icon glyphs, making it the warmest and most friendly of the Material Symbols style variants. It suits consumer-facing applications, wellness products, or any interface where a gentler visual tone is desirable. It remains a functional UI tool rather than a typographic resource.

name: Material Symbols Sharp
classification_notes: The sharp variant of the Material Symbols variable icon font, featuring icons with angular, squared-off corners for a more precise and technical appearance. Contrasts with the Rounded variant by emphasising crisp geometry.
summary: The sharp-cornered style of Google's Material Symbols icon font, suited to technical or structured interface contexts.
description: Material Symbols Sharp uses angular, unrounded corners throughout its icon set, giving it a more precise and assertive quality than the standard or rounded variants. It is well suited to productivity tools, dashboards, or enterprise applications where a structured, no-nonsense aesthetic is appropriate. As with all Material Symbols variants, it functions as a UI icon resource rather than a conventional typeface.

name: Maven Pro
classification_notes: Maven Pro features distinctive curved strokes and unique letterforms with a geometric foundation softened by subtle humanist touches, giving it an approachable yet modern quality. The slightly irregular rhythm of its characters lends it personality beyond a strict geometric sans.
summary: A geometric sans-serif with distinctive curved strokes and enough personality to stand out from conventional geometric designs.
description: Maven Pro occupies a pleasant middle ground between strict geometric construction and humanist warmth, making it versatile for branding, headlines, and UI work. Its unique letterforms — particularly the curved bowls and angled terminals — give it a distinctive flavour that avoids the coldness of purely geometric typefaces. It works well for tech branding, editorial headings, and interface copy where a modern but personable voice is needed.

name: McLaren
classification_notes: McLaren is a rounded display typeface with playful, informal letterforms and a slightly hand-drawn quality that gives it a casual, approachable character. Its chunky stroke weight and soft corners make it suitable for children's media, informal branding, or fun editorial contexts.
summary: A chunky, rounded display face with a playful hand-drawn quality suited to informal branding and children's content.
description: McLaren brings a cheerful, rounded informality to display typography, with letterforms that feel loosely drawn rather than mechanically constructed. It is best suited to headlines, logotypes, packaging, and signage where a warm and inviting tone is required. Its single Regular weight limits its flexibility, but within its intended casual register it performs with confidence.

name: Mea Culpa
classification_notes: Mea Culpa is a flowing, high-contrast display script with broad calligraphic strokes reminiscent of formal pointed-pen writing. Its dramatic thick-to-thin transitions and ornate letterforms position it firmly in the decorative script category.
summary: A dramatic, high-contrast display script drawing on the tradition of formal pointed-pen calligraphy.
description: Mea Culpa delivers sweeping, ornate letterforms with strong thick-to-thin contrast that evokes classical penmanship and formal invitation typography. It suits luxury branding, wedding stationery, editorial headings, and any context demanding a sense of occasion and elegance. Its high decorative register means it is best used sparingly at large sizes, as body text use would be entirely impractical.

name: Meddon
classification_notes: Meddon is a loose, informal handwritten script with uneven baseline and naturalistic letterforms suggesting a quickly penned note. The strokes have variable weight and a distinctly personal, unpretentious quality.
summary: A casual, hand-penned script with an charmingly rough, personal quality.
description: Meddon mimics the look of handwriting dashed off with a broad-nibbed pen, complete with irregular letterforms and a relaxed rhythm. It works best for informal invitations, greeting cards, or social media graphics where a human, unpretentious touch is needed. Its limited weight range makes it a single-use decorative choice rather than a workhorse.

name: MedievalSharp
classification_notes: MedievalSharp combines blackletter structural references with a sharp, angular aesthetic reminiscent of carved stone or woodblock lettering. The forms are stylised and decorative rather than historically faithful.
summary: A sharp, angular blackletter-influenced display face evoking medieval stonework and woodcut lettering.
description: MedievalSharp draws on Gothic blackletter traditions but pushes the letterforms toward a more geometric, pointed extreme. It suits fantasy gaming, heavy metal branding, Halloween graphics, and any project demanding an archaic or dramatic atmosphere. Its single weight and novelty character limit it strictly to display applications.

name: Medula One
classification_notes: Medula One features extremely thin inline strokes that reduce letterforms to their skeletal outlines, creating a striking high-contrast effect at large sizes. The design is minimal and architectural in feel.
summary: An ultra-thin inline display face that strips letterforms down to elegant skeletal outlines.
description: Medula One achieves its distinctive look by using hairline strokes to trace the inner and outer contours of letterforms, leaving them largely transparent. This makes it ideal for fashion editorial headlines, luxury branding, and large-format poster work where visual lightness and drama are both required. It is entirely unsuitable for small sizes or body text.

name: Meera Inimai
classification_notes: Meera Inimai is designed primarily for Tamil script readability at screen sizes, with a clean and legible Latin companion. The Tamil letterforms are open and well-spaced, optimised for digital reading environments.
summary: A clean humanist sans designed to serve Tamil script readability on screen alongside a capable Latin companion.
description: Meera Inimai prioritises legibility for Tamil readers on digital devices, with open apertures and generous spacing suited to body text rendering. Its Latin glyphs are competent and restrained, making it a practical choice for bilingual Tamil–English digital content. The single regular weight limits its typographic flexibility for complex editorial layouts.

name: Megrim
classification_notes: Megrim is built from modular geometric components with a cold, mechanical precision that gives it a strong retro-futurist or art deco flavour. The letterforms are constructed rather than drawn, emphasising structure over warmth.
summary: A rigidly modular geometric display face with a retro-futurist, art deco edge.
description: Megrim assembles its letterforms from repeating geometric units, producing a consistent mechanical rhythm that feels simultaneously vintage and forward-looking. It suits sci-fi branding, art deco-inspired editorial design, nightlife graphics, and headline work demanding strong structural personality. The single weight and decorative nature make it unsuitable for text-level use.

name: Meie Script
classification_notes: Meie Script is a refined, flowing formal script with elegant connections between letters and moderate contrast reminiscent of copperplate calligraphic traditions. The strokes are smooth and the overall tone is graceful and restrained.
summary: A graceful formal script with smooth, well-connected strokes drawn from copperplate calligraphic tradition.
description: Meie Script balances elegance with approachability, offering cleaner connections and more even rhythm than many free formal scripts available on Google Fonts. It is well suited to wedding stationery, boutique branding, packaging, and editorial pull quotes where a polished handwritten feel is required. The single weight limits hierarchy options but the quality of execution is above average for its category.

name: Menbere
classification_notes: Menbere is designed to serve Ethiopic (Ge'ez) script with a broad weight range, featuring clean geometric construction well-suited to both display and text use. The Latin companion follows geometric sans conventions consistent with the Ethiopic design philosophy.
summary: A well-developed geometric sans serving Ethiopic script across a broad weight range with strong multilingual capability.
description: Menbere addresses a genuine need for quality Ethiopic typefaces with a comprehensive weight family running from Thin to Bold, enabling proper typographic hierarchy in Amharic, Tigrinya, and other Ethiopic-script languages. The geometric construction gives it a contemporary feel appropriate for digital interfaces, editorial work, and branding contexts. Its multilingual scope and weight breadth make it one of the more serious Ethiopic offerings currently available on Google Fonts.

name: Meow Script
classification_notes: Meow Script is a fluid, high-energy brush script with generous connecting strokes and an expressive, calligraphic character drawn from broad-nib or brush lettering traditions. The letterforms are lively with natural variation in stroke width.
summary: A lively, fluid brush script with generous connecting strokes and expressive calligraphic energy.
description: Meow Script captures the spontaneous quality of skilled brush lettering, with natural stroke contrast and flowing connections that give it genuine charm at display sizes. It works well for packaging, social media graphics, boutique branding, and any headline application where warmth and personality are prioritised over formality. Vietnamese script support broadens its practical appeal for multilingual projects.

name: Merienda
classification_notes: Merienda is a lively, semi-connected script with a calligraphic flavour and moderate contrast. Its rounded forms and upright stance give it a warm, approachable character across a broad weight range.
summary: A warm, semi-connected display script with genuine calligraphic character and an unusually broad weight range.
description: Merienda's upright posture and rounded letterforms distinguish it from more formal cursive scripts, making it accessible without feeling trivial. The extended weight range — from Light to Black — gives designers substantial flexibility for headlines, packaging, and brand identities. Its Latin Extended and Vietnamese coverage makes it more broadly usable than most display scripts of its type.

name: Merriweather
classification_notes: Merriweather is a low-contrast, sturdy serif designed specifically for comfortable on-screen reading at small sizes. Its tall x-height, wide proportions, and slightly condensed letterforms optimise legibility in continuous text.
summary: A robustly designed screen serif built from the ground up for comfortable long-form reading on digital displays.
description: Merriweather's generous x-height and sturdy stroke weights ensure it holds up well at body sizes across a range of screen resolutions. Its Cyrillic coverage makes it a dependable choice for multilingual publishing projects. It is one of the most widely used Google Fonts serifs, earning its reputation as a reliable editorial and UI workhorse.

name: Merriweather Sans
classification_notes: Merriweather Sans shares the proportional logic of its serif companion but strips away the serifs in favour of open, humanist letterforms. It retains the tall x-height and sturdy weight range that define the Merriweather family.
summary: The sans-serif companion to Merriweather, sharing its robust screen-optimised design and humanist proportions.
description: Merriweather Sans pairs naturally with its serif counterpart for mixed typographic systems in editorial and interface design. Its open apertures and generous spacing ensure strong legibility at both text and display sizes on screen. The extended weight range and broad Latin coverage make it a practical, no-fuss choice for web and UI projects.

name: Metal
classification_notes: Metal is a highly ornamental display typeface designed to support the Khmer script alongside Latin, featuring elaborate, interlocking decorative elements inspired by traditional Cambodian metalwork. Its single weight limits its functional versatility.
summary: A dramatic, ornament-heavy display face rooted in traditional Cambodian metalwork aesthetics with Khmer and Latin support.
description: Metal's intricate, interlocking letterforms are clearly designed for large-scale decorative use rather than functional text setting. Its primary distinction is the rare combination of Khmer script support with an ornamental Latin companion, serving a specific cultural and linguistic niche. With only a single Regular weight, its application is narrow — best suited to titling, posters, or ceremonial graphic work.

name: Metal Mania
classification_notes: Metal Mania draws on heavy metal and rock poster lettering traditions, with jagged, distressed forms and exaggerated decorative spurs. It is a novelty face with very limited typographic utility beyond genre-specific contexts.
summary: A genre-specific display face channelling heavy metal poster lettering with aggressive, distressed forms.
description: Metal Mania's jagged spurs and rough texture make its intended audience immediately clear — band merchandise, rock event posters, and genre-aligned branding. Outside of that narrow context it has little flexibility, and the single-weight offering further limits its usefulness. It does its job with conviction, but the job itself is very specific.

name: Metamorphous
classification_notes: Metamorphous combines elements of uncial and early Renaissance letterforms with a distinctive hand-carved quality, producing a typeface that feels both ancient and stylised. Its high contrast and pronounced serifs give it a strong typographic personality.
summary: An atmospheric display serif blending uncial and early Renaissance influences with a hand-carved, lapidary quality.
description: Metamorphous occupies an unusual stylistic territory between historical type revival and contemporary decorative design. Its strong contrast and distinctive letterforms suit it to editorial headlines, book covers, and any project requiring a sense of antiquity or craft. The single weight means it functions best as a titling or accent face rather than a workhorse.

name: Metrophobic
classification_notes: Metrophobic is a clean, neutral sans-serif with humanist proportions and a slightly informal feel, designed with legibility on screen as a primary concern. Its modest character makes it functional for UI and body text but somewhat unremarkable as a design statement.
summary: A clean, screen-focused humanist sans with modest proportions and solid legibility credentials.
description: Metrophobic's neutral, open letterforms make it a competent choice for interface design and on-screen body text where readability is the priority. It lacks the design distinction of more celebrated humanist sans-serifs, but performs reliably at smaller sizes. Its single weight is a significant practical limitation for most design systems.

name: Michroma
classification_notes: Michroma is a stark, angular geometric sans-serif with a strongly technical and futuristic character, built on precise geometric forms with sharp terminals and tight spacing. Its aesthetic draws on industrial and technological visual culture.
summary: A sharp, angular geometric sans built for a technological aesthetic, with a distinctly futuristic and industrial character.
description: Michroma's precise geometry and hard terminals give it a convincing sci-fi and technical sensibility, making it well-suited to technology branding, gaming interfaces, and headline use in editorial contexts with a modern edge. Its single weight and restricted character set limit its versatility in broader typographic systems. At display sizes it makes a confident statement; as a text face it is too rigid and cold to be comfortable.

name: Micro 5
classification_notes: Micro 5 renders text as a tiny pixel grid, mimicking the appearance of early LED or LCD matrix displays. Each character is constructed from a minimal 5-pixel-tall bitmap-style structure.
summary: A pixel-matrix typeface that faithfully recreates the aesthetic of early digital LED displays at a tiny scale.
description: Micro 5 draws directly from the visual language of vintage electronics, rendering characters as discrete dot-matrix grids. It suits retro-themed branding, tech interfaces, and game UI where a lo-fi digital feel is intentional. Its mathematical character support broadens its appeal for technical or scientific contexts.

name: Micro 5 Charted
classification_notes: Micro 5 Charted shares the same pixel-grid construction as Micro 5 but is optimised for use in charts and data visualisations, with characters designed to align neatly on grid structures.
summary: A charted variant of Micro 5 designed to sit cleanly within data grids and technical diagrams.
description: Micro 5 Charted retains the lo-fi LED aesthetic of its sibling but applies it to structured data contexts, making it well suited to infographics, dashboards, and retro-styled tables. The character spacing and proportions are tuned for alignment within columnar layouts. It remains a niche choice but fills a specific gap for technically themed visual design.

name: Milonga
classification_notes: Milonga features ornate, high-contrast letterforms with a distinctly Latin American flavour, combining calligraphic stroke variation with decorative flourishes. The design evokes vintage Argentine tango poster typography.
summary: An ornate display serif with a strong Argentine flavour, evoking vintage tango posters and Latin American signage.
description: Milonga channels the decorative typography of early twentieth-century Buenos Aires, with high-contrast strokes and lavish detailing that suit headline and poster use. It works well for event branding, restaurant identities, or any project seeking a South American cultural reference. Its single weight limits versatility, but within its niche it is expressive and distinctive.

name: Miltonian
classification_notes: Miltonian presents heavily decorative capital letterforms inspired by Victorian and medieval illuminated lettering, with intricate inline and outline detail. It reads more as graphic ornament than functional text type.
summary: A heavily ornamented display face drawing on Victorian and illuminated manuscript traditions, best treated as graphic decoration.
description: Miltonian's letterforms are so elaborately detailed that legibility at small sizes is essentially abandoned in favour of visual impact. It suits drop caps, monogram design, certificates, and heritage-themed branding where a single decorative initial is the centrepiece. Practically a one-trick font, but it performs that trick with considerable craft.

name: Miltonian Tattoo
classification_notes: Miltonian Tattoo is a companion to Miltonian, sharing its ornate Victorian-illuminated structure but rendered with a slightly rougher, more textured quality that evokes hand-drawn tattoo flash lettering.
summary: The tattoo-inflected sibling of Miltonian, lending its ornate Victorian letterforms a rougher, hand-crafted edge.
description: Miltonian Tattoo adapts the parent design's elaborate ornamentation with subtle textural variation that references traditional tattoo lettering and flash art. It suits alternative fashion branding, band merchandise, or any project that needs an edge of counter-cultural grit alongside Victorian grandeur. Like its sibling, it is a purely decorative instrument with very limited range.

name: Mina
classification_notes: Mina is a clean, unpretentious humanist sans-serif designed with strong Bengali script support alongside Latin, making it a genuinely bilingual workhorse. Its letterforms are open and legible at text sizes.
summary: A practical humanist sans offering solid Latin and Bengali support in a clean, readable package.
description: Mina prioritises function over flair, delivering clear, open letterforms suited to body text, UI copy, and editorial work across Bengali and Latin scripts. Its dual-script design makes it particularly valuable for South Asian publishing, web apps, and communications targeting Bengali-speaking audiences. With only two weights it is limited in typographic range, but reliable within its scope.

name: Mingzat
classification_notes: Mingzat is designed primarily to support the Lepcha script of the Sikkim region, making it a rare and specialised typeface for an endangered writing system, paired with standard Latin glyphs. Its design is functional and understated.
summary: A quietly functional sans-serif whose primary distinction is its support for the endangered Lepcha script of Sikkim.
description: Mingzat serves a critical archival and cultural role by providing digital type for the Lepcha script, which has very few quality typeface options. The Latin component is straightforward and workable, though unremarkable on its own merits. For linguists, educators, and publishers working with Sikkimese or Tibeto-Burman language communities, it is essentially indispensable.

name: Miniver
classification_notes: Miniver is a single-weight display serif with exaggerated, swelling stroke terminals and a bold, calligraphic energy reminiscent of Victorian and Edwardian wood-type advertising faces. The letterforms are robust and highly characterful.
summary: A bold, characterful display serif with swelling terminals and Victorian advertising energy that commands attention at large sizes.
description: Miniver draws on the tradition of hand-lettered Victorian shop fronts and Edwardian broadsheets, delivering thick-stroked letterforms with a confident, almost theatrical presence. It is well suited to pub signage, artisan branding, food and drink packaging, and any headline application that benefits from heritage warmth. Its single weight and display-only temperament keep it firmly in specialist territory.

name: Miranda Sans
classification_notes: Miranda Sans is a clean humanist sans-serif with open apertures and a warm, approachable character. Its weight range from Regular to Bold gives it practical versatility for both text and interface use.
summary: A straightforward humanist sans with good legibility credentials and a sensible weight range for general-purpose use.
description: Miranda Sans sits comfortably in the humanist sans tradition, with letterforms that balance warmth and neutrality. It performs well in UI contexts and editorial body text, where its open shapes aid readability at smaller sizes. The Latin Extended coverage makes it a practical choice for multilingual projects.

name: Miriam Libre
classification_notes: Miriam Libre is a dual-script humanist sans designed to harmonise Hebrew and Latin letterforms at the same optical weight. The design draws on the Miriam typeface tradition in Israeli typography, updated for contemporary screen use.
summary: A carefully balanced Hebrew and Latin sans-serif that brings both scripts into visual harmony for bilingual publishing.
description: Miriam Libre is designed from the ground up to treat Hebrew and Latin as equal typographic partners, making it genuinely useful for Israeli publishing, signage, and digital interfaces. Its clean, upright forms work well at text sizes, and the Regular through Bold weights provide enough range for editorial hierarchies. It is one of the more reliable choices on Google Fonts for quality Hebrew typography.

name: Mirza
classification_notes: Mirza is a multi-script serif designed to unify Arabic Naskh calligraphic forms with complementary Latin letterforms. The design has an elegant, traditional character with moderate contrast and carefully crafted diacritics.
summary: An elegant calligraphic serif that bridges Arabic Naskh tradition and Latin typography with genuine typographic craft.
description: Mirza draws directly on the Naskh calligraphic tradition, giving the Arabic script a warm, literary quality that suits book typography, editorial work, and formal branding. The Latin companion is sensitively designed to match the weight and texture of the Arabic, avoiding the mismatch common in dual-script fonts. It works well for publications, certificates, and any context demanding a degree of cultural refinement.

name: Miss Fajardose
classification_notes: Miss Fajardose is a decorative display script with exaggerated swashes and a hand-lettered, pen-drawn character. Its single weight and ornate styling limit its practical utility to short headline or logotype applications.
summary: A heavily ornamented display script with generous swashes suited to decorative headlines and vintage-style branding.
description: Miss Fajardose has a flamboyant, pen-lettered quality with flourishes that recall Victorian and early twentieth-century commercial lettering. It is best deployed sparingly — logotypes, poster headlines, or packaging where a single striking word is required. Its single-weight limitation means it has no flexibility beyond that decorative role.

name: Mitr
classification_notes: Mitr is a Thai and Latin sans-serif with a friendly, slightly rounded quality and a wide Latin Extended character set. Its six weights make it a practical workhorse for Thai-language digital and print projects.
summary: A well-rounded Thai and Latin sans-serif with a generous weight range that handles both scripts with consistent warmth.
description: Mitr was designed to serve Thai and Latin typography with equal care, producing letterforms that share a gentle, approachable rhythm across both scripts. The six-weight range from ExtraLight to Bold makes it adaptable for everything from body text to display headings. It is a solid choice for Thai-language websites, apps, and editorial work where readability and warmth are priorities.

name: Mochiy Pop One
classification_notes: Mochiy Pop One is a Japanese display sans-serif with a chunky, rounded, pop-art character derived from the Mochiy design. It covers the full Japanese glyph set with a single weight optimised for large-size use.
summary: A bold, rounded Japanese display sans with a cheerful pop aesthetic built for large-scale use.
description: Mochiy Pop One brings a playful, cartoon-inflected energy to Japanese typography, with thick strokes and rounded terminals that give it an immediately friendly personality. Its full CJK coverage makes it practical for Japanese-language headlines, packaging, and digital banners. The single weight means it is a one-trick font, but it executes that trick with confidence.

name: Mochiy Pop P One
classification_notes: Mochiy Pop P One is a proportional-kana variant of Mochiy Pop One, sharing the same rounded, display-oriented design but with proportional spacing for the kana characters. The distinction between the two is subtle and primarily relevant for specific Japanese typesetting contexts.
summary: The proportional-kana sibling of Mochiy Pop One, sharing the same bold rounded character with adjusted kana spacing.
description: Mochiy Pop P One differs from Mochiy Pop One in its use of proportional kana spacing, which can produce a slightly more natural text rhythm in certain Japanese typesetting scenarios. Both fonts share the same chunky, pop-inflected display personality and are best used for headlines, packaging, and large-format digital content. The choice between the two will depend on specific Japanese typesetting requirements rather than aesthetic preference.

name: Modak
classification_notes: Modak is an exuberant display typeface for Devanagari and Latin, characterised by inflated, bubbly letterforms with very heavy stroke weights and rounded endings. It is designed purely for large-scale attention-grabbing use.
summary: An unapologetically bold and bubbly display typeface that brings the same inflated energy to Devanagari and Latin letterforms.
description: Modak was designed by Ek Type as a display face that treats Devanagari as a first-class citizen rather than an afterthought, with the same exaggerated, rounded weight applied consistently across both scripts. It is ideal for posters, packaging, and headlines where maximum visual impact is the goal. Its single weight and extreme styling make it a committed display tool with no pretensions toward body text use.

name: Modern Antiqua
classification_notes: Modern Antiqua draws on blackletter and old-style roman traditions, combining sharp, pointed terminals with a decorative, antiquated character. Its high contrast and ornamental quality make it unsuitable for body text but striking at display sizes.
summary: A decorative display serif blending blackletter influences with classical roman forms for an antiquated, ornamental effect.
description: Modern Antiqua occupies an unusual niche, channelling the weight and drama of blackletter while retaining roman legibility. It suits period-themed branding, certificates, editorial headlines, and packaging where a sense of heritage or gravitas is required. Its single weight limits versatility, but within its narrow use case it carries considerable presence.

name: Moderustic
classification_notes: Moderustic features rounded, almost bubble-like letterforms with uniformly circular strokes and open apertures, giving it a distinctly contemporary and playful geometric quality. Its wide script support across Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin makes it unusually practical for a font of this personality.
summary: A bubbly, rounded geometric sans with broad multilingual coverage and a warm, approachable character.
description: Moderustic stands out among rounded geometric sans-serifs for its generous Cyrillic and Greek support, making it a practical choice for international branding and digital products. Its soft, circular forms lend themselves well to tech startups, children's content, and consumer apps where friendliness and modernity are priorities. The range of six weights gives it more typographic flexibility than its playful appearance might suggest.

name: Mogra
classification_notes: Mogra is a bold, brush-influenced display typeface designed primarily for the Gujarati script, with a matching Latin companion that shares its heavy, expressive strokes. Its chunky forms and high energy make it purpose-built for attention-grabbing headlines.
summary: A bold, brush-style display face designed for Gujarati with an energetic Latin companion, built for maximum visual impact.
description: Mogra is a rare example of a display typeface that places Gujarati script at its centre rather than as an afterthought, giving South Asian design projects a genuinely expressive tool. Its thick, brush-inflected strokes make it well suited to posters, packaging, and festival branding where drama and cultural specificity matter. The single weight is a limitation, but within that constraint the typeface is confident and cohesive.

name: Mohave
classification_notes: Mohave is a wide, sturdy sans-serif with slightly condensed proportions, sharp terminals, and a utilitarian, industrial character reminiscent of technical lettering and signage. Its modest contrast and clean geometry give it a functional toughness.
summary: A wide, no-nonsense industrial sans with clean geometry and a tough, functional personality suited to headlines and branding.
description: Mohave draws on the visual language of engineering lettering and mid-century signage, making it a natural fit for automotive, construction, and technology branding. Its five-weight range from Light to Bold gives designers reasonable flexibility across both display and mid-sized text settings. The typeface performs particularly well in all-caps headlines where its wide, even proportions read with real authority.

name: Moirai One
classification_notes: Moirai One is a highly stylised display typeface with an irregular, hand-drawn quality that evokes traditional Korean brushwork and folk art aesthetics, extended to cover the full Hangul syllabary alongside Latin. Its expressive, almost chaotic strokes are firmly in novelty territory.
summary: A wildly expressive display face rooted in Korean folk art brushwork, offering striking visual character for both Hangul and Latin scripts.
description: Moirai One is a genuinely unusual entry in the Google Fonts library, providing designers working with Korean content a display option with real cultural character rather than a neutral workhorse. Its irregular, vigorous strokes make it unsuitable for body text but highly effective for posters, editorial covers, and culturally specific branding. The single Regular weight reinforces its status as a speciality typeface for specific moments rather than sustained reading.

name: Molengo
classification_notes: Molengo is a clean, low-key humanist sans-serif with open apertures, modest stroke contrast, and a readable, unassuming character that prioritises clarity over personality. Its single weight limits its range but it performs capably in body and UI contexts.
summary: A quiet, dependable humanist sans that prioritises legibility and neutrality above all else.
description: Molengo offers a restrained alternative to more characterful humanist sans-serifs, making it a safe choice for long-form web content, editorial text, and simple UI designs where distraction is unwelcome. Its open letterforms and comfortable spacing hold up well at small screen sizes, though the absence of additional weights restricts its usefulness in complex typographic systems. It is a workhorse rather than a showpiece, and performs that role reliably.

name: Molle
classification_notes: Molle is an italic, cursive display script with strong calligraphic roots, featuring pronounced pen-influenced stroke contrast and flowing, connected letterforms. Its decorative flourishes and single-weight format position it firmly as a headline or accent face.
summary: A flowing, calligraphic display script with confident pen-drawn stroke contrast and an elegant, expressive character.
description: Molle channels classical italic penmanship into a contemporary display script, making it well suited to wedding stationery, cosmetics branding, editorial mastheads, and any context where handcrafted elegance is the goal. Its pronounced contrast between thick and thin strokes gives it a premium, refined quality that simpler scripts lack. A single weight is the expected limitation for this genre, and within it Molle delivers consistent charm.

name: Momo Signature
classification_notes: Momo Signature is a casual, brush-influenced signature-style script with irregular baseline flow and a hand-lettered authenticity that mimics personal handwriting. Its informal, spontaneous character is narrowly suited to branding and short decorative use.
summary: A casual, hand-lettered signature script with natural irregularity and an authentic personal-branding feel.
description: Momo Signature mimics the fluid, imperfect quality of a handwritten signature, making it a natural choice for personal brands, boutique packaging, and lifestyle product labels seeking an artisanal or intimate tone. Vietnamese support is a notable inclusion that extends its practical reach for Southeast Asian markets. Like most fonts in this genre it is a one-trick face, and its single weight means it functions purely as an accent or headline element rather than a typographic system.

name: Momo Trust Display
classification_notes: A display-weight companion to Momo Trust Sans, designed for large-scale headline use with clean, confident letterforms. Likely shares the same underlying design principles as the text variant but optimised for impact at size.
summary: A display-only sans-serif built for bold headline work within the Momo Trust family.
description: Momo Trust Display is the large-scale counterpart to Momo Trust Sans, stripped back to a single weight for focused use in headlines and branding. Its clean construction makes it suitable for editorial titles and poster work. The extended Latin and Vietnamese script support broadens its utility across multilingual projects.

name: Momo Trust Sans
classification_notes: A multi-weight geometric sans with a wide weight range from ExtraLight to ExtraBold, suggesting a workhorse family designed for both text and interface use. Clean, rational letterforms with broad script coverage including Vietnamese.
summary: A versatile geometric sans-serif family with a broad weight range suited to both screen interfaces and editorial work.
description: Momo Trust Sans spans seven weights, giving designers considerable flexibility across text sizes and contexts. Its geometric foundations keep it feeling modern and neutral, while the inclusion of Latin Extended and Vietnamese scripts makes it a practical choice for multilingual digital products. It works equally well in body copy and interface labels.

name: Mona Sans
classification_notes: A wide-ranging humanist sans-serif with eight weights from ExtraLight to Black, developed by GitHub with strong variable font capabilities. Features slightly condensed proportions and a technical yet approachable character.
summary: GitHub's own humanist sans-serif, Mona Sans is a polished variable typeface that straddles technical precision and human warmth.
description: Mona Sans was developed by Degarism Studio for GitHub and released as open source, making it a well-considered typeface with serious institutional backing. Its eight-weight range and variable font architecture give it exceptional flexibility for responsive design systems. The slightly compressed proportions lend it a confident, modern character that suits both interfaces and editorial headlines.

name: Monda
classification_notes: A screen-oriented humanist sans with clear, open letterforms designed for readability on digital displays. The modest weight range of four steps suits UI and text applications rather than expressive display use.
summary: A practical, screen-friendly humanist sans-serif designed with clarity and digital readability in mind.
description: Monda is a clean humanist sans-serif optimised for screen use, with open apertures and generous spacing that keep text legible at small sizes. Its four-weight range makes it a solid workhorse for interface design and body copy without the fuss of an elaborate family. The Latin Extended and Vietnamese coverage adds quiet utility for multilingual digital projects.

name: Monofett
classification_notes: An unusual display typeface built on a monospaced grid but rendered with bold, decorative letterforms rather than utilitarian coding aesthetics. Essentially a novelty face with a single weight and limited application.
summary: Monofett is a chunky, decorative display face that borrows the monospaced grid for ornamental rather than functional purposes.
description: Monofett takes the structural logic of a monospaced typeface and turns it into something theatrical and decorative, making each character occupy equal width in a visually heavy, poster-ready form. It is strictly a one-trick display face with no realistic use in text settings. Its appeal is narrow but genuine for retro-flavoured poster work and novelty branding.

name: Monomakh
classification_notes: A revival-style display typeface rooted in historical Cyrillic letterforms, designed to reflect pre-Petrine Russian script traditions. Features distinctly archaic forms that are decorative and culturally specific.
summary: Monomakh is a historically informed Cyrillic display typeface drawing on pre-reform Russian letterform traditions.
description: Monomakh revives archaic Cyrillic letterforms associated with early printed and manuscript traditions, making it a distinctive choice for culturally specific editorial and heritage projects. Its unusual character shapes give it strong decorative personality, though this limits its applicability to contexts where historical or expressive resonance is the goal. Latin support is included but the typeface is clearly conceived around its Cyrillic identity.

name: Monomaniac One
classification_notes: An extreme display sans-serif notable for dramatically tall, narrow proportions that push letterforms to their structural limits. Japanese script support alongside Latin is unusual and makes it highly distinctive for East Asian-influenced design.
summary: Monomaniac One is an aggressively tall, condensed display sans-serif with rare Japanese script support that amplifies its dramatic character.
description: Monomaniac One takes condensed sans-serif design to an extreme, with letterforms that appear almost impossibly narrow and elongated for high-impact headline use. The inclusion of Japanese script alongside Latin and Latin Extended is a genuinely unusual combination that opens up cross-cultural typographic possibilities. It is a one-weight face with a specific, expressive purpose — posters, titles, and anywhere vertical tension is the point.

name: Monoton
classification_notes: A retro display typeface defined by inline double-stripe detailing across all letterforms, creating a neon-sign or illuminated-lettering aesthetic. Available in a single weight only, making it strictly a headline or decorative face.
summary: Monoton's defining characteristic is its inline double-stripe ornamentation, evoking neon signage and mid-century display lettering.
description: Monoton is a purely decorative display typeface whose inline striping gives every letterform a luminous, neon-sign quality reminiscent of mid-twentieth-century entertainment signage and arcade aesthetics. Its single weight limits versatility, but within its niche it is immediately effective for retro branding, event posters, and any context that calls for nostalgic showmanship. It is essentially a one-trick face, but it executes that trick with conviction.

name: Monsieur La Doulaise
classification_notes: A formal copperplate-influenced cursive script with elegant, flowing letterforms and fine hairline strokes. The high contrast between thick and thin strokes gives it a distinctly calligraphic, aristocratic character.
summary: A refined, high-contrast copperplate script with a distinctly French aristocratic elegance.
description: Monsieur La Doulaise draws from classical copperplate engraving traditions, delivering sweeping strokes and dramatic contrast that feel simultaneously antique and sophisticated. It suits wedding invitations, luxury packaging, and editorial headings where a sense of formal ceremony is required. Best reserved for display sizes, as its fine hairlines render poorly at small scales.

name: Montaga
classification_notes: A delicate, classical serif with moderate contrast and humanist proportions reminiscent of Renaissance typefaces. Its fine strokes and traditional construction give it a refined, bookish quality.
summary: A restrained, classically proportioned serif that carries quiet sophistication without demanding attention.
description: Montaga draws on old-style traditions with its angled stress, moderate stroke contrast, and humanist proportions that feel comfortable in both print and screen editorial contexts. Its single Regular weight limits versatility, making it most suitable for display headings, pull quotes, or accent text in designed layouts. The overall impression is understated and literary, well suited to book covers, journals, and cultural publications.

name: Montagu Slab
classification_notes: A contemporary slab serif with optical sizing baked into its design, featuring sturdy bracketed serifs and carefully modulated stroke contrast across a broad weight range. Its construction bridges classical text tradition with modern display versatility.
summary: A seriously well-engineered slab serif designed with optical sizing in mind, equally at home in body text and bold headlines.
description: Montagu Slab was developed by Font Bureau with careful attention to how letterforms need to shift across different sizes, resulting in a family that feels appropriately robust at display sizes and comfortably readable at text sizes. The range from Thin to Bold gives designers genuine flexibility across editorial, branding, and UI contexts. Its bracketed serifs and measured contrast lend warmth without sacrificing authority.

name: MonteCarlo
classification_notes: A refined, flowing formal script with continuous connecting strokes and moderate contrast, evoking handwritten copperplate penmanship. The letterforms are elegant and consistent, designed for legibility at display sizes.
summary: A graceful, connecting formal script that balances copperplate tradition with accessible legibility.
description: MonteCarlo delivers the flowing elegance of formal penmanship without the extreme hairline fragility found in some copperplate revivals, making it more practical across print and digital applications. It suits wedding stationery, luxury branding, greeting cards, and editorial accents where a personal yet polished handwritten quality is desired. The Latin Extended and Vietnamese coverage makes it notably more useful than many scripts in its category.

name: Montez
classification_notes: A loose, informal looping script with a hand-drawn quality and relatively low stroke contrast. The letterforms feel relaxed and approachable rather than formally calligraphic.
summary: A simple, looping casual script with an informal, hand-written charm suited to lighthearted display use.
description: Montez offers a breezy, unpretentious script aesthetic that works for informal branding, social media graphics, and decorative headings where approachability matters more than refinement. Its single Regular weight and limited script vocabulary means it functions best in short bursts of text at large sizes. It lacks the sophistication of more carefully crafted scripts but delivers a cheerful, accessible personality.

name: Montserrat
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif inspired by the signage and urban lettering of the Montserrat neighbourhood of Buenos Aires, with near-perfect circular forms and clean, modernist construction. Its extensive weight range and Cyrillic coverage make it exceptionally versatile.
summary: One of the most widely used geometric sans-serifs on the web, combining modernist construction with warmth drawn from early twentieth-century urban lettering.
