description: Montserrat draws from the hand-painted signs of the Buenos Aires neighbourhood of the same name, giving its geometric foundations a subtle humanist warmth that distinguishes it from colder counterparts such as Futura. Its nine weights from Thin to Black, combined with Cyrillic and Latin Extended coverage, make it one of the most practically versatile typefaces in the Google Fonts catalogue. It performs strongly in branding, UI, editorial headlines, and marketing contexts across both digital and print.

name: Montserrat Alternates
classification_notes: A companion to Montserrat featuring alternative letterform constructions on select characters, offering distinctive glyphs with a more distinctive visual personality. The core geometric structure is identical to Montserrat.
summary: Montserrat Alternates provides the same broad family as Montserrat but with distinctive alternate letterforms for designers seeking a more individual character.
description: Sharing its weight range and Cyrillic coverage with the main Montserrat family, the Alternates cut swaps in stylistically distinct glyphs that give logotypes and editorial headings a more personalised, less ubiquitous feel. It is particularly useful in branding contexts where Montserrat's underlying geometry is desired but its sheer familiarity on the web is a concern. In practice, the two families can be mixed to create subtle typographic contrast within a single visual system.

name: Montserrat Underline
classification_notes: An unconventional variant of Montserrat in which every character carries a built-in underline as an integral part of the letterform rather than as a typographic decoration. This structural feature makes it a highly specific display tool.
summary: A novel variant of Montserrat with underlines built directly into the glyphs, turning a typographic convention into a structural design feature.
description: Montserrat Underline is a curiosity within the Montserrat family: the underline is not a CSS decoration but a drawn element that forms part of each letterform, creating a consistent baseline rule that cannot be toggled off. This makes it a highly specialised tool, best deployed for short headlines, logotypes, or poster work where the continuous ruled line is an intentional aesthetic statement. Its practical applications are narrow, but within those applications it delivers a bold and unusual visual effect.

name: Moo Lah Lah
classification_notes: Moo Lah Lah features bold, chunky letterforms with a playful, cartoonish quality and exaggerated rounded strokes. The design has a circus or burlesque flavour with strong visual impact at large sizes.
summary: A chunky, circus-inflected display face with bold personality and retro showbiz energy.
description: Moo Lah Lah draws on vintage carnival and burlesque lettering traditions, delivering thick, rounded forms with a deliberately over-the-top character. It works best at large sizes for posters, event branding, and novelty headlines. Its single weight limits versatility, making it a one-purpose decorative tool rather than a workhorse.

name: Mooli
classification_notes: Mooli is a clean, single-weight rounded sans-serif with softly terminated strokes and open apertures that give it a friendly, approachable tone. The letterforms are simple and legible without heavy stylisation.
summary: A clean rounded sans with soft terminals and an open, friendly disposition suited to digital interfaces.
description: Mooli's gently rounded stroke endings and generous spacing make it comfortable on screen at moderate sizes, lending itself to app interfaces, children's content, and informal branding. Its single weight restricts typographic hierarchy but the consistent softness keeps compositions feeling cohesive. It reads well at body sizes despite being a single-weight face.

name: Moon Dance
classification_notes: Moon Dance is a flowing, cursive display script with loose, informal letterforms that suggest handwritten brush or pen strokes. Connections between letters are fluid and the overall tone is romantic and expressive.
summary: A flowing cursive display script with romantic, handwritten energy ideal for decorative headline use.
description: Moon Dance mimics the sweeping strokes of informal handwriting, giving it warmth and personality suited to wedding stationery, lifestyle branding, and editorial headers. Its generous ascenders and descenders demand ample line spacing, and it functions best at large display sizes where its flowing character can breathe. Pairing it with a neutral sans provides effective contrast.

name: Moul
classification_notes: Moul is a bold, decorative Khmer display typeface with high contrast strokes and ornate details rooted in traditional Khmer script conventions. Its Latin counterpart is minimal, as the design is clearly centred on Khmer display use.
summary: A bold, high-contrast Khmer display face with ornate traditional detailing suited to headlines and signage.
description: Moul brings a decorative, high-contrast aesthetic to Khmer script, drawing on calligraphic traditions to produce a typeface suited to posters, signage, and ceremonial or editorial headlines. The strong stroke contrast and elaborate form details make it unsuitable for body text but visually commanding at large sizes. It is an important resource for Khmer-language display typography on the web.

name: Moulpali
classification_notes: Moulpali is a clean, low-contrast sans-serif designed primarily for the Khmer script, offering legible and unpretentious letterforms suited to body and UI use. The Latin glyphs are functional and harmonious with the Khmer.
summary: A clean, functional Khmer sans-serif designed for legibility in body text and digital interfaces.
description: Moulpali prioritises readability for Khmer script at text sizes, with even stroke weights and open letterforms that hold up well on screen. Its single weight limits typographic range but its neutral, unpretentious character makes it a reliable workhorse for Khmer-language web content. The accompanying Latin glyphs are competent and sit comfortably alongside the Khmer.

name: Mountains of Christmas
classification_notes: Mountains of Christmas is a festive display typeface with chunky, rounded letterforms adorned with seasonal motifs and a deliberately jolly, childlike quality. It is explicitly designed for Christmas-themed applications.
summary: A cheerful, overtly festive display face designed exclusively for Christmas-themed decoration and seasonal graphics.
description: Mountains of Christmas leans fully into seasonal novelty, with rounded, candy-coloured-ready letterforms and a jovial disposition suited to holiday cards, seasonal posters, and festive packaging. Its two weights offer minimal range, and the face has essentially no utility outside Christmas contexts. Within that narrow brief it delivers exactly what is needed with uncomplicated good cheer.

name: Mouse Memoirs
classification_notes: Mouse Memoirs is a narrow, all-caps-leaning display sans with a retro mid-century American feel, recalling hand-lettered signage and cartoon title cards. Letterforms are compact with slightly uneven, informal construction.
summary: A compact retro display sans with mid-century American cartoon and signage influences, best used at headline scale.
description: Mouse Memoirs evokes the hand-lettered title cards and fairground signage of 1950s American popular culture, making it effective for nostalgic branding, retro posters, and playful editorial headlines. Its narrow proportions allow for dense, punchy headline settings without excessive width. The single weight and narrow stylistic range limit it to decorative display use.

name: Mozilla Headline
classification_notes: Mozilla Headline is a confident, clean geometric sans-serif developed for Mozilla's brand identity, featuring a wide weight range from ExtraLight to Bold with consistent, rationalist letterforms. It has a modern, tech-forward character with subtle humanist touches that prevent sterility.
summary: Mozilla's own geometric sans, built for brand headlines with a strong weight range and a confident, tech-forward presence.
description: Designed as part of Mozilla's visual identity, Mozilla Headline brings systematic, geometric construction to a versatile type family spanning six weights from ExtraLight to Bold. It is well-suited to editorial layouts, tech branding, and campaign headlines where clarity and authority are required. The broad weight range enables nuanced typographic hierarchy within a single, coherent typeface.

name: Mozilla Text
classification_notes: Mozilla Text is a clean, highly legible humanist sans-serif designed specifically for extended reading on screen. It features open apertures, generous x-height, and subtle humanist details that aid readability across a wide range of weights.
summary: A screen-optimised humanist sans built for sustained reading comfort across digital interfaces.
description: Mozilla Text prioritises legibility above all, with open letterforms, a sturdy x-height, and carefully balanced stroke contrast suited to body copy and UI environments. Its range from ExtraLight to Bold makes it versatile across editorial and interface design. The extended Latin coverage ensures broad multilingual utility in digital products.

name: Mr Bedfort
classification_notes: Mr Bedfort is a flowing formal script with looping ascenders and descenders drawn in the copperplate tradition. The letterforms are elegant but slightly restrained compared to more flamboyant display scripts.
summary: A composed formal script with copperplate sensibility, suited to refined branding and stationery.
description: Mr Bedfort channels the elegance of traditional copperplate penmanship with graceful loops and consistent slant. It works well for wedding stationery, boutique packaging, and logotypes where a touch of classic formality is required. Being a single-weight script, it is best reserved for display-size applications rather than running text.

name: Mr Dafoe
classification_notes: Mr Dafoe is a bold, energetic brush-influenced script with dramatic thick-thin contrast and exaggerated swash characteristics. It reads as confident and expressive at large display sizes.
summary: A bold, swash-heavy display script with the swagger of a mid-century showcard letterform.
description: Mr Dafoe makes a strong impression through its pronounced stroke contrast, generous slant, and sweeping terminals that give it a theatrical, vintage-inflected character. It is well suited to poster headlines, logotypes, and packaging where a sense of confident personality is the goal. At smaller sizes the detail collapses, so it is strictly a display-use typeface.

name: Mr De Haviland
classification_notes: Mr De Haviland is an elaborate formal script with highly decorative capitals and flowing lowercase letterforms rooted in Victorian copperplate tradition. The flourishes are prominent and lend the face a distinctly ornamental character.
summary: A lavishly flourished formal script drawing on Victorian copperplate traditions for ornamental display use.
description: Mr De Haviland's sweeping capital letters and intricately looped ascenders make it one of the more decorative formal scripts available on Google Fonts. It suits luxury branding, invitations, and fashion editorial where overt elegance is the desired tone. Restraint is advised when pairing it with other decorative elements, as the letterforms are already highly ornamental.

name: Mrs Saint Delafield
classification_notes: Mrs Saint Delafield is a refined, high-contrast formal script with confident strokes and graceful connections between letters. It sits in the English roundhand tradition, balancing flourish with legibility.
summary: An assured English roundhand script that balances decorative flourish with genuine legibility at display sizes.
description: Mrs Saint Delafield carries the hallmarks of refined penmanship — consistent slant, well-judged thick-thin contrast, and letters that connect with natural rhythm. It performs well in upmarket branding, wine labels, and editorial contexts where a sense of handcrafted elegance is needed. Like most formal scripts, it is unsuitable for body text and should be used sparingly at headline scale.

name: Mrs Sheppards
classification_notes: Mrs Sheppards is a loose, expressive script with an informal ink-drawn quality that distinguishes it from more rigid formal scripts. Letterforms vary in weight and rhythm, giving it an authentic handwritten feel.
summary: A lively hand-drawn script with irregular rhythm and ink-like authenticity that sets it apart from polished formal scripts.
description: Mrs Sheppards mimics the natural variation of a pen moving quickly across paper, with strokes that swell and taper in an organic, imperfect manner. This makes it a strong choice for artisanal branding, café signage, and packaging where a handcrafted, approachable personality is the goal. Its informality means it pairs better with sturdy sans-serifs than with other ornamental faces.

name: Ms Madi
classification_notes: Ms Madi is a contemporary loose brush script with flowing connections, natural ink-like variation in stroke weight, and an easy, modern calligraphic sensibility. Vietnamese script support broadens its utility significantly.
summary: A fluid modern brush script with broad language support including Vietnamese, offering an accessible calligraphic aesthetic.
description: Ms Madi strikes a balance between the spontaneity of brush lettering and the consistency required of a digital typeface, with smooth connections and well-judged stroke variation. Its Vietnamese support is a notable practical advantage over many comparable scripts on Google Fonts. It suits social media graphics, lifestyle branding, and editorial headlines where warmth and motion are priorities.

name: Mukta
classification_notes: Mukta is a humanist sans-serif designed for simultaneous use across Devanagari and Latin scripts, with forms carefully harmonised across both writing systems. It offers an unusually wide weight range for a Devanagari typeface on Google Fonts.
summary: A harmoniously designed humanist sans covering Devanagari and Latin with an impressive seven-weight range for multilingual body text use.
description: Mukta was developed with a clear focus on bringing typographic parity to Devanagari-script users, offering a clean and legible companion to its Latin counterpart across all seven weights. The letterforms are open and friendly without being overtly playful, making Mukta well suited to editorial, UI, and extended digital reading in Hindi, Marathi, and other Devanagari-using languages. It is one of the most practically useful multilingual sans-serifs available on Google Fonts for South Asian digital publishing.

name: Mukta Mahee
classification_notes: Mukta Mahee is part of the Mukta superfamily, designed to harmonise Gurmukhi script with Latin. The letterforms are clean and functional, optimised for readability across both scripts at text sizes.
summary: A multi-script workhorse that pairs Gurmukhi and Latin in a clean, readable humanist sans.
description: Mukta Mahee is part of the broader Mukta superfamily, each member designed to support a different Indic script alongside Latin. The Gurmukhi and Latin letterforms share consistent proportions and stroke weight, making it well-suited to bilingual publishing, apps, and long-form digital text. Its generous range of weights gives designers practical flexibility without sacrificing cohesion.

name: Mukta Malar
classification_notes: Mukta Malar extends the Mukta superfamily to Tamil script, maintaining the family's characteristic open, even-stroked construction. The Tamil and Latin letterforms are carefully balanced for mixed-script typesetting.
summary: A Tamil-and-Latin humanist sans built for harmonious bilingual typesetting across digital and print.
description: Mukta Malar is the Tamil-script member of the Mukta superfamily, sharing the same design philosophy of optical consistency across scripts. The letterforms are open and legible, making the family a reliable choice for Tamil-language digital interfaces, publications, and educational materials. Seven weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold provide broad utility for both text and display use.

name: Mukta Vaani
classification_notes: Mukta Vaani adds Gujarati script support to the Mukta superfamily, with letterforms calibrated to sit harmoniously alongside Latin text. The design prioritises legibility and neutral tone over stylistic expression.
summary: The Gujarati-script member of the Mukta family, offering dependable bilingual text performance.
description: Mukta Vaani covers Gujarati and Latin scripts within the cohesive Mukta superfamily framework, ensuring consistent optical weight and x-height across both writing systems. It is a practical choice for Gujarati-language websites, apps, and printed matter where Latin text must coexist without visual conflict. The seven-weight range makes it adaptable from fine body copy to medium-weight headings.

name: Mulish
classification_notes: Mulish is a minimalist geometric sans with low contrast and clean, near-circular letterforms influenced by the classic grotesque tradition. It strikes a balance between geometric purity and approachable warmth, making it versatile across UI and editorial contexts.
summary: A refined, minimalist geometric sans that sits comfortably between cold geometry and humanist warmth.
description: Mulish draws on geometric sans traditions while softening its forms enough to remain friendly and readable at small sizes, making it popular for product interfaces and digital editorial work. Its near-neutral character means it rarely dominates a layout, functioning well as a reliable supporting typeface for branding and UI systems. The broad weight range, including a Black weight, gives designers room to create clear typographic hierarchy.

name: Murecho
classification_notes: Murecho is a clean, low-contrast geometric sans with a notably large x-height and open apertures, supporting Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek scripts. Its even, modular construction gives it a neutral, functional character suited to screen use.
summary: A low-contrast geometric sans covering Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek with a functional, screen-friendly character.
description: Murecho offers broad multilingual coverage in a clean geometric package, making it a practical choice for international digital products and interfaces requiring Cyrillic or Greek alongside Latin. Its large x-height and open apertures maintain legibility at small sizes, where many geometric typefaces struggle. The nine weights from Thin to Black provide ample range for building typographic systems.

name: MuseoModerno
classification_notes: MuseoModerno is a distinctive geometric display typeface with modular, architectural letterforms featuring pronounced circular elements and a futuristic sensibility. The design is unapologetically decorative, built for large-size use in branding and headlines.
summary: A bold, modular geometric display face with a futuristic, architectural character that commands attention at large sizes.
description: MuseoModerno combines circular geometric construction with a self-consciously contemporary aesthetic, making it well-suited to tech branding, event identities, and editorial headlines where personality is a priority. Its modular letterforms carry a retro-futurist edge that works particularly well in poster and packaging contexts. The variable weight axis gives it flexibility that pure novelty display faces rarely offer.

name: My Soul
classification_notes: My Soul is a flowing, high-contrast display script with generous loops and an elegant calligraphic character. The letterforms have a personal, handwritten quality designed for decorative single-weight use.
summary: An elegant high-contrast display script with a flowing, calligraphic quality suited to decorative headline use.
description: My Soul draws on formal cursive calligraphy traditions, offering generous swashes and fluid connections between letters that make it feel personal and refined at large sizes. It is best deployed sparingly in wedding stationery, lifestyle branding, social media graphics, and decorative headlines rather than extended text. As a single-weight script it offers little flexibility, limiting its utility to purely expressive applications.

name: Mynerve
classification_notes: Mynerve is an informal handwritten script with irregular, naturalistic letterforms that evoke casual pen writing. Its slightly uneven baseline and relaxed stroke quality give it an authentically hand-drawn character.
summary: A casual, naturalistic handwritten script with an authentically informal quality and Greek script support.
description: Mynerve mimics the loose energy of everyday pen handwriting, with slightly irregular letterforms and relaxed connections that avoid the over-polished look of formal calligraphic scripts. Its inclusion of Greek script alongside Latin makes it a rare choice for informal multilingual design work. It suits social media graphics, lifestyle branding, and packaging where an approachable, human touch is the priority.

name: Mystery Quest
classification_notes: Mystery Quest features irregular, hand-drawn letterforms with a spooky, playful character reminiscent of Halloween and gothic comic aesthetics. The uneven strokes and quirky details make it suited exclusively to novelty display contexts.
summary: A novelty display typeface with spooky, hand-drawn charm aimed squarely at Halloween and gothic-themed projects.
description: Mystery Quest leans heavily into its single-use personality, offering wobbly, irregular letterforms that evoke mystery and camp horror. It works best at large sizes for posters, game titles, or seasonal event branding. Its charm is narrow but effective within that niche.

name: NTR
classification_notes: NTR is a clean, functional sans-serif designed to support the Telugu script alongside Latin, with open, legible forms that prioritise readability at text sizes. Its design is understated and workhorse in nature.
summary: A modest, readable sans-serif built primarily to serve Telugu and Latin scripts together in digital environments.
description: NTR fills a practical gap by providing a single typeface that handles Telugu and Latin text with consistent visual weight and rhythm. Its forms are plain and unassuming, making it suitable for body text in multilingual web and app contexts. It offers little personality but delivers solid functional utility.

name: Nabla
classification_notes: Nabla is a striking colour font built on the COLRv1 specification, featuring a three-dimensional isometric block structure with customisable gradient depth and perspective. It pushes the boundaries of what a typeface can do technically and visually.
summary: A technically groundbreaking COLRv1 colour font that renders each letter as a fully three-dimensional isometric block with customisable depth and gradients.
description: Nabla exploits modern colour font technology to produce letterforms that appear physically extruded, with gradient-controlled lighting effects adjustable via font variation axes. It supports Latin and Cyrillic Extended scripts, broadening its reach beyond purely Latin display use. The typeface is best deployed at large sizes for headlines, posters, and digital campaigns where its visual spectacle can be fully appreciated.

name: Namdhinggo
classification_notes: Namdhinggo is a carefully crafted serif designed specifically to support the Limbu script alongside Latin and Latin Extended, with multiple weights enabling typographic hierarchy in underserved language contexts. Its design balances cultural authenticity with contemporary legibility.
summary: A multi-weight serif typeface offering rare, high-quality support for the Limbu script alongside Latin, filling a significant gap in digital typography for that writing system.
description: Namdhinggo provides five weights from Regular to ExtraBold, giving designers and publishers genuine flexibility when setting Limbu-language text — a script with extremely limited font coverage. Its letterforms are humanist in spirit, with warm, readable proportions that serve editorial and book contexts well. This is a genuinely useful contribution to multilingual type on the web.

name: Nanum Brush Script
classification_notes: Nanum Brush Script captures the energy of a freely applied ink brush, with expressive, spontaneous strokes that reflect traditional Korean calligraphic practice. The result is warm and distinctly handmade in character.
summary: An expressive brush script that brings the spontaneity of Korean ink calligraphy to both Hangul and Latin characters.
description: Nanum Brush Script is part of the widely used Nanum family developed for Korean digital typography, and its brush variant conveys genuine calligraphic energy. The strokes vary naturally in weight and direction, lending warmth and personality to display-sized text. It suits branding, editorial headlines, and cultural projects where a Korean or East Asian aesthetic is appropriate.

name: Nanum Gothic
classification_notes: Nanum Gothic is a clean, screen-optimised sans-serif designed for comfortable reading of Korean Hangul alongside Latin text, with multiple weights supporting typographic hierarchy. It is one of the most widely used Korean web fonts owing to its neutral, legible character.
summary: One of the most popular Korean web fonts, Nanum Gothic delivers clean, neutral sans-serif letterforms optimised for on-screen reading of Hangul and Latin alike.
description: Nanum Gothic was developed by Sandoll Communications for Naver and has become a default choice for Korean digital publishing due to its excellent screen performance and neutral personality. Three weights — Regular, Bold, and ExtraBold — provide enough range for most UI and editorial hierarchies. Its broad adoption makes it a reliable, safe choice for any Korean-language web or app project.

name: Nanum Gothic Coding
classification_notes: Nanum Gothic Coding is a monospaced typeface designed to render Korean Hangul and Latin characters at equal widths, making it functional for code editors and terminal environments that must handle mixed-script content. Its forms are clear and utilitarian.
summary: A monospaced coding font that handles Korean Hangul and Latin characters at consistent widths, making it a practical choice for mixed-script development environments.
description: Nanum Gothic Coding addresses the specific challenge of writing Korean code or comments within editors that require fixed-width glyph spacing across both Hangul and Latin. Its letterforms are simple and functional, prioritising clarity over personality. It is a niche but genuinely useful tool for Korean-speaking developers.

name: Nanum Myeongjo
classification_notes: Nanum Myeongjo adapts the classical Myeongjo (Mincho) serif tradition — characterised by horizontal strokes with triangular terminals and strong stroke contrast — for screen use across Korean and Latin scripts. It carries a formal, literary quality well-suited to long-form reading.
summary: A refined Korean serif in the Myeongjo tradition, offering strong stroke contrast and elegant letterforms suited to editorial and long-form digital reading.
description: Nanum Myeongjo brings the formal authority of traditional Korean book typography to the screen, with three weights that serve everything from body text to headline use. Its high stroke contrast and precise terminal details give it a classical, literary feel appropriate for news, publishing, and cultural platforms. Like its Nanum Gothic sibling, it benefits from widespread adoption and thorough Korean glyph coverage.

name: Nanum Pen Script
classification_notes: A casual, brush-drawn script designed specifically for Korean and Latin text, mimicking the look of handwriting with a felt-tip or brush pen. Letterforms are loose and energetic with natural stroke variation.
summary: A lively brush script that captures the spontaneity of Korean handwriting with genuine warmth.
description: Nanum Pen Script replicates the fluid, uneven quality of writing with a felt-tip pen, making it feel personal and immediate. It works well for informal headings, social content, and any context where approachability matters. Its dual support for Korean and Latin scripts makes it particularly valuable for bilingual Korean design work.

name: Narnoor
classification_notes: Designed primarily to support the Gunjala Gondi script alongside Latin, with clean, upright letterforms suited to text and UI use. A functional, script-support-first design with limited stylistic ambition in its Latin component.
summary: A utilitarian sans-serif built to serve the underrepresented Gunjala Gondi script alongside Latin text.
description: Narnoor is a purposeful type project aimed at bringing digital support to the Gunjala Gondi script, spoken by communities in central India. The Latin component is clean and functional, designed to harmonise rather than dominate. It is best used in contexts where correct script rendering takes priority over stylistic expression.

name: Nata Sans
classification_notes: A wide-ranging geometric sans with a broad nine-weight range and support for Cyrillic and extended Latin, featuring clean, neutral letterforms with minimal eccentricity. Constructed rather than drawn, with consistent stroke widths and open apertures.
summary: A comprehensive geometric sans covering nine weights with solid Cyrillic support and dependable neutrality.
description: Nata Sans positions itself as a workhorse geometric sans, offering an unusually complete weight range from Thin to Black for versatile typographic hierarchies. Its Cyrillic coverage makes it a practical choice for Russian and Eastern European digital products. The neutral, constructed character keeps it adaptable across branding, UI, and editorial contexts.

name: National Park
classification_notes: Inspired by the carved and routed lettering found on American national park signage, featuring slightly flared stroke terminals and an outdoor, utilitarian character. Letterforms have a distinctive open, airy quality with subtle vintage inflections.
summary: A signage-inspired sans evoking the carved park lettering of mid-century American national parks.
description: National Park draws directly from the routed wooden and stone signage aesthetic developed by the US National Park Service, giving it an unmistakable outdoor and heritage quality. The slightly flared terminals and confident stroke weight make it well-suited to branding, posters, and editorial headlines where a sense of place and craft is desired. It carries enough distinctiveness to stand out without tipping into overt novelty.

name: Neonderthaw
classification_notes: A connected display script with neon-sign aesthetics, featuring glowing, rounded letterforms that mimic the continuous bent-tube quality of neon lighting. Designed for atmosphere and novelty rather than extended reading.
summary: A neon-tube script that convincingly evokes the glow and continuous flow of bent-glass signage.
description: Neonderthaw leans fully into its neon-sign concept, with rounded, unbroken strokes that suggest a single continuous tube of light. It is a one-mood typeface, best deployed in short bursts for posters, event graphics, or any context calling for retro nightlife atmosphere. Versatility is not the point; committed character is.

name: Nerko One
classification_notes: A chunky, hand-lettered casual script with rounded, bouncy letterforms and a playful, informal energy. Strokes are thick and confident with a felt-pen quality, making it legible at display sizes.
summary: A bold, rounded casual script with an honest hand-lettered friendliness suited to playful branding.
description: Nerko One has the cheerful confidence of marker-written lettering, with thick rounded strokes that read well as headlines and short display copy. It suits children's products, informal food and beverage branding, and any project where approachability is a primary concern. The single weight limits its versatility, but within its register it performs with conviction.

name: Neucha
classification_notes: A casual, upright handwriting face with Cyrillic and Latin support, mimicking natural ballpoint or pencil handwriting with slightly rough, textured strokes. Letterforms are disconnected and informal, sitting close to actual handwriting.
summary: An unpretentious handwriting script with genuine Cyrillic character and a relaxed, pencil-written texture.
description: Neucha reproduces the look of casual Russian handwriting with convincing irregularity and warmth, making it one of the more authentic Cyrillic script options on Google Fonts. Its slightly rough stroke quality adds texture and personality at display sizes, where it works well for informal headers, quotes, and editorial accents. It does not attempt to serve body text, and is all the more honest for it.

name: Neuton
classification_notes: A contemporary old-style serif with classical proportions, moderate contrast, and bracketed serifs that draw on Renaissance type traditions. Designed for efficient text setting with a quiet elegance across an unusually wide weight range.
summary: A refined old-style serif with classical proportions and a wide weight range that earns its place in long-form text.
description: Neuton brings together Renaissance serif traditions with practical screen-era spacing, making it a solid choice for editorial design, book-style web layouts, and any project requiring confident text typography. The weight range from ExtraLight to ExtraBold offers genuine compositional flexibility for building clear typographic hierarchies. It avoids fashionable quirks in favour of quiet, lasting readability.

name: New Amsterdam
classification_notes: A tall, narrow sans-serif with strong vertical stress and minimal stroke contrast. Designed for compact headline use with a modern, utilitarian character.
summary: A strikingly tall condensed sans-serif built for maximum impact in tight horizontal spaces.
description: New Amsterdam occupies very little horizontal space while commanding attention through its height and clean geometry. It suits newspaper headlines, posters, and any context where vertical drama is needed without sacrificing legibility. Its single regular weight limits versatility but makes its purpose clear.

name: New Rocker
classification_notes: A heavily stylised display face drawing on blackletter and rock music aesthetics, with sharp, angular forms and exaggerated decorative strokes. Strictly a novelty typeface.
summary: An aggressive blackletter-inspired display face aimed squarely at rock, metal, and counterculture aesthetics.
description: New Rocker channels the visual language of heavy metal album artwork and underground posters, with jagged strokes and dramatic angular terminals. Its application is essentially limited to genre-specific branding, event flyers, and band merchandise. Beyond that narrow context it quickly becomes incongruous.

name: New Tegomin
classification_notes: A serif designed to harmonise Japanese and Latin scripts, with gentle, calligraphic-influenced letterforms and moderate stroke contrast. The Latin component carries a quiet, oldstyle character complementing the Japanese glyphs.
summary: A carefully considered bilingual serif that brings Latin and Japanese scripts into quiet, harmonious coexistence.
description: New Tegomin addresses the genuine challenge of pairing Latin and Japanese type without either script feeling like an afterthought. The letterforms are restrained and bookish, making it well suited to literary publishing, academic documents, and long-form web content in both scripts. Its single weight restricts its flexibility in complex typographic hierarchies.

name: News Cycle
classification_notes: A straightforward American gothic sans-serif with open apertures and minimal stylistic flourish, designed with legibility in mind for text-heavy editorial contexts. Influenced by the condensed grotesque tradition of newspaper typography.
summary: A no-nonsense American gothic sans-serif with Cyrillic and Greek support, built for reliable editorial service.
description: News Cycle takes its cues from the condensed grotesque faces that dominated newspaper printing for much of the twentieth century. Its open counters and even spacing make it a dependable choice for body text on screen and in print at moderate sizes. Cyrillic and Greek coverage extends its reach across multilingual publications.

name: Newsreader
classification_notes: A high-contrast text serif with sharp, refined details and a broad weight range, designed explicitly for extended reading on screen at small sizes. It draws on classical editorial serif traditions while optimising for digital rendering.
summary: A polished, high-contrast text serif engineered for demanding editorial and long-form reading on screen.
description: Newsreader was developed by Production Type with a clear focus on screen legibility across a wide range of sizes and weights, from delicate extralight to punchy extrabold. Its sharp serifs and generous x-height give it a confident editorial presence suited to news platforms, magazines, and digital publishing. Vietnamese script support adds useful coverage for Southeast Asian markets.

name: Niconne
classification_notes: A casual, flowing script with lively, slightly irregular letterforms that suggest handwritten informality without sacrificing basic legibility. Stroke contrast is moderate and connections between letters are smooth.
summary: A warm, unpretentious informal script that conveys relaxed handwritten charm without tipping into illegibility.
description: Niconne strikes a balance between the looseness of casual handwriting and the consistency required for functional typography. It works well for greeting cards, artisan branding, food packaging, and any context requiring approachable personality. Its single weight means it cannot carry typographic hierarchy on its own.

name: Niramit
classification_notes: A clean, humanist sans-serif with Thai and Latin support, featuring open letterforms and a full weight range that makes it genuinely useful for both display and body text. The Thai glyphs are carefully harmonised with the Latin.
summary: A well-rounded humanist sans-serif offering strong Thai and Latin coverage across a useful weight range.
description: Niramit is one of the more thorough attempts on Google Fonts to serve Thai and Latin typography with genuine parity between scripts. Its open, friendly letterforms and six weights make it a credible choice for interfaces, editorial design, and corporate communications targeting Thai-speaking audiences. The Latin component holds up respectably in text settings, though it lacks the distinctiveness of purpose-built Latin typefaces.

name: Nixie One
classification_notes: A quirky, geometric display face with slightly rounded, open letterforms and a distinct retro-technological character. Its forms suggest mid-century scientific or electronic instrument typography.
summary: A characterful geometric display face with a retro-technological edge that recalls mid-century scientific typography.
description: Nixie One takes its name and spirit from the illuminated Nixie tube displays used in vintage electronic instruments, giving it an inherently nostalgic and technical personality. Its open counters and slightly idiosyncratic letterforms make it memorable at large display sizes for headlines, branding, and themed editorial work. A single weight limits its flexibility, but within its niche it is distinctive.

name: Nobile
classification_notes: Nobile features slightly humanist proportions with a sturdy, upright construction suited to screen use. Its clean strokes and modest contrast give it a dependable, readable quality across weights.
summary: A solid, screen-optimised humanist sans with broad Cyrillic support and reliable legibility across weights.
description: Nobile was designed with digital clarity in mind, making it a dependable choice for interfaces, editorial body copy, and multilingual web projects. Its even spacing and moderate proportions hold up well at smaller sizes, while the bold weight carries enough weight for headlines. The Cyrillic extension broadens its practical appeal considerably.

name: Nokora
classification_notes: Nokora is a Khmer-primary typeface with a full Latin companion, designed for legibility in Southeast Asian language contexts. Its wide weight range from Thin to Black makes it versatile for both text and display use.
summary: A comprehensive Khmer and Latin sans designed primarily to serve Cambodian script with genuine typographic range.
description: Nokora fills an important gap in multilingual type by pairing a well-considered Khmer design with a functional Latin companion across nine weights. It is best suited to digital publishing, educational materials, and interfaces serving Khmer-speaking audiences. The breadth of the weight range makes it unusually flexible for a script-focused family.

name: Norican
classification_notes: Norican is a flowing, upright script with bold, confident strokes reminiscent of sign-painting traditions. Its letterforms have strong contrast and a slightly casual, hand-lettered quality.
summary: A bold, confident upright script with sign-painting energy and strong contrast, well suited to display use.
description: Norican draws on lettering traditions associated with commercial sign-painting, giving it a robust, characterful presence at large sizes. It works well for logotypes, packaging headlines, and editorial display settings where a hand-crafted quality is desired. As a single-weight Regular, its application is best kept to short, prominent settings.

name: Nosifer
classification_notes: Nosifer is an extreme decorative display face evoking dripping blood or melting forms, firmly in the horror novelty tradition. It is entirely one-trick, built for shock value rather than typographic sophistication.
summary: A horror-themed novelty display font with dripping letterforms designed purely for dramatic, themed settings.
description: Nosifer leans hard into Halloween and horror aesthetics, with forms that mimic dripping liquid or melting material. Its use cases are narrow: event posters, horror-themed branding, and seasonal graphics where subtlety is actively unwanted. Outside those contexts it has little typographic utility.

name: Notable
classification_notes: Notable is a single-weight, wide display sans with strong, bold letterforms and a somewhat geometric construction. Its limited weight range restricts versatility, positioning it purely as a headline or titling face.
summary: A wide, bold single-weight display sans built for strong headlines and short-form titling.
description: Notable's expanded proportions and confident weight give it a commanding presence in headline and poster contexts. It suits branding, titling, and display settings where impact matters more than nuance. The single-weight limitation means it functions best as part of a mixed-family pairing rather than a standalone workhorse.

name: Nothing You Could Do
classification_notes: Nothing You Could Do is a casual, loose handwriting script with irregular baseline and natural pen-like strokes. It mimics authentic handwritten notes with a spontaneous, unpolished quality.
summary: A loose, casual handwriting script that convincingly mimics an authentic handwritten note.
description: Nothing You Could Do captures the informal energy of genuine handwriting, with irregular letterforms and natural variation that feel spontaneous rather than mechanical. It suits greeting cards, personal branding, social media graphics, and any context where warmth and informality are the goal. As a single-weight script, it is best used sparingly at display sizes.

name: Noticia Text
classification_notes: Noticia Text is a newspaper-influenced serif with moderate contrast and sturdy serifs designed for sustained reading in print and on screen. Its robust construction and ink-trap-influenced details reflect a clear editorial heritage.
summary: A sturdy, newspaper-inspired text serif engineered for comfortable extended reading in editorial contexts.
description: Noticia Text draws on the traditions of press typography, with letterforms designed to maintain clarity under demanding reproduction conditions. It is a natural fit for long-form editorial content, news websites, and print publications where readability at small sizes is paramount. Vietnamese language support adds meaningful utility for multilingual publishing projects.

name: Noto Color Emoji
classification_notes: Noto Color Emoji is a full-colour emoji font providing comprehensive Unicode emoji coverage in a consistent, cross-platform style. It is a functional utility typeface rather than a traditional type design.
summary: Google's comprehensive colour emoji font, providing consistent full-colour Unicode emoji across platforms.
description: Noto Color Emoji is part of Google's broader Noto project aimed at eliminating tofu — blank boxes for unsupported characters — across all writing systems. It provides reliable, consistent emoji rendering for web and application environments where system emoji may be unavailable or inconsistent. Its value is purely functional and universal rather than aesthetic.

name: Noto Emoji
classification_notes: A variable-weight emoji font providing standardised Unicode emoji glyphs in a consistent, outlined style. Designed to integrate with the broader Noto family for harmonious cross-script text.
summary: Noto Emoji provides a clean, weight-variable set of outline emoji glyphs built to harmonise with the rest of the Noto superfamily.
description: Unlike colour emoji fonts, Noto Emoji renders glyphs as monochromatic outlines, making it suitable for print and contexts where colour fonts are unsupported. Its variable weight axis allows glyphs to match the typographic weight of surrounding text. It is most useful for documents, presentations, or interfaces requiring consistent, platform-neutral emoji rendering.

name: Noto Kufi Arabic
classification_notes: A geometric, rectilinear Arabic typeface in the Kufi tradition, offering an unusually wide weight range from Thin to Black. Its angular, modular construction gives it a modern, structured appearance.
summary: Noto Kufi Arabic applies geometric Kufi calligraphic principles to a broad nine-weight variable system suited to modern Arabic typography.
description: The Kufi style's angular letterforms lend themselves to display and headline use, where their structural clarity reads well at large sizes. The extensive weight range makes the family versatile enough for branding, UI, and editorial Arabic content. Latin characters are included to support mixed-language documents cleanly.

name: Noto Music
classification_notes: A highly specialised symbol font providing Unicode-encoded music notation characters, including noteheads, clefs, rests, and accidentals. It is not a text typeface in the conventional sense.
summary: Noto Music is a single-weight symbol font covering Unicode music notation characters for use in plain-text and mixed-script documents.
description: Designed as part of Google's Noto project to eliminate tofu across all Unicode blocks, this font serves a narrow but important technical purpose. It enables correct rendering of music symbols in web pages, documents, and applications without requiring specialist notation software. It is not intended for conventional typesetting and has no real design character beyond functional Unicode compliance.

name: Noto Naskh Arabic
classification_notes: A calligraphically rooted Arabic typeface in the Naskh style, the most widely used script style for modern Arabic text setting. It features clear contrast, open counters, and strong text-setting legibility.
summary: Noto Naskh Arabic is a dependable, calligraphically informed Naskh-style typeface built for extended Arabic body text on screen and in print.
description: The Naskh style's balanced proportions and moderate stroke contrast make this an excellent choice for long-form reading in Arabic, whether in news, publishing, or digital interfaces. Its four weights provide sufficient range for editorial hierarchies without overpowering the text. Latin characters are included for clean bilingual typesetting.

name: Noto Nastaliq Urdu
classification_notes: A complex, calligraphic typeface implementing the diagonal Nastaliq script style, the traditional prestige style for Urdu writing. The letterforms cascade diagonally from right to left with significant vertical overlap between clusters.
summary: Noto Nastaliq Urdu faithfully renders the demanding Nastaliq calligraphic style, the culturally preferred script form for Urdu publishing and literature.
description: Nastaliq is among the most technically complex scripts to implement digitally, requiring thousands of contextual glyph variants and precise vertical stacking rules. This font is significant for Urdu speakers who have historically had limited high-quality digital options in their preferred script style. It is best used for Urdu-language publishing, cultural content, and literary contexts where Naskh would feel inappropriate.

name: Noto Rashi Hebrew
classification_notes: A typeface based on the Rashi semi-cursive Hebrew script, historically used for rabbinic commentary and distinct from square Hebrew type. It carries strong scholarly and religious text associations.
summary: Noto Rashi Hebrew digitises the distinctive semi-cursive Rashi script style used for centuries in Jewish religious and scholarly texts.
description: The Rashi style derives from Sephardic cursive Hebrew and has been used since the early days of Hebrew printing to set commentary alongside square-script primary texts. Its nine-weight range is unusually broad for such a specialised style, offering flexibility for contemporary applications beyond traditional religious publishing. It is the appropriate choice for Talmudic content, academic Judaica, and any context requiring authentic Rashi-style Hebrew.

name: Noto Sans
classification_notes: The flagship sans-serif of the vast Noto superfamily, designed to provide harmonious text rendering across Cyrillic, Devanagari, and Latin scripts at all weights. Its humanist proportions prioritise legibility and neutrality over stylistic personality.
summary: Noto Sans is a reliable, neutral humanist sans-serif covering multiple scripts as part of Google's mission to support all of Unicode without rendering gaps.
description: Designed to be invisible in the best sense, Noto Sans prioritises consistent metrics and optical harmony across its many supported scripts over any distinctive character. It functions as a solid default for multilingual interfaces, documents, and web applications where broad script coverage is essential. Its wide weight range ensures adequate typographic hierarchy without the need to mix families.

name: Noto Sans Adlam
classification_notes: A Unicode-compliant sans-serif supporting the Adlam script used by the Fulani people of West Africa, alongside Latin. The letterforms are designed to reflect the relatively recent, codified nature of the Adlam script.
summary: Noto Sans Adlam provides one of the few high-quality digital typefaces for the Adlam script, supporting literacy and digital access for Fulani speakers.
description: The Adlam script was created in the 1980s and formally encoded in Unicode, making quality digital type for it a relatively recent priority. This family fills a critical gap for communities across Guinea, Mali, Nigeria, and surrounding regions who use Adlam for everyday writing. Its four weights and clean construction make it functional for both screen reading and document typesetting in Fulani-language contexts.

name: Noto Sans Adlam Unjoined
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to render the Adlam script in its unjoined form with clean, legible strokes. Supports the Fula language written in the Adlam alphabet alongside Latin.
summary: A clean, functional sans-serif covering the unjoined Adlam script, part of Google's ambitious Noto universal type system.
description: Noto Sans Adlam Unjoined renders the Adlam alphabet — developed for the Fulani people of West Africa — in a discrete, unjoined letterform style suited to digital reading. The design is neutral and workhorse in character, prioritising legibility over expression. It is best suited to body text, educational materials, and interfaces requiring Adlam script support.

name: Noto Sans Anatolian Hieroglyphs
classification_notes: An extremely specialised typeface rendering ancient Anatolian hieroglyphs in a regularised sans-serif structure, intended primarily for scholarly and reference use. Available in a single weight, reflecting the niche nature of the script.
summary: A highly specialised single-weight typeface encoding ancient Anatolian hieroglyphs for scholarly and archival digital use.
description: Noto Sans Anatolian Hieroglyphs exists to fulfil a very specific Unicode coverage requirement, rendering a script used in ancient Anatolia during the Bronze and Iron Ages. The glyphs are regularised into a consistent sans-serif framework to ensure digital reproducibility rather than historical authenticity. Its practical application is limited almost entirely to academic publishing, linguistics research, and digital archives.

name: Noto Sans Arabic
classification_notes: A comprehensive Arabic sans-serif with a broad weight range from Thin to Black, designed within the Noto system to harmonise with Latin text. The Arabic letterforms are clean and modular with strong stroke contrast management across weights.
summary: A versatile, full-range Arabic sans-serif designed for seamless bilingual use alongside Latin text in digital and print environments.
description: Noto Sans Arabic offers one of the most extensive weight ranges of any Arabic typeface freely available, spanning nine weights from Thin to Black. The letterforms are designed with optical consistency in mind, ensuring that Arabic and Latin text set together feel harmonious in weight and scale. It is well suited to interface design, editorial publishing, and any project requiring robust Arabic language support.

name: Noto Sans Armenian
classification_notes: Covers the Armenian script across a wide weight range within the Noto framework, with letterforms that reflect the distinctive angular and rounded qualities of Armenian. Designed for comfortable bilingual use with Latin text.
summary: A solid, wide-ranging Armenian sans-serif within the Noto family, built for reliable bilingual use across digital and print contexts.
description: Noto Sans Armenian provides nine weights of coverage for the Armenian script, one of the world's oldest alphabets still in use. The design respects the distinctive character of Armenian letterforms — with their characteristic curves and terminals — while integrating smoothly with the Latin Noto system. It is a dependable choice for publishing, signage, and digital products targeting Armenian-speaking audiences.

name: Noto Sans Avestan
classification_notes: A single-weight specialised typeface encoding the Avestan script, an ancient Iranian liturgical writing system. Follows the right-to-left orientation of the script with regularised sans-serif construction.
summary: A minimal, single-weight typeface encoding the ancient Avestan script for scholarly and liturgical digital publishing.
description: Noto Sans Avestan addresses the Unicode encoding of the Avestan script, used historically for transcribing Zoroastrian religious texts. Available in a single weight only, its application is inherently narrow, serving academic linguistics, historical research, and religious publishing. The design follows the Noto philosophy of neutral legibility, keeping ornamentation to a minimum.

name: Noto Sans Balinese
classification_notes: Covers the Balinese script — a complex abugida with numerous diacritical marks — in a clean sans-serif style with four weights. The design manages the vertical complexity of Balinese letterforms with careful spacing and stacking logic.
summary: A carefully constructed sans-serif typeface supporting the complex Balinese script across four weights for digital and print use.
description: The Balinese script poses significant typographic challenges due to its stacked diacritics and combining marks, and Noto Sans Balinese addresses these with measured engineering and clean design. Four weights — Regular through Bold — provide sufficient range for basic typographic hierarchy in Balinese-language content. Its most likely applications are in educational materials, cultural preservation projects, and regional government or media publishing in Bali.

name: Noto Sans Bamum
classification_notes: Covers the Bamum script — developed in Cameroon in the early twentieth century — across four weights in a clean, open sans-serif style. The Bamum characters have a distinctive geometric-syllabic structure that the design accommodates with care.
summary: A functional four-weight sans-serif encoding the Bamum script of Cameroon, serving cultural preservation and linguistic documentation.
description: Noto Sans Bamum provides digital support for a relatively recently created script, developed by Sultan Ibrahim Njoya of the Bamum kingdom in Cameroon around the turn of the twentieth century. The typeface is clean and neutral, designed to represent the syllabic characters with clarity rather than stylistic flourish. Primary use cases include cultural heritage documentation, academic linguistics, and regional publishing for the Bamum people.

name: Noto Sans Bassa Vah
classification_notes: Covers the Bassa Vah script — a script for the Bassa language of Liberia — in a clean, lightly geometric sans-serif across four weights. The design follows the Noto system's neutral approach, rendering a historically suppressed script with digital dignity.
summary: A respectful four-weight sans-serif rendering the Bassa Vah script of Liberia, supporting a language and writing system with a complex colonial history.
description: The Bassa Vah script was historically suppressed during the colonial period in Liberia and has undergone significant revival efforts in recent decades. Noto Sans Bassa Vah gives it a clean, legible digital form across four weights, making it usable for contemporary publishing and educational purposes. The typeface is a quiet but meaningful contribution to language and cultural preservation.

name: Noto Sans Batak
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to eliminate tofu (missing glyph boxes) across all scripts. Provides clean, neutral letterforms for the Batak script of North Sumatra.
summary: A no-frills sans-serif designed to bring consistent, legible coverage to the Batak script within Google's universal Noto type system.
description: Noto Sans Batak serves a highly specific linguistic purpose, providing reliable digital representation for the Batak script used by communities in North Sumatra, Indonesia. Its design prioritises legibility and neutrality over personality, making it a functional workhorse rather than an expressive choice. Best used in applications, operating systems, or web content requiring accurate Batak script rendering.

name: Noto Sans Bengali
classification_notes: A full-weight-range Noto sans-serif covering the Bengali script, with letterforms carefully constructed to honour the script's characteristic horizontal headline stroke (matra). Offers nine weights for typographic flexibility.
summary: A comprehensive nine-weight sans-serif providing robust, legible coverage of the Bengali script as part of Google's Noto family.
description: Noto Sans Bengali stands out within the Noto project for its unusually broad weight range, from Thin to Black, giving designers genuine typographic control when working with Bengali content. The letterforms respect the script's structural conventions while maintaining the neutrality that defines the Noto family. It is well suited to body text, UI design, and editorial work in Bengali-language digital products.

name: Noto Sans Bhaiksuki
classification_notes: Covers the Bhaiksuki script, a historical Brahmic script once used in parts of the Indian subcontinent. Single-weight offering focused on accurate Unicode representation.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif providing essential digital support for the rare historical Bhaiksuki script.
description: Noto Sans Bhaiksuki fulfils a narrow but important archival and scholarly purpose, enabling the digital encoding and display of a script with limited modern usage. Its design adheres to Noto's neutral aesthetic principles, prioritising correctness and legibility above all else. Primary use cases are academic publications, Unicode reference tools, and multilingual archival systems.

name: Noto Sans Brahmi
classification_notes: Supports the ancient Brahmi script, one of the oldest writing systems of South Asia and the ancestor of most modern Indic scripts. A single-weight scholarly and archival tool.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif enabling digital representation of the ancient Brahmi script, ancestor of most South and Southeast Asian writing systems.
description: Noto Sans Brahmi is a typeface of scholarly rather than commercial significance, designed to ensure accurate on-screen rendering of one of history's most consequential scripts. The letterforms follow Noto's consistent neutral framework while accommodating the structural demands of Brahmi's complex characters. It is most relevant for epigraphic databases, academic research platforms, and Unicode compliance testing.

name: Noto Sans Buginese
classification_notes: Provides clean sans-serif coverage for the Buginese script of South Sulawesi, Indonesia. Single-weight offering designed for accurate Unicode compliance.
summary: A focused single-weight sans-serif providing reliable digital support for the Buginese script of South Sulawesi within the Noto family.
description: Noto Sans Buginese addresses the digital typographic needs of a script used by one of Indonesia's major ethnic groups, the Bugis people. Consistent with all Noto fonts, it places legibility and Unicode accuracy ahead of stylistic expression. Its practical applications include localisation work, cultural preservation platforms, and operating system-level script support.

name: Noto Sans Buhid
classification_notes: Covers the Buhid script, a historic Brahmic script indigenous to Mindoro in the Philippines. Offered in a single weight, maintaining Noto's characteristic neutrality.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif supporting the Buhid script, one of the indigenous writing systems of the Philippines.
description: Noto Sans Buhid serves a small but culturally significant linguistic community, providing digital type support for a script that sits within the family of Philippine indigenous scripts. The design is spare and functional, consistent with Noto's pan-script mission of eliminating missing glyph rendering. It is best suited to cultural heritage projects, linguistic documentation, and Unicode-compliant publishing.

name: Noto Sans Canadian Aboriginal
classification_notes: A nine-weight Noto sans-serif covering Canadian Aboriginal Syllabics, a script used across a wide range of Indigenous Canadian languages. The generous weight range reflects the script's active use in contemporary communities.
summary: A full nine-weight sans-serif covering Canadian Aboriginal Syllabics, reflecting the script's continued vitality across Indigenous communities in Canada.
description: Noto Sans Canadian Aboriginal is among the more practically ambitious entries in the Noto project, offering a complete weight range that acknowledges the script's active role in education, government, and community publishing. The syllabic letterforms are rendered with the same neutral clarity that defines the Noto family, ensuring consistency across multilingual documents. It is well suited to government communications, educational materials, and digital products serving Indigenous Canadian audiences.

name: Noto Sans Carian
classification_notes: Supports the Carian script, an ancient Anatolian writing system used in what is now southwestern Turkey. A single-weight typeface of primarily scholarly interest.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif providing digital coverage for the ancient Carian script, used in historical Anatolia and of primarily academic significance today.
description: Noto Sans Carian is a specialised tool for scholars and institutions working with ancient Anatolian languages, enabling accurate on-screen and print rendering of a script deciphered only in the late twentieth century. The letterforms adhere to Noto's neutral design principles, adapted to the angular and unfamiliar character shapes of Carian. Primary applications include epigraphic databases, classical studies publishing, and Unicode reference documentation.

name: Noto Sans Caucasian Albanian
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to provide harmonious coverage of the rare Caucasian Albanian script alongside Latin. Clean, neutral letterforms prioritise legibility and consistency across scripts.
summary: A clean, neutral sans-serif providing essential digital support for the endangered Caucasian Albanian script.
description: Noto Sans Caucasian Albanian exists primarily as a scholarly and preservation resource, enabling the digital representation of a script used historically in the Caucasus region. Its neutral design ensures it pairs harmoniously with other Noto family members. Best suited to academic, archival, and linguistic documentation contexts.

name: Noto Sans Chakma
classification_notes: Designed as part of the Noto project to cover the Chakma script of Bangladesh and northeast India, with clean and legible neutral letterforms. Single weight reflects its functional, coverage-focused purpose.
summary: A functional, single-weight sans-serif delivering reliable digital support for the Chakma script.
description: Noto Sans Chakma addresses the need for consistent on-screen rendering of the Chakma script, spoken by communities across Bangladesh and India. Its design is deliberately restrained, prioritising clarity over personality. It serves linguistic, educational, and governmental digital applications above all else.

name: Noto Sans Cham
classification_notes: One of the more fully developed Noto script-specific families, offering a wide nine-weight range for the Cham script of Vietnam and Cambodia. Letterforms are clean and upright, balancing the rounded qualities of Cham with Noto's systematic neutrality.
summary: A well-developed, nine-weight sans-serif offering rare typographic flexibility for the Cham script.
description: Noto Sans Cham stands out within the Noto family for its broad weight range, making it genuinely useful for editorial hierarchy and emphasis in Cham-language publishing. The script's curved, flowing characters are rendered with clarity and consistency across all weights. It is well suited to digital media, educational materials, and cultural preservation projects for Cham-speaking communities.

name: Noto Sans Cherokee
classification_notes: A thoroughly developed Noto subfamily covering the Cherokee syllabary with a full nine-weight range, reflecting the relative vitality of the Cherokee writing community. Letterforms integrate smoothly with Latin characters while respecting the distinctive geometry of Cherokee.
summary: A comprehensive, nine-weight sans-serif providing serious typographic support for the Cherokee syllabary.
description: Noto Sans Cherokee is among the more complete Noto script families, reflecting the ongoing cultural and digital vitality of the Cherokee Nation and its writing system. The full weight range enables genuine typographic hierarchy in Cherokee-language publications, signage, and digital platforms. Its neutral sans-serif approach ensures compatibility with broader Noto family deployments.

name: Noto Sans Chorasmian
classification_notes: A single-weight Noto family member covering Chorasmian, an ancient Iranian script with very limited modern usage. Letterforms follow the clean Noto approach, optimised for scholarly legibility rather than expressive typographic range.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif covering the rare ancient Chorasmian script for academic and archival use.
description: Noto Sans Chorasmian addresses the digitisation of an ancient Iranian script historically used along the Silk Road, making it valuable primarily to historians, linguists, and archaeologists. As with most single-weight Noto additions, its scope is functional rather than expressive. It enables consistent, standards-compliant rendering in scholarly databases and Unicode-compliant software.

name: Noto Sans Coptic
classification_notes: Covers the Coptic script, the liturgical language of Egyptian Christians, within the Noto family's systematic neutral framework. Single weight, with letterforms that carefully reflect the uncial-influenced forms of Coptic while maintaining on-screen legibility.
summary: A clean, neutral sans-serif enabling digital support for the Coptic script used in Egyptian Christian liturgy.
description: Noto Sans Coptic provides essential Unicode coverage for Coptic, a script descended from Greek uncials and still used liturgically by Coptic Orthodox Christians. Its neutral sans treatment is somewhat at odds with Coptic's historical associations with manuscript culture, but serves the practical goal of consistent digital rendering. Primary applications include religious digital publishing, academic transcription, and archival work.

name: Noto Sans Cuneiform
classification_notes: One of the most historically ambitious Noto families, covering cuneiform — one of the world's oldest writing systems with thousands of complex wedge-shaped characters. Single weight by necessity, optimised for legibility at display sizes given the intricate nature of cuneiform signs.
summary: An extraordinary undertaking that brings digital type support to cuneiform, one of humanity's oldest writing systems.
description: Noto Sans Cuneiform is a landmark of Unicode font engineering, encoding thousands of wedge-based signs used across ancient Mesopotamia for over three millennia. Given the complexity and scale of the cuneiform repertoire, a single weight is the pragmatic choice, and the designs are best rendered at larger sizes. It is an indispensable resource for Assyriologists, historians, and digital humanities projects.

name: Noto Sans Cypriot
classification_notes: Covers the ancient Cypriot syllabary, a Bronze Age script used to write Greek in Cyprus, as part of the Noto project. Single weight with careful attention to the angular, incised quality of the original inscriptions translated into a consistent sans-serif framework.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif enabling digital representation of the ancient Cypriot syllabary for scholarly purposes.
description: Noto Sans Cypriot provides Unicode-compliant coverage of the Cypriot syllabary, an ancient script used in Cyprus from roughly the 11th to the 4th century BCE. Its restrained sans-serif interpretation translates the angular, lapidary character of inscribed originals into a digitally legible form. Use cases are almost exclusively academic, encompassing epigraphy, ancient Greek studies, and digital humanities research.

name: Noto Sans Cypro Minoan
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to provide coverage for the undocumented Cypro-Minoan script used in ancient Cyprus. Clean, minimal letterforms prioritise legibility and scholarly utility over aesthetic expression.
summary: A highly specialised Noto family member providing the only widely available digital typeface for the ancient Cypro-Minoan script.
description: Noto Sans Cypro Minoan serves a narrow but vital purpose: enabling digital rendering of one of the ancient world's least-understood scripts. Its design follows the clean Noto sans-serif conventions to maintain visual consistency across the broader family. Use cases are almost exclusively academic, archival, and scholarly publishing.

name: Noto Sans Deseret
classification_notes: Designed to render the Deseret alphabet, a 19th-century phonetic script created for English by the early Latter-day Saint community. Letterforms are clean and upright, following Noto's neutral sans-serif conventions.
summary: The definitive digital typeface for the Deseret alphabet, a rare 19th-century phonetic script for English, delivered in Noto's characteristically neutral style.
description: Noto Sans Deseret is a single-weight font providing essential Unicode coverage for a script that sees limited but dedicated use among historians and Latter-day Saint communities. Its neutral, unadorned letterforms are consistent with the broader Noto family, ensuring harmonious coexistence with Latin text. Primary applications are academic research, historical transcription, and specialised digital archives.

name: Noto Sans Devanagari
classification_notes: A thorough, multi-weight Devanagari sans-serif designed with careful attention to script-specific stroke modulation and proportions, paired with harmonious Latin glyphs. The wide weight range makes it one of the most versatile Devanagari typefaces available.
summary: One of the most comprehensive and carefully crafted Devanagari sans-serif typefaces available, spanning nine weights for broad typographic versatility.
description: Noto Sans Devanagari addresses a genuine need for a high-quality, weight-rich typeface supporting Hindi, Sanskrit, Marathi, and numerous other Devanagari-script languages. Its stroke modulation respects the calligraphic origins of the script whilst delivering a contemporary, screen-optimised result. It is well suited to editorial design, user interfaces, and any project requiring serious multilingual Latin–Devanagari typesetting.

name: Noto Sans Display
classification_notes: The display-optimised cut of the Noto Sans family, with tighter spacing and refined proportions intended for headline sizes in Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts. Maintains the family's neutral, pan-European character.
summary: A display-tuned variant of Noto Sans optimised for large-scale use across Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts with a full nine-weight range.
description: Noto Sans Display is purpose-built for headline and titling applications, with metrics and spacing calibrated for larger point sizes rather than extended reading. Its coverage of Cyrillic and Greek alongside Latin makes it a rare, reliable choice for multilingual display work in European contexts. The broad weight range adds flexibility for expressive typographic hierarchies.

name: Noto Sans Duployan
classification_notes: Provides digital coverage for Duployan shorthand, a cursive stenographic system developed in the 19th century. Letterforms necessarily follow the simple, stroke-based geometry of the shorthand system itself.
summary: A highly specialised typeface enabling digital encoding of Duployan shorthand, a 19th-century cursive stenographic script with limited modern use.
description: Noto Sans Duployan fulfils a narrow archival and scholarly function, providing Unicode-compliant rendering of a stenographic system once widely used in France and beyond. The two-weight offering is unusually generous for so rare a script, suggesting its utility in facsimile and archival reproduction contexts. Its primary audience is linguists, historians, and archivists working with historical shorthand documents.

name: Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs
classification_notes: Provides Unicode coverage for Egyptian hieroglyphs, translating complex pictographic signs into a consistent, digitally usable format. Each glyph is rendered as a clean, outlined form to ensure legibility at text sizes.
summary: A singular typeface providing standardised digital rendering of Egyptian hieroglyphs, bringing one of history's oldest writing systems into Unicode compliance.
description: Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs is an extraordinary undertaking in the Noto project, encoding over a thousand distinct hieroglyphic signs for scholarly and archival use. The glyphs are drawn with clean outlines that balance fidelity to historical forms with on-screen clarity. Its use cases span Egyptology, museum publishing, educational materials, and any digital context requiring authentic hieroglyphic text.

name: Noto Sans Elbasan
classification_notes: Covers the Elbasan script, a rare Albanian alphabet created in the 18th century and used in very limited historical contexts. Follows Noto's clean, upright sans-serif model with minimal stylistic intervention.
summary: A single-weight typeface providing the rare digital coverage needed for the Elbasan script, an 18th-century Albanian alphabet with a narrow scholarly following.
description: Noto Sans Elbasan is among the more obscure members of the Noto family, targeting a script used in only a handful of surviving manuscripts. Its clean, unadorned letterforms are consistent with the project's broader approach of neutrality and utility. The typeface serves historians and linguists studying early Albanian writing traditions.

name: Noto Sans Elymaic
classification_notes: Provides digital support for the Elymaic script, an ancient Aramaic-derived writing system used in the Elymais region of modern-day Iran. Letterforms are rendered with the clean, restrained approach characteristic of the Noto project.
summary: A single-weight Noto typeface enabling digital rendering of the Elymaic script, an ancient Iranian writing system of significant archaeological interest.
description: Noto Sans Elymaic addresses the preservation and study of an ancient script attested primarily on coins and rock inscriptions from the Elymais kingdom. Like other scholarly Noto additions, its design prioritises Unicode compliance and legibility over aesthetic ambition. Its audience is almost exclusively epigraphers, ancient historians, and digital humanities researchers.

name: Noto Sans Ethiopic
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to render Ethiopic script (Ge'ez) with clean, neutral strokes that harmonise with Latin companions. The wide weight range supports diverse typographic hierarchies across multilingual documents.
summary: A reliable, neutral sans-serif covering the Ethiopic script as part of the comprehensive Noto typeface system.
description: Noto Sans Ethiopic is engineered to eliminate tofu — the blank rectangles that appear when a system lacks font support — for Ethiopic (Ge'ez) text. Its nine weights make it genuinely useful across interface design, editorial layouts, and document typography wherever Amharic, Tigrinya, or related languages appear. The design prioritises consistency with other Noto family members to enable smooth multilingual typesetting.

name: Noto Sans Georgian
classification_notes: Designed within the Noto system to support Georgian script alongside Cyrillic Extended and Greek Extended, maintaining a neutral, even texture across all three. The nine weights give it considerable flexibility for both display and text applications.
summary: A neutral, well-weighted sans-serif built to handle Georgian script faithfully within the broader Noto typographic system.
