description: Noto Sans Georgian tackles one of the more distinctive scripts in the Noto collection, with Georgian's unique rounded forms handled cleanly and without affectation. The inclusion of Cyrillic Extended and Greek Extended makes it a strong choice for multilingual publishing across the South Caucasus region. Nine weights from Thin to Black ensure it performs equally well in interface design, long-form reading, and headline use.

name: Noto Sans Glagolitic
classification_notes: A single-weight Noto entry covering Glagolitic, the oldest known Slavic script, paired with Cyrillic Extended and Latin. The design adapts the angular, geometric character of Glagolitic into a clean sans-serif framework.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif providing rare digital support for Glagolitic, the ancient Slavic script devised by Saints Cyril and Methodius.
description: Noto Sans Glagolitic fills a significant gap in Unicode font coverage, making one of the world's oldest alphabets accessible in modern digital environments. Its primary utility is scholarly, supporting historians, linguists, and digital humanities projects that require accurate Glagolitic text rendering. The single Regular weight reflects the specialised, functional nature of the face rather than any aspiration toward typographic expression.

name: Noto Sans Gothic
classification_notes: Covers Gothic (Gothic script as used in the Bible of Wulfila), a historical script with angular, symmetrical letterforms, paired with Latin and Latin Extended for scholarly contexts. Single weight only, reflecting its highly specialised purpose.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif offering digital support for the Gothic script of the 4th-century Wulfila Bible, serving scholarly and historical research.
description: Noto Sans Gothic addresses the needs of academics and researchers working with ancient Gothic texts, a script largely absent from mainstream font libraries. The letterforms retain the distinctive diamond-tipped strokes and bilateral symmetry characteristic of Wulfilan Gothic while fitting within the consistent visual logic of the Noto system. Its application is almost exclusively academic, enabling proper typesetting of Gothic-language manuscripts and linguistic studies.

name: Noto Sans Grantha
classification_notes: Designed to support Grantha, a South Indian script historically used to write Sanskrit and Tamil, with clean, neutral strokes that respect the script's complex ligatures and conjunct characters. Single weight reflects its specialist scholarly application.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif providing essential digital support for Grantha, a classical South Indian script used historically for Sanskrit texts.
description: Noto Sans Grantha addresses a significant gap in South Asian script coverage, enabling scholars and religious communities to render Grantha accurately in digital environments. The script's complex conjunct characters and vowel marks are handled with care to preserve legibility and scholarly integrity. Its pairing with Latin and Latin Extended makes it practical for academic publications that mix English commentary with original Grantha source material.

name: Noto Sans Gujarati
classification_notes: A comprehensive Noto entry for Gujarati script, offering nine weights that handle the script's distinctive headline stroke (shirorekha equivalent) and complex matras cleanly. The design harmonises with other Noto family members for multilingual typesetting.
summary: A well-constructed, full-weight-range sans-serif supporting Gujarati script as part of the Noto universal typography system.
description: Noto Sans Gujarati provides the full nine-weight spectrum needed for serious multilingual typographic work in Gujarati, one of India's major scripts. Its clean handling of the script's distinctive headline bar and intricate vowel signs makes it suitable for everything from mobile interfaces to editorial design. The consistent visual relationship with other Noto scripts makes it the natural choice for documents and products requiring simultaneous Gujarati and Latin text.

name: Noto Sans Gunjala Gondi
classification_notes: Part of the Noto project covering Gunjala Gondi, a recently devised script for the Gondi language of central India, with four weights offering modest but practical typographic range. The design follows Noto's neutral, functional aesthetic.
summary: A four-weight sans-serif supporting Gunjala Gondi, a contemporary script created for the Gondi-speaking communities of central India.
description: Noto Sans Gunjala Gondi is one of the more specialist entries in the Noto collection, providing digital support for a script that was only formalised in the 20th century and encoded in Unicode relatively recently. The four available weights — Regular through Bold — give it enough range for basic editorial and educational use cases serving Gondi-language communities. Its existence reflects the Noto project's commitment to linguistic equity for minority and endangered language speakers.

name: Noto Sans Gurmukhi
classification_notes: A nine-weight Noto entry for Gurmukhi, the script used for Punjabi, with careful attention to the script's distinctive headline stroke and complex conjunct characters. Designed to integrate smoothly with Latin text in multilingual settings.
summary: A thorough, nine-weight sans-serif covering Gurmukhi script for Punjabi-language typography within the Noto universal type system.
description: Noto Sans Gurmukhi delivers the weight range and script fidelity needed for professional Punjabi typography across print and digital applications. The headline bar shared with Devanagari and other Indic scripts is handled consistently, and the complex stacking diacritics that Gurmukhi requires are rendered with precision. It is the practical default choice for any product or publication serving Punjabi-speaking audiences globally.

name: Noto Sans HK
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto superfamily, designed to provide harmonious coverage of Traditional Chinese (Hong Kong standard) alongside Latin and Cyrillic scripts. Offers a full nine-weight range for versatile typographic use.
summary: A comprehensive Traditional Chinese sans-serif covering Hong Kong standards with a broad weight range suitable for digital and print use.
description: Noto Sans HK is designed to eliminate tofu — the blank rectangles that appear when a system lacks a required glyph — across Traditional Chinese as used in Hong Kong, alongside Latin and Cyrillic. Its nine weights make it adaptable for everything from body text in apps to bold editorial headlines. It is a reliable workhorse for any project requiring consistent multilingual typographic coverage.

name: Noto Sans Hanifi Rohingya
classification_notes: Designed as part of the Noto project to support the Hanifi Rohingya script, a relatively modern alphabet created in the 1990s for the Rohingya language. Four weights provide basic typographic hierarchy.
summary: A purposeful sans-serif supporting the Hanifi Rohingya script, one of the few typefaces to address this underserved writing system.
description: Noto Sans Hanifi Rohingya provides essential typographic support for the Rohingya language community, covering a script with very limited typeface availability worldwide. The four-weight range allows for basic editorial and document hierarchies. Its primary value is functional and humanitarian, making digital content accessible in the Rohingya language.

name: Noto Sans Hanunoo
classification_notes: Covers the Hanunoo script, an indigenous Philippine abugida used by the Mangyan people of Mindoro. Single-weight offering typical of Noto's coverage of rare scripts.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif preserving digital access to the Hanunoo script, an endangered indigenous Philippine writing system.
description: Noto Sans Hanunoo fulfils the Noto project's mission of universal script coverage, addressing the Hanunoo abugida used by the Mangyan people of the Philippines. With only a regular weight, its applications are limited to body text and basic document use. Its significance is cultural and archival, supporting the digital preservation of a script at risk of obsolescence.

name: Noto Sans Hatran
classification_notes: Supports the Hatran Aramaic script, an ancient writing system used in Hatra (modern Iraq) during the Parthian period. Single-weight, scholarly in application.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif enabling digital rendering of the ancient Hatran Aramaic script for scholarly and archival purposes.
description: Noto Sans Hatran addresses the needs of linguists, historians, and digital archivists working with ancient Aramaic inscriptions from Hatra. As a single-weight font with a narrow use case, it is fundamentally a reference and research tool rather than a design instrument. It exemplifies the Noto project's commitment to encoding even the most obscure historical scripts.

name: Noto Sans Hebrew
classification_notes: A full nine-weight Hebrew sans-serif from the Noto family, with carefully considered letterforms that respect the optical characteristics of the Hebrew script while harmonising with Latin counterparts.
summary: A thorough nine-weight Hebrew sans-serif that pairs harmoniously with Latin script for multilingual documents and digital interfaces.
description: Noto Sans Hebrew brings the full weight range typical of a professional sans-serif family to Hebrew, making it one of the more typographically versatile Hebrew options available on Google Fonts. Its design balances the traditional proportions of Hebrew letterforms with a neutral, modern sensibility suited to UI, editorial, and body text. The inclusion of Cyrillic Extended and Greek Extended broadens its utility for complex multilingual projects.

name: Noto Sans Imperial Aramaic
classification_notes: Covers Imperial Aramaic, a standardised script used across the Achaemenid Persian Empire. Single-weight, designed primarily for scholarly and Unicode-compliance purposes.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif supporting Imperial Aramaic, designed to meet the needs of historians and linguists working with ancient Persian-era inscriptions.
description: Noto Sans Imperial Aramaic provides Unicode-compliant rendering of the Imperial Aramaic script, which served as the administrative lingua franca of the Achaemenid Empire. Its application is almost exclusively academic, supporting digital editions of ancient texts and linguistic databases. Like all Noto historical script fonts, it prioritises correctness and legibility over stylistic expression.

name: Noto Sans Indic Siyaq Numbers
classification_notes: Provides Unicode support for Indic Siyaq Numbers, a specialised numeral system used historically in accounting across the Indian subcontinent. Single-weight, highly specialised.
summary: A single-weight font dedicated to Indic Siyaq numerals, a rare historical accounting notation with extremely narrow scholarly application.
description: Noto Sans Indic Siyaq Numbers addresses one of the most specialised Unicode blocks, covering a numeral system used in historical South Asian financial and administrative records. Its audience is limited almost entirely to palaeographers, archivists, and digital humanities researchers. The font demonstrates the Noto project's exhaustive approach to Unicode coverage, however niche the requirement.

name: Noto Sans Inscriptional Pahlavi
classification_notes: Supports Inscriptional Pahlavi, a Middle Persian script found in stone inscriptions from the Parthian and early Sasanian periods. Single-weight, designed for historical and scholarly use.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif enabling accurate digital representation of Inscriptional Pahlavi, an ancient Middle Persian writing system.
description: Noto Sans Inscriptional Pahlavi serves historians and linguists working with Middle Persian rock inscriptions from the Parthian and early Sasanian eras. The font provides Unicode-compliant glyph coverage with clean, legible forms appropriate for academic publication and digital archiving. Its utility is confined to specialist research contexts, fulfilling a critical but narrow typographic need.

name: Noto Sans Inscriptional Parthian
classification_notes: Designed specifically to render the ancient Inscriptional Parthian script used in stone and metal inscriptions from the Arsacid Empire. The design is minimal and functional, supporting a script with very limited modern use.
summary: A specialist Noto face covering the rare Inscriptional Parthian script, existing primarily for scholarly and archival purposes.
description: Noto Sans Inscriptional Parthian is part of Google's Noto project, which aims to eliminate tofu — missing character boxes — across all scripts. This face supports the right-to-left Inscriptional Parthian script, an ancient writing system used across the Parthian Empire. Its use case is essentially limited to academic texts, historical research, and digital heritage projects.

name: Noto Sans JP
classification_notes: A comprehensive Japanese-primary sans-serif with an extensive nine-weight range, harmonising Latin, Cyrillic, and Japanese glyphs within a consistent, neutral design system. Clean strokes and uniform proportions make it highly versatile across digital and print applications.
summary: Google's definitive Japanese sans-serif, offering exceptional script coverage and a refined nine-weight range for seamless multilingual typography.
description: Noto Sans JP is the cornerstone CJK Japanese typeface in the Noto family, built to provide harmonious text rendering across Japanese, Latin, and Cyrillic scripts in a single cohesive system. Its neutral grotesque character makes it equally suited to body text, user interfaces, and editorial layouts. The broad weight range gives designers considerable flexibility, and its open-source availability makes it a practical default for any project requiring robust Japanese language support.

name: Noto Sans Javanese
classification_notes: Designed to faithfully render the Javanese script (Aksara Jawa), a traditional abugida script used in Java, Indonesia, with clean, structured strokes that respect the script's curvilinear heritage. Offered in four weights, giving it more typographic range than many Noto script-specific faces.
summary: A dedicated sans-serif for the Javanese script, providing clean and culturally respectful rendering of a traditional Southeast Asian writing system.
description: Noto Sans Javanese supports the Aksara Jawa script, preserving an important cultural and literary tradition in digital typography. Its four-weight range — from Regular to Bold — offers modest but meaningful typographic flexibility compared to single-weight Noto script faces. Primary use cases include Javanese-language publishing, educational materials, and digital archiving of Javanese cultural texts.

name: Noto Sans KR
classification_notes: A comprehensive Korean-primary sans-serif with nine weights, balancing Hangul, Latin, and Cyrillic scripts within a unified neutral design system. Stroke terminals and proportions are carefully calibrated for legibility across digital and print contexts.
summary: Google's authoritative Korean sans-serif, delivering a wide weight range and harmonised script coverage for professional multilingual typography.
description: Noto Sans KR mirrors the scope and ambition of Noto Sans JP but centres on the Korean Hangul script, with full Latin and Cyrillic companions. Its nine weights make it one of the most flexible Korean typefaces available for free, covering everything from delicate light text to heavy display headings. It is a dependable workhorse for web applications, mobile interfaces, and editorial work requiring Korean language support.

name: Noto Sans Kaithi
classification_notes: A single-weight face designed to support Kaithi, a historical Brahmic script once widely used for administrative and literary purposes in northern India. The design adheres to the Noto project's clean, unornamented approach while respecting the script's structural conventions.
summary: A functional Noto face for the Kaithi script, serving the scholarly and archival needs of a historically significant North Indian writing system.
description: Noto Sans Kaithi addresses a script that was once prevalent in Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, and surrounding regions for legal and literary documents before falling out of common use. Its single Regular weight reflects the limited current demand, with the face serving primarily researchers, historians, and digital preservation projects. It ensures that legacy Kaithi texts can be faithfully rendered in modern digital environments.

name: Noto Sans Kannada
classification_notes: A multi-weight Noto face for the Kannada script, one of the major Dravidian writing systems of South India, featuring clean and carefully constructed letterforms across nine weights. The design balances the rounded, curvilinear nature of Kannada with the neutral consistency of the Noto system.
summary: A thorough nine-weight sans-serif for the Kannada script, bringing broad typographic range to one of South India's major writing systems.
description: Noto Sans Kannada supports the official script of Karnataka and one of the oldest Dravidian scripts still in active use. Its nine-weight range makes it one of the more fully developed Noto Indic faces, suitable for everything from body text in Kannada-language publications to bold headlines in digital interfaces. The clean construction integrates well with Latin companions, making it a reliable choice for bilingual Kannada–English design work.

name: Noto Sans Kawi
classification_notes: Designed to support the Kawi script, an ancient writing system from Southeast Asia used historically for Sanskrit and Old Javanese texts, rendered with clean structured strokes consistent with the Noto design language. Four weights offer modest typographic flexibility for an archaic script.
summary: A structured Noto face for the ancient Kawi script, enabling digital rendering of a historically significant Southeast Asian writing system.
description: Noto Sans Kawi supports a script used across medieval Southeast Asia, particularly in Java and Bali, for inscriptions and manuscripts in Sanskrit and Old Javanese. Its inclusion in the Noto project ensures that Kawi texts can be displayed without character substitution in modern digital environments. The face is suited primarily to scholarly, archival, and cultural heritage applications rather than contemporary commercial use.

name: Noto Sans Kayah Li
classification_notes: A four-weight sans-serif face designed for the Kayah Li script, a relatively recent writing system developed in the 1960s for the Kayah language of Myanmar. The letterforms are open and legible, following the Noto project's humanist sans approach.
summary: A clean four-weight Noto face for the Kayah Li script, supporting digital typography for a minority language community in Myanmar.
description: Noto Sans Kayah Li covers the script created for the Red Karen (Kayah) people of Myanmar, a writing system that has only existed since the mid-twentieth century. Its four-weight range — Regular through Bold — provides practical typographic options for community publishing, educational materials, and digital communication in the Kayah language. As with all Noto faces, its primary value lies in ensuring equitable script representation across global digital platforms.

name: Noto Sans Kharoshthi
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to provide harmonious coverage of the ancient Kharoshthi script used historically in the northwestern Indian subcontinent. Follows the clean, neutral Noto design principles with minimal stylistic flourish.
summary: A single-weight Noto companion font providing essential coverage for the ancient Kharoshthi script.
description: Noto Sans Kharoshthi serves scholars, historians, and linguists who need to render the ancient Kharoshthi script, historically used in regions of modern Pakistan and Afghanistan. Its single Regular weight reflects the specialised, archival nature of the script's contemporary use. As part of the broader Noto family, it pairs harmoniously with other Noto fonts for multilingual documents.

name: Noto Sans Khmer
classification_notes: A nine-weight Noto sans designed for the Khmer script of Cambodia, featuring the characteristic rounded strokes and distinctive subscript consonant clusters of Khmer letterforms within a clean, functional framework.
summary: A well-weighted Noto sans offering broad typographic range for the Khmer script across digital and print contexts.
description: Noto Sans Khmer covers the full Khmer script with a generous nine-weight range from Thin to Black, making it one of the more versatile Noto script companions. This weight breadth supports everything from body text in apps and websites to headline use in Cambodian-language publishing. Its neutral design ensures legibility at small sizes while remaining culturally appropriate to the script's visual traditions.

name: Noto Sans Khojki
classification_notes: Designed to render the Khojki script, a historical mercantile script of the Sindh and Punjab regions associated with the Ismaili Muslim community. Available in a single Regular weight reflecting its specialised scholarly and archival usage.
summary: A single-weight Noto font providing rare digital coverage for the historical Khojki script.
description: Noto Sans Khojki addresses the need to digitally reproduce the Khojki script, historically used by Khoja communities for religious and commercial texts in South Asia. Its inclusion in the Noto project reflects Google's commitment to preserving and enabling marginalised and historical writing systems. The single Regular weight suits its primarily archival, academic, and cultural preservation use cases.

name: Noto Sans Khudawadi
classification_notes: Provides coverage for Khudawadi, also known as Khudabadi or Sindhi, a script historically used by the trading communities of Sindh. Follows the standard Noto neutral sans approach in a single Regular weight.
summary: A single-weight Noto font enabling digital rendering of the Khudawadi script for Sindhi heritage and scholarly use.
description: Noto Sans Khudawadi supports the Khudawadi script, a Brahmic script historically used by the Sindhi trading communities of the Indian subcontinent. Like other specialised Noto script fonts, it exists primarily to ensure no script is left without digital representation, serving researchers, cultural institutions, and diaspora communities. It pairs seamlessly with other Noto fonts in multilingual typographic settings.

name: Noto Sans Lao
classification_notes: A nine-weight Noto sans for the Lao script, featuring the characteristic rounded, loopless letterforms of the standard modern Lao style. Designed for legibility across screen and print at a wide range of sizes.
summary: A versatile, nine-weight Noto sans delivering clean, loopless Lao script coverage for digital and print use.
description: Noto Sans Lao provides comprehensive weight coverage for the Lao script in the contemporary loopless style, which is well-suited to screen rendering and modern document design. The nine-weight range from Thin to Black makes it capable of supporting the full typographic hierarchy of Lao-language publications, websites, and apps. Its neutral character ensures it functions as a reliable workhorse for Lao-script content.

name: Noto Sans Lao Looped
classification_notes: A nine-weight Noto variant specifically designed using the traditional looped style of Lao letterforms, which includes the characteristic terminal loops found in handwritten and older printed Lao. Offers an alternative to the loopless Noto Sans Lao.
summary: A nine-weight Noto sans capturing the traditional looped style of Lao script for culturally authentic typesetting.
description: Noto Sans Lao Looped distinguishes itself from the standard Noto Sans Lao by preserving the traditional looped terminals that characterise classical Lao letterforms, which many readers associate with formal and literary Lao writing. This makes it a culturally richer choice for contexts where authenticity to Lao typographic heritage matters, such as government publications, educational materials, and cultural media. It retains the full nine-weight range for typographic flexibility.

name: Noto Sans Lepcha
classification_notes: Provides digital coverage for the Lepcha script, also known as Róng, used by the Lepcha people of Sikkim and surrounding regions in the eastern Himalayas. Available in a single Regular weight suited to its specialised usage.
summary: A single-weight Noto font ensuring digital preservation and display of the endangered Lepcha script.
description: Noto Sans Lepcha supports the Lepcha script, a writing system used by a small community in Sikkim, Darjeeling, and Bhutan, and considered at risk of digital extinction without resources like the Noto project. Its inclusion reflects the Noto initiative's commitment to endangered and minority scripts alongside widely spoken languages. It is primarily of value to linguists, cultural preservation projects, and Lepcha community initiatives.

name: Noto Sans Limbu
classification_notes: Designed to render the Limbu script, also known as Sirijanga, used by the Limbu people of eastern Nepal and the Darjeeling hills. A single Regular weight reflects the script's specialised and minority-language context.
summary: A single-weight Noto font providing essential digital support for the Limbu script of Nepal and northeast India.
description: Noto Sans Limbu covers the Limbu script, a Brahmic abugida used to write the Limbu language spoken across parts of Nepal and India's Sikkim and West Bengal states. As a minority script with limited digital presence, its Noto representation is significant for community literacy efforts, educational publishing, and cultural documentation. It integrates smoothly with other Noto fonts for combined Latin and Limbu typesetting.

name: Noto Sans Linear A
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, this font provides coverage for Linear A, the undeciphered script of the ancient Minoan civilisation. Designed purely for scholarly and archival purposes with minimal stylistic variation.
summary: A specialist Noto font providing Unicode coverage for Linear A, the ancient and still-undeciphered Minoan script.
description: Noto Sans Linear A exists primarily to support linguists, historians, and archivists who need to render this ancient script digitally. Its use cases are narrow and highly academic, with no practical application in general typography. As with all Noto scripts, consistency with the broader Noto family is prioritised over expressive design.

name: Noto Sans Linear B
classification_notes: Another entry in the Noto project covering Linear B, the syllabic script used to write Mycenaean Greek. Provides single-weight coverage for a script of purely historical and scholarly interest.
summary: A specialist Noto font covering Linear B, the ancient syllabic script of Mycenaean Greek, intended for scholarly digital use.
description: Noto Sans Linear B fills a Unicode gap for researchers and academics working with ancient Mycenaean texts. Like its Linear A counterpart, it offers a single weight with no typographic flexibility beyond basic script rendering. Its value is purely functional within specialist historical and linguistic contexts.

name: Noto Sans Lisu
classification_notes: Covers the Lisu script used by the Lisu people of Southwest China and Southeast Asia, with a modest weight range from Regular to Bold. Clean and functional in keeping with the broader Noto design philosophy.
summary: A clean, functional Noto sans covering the Lisu script with a useful weight range for everyday text use.
description: Noto Sans Lisu supports digital communication for Lisu-speaking communities by providing a reliable, neutral typeface across four weights. Its design follows the consistent Noto approach: unobtrusive and legible at text sizes. It is well suited to body text, interfaces, and any context requiring dependable multilingual support.

name: Noto Sans Lycian
classification_notes: Provides Unicode coverage for Lycian, an ancient Anatolian script extinct since antiquity, available in a single weight only. Intended entirely for scholarly and archival digital reproduction.
summary: A minimal single-weight Noto font covering the ancient Lycian script for scholarly and archival digital purposes.
description: Noto Sans Lycian serves the narrow purpose of rendering the extinct Lycian script in digital environments, primarily for academic research and historical documentation. With only a single weight and no Latin support, its utility is extremely limited outside specialist contexts. It exists to complete Unicode coverage rather than to serve any general typographic need.

name: Noto Sans Lydian
classification_notes: Covers the Lydian script, another extinct ancient Anatolian writing system, in a single regular weight with Latin Extended support. Designed for archival completeness within the Noto project.
summary: A single-weight Noto font providing coverage for the extinct ancient Lydian script alongside Latin Extended characters.
description: Noto Sans Lydian exists to ensure that the ancient Lydian script can be rendered accurately in modern digital environments, primarily for historians and linguists. Its inclusion of Latin Extended characters allows it to sit alongside other Noto fonts in multilingual documents without disruption. Practical applications are confined entirely to academic and archival work.

name: Noto Sans Mahajani
classification_notes: Supports Mahajani, a historical mercantile script of northern India, in a single regular weight. Follows the standard Noto design approach with clean, neutral letterforms suited to scholarly reproduction.
summary: A single-weight Noto font covering Mahajani, a historical North Indian mercantile script, for digital scholarly use.
description: Noto Sans Mahajani provides essential Unicode support for the Mahajani script, historically used by merchants and traders across northern India. Its single weight and neutral design reflect the Noto project's priority of faithful, unadorned script reproduction. Use is largely confined to academic research, archival digitisation, and cultural heritage projects.

name: Noto Sans Malayalam
classification_notes: A well-developed Noto offering for Malayalam, one of the major Dravidian scripts of South India, featuring a full nine-weight range from Thin to Black. The letterforms are clean and optimised for both screen and print legibility across a broad range of sizes.
summary: A comprehensive nine-weight Noto sans covering Malayalam with strong legibility credentials for both body text and display use.
description: Noto Sans Malayalam is one of the more fully realised entries in the Noto family, offering the kind of weight breadth typically associated with major Latin typefaces. This makes it genuinely versatile for Malayalam-language publishing, digital interfaces, and editorial design. Its clean, neutral construction pairs well with other Noto fonts in multilingual settings.

name: Noto Sans Mandaic
classification_notes: Covers Mandaic, a cursive script used by the Mandaean religious community, with right-to-left directionality and a single weight. Designed to meet the Noto project's goal of universal Unicode script coverage.
summary: A single-weight Noto font providing Unicode coverage for Mandaic, the right-to-left script of the ancient Mandaean community.
description: Noto Sans Mandaic supports the digital preservation and communication needs of the Mandaean community, whose script is among the world's oldest continuously used writing systems. The single-weight design is functional and faithful, adhering to the Noto project's neutral house style. Its primary use cases are religious, cultural, and academic in nature.

name: Noto Sans Manichaean
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to render Manichaean script with neutral, unobtrusive letterforms that prioritise legibility and cross-script harmony. Single weight reflects its specialist, archival purpose.
summary: A highly specialised sans-serif supporting the ancient Manichaean script as part of Google's universal Noto type system.
description: Noto Sans Manichaean exists to fill a critical gap in digital typography, enabling the rendering of a historically significant religious script without tofu (missing glyph boxes). Its design is purposefully neutral, subordinating aesthetic ambition to scholarly and archival utility. It is best suited to academic publications, linguistic research tools, and Unicode-compliant digital platforms.

name: Noto Sans Marchen
classification_notes: Designed within the Noto framework to support the Marchen script of the Tibetan plateau, with letterforms that respect the script's structural complexity while maintaining inter-family consistency. Available in a single weight.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif covering the rare Marchen script, created to ensure complete Unicode text representation across Google's Noto family.
description: Noto Sans Marchen addresses one of the more obscure corners of the Unicode standard, providing digital support for a script with an extremely limited user base outside specialist scholarship. The letterforms are drawn to complement the broader Noto ecosystem without misrepresenting the script's native visual character. Its practical applications are confined to academic, archival, and linguistic computing contexts.

name: Noto Sans Masaram Gondi
classification_notes: Part of the Noto project's commitment to pan-Unicode coverage, this face supports the Masaram Gondi script used by the Gondi people of central India. Clean, regularised letterforms adhere to Noto's systematic design approach.
summary: A purpose-built sans-serif supporting Masaram Gondi script, contributing to Noto's mission of universal digital typographic coverage.
description: Noto Sans Masaram Gondi provides a standardised digital rendering of a script that has historically been underrepresented in computing environments. The design follows Noto's disciplined, neutral approach, ensuring compatibility and harmony when used alongside other scripts in the family. It is most relevant for cultural preservation projects, government language initiatives, and inclusive digital publishing targeting central Indian communities.

name: Noto Sans Math
classification_notes: A highly specialised member of the Noto family providing an extensive set of mathematical symbols and operators to complement standard Latin text. Letterforms are precise and technically driven, prioritising symbol clarity over stylistic expression.
summary: A technical sans-serif providing comprehensive mathematical notation support within the Noto type system.
description: Noto Sans Math fills a critical need in scientific and academic digital publishing by supplying a vast range of mathematical operators, arrows, and specialist symbols consistent with the Noto family's visual language. Its design prioritises clarity at text sizes and on screen, where precision in distinguishing similar glyphs is essential. It is indispensable for mathematical typesetting, STEM educational platforms, and any environment requiring Unicode-compliant formula rendering.

name: Noto Sans Mayan Numerals
classification_notes: An exceptionally specialised face within the Noto project, reproducing the dot-and-bar numeral system of the ancient Maya. The glyphs are structured, geometric adaptations of historical symbols rendered in a digitally consistent format.
summary: A niche Noto family member digitising Mayan numeral glyphs for Unicode-compliant scholarly and educational use.
description: Noto Sans Mayan Numerals occupies a uniquely narrow typographic niche, serving researchers, educators, and institutions concerned with pre-Columbian Mesoamerican history and mathematics. The glyphs faithfully represent the vigesimal dot-and-bar system within a framework consistent with the broader Noto ecosystem. Its applications are effectively limited to academic publications, museum digital displays, and specialist educational materials.

name: Noto Sans Medefaidrin
classification_notes: Supports Medefaidrin, a constructed script created in the early twentieth century by a West African religious community, with a modest weight range from Regular to Bold. The design reflects Noto's systematic, neutral approach to lesser-known scripts.
summary: A multi-weight Noto sans-serif supporting the Medefaidrin script, one of the few constructed African scripts to achieve Unicode encoding.
description: Noto Sans Medefaidrin is notable within the Noto project for offering a small but meaningful weight range — Regular through Bold — reflecting a degree of typographic utility beyond single-weight script companions. Medefaidrin, developed by the Oberi Okaime religious movement in Nigeria, is a historically fascinating constructed script, and this font ensures its digital preservation and scholarly accessibility. Use cases span cultural documentation, linguistic databases, and Unicode reference tools.

name: Noto Sans Meetei Mayek
classification_notes: One of the more fully developed script-specific Noto faces, offering a nine-weight range from Thin to Black for the Meetei Mayek script of Manipur, India. The breadth of weights signals genuine typographic utility for a living, actively used writing system.
summary: An unusually complete nine-weight Noto sans-serif for Meetei Mayek, reflecting the script's status as a living writing system with real typographic demands.
description: Noto Sans Meetei Mayek stands apart from many of its Noto siblings by offering a full typographic weight spectrum, acknowledging that Meetei Mayek is an official script of the Indian state of Manipur with an active user community. The extended weight range makes it genuinely useful for editorial, interface, and display work within Meitei-language publishing and digital products. It represents the Noto project at its most practically considered, balancing scholarly comprehensiveness with real-world usability.

name: Noto Sans Mende Kikakui
classification_notes: Supports Mende Kikakui, a syllabic script created in the early twentieth century for the Mende language of Sierra Leone and Liberia. Single-weight design follows Noto's standard neutral framework for preserving minority and historical scripts.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif preserving the Mende Kikakui syllabary in digital form, supporting a historically significant West African script.
description: Noto Sans Mende Kikakui provides essential digital infrastructure for a script invented by Mohamed Turay in Sierra Leone, which saw significant use in the early twentieth century and remains a point of cultural pride. The font's neutral construction integrates cleanly within the Noto system, prioritising accurate glyph representation over stylistic distinction. Its relevance is primarily archival and scholarly, serving linguists, cultural historians, and digital humanities researchers working with West African languages.

name: Noto Sans Meroitic
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to eliminate tofu (missing glyph boxes) across all scripts. Covers the ancient Meroitic script used in the Kingdom of Kush, rendered in a clean, neutral sans-serif style.
summary: A functional, no-frills sans-serif providing coverage for the ancient Meroitic script as part of Google's universal Noto type system.
description: Noto Sans Meroitic exists primarily to ensure digital text in the historical Meroitic script is rendered correctly across platforms. Its design is deliberately neutral, prioritising legibility and consistency with the broader Noto family over stylistic expression. It is a specialist tool for scholars, historians, and software developers working with ancient Sudanese texts.

name: Noto Sans Miao
classification_notes: Part of the Noto project, providing coverage for the Miao script (also known as Pollard script), used for several minority languages in China and Southeast Asia. Maintains the clean, consistent neutrality of the Noto family.
summary: A utilitarian Noto family member covering the Miao script, ensuring digital text rendering for minority language communities in East and Southeast Asia.
description: Noto Sans Miao is a specialist typeface serving communities whose languages are written in the Pollard script, including Hmong and other minority groups in China. Like all Noto fonts, it prioritises functional consistency and cross-platform compatibility over aesthetic ambition. It is invaluable for linguists, educators, and developers building software for these communities.

name: Noto Sans Modi
classification_notes: Covers the Modi script, a historical cursive script used to write Marathi in Maharashtra, India. Rendered in the clean, neutral Noto sans-serif style to ensure consistent cross-platform display.
summary: A specialist Noto typeface providing digital coverage for the Modi script, a historical writing system of Maharashtra, India.
description: Noto Sans Modi addresses the need for reliable digital rendering of the Modi script, which was used for administrative and commercial purposes in Maharashtra for several centuries. The design adheres closely to the neutral, functional aesthetic of the wider Noto family, ensuring visual harmony when combined with other Noto scripts. Archivists, historians, and developers working with Marathi historical documents are its primary audience.

name: Noto Sans Mongolian
classification_notes: Provides coverage for Mongolian script, which runs vertically, within the Noto family framework. The design accommodates the unique directional and structural requirements of classical Mongolian while maintaining Noto's neutral character.
summary: A Noto family typeface handling the distinctive vertical Mongolian script, ensuring reliable cross-platform rendering for one of the world's few vertically written living scripts.
description: Noto Sans Mongolian tackles one of the more technically demanding scripts in the Unicode standard, given Mongolian's vertical writing direction and complex joining behaviour. The font is designed to render correctly across operating systems and browsers, making it an essential resource for Mongolian-language digital publishing and software. Developers and publishers working with content in Mongolia or Inner Mongolia will find it indispensable.

name: Noto Sans Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced member of the Noto superfamily, covering Latin, Cyrillic, and Greek scripts across a wide range of weights. Clean and highly legible, it follows the neutral geometric principles of the Noto Sans design.
summary: The monospaced companion to Noto Sans, offering broad script coverage and an extensive weight range for coding, UI, and technical applications.
description: Noto Sans Mono brings the Noto family's commitment to universal script support and visual consistency to the monospaced format, making it suitable for code editors, terminal applications, and tabular data. Its wide weight range, from Thin to Black, gives designers and developers unusual flexibility for a monospaced typeface. Coverage of Cyrillic and Greek alongside Latin makes it a strong choice for internationally facing technical projects.

name: Noto Sans Mro
classification_notes: Covers the Mro script, a relatively recent script invented in the 1980s for the Mru language spoken in Bangladesh and Myanmar. Follows the standard neutral Noto sans-serif design approach.
summary: A specialist Noto typeface providing digital support for the Mro script, used by the Mru people of Bangladesh and Myanmar.
description: Noto Sans Mro ensures that the Mro script, one of the more recently encoded scripts in Unicode, is rendered consistently across digital platforms. Its design is modest and functional, in keeping with the Noto family's purpose of eliminating missing glyph boxes rather than making aesthetic statements. It serves linguists, NGOs, and developers working on digital literacy tools for the Mru-speaking community.

name: Noto Sans Multani
classification_notes: Provides coverage for the Multani script, a historical script used to write the Saraiki language in the Multan region of Pakistan. Maintains the clean, neutral aesthetic consistent across the Noto project.
summary: A Noto family typeface supporting the Multani script, a historical writing system from the Multan region of modern-day Pakistan.
description: Noto Sans Multani ensures the historical Multani script is accurately and consistently rendered in digital environments, serving researchers and communities with an interest in the Saraiki language's written heritage. Like its Noto siblings, it is designed with functional precision rather than decorative intent. It is primarily of interest to scholars of South Asian scripts and developers building Unicode-compliant software.

name: Noto Sans Myanmar
classification_notes: A comprehensive Noto typeface for Myanmar script, featuring an unusually wide weight range from Thin to Black. The design respects the rounded, complex stroke structures of Myanmar letterforms while maintaining the family's neutral legibility.
summary: The most fully developed Noto sans-serif for Myanmar script, offering a substantial nine-weight range that makes it genuinely useful for editorial and UI work in Burmese.
description: Noto Sans Myanmar stands out within the Noto family for its breadth of weights, enabling typographers to set everything from delicate body text to bold headlines in the Myanmar script. The design carefully adapts Noto's neutral humanist principles to the rounded, stacked structure of Burmese letterforms, achieving strong legibility across sizes. It is the go-to choice for publishers, app developers, and broadcasters producing content in Myanmar.

name: Noto Sans NKo
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to eliminate tofu (missing glyph boxes) across all scripts. Supports the NKo script used for Manding languages of West Africa, with clean, neutral letterforms.
summary: A clean, utilitarian sans-serif providing essential NKo script support as part of Google's universal Noto type system.
description: Noto Sans NKo serves the NKo script, an alphabet created in 1949 for writing Manding languages across West Africa. Its single Regular weight reflects the functional, coverage-first philosophy of the broader Noto project. Best suited to body text, digital interfaces, and any context requiring reliable multilingual rendering.

name: Noto Sans NKo Unjoined
classification_notes: An unjoined variant of Noto Sans NKo that presents the script's letters in separated rather than cursively connected forms, aiding legibility at smaller sizes. Offers a broader weight range than its joined counterpart.
summary: The unjoined companion to Noto Sans NKo, separating letterforms for improved clarity and offering four weights for greater typographic flexibility.
description: Where the standard NKo variant follows the script's natural cursive joining, this unjoined version isolates each character, making it particularly useful for educational materials, labelling, and contexts where legibility at small sizes is critical. The four-weight range from Regular to Bold makes it more versatile for hierarchy-driven design work. It remains a functional workhorse within the Noto ecosystem.

name: Noto Sans Nabataean
classification_notes: Covers the ancient Nabataean script, an Aramaic-derived alphabet used by the Nabataean kingdom around the first century BCE to first century CE. A single Regular weight reflects its specialist, scholarly application.
summary: A highly specialised Noto sans-serif providing digital coverage for the ancient Nabataean script, serving historians and linguists above all else.
description: Noto Sans Nabataean exists primarily to ensure the Nabataean script — the ancient precursor to the Arabic alphabet — can be rendered digitally without missing glyphs. Its single weight is sufficient for its narrow scholarly use cases, including academic publications, digital archives, and historical texts. It is an essential utility font rather than a design tool.

name: Noto Sans Nag Mundari
classification_notes: Supports Nag Mundari, a relatively recently standardised script for the Mundari language of eastern India, encoded in Unicode in 2021. Clean, upright letterforms across four weights follow the Noto project's neutral design philosophy.
summary: One of the newer additions to the Noto family, bringing reliable digital support to the Nag Mundari script with a practical four-weight range.
description: Noto Sans Nag Mundari addresses a genuine typographic gap for speakers of the Mundari language in Jharkhand, India, following the script's recent Unicode inclusion. Four weights from Regular to Bold allow for basic typographic hierarchy in documents and interfaces. Like all Noto fonts, its design prioritises consistency and neutrality over stylistic expression.

name: Noto Sans Nandinagari
classification_notes: Covers the Nandinagari script, a historical abugida from southern India related to Devanagari, used in medieval manuscripts. A single Regular weight suits its primarily archival and academic audience.
summary: A specialist Noto typeface providing digital coverage for the historical Nandinagari script, essential for academic and archival work on medieval South Indian texts.
description: Noto Sans Nandinagari ensures the Nandinagari script — a regional variant of Devanagari used in Karnataka and surrounding areas between the 8th and 19th centuries — can be rendered accurately in digital environments. Its single Regular weight is appropriate for its predominantly scholarly use in editing manuscripts, historical databases, and linguistic research. The design adheres closely to the Noto family's clean, unobtrusive aesthetic.

name: Noto Sans New Tai Lue
classification_notes: Supports New Tai Lue, a script used by the Tai Lue people of Yunnan, China and parts of Southeast Asia, reformed in the 1950s from the traditional Tai Tham script. Four weights make it among the more typographically complete Noto scripts for minority languages.
summary: A four-weight Noto sans-serif supporting the New Tai Lue script, offering genuine typographic versatility for a living minority language of Southwest China and Southeast Asia.
description: New Tai Lue is an actively used script, and Noto Sans New Tai Lue's four-weight range makes it one of the more practically useful minority-script fonts in the Noto collection. It suits community publishing, educational materials, and digital content for Tai Lue speakers. Its clean, neutral design integrates smoothly with Latin text in multilingual documents.

name: Noto Sans Newa
classification_notes: Covers the Newa script, a historical Brahmic abugida from the Kathmandu Valley used to write Nepal Bhasa (Newari). A single Regular weight reflects its specialist role in cultural and archival applications.
summary: A focused, single-weight Noto typeface providing digital coverage for the Newa script, supporting the written heritage of the Newar people of Nepal.
description: Noto Sans Newa addresses the typographic needs of Nepal Bhasa, a language with a long literary tradition in the Kathmandu Valley. The script is still in active use for religious, cultural, and literary purposes, making this font a meaningful contribution beyond pure archival utility. Its single weight limits typographic expression but is adequate for text rendering and digital preservation work.

name: Noto Sans Nushu
classification_notes: Supports Nushu, a unique syllabic script historically used exclusively by women in Hunan province, China, representing a rare example of a gender-specific writing system. A single Regular weight is appropriate for its specialist scholarly and cultural audience.
summary: A culturally significant addition to the Noto family, providing the only widely available digital typeface for Nushu, the world's only known script created and used exclusively by women.
description: Nushu was used for centuries by women in Jiangyong County, Hunan, to communicate outside the male-dominated literary tradition, and was encoded in Unicode in 2017. Noto Sans Nushu makes this fragile heritage accessible in digital form, serving researchers, cultural institutions, and communities working to preserve the script. As with all Noto fonts, its single weight and neutral design prioritise faithful rendering over typographic flourish.

name: Noto Sans Ogham
classification_notes: Designed to render the ancient Ogham script, a linear writing system used in early medieval Ireland, with clean, minimal strokes. The letterforms are highly specialised, consisting of notches and lines arranged along a central stem.
summary: A highly specialised typeface built solely to render the ancient Irish Ogham script in digital contexts.
description: Noto Sans Ogham is part of Google's Noto project, aimed at eliminating tofu across all Unicode scripts. Its primary purpose is scholarly, archival, and educational use where accurate representation of Ogham — a script of notched lines — is required. It holds little practical application outside linguistics, historical research, or heritage documentation.

name: Noto Sans Ol Chiki
classification_notes: Supports the Ol Chiki script, created in the 20th century for the Santali language spoken across South Asia, with clean and upright letterforms. The design is functional and neutral, prioritising legibility across its modest weight range.
summary: A functional sans-serif supporting the Ol Chiki script, designed to serve the Santali-speaking communities of South Asia.
description: Part of the comprehensive Noto family, Noto Sans Ol Chiki provides reliable coverage for a script that has historically been underserved in digital typography. Its range of four weights — Regular through Bold — makes it more versatile than most Noto script subsets, enabling basic typographic hierarchy. The primary use cases are digital publishing, educational materials, and UI applications serving Santali language users.

name: Noto Sans Old Hungarian
classification_notes: Covers the Old Hungarian script, a historical runic alphabet used by Hungarians before the adoption of the Latin alphabet, rendered with clean sans-serif construction. The glyphs reflect the angular, carved character of the original script.
summary: A single-weight typeface reproducing the Old Hungarian runic script for scholarly and heritage use.
description: Noto Sans Old Hungarian fills a niche Unicode gap, making the ancient Hungarian runic alphabet accessible in digital environments. Its application is almost exclusively academic, historical, or cultural — appearing in linguistic research, heritage publications, and educational materials about pre-Latin Hungarian writing. The single Regular weight reflects its narrow, specialised scope.

name: Noto Sans Old Italic
classification_notes: Reproduces Old Italic, the ancient script used by Etruscan and other early Italic civilisations, with clean digital letterforms that reflect the angular, right-to-left writing tradition. Available in a single weight, it is strictly a scholarly utility typeface.
summary: A utility typeface rendering the Old Italic script of ancient Etruscan and early Italian civilisations in a clean digital form.
description: Noto Sans Old Italic is purpose-built for Unicode compliance and scholarly accessibility, allowing historians and linguists to accurately represent pre-Roman Italic scripts in digital documents. Its clean construction avoids overt stylisation, keeping the focus on faithful character reproduction. Use cases are limited to academic publishing, archaeological documentation, and cultural heritage projects.

name: Noto Sans Old North Arabian
classification_notes: Supports Old North Arabian, a group of ancient scripts from the Arabian Peninsula predating Arabic, rendered with minimal sans-serif clarity. The single weight and narrow scope make it strictly a preservation and research tool.
summary: A narrow-purpose typeface digitising the ancient Old North Arabian scripts for academic and linguistic use.
description: Noto Sans Old North Arabian addresses a highly specialised corner of Unicode, ensuring that ancient inscriptional scripts from the Arabian Peninsula can be reproduced digitally. It is of interest almost exclusively to epigraphers, historical linguists, and scholars of ancient Near Eastern cultures. As with all Noto script subsets, its design prioritises accuracy over aesthetic ambition.

name: Noto Sans Old Permic
classification_notes: Covers Old Permic, a historical script devised in the 14th century for the Komi language of northern Russia, with clean upright letterforms adapted for digital rendering. The typeface also includes Cyrillic Extended and Latin characters, reflecting the script's documented relationship with those systems.
summary: A preservation typeface rendering the medieval Old Permic script, historically used for the Komi language of northern Russia.
description: Noto Sans Old Permic supports a script with a documented but limited historical corpus, making it a tool primarily for linguists and cultural historians working with Komi heritage materials. The inclusion of Cyrillic Extended and Latin support reflects the broader Noto design philosophy of providing contextual Unicode coverage. Its application outside specialist academic work is negligible.

name: Noto Sans Old Persian
classification_notes: Reproduces Old Persian cuneiform, the wedge-shaped script of the Achaemenid Empire, adapted into clean digital glyphs. The angular, pictographic nature of the script gives it a visually distinctive character wholly unlike alphabetic typefaces.
summary: A single-weight typeface digitising Old Persian cuneiform for scholarly documentation and Unicode completeness.
description: Noto Sans Old Persian brings one of the ancient world's most recognisable scripts — the cuneiform of Achaemenid royal inscriptions — into digital typography. Its bold, wedge-shaped glyphs are visually arresting compared with most text typefaces, though its use is strictly confined to historical, archaeological, and academic contexts. It fulfils Google's Noto mission of ensuring no Unicode character is left without a rendered form.

name: Noto Sans Old Sogdian
classification_notes: Supports Old Sogdian, an ancient script from Central Asia predating the more widely known Sogdian script, rendered with clean digital letterforms that reflect its cursive, right-to-left flow. Available in a single weight, it is purely a scholarly and archival typeface.
summary: A highly specialised typeface reproducing the Old Sogdian script of ancient Central Asia for academic and archival purposes.
description: Noto Sans Old Sogdian ensures digital representation of a script associated with the merchants and diplomats of the ancient Silk Road, of primary interest to Central Asian historians and linguists. Its clean rendering captures the cursive, right-to-left structure of the original inscriptions as faithfully as a digital sans-serif form allows. Outside specialist scholarship, this typeface has virtually no practical application.

name: Noto Sans Old South Arabian
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to render Old South Arabian script — an ancient South Semitic alphabet — without tofu (missing glyph boxes). Clean, neutral letterforms prioritise legibility and historical accuracy over stylistic expression.
summary: A highly specialised sans-serif designed solely to support the Old South Arabian script within the Noto universal type system.
description: Noto Sans Old South Arabian exists to fill a precise gap in Unicode coverage, rendering an ancient script used across the Arabian Peninsula before the Common Era. Its design is functional rather than expressive, subordinating aesthetics to scholarly and archival utility. It is best suited to academic texts, linguistic databases, and digital heritage projects requiring accurate script representation.

name: Noto Sans Old Turkic
classification_notes: Designed as part of the Noto project to support Old Turkic, a runic script used by early Turkic peoples of Central Asia. Letterforms are rendered with minimal stylistic intervention, prioritising Unicode compliance and historical fidelity.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif built to accurately represent the Old Turkic runic script within Google's Noto family.
description: Noto Sans Old Turkic addresses the need for a reliable digital rendering of a script once carved into stone monuments across the Eurasian steppe. The design makes no concession to decorative purpose, functioning purely as a scholarly and archival tool. Its primary audience is researchers, historians, and institutions working with early Turkic language materials.

name: Noto Sans Oriya
classification_notes: A comprehensive Noto sans-serif supporting the Oriya (Odia) script of eastern India, offered across nine weights from Thin to Black. The design balances the curved, loopless characteristics of the Odia script with the neutral consistency expected across the Noto family.
summary: A well-weighted Noto sans-serif providing broad support for the Odia script across nine weights, making it one of the more versatile entries in the family.
description: Noto Sans Oriya stands out within the Noto collection by offering a full weight range, making it genuinely usable for both body text and headline settings in Odia-language publishing. The letterforms respect the distinctive rounded strokes and descending baseline characteristic of the Odia script while remaining legible at small sizes on screen. It suits digital publishing, apps, and web interfaces serving Odia-speaking communities in the state of Odisha and its diaspora.

name: Noto Sans Osage
classification_notes: Part of the Noto project, this face supports the Osage script, a unified alphabet developed in the 20th century for the Osage Nation of North America. Clean, upright letterforms reflect the script's relatively modern, regularised design.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif providing digital support for the Osage script, a writing system created for an indigenous North American nation.
description: Noto Sans Osage serves a culturally specific and important function, giving the Osage script a reliable digital presence within the Unicode standard. The Osage script was created by linguist Herman Mongrain Lookout and officially encoded in Unicode in 2016, making proper font support a matter of cultural preservation. This face is valuable for educational materials, government documents, and cultural resources produced by or for the Osage Nation.

name: Noto Sans Osmanya
classification_notes: Supports Osmanya, a script created in the early 20th century to write the Somali language, characterised by distinctive letterforms that blend elements of Latin, Arabic, and Ethiopic influences. The Noto rendering is clean and functional, befitting the script's relatively modern origins.
summary: A dedicated Noto sans-serif for the Osmanya script, providing digital access to an early 20th-century writing system devised for the Somali language.
description: Noto Sans Osmanya supports a script invented around 1920 by Osman Yusuf Kenadid and now encoded in Unicode, ensuring it can be reliably rendered in digital environments. The typeface serves a niche but meaningful role in preserving and promoting a non-Latin writing system with direct ties to Somali cultural identity. Its use cases include archival digitisation, academic research, and resources supporting Somali linguistic heritage.

name: Noto Sans Pahawh Hmong
classification_notes: Renders the Pahawh Hmong script, an indigenous writing system created by Shong Lue Yang in the mid-20th century for the Hmong people of Southeast Asia. The design follows Noto's neutral principles, faithfully encoding the script's distinctive syllabic structure.
summary: A functional Noto sans-serif enabling reliable digital rendering of Pahawh Hmong, a mid-20th-century script of deep cultural significance to the Hmong people.
description: Pahawh Hmong is a uniquely structured script — a semi-syllabary with a complex coda-onset ordering — and Noto Sans Pahawh Hmong provides the Unicode coverage necessary to display it correctly in digital contexts. The typeface was designed with scholarly accuracy in mind, serving linguists, cultural organisations, and Hmong communities seeking to maintain and transmit their indigenous writing system. It is especially relevant to diaspora education and digital archiving projects.

name: Noto Sans Palmyrene
classification_notes: Supports Palmyrene, an ancient Aramaic-derived script used in the city of Palmyra in modern-day Syria from approximately the 1st century BCE to the 3rd century CE. The Noto design renders the script with archaeological fidelity, prioritising correctness over ornamentation.
summary: A scholarly Noto sans-serif providing Unicode support for Palmyrene, an ancient Semitic script from the historical city of Palmyra.
description: Noto Sans Palmyrene serves epigraphers, historians, and classicists working with inscriptions from one of antiquity's great trading cities. The script was used for funerary, civic, and religious texts and became extinct following the city's decline, making reliable digital fonts essential for ongoing academic study. Its application is almost entirely specialist — appearing in journal articles, digital humanities projects, and museum catalogues.

name: Noto Sans Pau Cin Hau
classification_notes: Covers the Pau Cin Hau script, an indigenous writing system created in the late 19th century by a religious leader among the Tedim Chin people of Myanmar. The Noto treatment is minimal and functional, encoding the script's distinctive angular letterforms accurately.
summary: A specialised Noto sans-serif providing digital support for Pau Cin Hau, a rare indigenous script from the Chin Hills of Myanmar.
description: Pau Cin Hau was developed around 1894–98 by Pau Cin Hau, a spiritual and community leader, and exists in two forms — a logographic and a later alphabetic version, both encoded in Unicode. Noto Sans Pau Cin Hau ensures the script can be rendered correctly on modern devices, serving a critical role in the digital preservation of a minority writing system with limited font support elsewhere. It is most relevant to linguists, cultural preservation bodies, and communities within the Chin ethnic group working to document and revitalise this script.

name: Noto Sans PhagsPa
classification_notes: Part of the Noto superfamily, designed to render Phags-pa script — a Mongolian-derived script commissioned by Kublai Khan — with clean, neutral strokes that harmonise with Latin companions. Functional rather than expressive, prioritising legibility and Unicode completeness.
summary: A specialist Noto companion font covering the rare Phags-pa script alongside standard Latin, built purely for Unicode completeness.
description: Noto Sans PhagsPa serves a narrow but important archival and scholarly purpose, enabling digital rendering of the Phags-pa script used in Mongol Empire inscriptions. Its design is restrained and functional, consistent with the broader Noto family's goal of eliminating tofu — the blank boxes that appear when a font lacks a required glyph. Best suited to academic publishing, multilingual databases, and Unicode-compliant digital archives.

name: Noto Sans Phoenician
classification_notes: A Noto family extension providing coverage of the ancient Phoenician script, one of the earliest alphabets and the ancestor of most modern Western scripts. Glyphs are drawn with neutral, upright strokes consistent with the Noto system.
summary: A functional Noto extension covering Phoenician script, designed for scholarly and historical digital text rendering.
description: Noto Sans Phoenician exists primarily to support academic, epigraphic, and Unicode-compliant publishing that involves one of history's most significant writing systems. The design makes no aesthetic statement beyond consistency with the Noto family's clean, unobtrusive approach. It is an essential utility font for historians, linguists, and developers working with ancient Semitic scripts.

name: Noto Sans Psalter Pahlavi
classification_notes: Part of the Noto project, this font covers Psalter Pahlavi — a Middle Persian script used primarily in religious manuscripts — alongside Latin. The glyphs follow the Noto family's neutral, regularised approach to complex script rendering.
summary: A highly specialised Noto font enabling digital rendering of Psalter Pahlavi, the script of Zoroastrian religious texts.
description: Noto Sans Psalter Pahlavi addresses an extremely narrow use case: the typesetting and digital display of Psalter Pahlavi, a cursive Aramaic-derived script used in medieval Zoroastrian liturgical manuscripts. Like all Noto fonts, it prioritises correctness and compatibility over stylistic ambition. It is indispensable for scholars, digital humanities projects, and institutions preserving Iranian religious heritage.

name: Noto Sans Rejang
classification_notes: A Noto family member supporting Rejang, an indigenous script of southern Sumatra used by the Rejang people of Bengkulu, Indonesia. Designed with the consistent neutral weight and clean construction characteristic of all Noto sans-serif fonts.
summary: A rare Noto extension covering Rejang, an endangered indigenous Sumatran script, built for Unicode completeness and digital preservation.
description: Noto Sans Rejang supports the Rejang script, a historic abugida from Bengkulu province in Indonesia that remains in limited contemporary use. Its inclusion in the Noto project reflects a commitment to preserving endangered writing systems in the digital sphere. The font will appeal to researchers, language preservation organisations, and developers working with Indonesian regional languages.

name: Noto Sans Runic
classification_notes: A Noto family extension covering Runic script — the ancient Germanic writing system used across Northern Europe — with clean, neutral strokes designed for readability on screen. Consistent in weight and proportion with the wider Noto sans-serif system.
summary: A clean, functional Noto font covering Runic script for Unicode-compliant rendering of historical Germanic inscriptions.
description: Noto Sans Runic provides digital support for the Elder Futhark and other runic alphabets, enabling historians, linguists, and enthusiasts to typeset ancient Norse and Germanic texts accurately. The design is deliberately neutral and modern rather than decorative or evocative of runic carving traditions. It is best used in academic and archival contexts where correctness and cross-platform compatibility matter more than atmosphere.

name: Noto Sans SC
classification_notes: Noto Sans SC is the Simplified Chinese branch of Google's Noto CJK superfamily, covering tens of thousands of Chinese characters alongside Latin and Cyrillic. It features a clean, upright humanoid structure with even stroke weights calibrated for both large display sizes and small body text.
summary: The definitive open-source Simplified Chinese sans-serif, offering an exceptional weight range and broad script coverage for professional digital use.
description: Noto Sans SC is one of the most comprehensive Chinese typefaces available at no cost, supporting the full Simplified Chinese character set alongside Latin and Cyrillic scripts with nine weights from Thin to Black. Its clean, modern structure makes it equally suited to body text, user interface design, and editorial headlines, giving it rare versatility across digital and print contexts. For designers working on Chinese-language products or multilingual platforms, it is an authoritative and highly dependable choice.

name: Noto Sans Samaritan
classification_notes: A Noto family font covering the Samaritan script, an ancient Semitic writing system still used liturgically by the Samaritan community. Drawn with consistent neutral strokes in line with the Noto system's approach to minority and historical scripts.
summary: A specialist Noto font enabling accurate digital rendering of the Samaritan script, one of the world's oldest continuously used writing systems.
description: Noto Sans Samaritan supports a living yet extremely rare script used by fewer than a thousand people in religious and cultural contexts, making it one of the more remarkable inclusions in the Noto project. The font is designed for correctness and Unicode conformance rather than aesthetic expression. It serves scholars of Semitic languages, biblical studies researchers, and digital archivists documenting Samaritan cultural heritage.

name: Noto Sans Saurashtra
classification_notes: A Noto extension covering Saurashtra, an abugida used by the Saurashtra community primarily in Tamil Nadu, India. The glyphs are constructed with the same regularised, neutral weight and clean geometry found across the Noto sans-serif family.
summary: A functional Noto font covering Saurashtra script, supporting digital preservation of a minority South Indian writing tradition.
description: Noto Sans Saurashtra provides Unicode-compliant support for a script used by the Saurashtra-speaking community of South India, where the language is classified as vulnerable. Its design is deliberately plain and systematic, prioritising correct glyph forms over visual distinction. Language preservation organisations, Indian regional government bodies, and digital humanities researchers will find it an essential tool.

name: Noto Sans Sharada
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to provide universal script coverage with a neutral, upright sans-serif form. Supports the Sharada script used historically for Sanskrit and Kashmiri.
summary: A functional sans-serif designed solely to provide clean, reliable coverage of the Sharada script within the Noto universal type system.
description: Noto Sans Sharada exists primarily as a preservation and accessibility tool, ensuring the historical Sharada script renders without tofu across digital platforms. Its design is deliberately neutral and unadorned, prioritising legibility over personality. It is best suited to scholarly, archival, or multilingual documents requiring Sharada script support.

name: Noto Sans Shavian
classification_notes: A Noto family member supporting the Shavian alphabet, a constructed phonetic script for English devised by George Bernard Shaw. The design follows the clean, unornamented Noto sans-serif approach.
summary: A minimal sans-serif providing digital coverage for the Shavian alphabet, a phonetic script invented for the English language.
description: Noto Sans Shavian serves the niche but dedicated community of Shavian script users, rendering the constructed alphabet cleanly across digital environments. Its single-weight offering reflects the script's limited contemporary use rather than any editorial ambition. It is a purely functional tool for linguists, enthusiasts, and any platform committed to complete Unicode coverage.

name: Noto Sans Siddham
classification_notes: Supports the Siddham script, an ancient Brahmic writing system used in East Asian Buddhist traditions. Follows the Noto project's consistent, neutral sans-serif framework.
summary: A neutral sans-serif within the Noto system, providing digital support for the rare and historically significant Siddham script.
description: Noto Sans Siddham addresses a highly specialised typographic need, rendering the Siddham script used in Vajrayana Buddhist manuscripts and ritual contexts across Japan, China, and Korea. The design adheres to the Noto project's principle of functional consistency, avoiding stylistic embellishment. Its primary audience is academic researchers, religious institutions, and platforms pursuing comprehensive Unicode script coverage.

name: Noto Sans SignWriting
classification_notes: An unusually complex Noto family member supporting SignWriting, a system for visually transcribing signed languages. The glyphs are highly pictographic and diagrammatic rather than conventional letterforms.
summary: A highly unconventional Noto font providing digital rendering for SignWriting, the visual notation system used to transcribe signed languages.
description: Noto Sans SignWriting is unlike almost any other typeface in the Noto collection, as its glyphs represent body positions, hand shapes, and movements rather than alphabetic characters. It serves deaf communities, linguists, and educators who use SignWriting to document and communicate in sign languages. The font is an engineering and accessibility achievement, though its visual character is entirely dictated by the notation system it encodes.

name: Noto Sans Sinhala
classification_notes: One of the more fully developed Noto sans-serif families, offering a nine-weight range for the Sinhala script used in Sri Lanka. The design balances the curved, looped forms of Sinhala with the Noto project's clean, screen-optimised aesthetic.
summary: A well-developed, nine-weight sans-serif supporting the Sinhala script, offering genuine typographic flexibility rare among scripts of its size.
description: Noto Sans Sinhala stands out within the Noto collection for its broad weight range, from Thin to Black, making it genuinely useful for multi-level typographic hierarchies in Sinhala-language publishing and digital interfaces. The design handles the distinctive circular and looped forms of the Sinhala script with clarity and consistency. It is well suited to editorial layouts, UI design, and body text in Sri Lankan digital and print contexts.

name: Noto Sans Sogdian
classification_notes: Provides coverage for the Sogdian script, an ancient Iranian alphabet that served as a major trade and diplomatic script along the Silk Road. Follows the standard Noto single-weight, neutral sans-serif approach.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif preserving digital access to the Sogdian script, a historically important writing system of the ancient Silk Road.
description: Noto Sans Sogdian fulfils a scholarly and archival function, ensuring the Sogdian script — which influenced the development of many later writing systems — is representable in modern digital environments. Its design is plain and functional, offering no stylistic interpretation beyond accurate glyph rendering. The font is primarily of use to historians, linguists, and digital humanities projects dealing with Central Asian textual heritage.

name: Noto Sans Sora Sompeng
classification_notes: A four-weight Noto family supporting the Sorang Sompeng script, created in the 20th century for the Sora language of eastern India. The modest weight range gives it slightly more typographic utility than single-weight Noto script fonts.
summary: A four-weight sans-serif supporting the Sorang Sompeng script, offering basic typographic range for the Sora language of India.
description: Noto Sans Sora Sompeng provides digital coverage for a relatively modern script, devised in the 1930s by Mangei Gomango to write the Sora language spoken in Odisha and Andhra Pradesh. With four weights available, it allows for modest but functional typographic hierarchy in Sora-language materials.
