It serves community publishing, educational resources, and platforms committed to supporting India's linguistic diversity.

name: Noto Sans Soyombo
classification_notes: Supports the Soyombo script, a complex abugida created in the 17th century by Mongolian scholar Zanabazar for writing Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Mongolian. A single-weight Noto design following the project's neutral, functional approach.
summary: A single-weight Noto sans-serif supporting the elaborate Soyombo script, a historically significant abugida created for Buddhist scriptural use in 17th-century Mongolia.
description: Noto Sans Soyombo ensures that the intricate, architecturally composed forms of the Soyombo script are renderable in contemporary digital environments. The script holds particular cultural significance as its primary symbol, the Soyombo emblem, features on the Mongolian national flag. The font serves scholars of Mongolian history, Buddhist studies, and any institution working to preserve Central Asian manuscript traditions digitally.

name: Noto Sans Sundanese
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to support the Sundanese script of West Java with clean, neutral letterforms. The design balances script-specific requirements with consistency across the broader Noto family.
summary: A clean, utilitarian sans-serif supporting the Sundanese script as part of Google's comprehensive Noto type system.
description: Noto Sans Sundanese provides reliable coverage of the Sundanese script used in West Java, Indonesia, sitting within the vast Noto family designed to eliminate tofu — the blank boxes that appear when a system lacks font support. Its four weights make it practical for both body text and heading use in digital interfaces. It is best suited to multilingual web and app projects requiring Sundanese script support.

name: Noto Sans Sunuwar
classification_notes: A minimal, single-weight sans-serif designed to support the Sunuwar script, a relatively recently standardised writing system used by the Koiч people of Nepal. Letterforms are kept neutral to serve functional script representation.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif providing essential digital coverage for the Sunuwar script of Nepal within the Noto family.
description: Noto Sans Sunuwar addresses the need for standardised digital representation of the Sunuwar script, used by the Koiч community in Nepal and India. As one of the more recently encoded scripts in Unicode, its inclusion in the Noto family ensures basic digital legibility and accessibility. Its single Regular weight limits typographic flexibility, making it primarily a functional tool for text rendering rather than expressive typography.

name: Noto Sans Syloti Nagri
classification_notes: Designed to support Sylheti Nagri, a historical script used to write the Sylheti language of Bangladesh and northeastern India. The design follows the neutral, consistent aesthetic of the wider Noto project.
summary: A neutral single-weight sans-serif supporting the Syloti Nagri script within Google's Noto family.
description: Noto Sans Syloti Nagri provides Unicode-compliant rendering of the Syloti Nagri script, historically used for the Sylheti language spoken across Bangladesh and the Sylhet region of India. The single Regular weight reflects the font's primary purpose as a functional text solution for digital environments rather than a full typographic system. It is most relevant to developers and publishers working with South Asian language content requiring this specific script.

name: Noto Sans Symbols
classification_notes: A wide-ranging symbols font providing coverage of miscellaneous Unicode symbol blocks including currency, arrows, mathematical operators, and dingbats. Its nine-weight range is unusually broad for a symbols typeface, enabling nuanced visual hierarchies.
summary: A comprehensive, nine-weight symbols font covering a broad range of Unicode miscellaneous symbol blocks for digital use.
description: Noto Sans Symbols fills critical gaps in multilingual typographic systems by providing consistent, neutral glyphs for symbols not covered by standard Latin fonts. Its extensive weight range from Thin to Black makes it unusually versatile for a symbols font, allowing designers to match symbol weight to accompanying text. It is particularly valuable in technical documentation, UI design, and any digital environment requiring consistent rendering of currency signs, arrows, and mathematical notation.

name: Noto Sans Symbols 2
classification_notes: A single-weight companion to Noto Sans Symbols, covering additional Unicode symbol ranges including Braille patterns, ancient symbols, and supplementary pictographic blocks. Designed to complement rather than duplicate Noto Sans Symbols.
summary: A single-weight supplementary symbols font extending Noto's coverage into Braille, ancient scripts, and additional Unicode pictographic ranges.
description: Noto Sans Symbols 2 was created to handle the overflow of Unicode symbol blocks not covered by the original Noto Sans Symbols, including Braille patterns, Mahjong tiles, domino tiles, and various ancient symbolic scripts. Its single Regular weight reflects its role as a fallback and gap-filler within comprehensive multilingual font stacks. Developers and accessibility-focused projects will find it essential for ensuring no symbol block renders as an empty box.

name: Noto Sans Syriac
classification_notes: Supports the Syriac script in its general Unicode encoding with a nine-weight range, following the neutral, legible conventions of the Noto project. The design accommodates the right-to-left directionality and connecting letterforms inherent to Syriac.
summary: A nine-weight sans-serif supporting the Syriac script with the consistent neutrality of the broader Noto type system.
description: Noto Sans Syriac provides digital typographic coverage for the Syriac script, a writing system used by Assyrian, Aramaic, and other Middle Eastern language communities. Its nine-weight range is generous for a script-specific Noto font, enabling proper typographic hierarchies in publications, apps, and interfaces serving Syriac-speaking audiences. The design handles right-to-left text flow and the connected cursive nature of Syriac letterforms within a clean, screen-optimised aesthetic.

name: Noto Sans Syriac Eastern
classification_notes: Specifically targets the Eastern variant of the Syriac script, used primarily by Eastern Assyrian Christian communities, with nine weights following the Noto project's neutral design principles. Distinct from Noto Sans Syriac in its script-variant specificity.
summary: A nine-weight sans-serif tailored to the Eastern dialect variant of the Syriac script, serving Eastern Assyrian communities digitally.
description: Noto Sans Syriac Eastern distinguishes itself from the base Noto Sans Syriac by addressing the specific letterform conventions of the Eastern Syriac dialect, associated with the Church of the East and Eastern Assyrian Christian communities. Its nine weights provide a full range of typographic options for publications, liturgical texts, and digital interfaces in this tradition. The separation into distinct fonts reflects Google's commitment to precise script fidelity within the Noto project.

name: Noto Sans Syriac Western
classification_notes: Addresses the Western variant of the Syriac script, associated with the Syriac Orthodox and Western Syriac liturgical traditions, with nine weights consistent with the Noto project's design language. Complements Noto Sans Syriac Eastern as a dialect-specific counterpart.
summary: A nine-weight sans-serif covering the Western Syriac script variant, serving Syriac Orthodox and Western liturgical communities with digital typography.
description: Noto Sans Syriac Western provides dedicated typographic support for the Western Syriac script variant used by the Syriac Orthodox Church and related Western Aramaic communities. Like its Eastern counterpart, it offers nine weights, giving publishers and developers a complete palette for hierarchical text setting in this tradition. The distinction from Noto Sans Syriac Eastern reflects the meaningful letterform and diacritical differences between the two script variants, ensuring authentic and accurate text rendering.

name: Noto Sans TC
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to harmonise Traditional Chinese characters with Latin and Cyrillic scripts at matching optical weights. The nine-weight range provides considerable typographic flexibility across CJK and Latin text.
summary: A comprehensive Traditional Chinese sans-serif designed to unify CJK and Latin scripts within a single harmonious family.
description: Noto Sans TC is part of Google's ambitious Noto project, which aims to support every Unicode script without visual conflict. Its nine weights make it equally suitable for body text, interface design, and editorial headlines across Traditional Chinese, Cyrillic, and Latin contexts. It is a reliable workhorse for digital products and documents requiring robust multilingual support.

name: Noto Sans Tagalog
classification_notes: A single-weight sans-serif designed specifically to support the Baybayin-derived Tagalog script alongside Latin, fulfilling Google's Noto mission of eliminating tofu across Unicode. The design is clean and utilitarian with no stylistic flourishes.
summary: A plain, functional sans-serif providing essential Unicode coverage for the historical Tagalog script.
description: Noto Sans Tagalog exists primarily to fill a Unicode gap, rendering the Baybayin-derived Tagalog script accurately alongside Latin text. With only a single weight available, its typographic application is limited, but it is invaluable for scholarly, archival, or cultural projects requiring authentic script representation. It is best used where script accuracy matters more than expressive typographic range.

name: Noto Sans Tagbanwa
classification_notes: A minimal, single-weight sans-serif supporting the rare Tagbanwa script of Palawan in the Philippines, alongside Latin characters, as part of the Noto universal coverage initiative. The letterforms are stripped back and functional.
summary: A utilitarian sans-serif providing rare Unicode support for the Tagbanwa script of the Philippines.
description: Noto Sans Tagbanwa addresses one of the more obscure corners of Unicode, supporting the indigenous Tagbanwa script used by communities in Palawan alongside standard Latin characters. As a single-weight typeface, it has little typographic versatility but serves an important role in digital preservation and cultural documentation. Its practical use is largely confined to linguistic, academic, and archival contexts.

name: Noto Sans Tai Le
classification_notes: A clean, single-weight sans-serif supporting the Tai Le script used by the Tai Le people of Yunnan, China, alongside Latin characters. The letterforms follow the geometric, angular nature of the Tai Le writing system.
summary: A functional single-weight sans-serif offering essential Unicode support for the Tai Le script of Yunnan.
description: Noto Sans Tai Le provides digital text rendering for the Tai Le script, a minority writing system used primarily in Yunnan Province, China, pairing it with Latin characters in a consistent optical style. Its single-weight offering limits typographic hierarchy but ensures that Tai Le text can be displayed without fallback glyphs on modern systems. It serves specialist linguistic, educational, and cultural documentation purposes.

name: Noto Sans Tai Tham
classification_notes: One of the more developed Noto scripts fonts, offering four weights for the ornate Tai Tham script used in northern Thailand and Laos, alongside Latin characters. The design accommodates Tai Tham's complex stacking diacritics with careful spacing.
summary: A four-weight sans-serif delivering reliable Unicode support for the complex Tai Tham script of northern Thailand and Laos.
description: Noto Sans Tai Tham is among the more capable entries in the Noto family for minority scripts, providing four weights that allow modest typographic hierarchy alongside its Latin complement. The Tai Tham script's elaborate stacking diacritics present significant technical challenges, and this typeface handles them with the characteristic Noto care for legibility and neutrality. It is best suited to educational materials, cultural publications, and digital platforms serving Tai Tham-speaking communities.

name: Noto Sans Tai Viet
classification_notes: A single-weight sans-serif supporting the Tai Viet script used by the Tai Dam, Tai Dón, and related communities across Vietnam and Laos, paired with Latin characters. The design is neutral and functional in keeping with the broader Noto system.
summary: A plain, single-weight sans-serif offering Unicode coverage for the Tai Viet script of mainland Southeast Asia.
description: Noto Sans Tai Viet supports the Tai Viet script, which encodes the languages of several Tai-speaking communities in Vietnam and Laos, alongside a standard Latin character set. Its single weight limits its typographic flexibility considerably, making it a tool for accessibility and preservation rather than expressive design. It is most useful in digital applications, government documents, and cultural resources targeting Tai Dam and related communities.

name: Noto Sans Takri
classification_notes: A single-weight sans-serif supporting the Takri script, a historical Brahmic script once used across the Punjab and Himachal Pradesh regions, alongside Latin characters. The design is spare and utilitarian, consistent with the Noto project's systematic approach.
summary: A minimal single-weight sans-serif providing digital support for the historical Takri script of the northwestern Indian subcontinent.
description: Noto Sans Takri supports the Takri script, a Brahmic writing system historically used in parts of Punjab, Himachal Pradesh, and Jammu, pairing it with Latin for mixed-text documents. Its single weight and functional design mean it is suited primarily to scholarly, archival, and digitisation projects rather than expressive typographic work. It plays a quiet but important role in preserving access to historical documents written in Takri.

name: Noto Sans Tamil
classification_notes: A full-range nine-weight sans-serif supporting the Tamil script alongside Latin, designed as part of the Noto project with careful attention to the optical harmony between Tamil letterforms and their Latin counterparts. The Tamil glyphs balance the script's characteristic curves with a clean, screen-optimised construction.
summary: A nine-weight Tamil and Latin sans-serif offering broad typographic range for one of the world's oldest living scripts.
description: Noto Sans Tamil is one of the more fully realised entries in the Noto family, covering Tamil — a script with a history stretching back over two millennia — with a nine-weight range that supports everything from light body text to bold display use. Its design harmonises the flowing, circular nature of Tamil letterforms with a contemporary sans-serif sensibility suited to digital interfaces and print. It is an excellent choice for applications, websites, and publications serving Tamil-speaking audiences across India, Sri Lanka, and the global diaspora.

name: Noto Sans Tamil Supplement
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to provide harmonious coverage of the Tamil Supplement Unicode block with a neutral, legible sans-serif construction. Designed primarily for completeness rather than stylistic expression.
summary: A utilitarian sans-serif covering the Tamil Supplement Unicode block as part of Google's comprehensive Noto typeface system.
description: Noto Sans Tamil Supplement exists to eliminate tofu — the blank boxes that appear when a system lacks a font for a given script — specifically for Tamil Supplement characters. With only a Regular weight, its application is narrow and largely technical, suited to developers and publishers needing complete Unicode coverage. It functions best as a fallback or supplementary font rather than a primary display or text choice.

name: Noto Sans Tangsa
classification_notes: Designed to support the Tangsa script used by the Tangsa people of Northeast India and Myanmar, with a clean geometric construction consistent with Noto's cross-script harmony. Offers a modest but functional weight range for a minority script.
summary: A clean, functional sans-serif supporting the Tangsa script, providing rare digital type coverage for a minority writing system of Northeast India and Myanmar.
description: Noto Sans Tangsa is a significant contribution to digital typography for an underserved community, offering four weights from Regular to Bold for the Tangsa script. Its neutral, legible construction aligns with the broader Noto family's design philosophy, ensuring visual consistency when mixed with Latin text. Primary use cases are documentation, educational materials, and digital publishing for Tangsa-language content.

name: Noto Sans Telugu
classification_notes: A full-featured sans-serif for the Telugu script, offering a comprehensive nine-weight range that mirrors the flexibility of major Latin Noto fonts. Telugu's complex conjunct characters and circular forms are rendered with careful optical balance.
summary: One of the most weight-complete Noto sans-serif fonts for a South Indian script, offering nine weights for versatile Telugu-language publishing.
description: Noto Sans Telugu supports the full Unicode Telugu block with a nine-weight range from Thin to Black, making it genuinely useful for editorial, UI, and branding work in Telugu-language contexts. The design handles Telugu's characteristically rounded, complex letterforms with clarity and consistency, maintaining legibility at both display and text sizes. It is a reliable workhorse for digital and print publishing targeting Telugu-speaking audiences.

name: Noto Sans Thaana
classification_notes: Provides full coverage of the Thaana script used for the Maldivian Dhivehi language, with a nine-weight range and right-to-left writing direction support. Construction follows Noto's neutral, harmonious design approach.
summary: A well-weighted sans-serif covering the Thaana script for Dhivehi, with nine weights making it one of the more complete Noto offerings for a right-to-left script.
description: Noto Sans Thaana supports the right-to-left Thaana script used to write Dhivehi, the official language of the Maldives, across a full nine-weight range. The neutral sans-serif approach integrates cleanly with Latin text, making it practical for bilingual interfaces, government documents, and digital publishing. Its weight breadth elevates it above a mere fallback font, giving designers genuine typographic hierarchy for Dhivehi-language work.

name: Noto Sans Thai
classification_notes: A comprehensive Thai-script sans-serif with nine weights, featuring letterforms that follow a loopless construction consistent with contemporary Thai sans-serif conventions. Designed for strong cross-script harmony with Latin characters.
summary: A versatile, loopless Thai sans-serif with a full nine-weight range, suited for modern digital interfaces and editorial work in Thai.
description: Noto Sans Thai uses a loopless construction — the more contemporary of the two dominant Thai sans-serif conventions — giving it a clean, modern character appropriate for UI design, digital publishing, and branding. Nine weights from Thin to Black provide the same typographic flexibility available to Latin designers, making it a strong choice for Thai-language products. It pairs naturally with other Noto Latin fonts, ensuring visual consistency in multilingual layouts.

name: Noto Sans Thai Looped
classification_notes: A nine-weight Thai sans-serif using a looped construction, which retains the traditional terminal loops found in Thai calligraphic tradition. This distinguishes it from its sibling Noto Sans Thai and makes it feel warmer and more familiar to some Thai readers.
summary: The looped variant of Noto's Thai sans-serif, retaining traditional calligraphic terminals for a warmer feel across a full nine-weight range.
description: Noto Sans Thai Looped offers the same broad weight range as Noto Sans Thai but with looped stroke terminals that echo traditional Thai calligraphy, making it more approachable and familiar for many Thai readers. This distinction matters in editorial and consumer-facing contexts where the looped style is culturally preferred. It is an excellent choice for body text, long-form reading, and any application where the looped Thai convention feels more natural.

name: Noto Sans Tifinagh
classification_notes: Covers the Tifinagh script used for writing Tamazight (Berber) languages, with a single Regular weight and a neutral geometric construction that suits the angular, geometric nature of the script. Part of Noto's mission to support minority and indigenous scripts.
summary: A clean, single-weight sans-serif for the Tifinagh script, providing essential digital type support for Tamazight and Berber languages.
description: Noto Sans Tifinagh supports the geometric Tifinagh script used for Tamazight and related Berber languages across North Africa, filling an important gap in digital typography for these communities. The single Regular weight limits its typographic versatility, but it serves its core purpose of ensuring readable, well-formed Tifinagh text on screen and in print. Its geometric letterforms align naturally with the angular structure of the script and integrate acceptably with Noto's Latin fonts.

name: Noto Sans Tirhuta
classification_notes: Supports the Tirhuta script, a Brahmic abugida used historically and in some contemporary contexts for the Maithili language of Nepal and India. A single Regular weight reflects the niche, preservation-oriented nature of this script's digital support.
summary: A single-weight sans-serif for the Tirhuta script, offering rare digital coverage for the Maithili-language writing tradition of Nepal and northern India.
description: Noto Sans Tirhuta addresses a significant gap in Unicode font coverage by supporting the Tirhuta script, which has limited digital type representation outside of specialised scholarly tools. The Regular weight is sufficient for basic text rendering, documentation, and academic publishing relating to the Maithili language. As with other minority-script Noto fonts, its primary value is in linguistic preservation and accessibility rather than stylistic flexibility.

name: Noto Sans Ugaritic
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to render Ugaritic cuneiform script without tofu. A single-weight technical face serving a niche ancient script used primarily in scholarly and archival contexts.
summary: A utilitarian Noto family member providing clean, reliable rendering of the ancient Ugaritic cuneiform script.
description: Noto Sans Ugaritic exists to solve a specific typographic gap: the faithful display of Ugaritic, an extinct Semitic script written in cuneiform. Its single Regular weight reflects the limited demand for stylistic variation in a script used almost exclusively by historians and linguists. It pairs silently with the broader Noto ecosystem for multilingual documents.

name: Noto Sans Vai
classification_notes: A member of the Noto Sans family designed to support the Vai syllabary, a West African script from Liberia. Single-weight only, prioritising legibility and Unicode completeness over typographic range.
summary: A clean, functional Noto typeface enabling reliable digital rendering of the Vai syllabary.
description: Noto Sans Vai addresses the underserved Vai script, historically used by the Vai people of Liberia and Sierra Leone. As a single-weight design, it prioritises Unicode coverage and visual consistency over expressive range. Its primary role is ensuring Vai text displays correctly within multilingual digital environments.

name: Noto Sans Vithkuqi
classification_notes: Supports Vithkuqi, a rare Albanian script invented in the 19th century, with a notably wider weight range than most Noto script-support faces. The multi-weight offering makes it slightly more versatile for typographic hierarchy.
summary: One of the more weight-flexible Noto script faces, supporting the rare historical Vithkuqi alphabet across four weights.
description: Noto Sans Vithkuqi is distinguished within the Noto script-support family by offering Regular, Medium, SemiBold, and Bold weights — a rarity for such a niche historical script. The Vithkuqi alphabet was created in the 1870s for the Albanian language and has seen renewed scholarly interest. This breadth of weights allows modest typographic hierarchy in documents focusing on Albanian cultural heritage.

name: Noto Sans Wancho
classification_notes: Designed to support the Wancho script, a recently created writing system for the Wancho language of Northeast India, formalised in 2001. A single-weight technical design consistent with the Noto project's coverage goals.
summary: A purpose-built Noto typeface supporting Wancho, a modern script created for a language of Northeast India.
description: Noto Sans Wancho provides digital typographic support for the Wancho script, which was formally encoded in Unicode 12.0. The design follows the restrained, neutral conventions of the Noto family, prioritising legibility and encoding completeness. It is most relevant for government, educational, and cultural materials serving the Wancho-speaking community.

name: Noto Sans Warang Citi
classification_notes: Covers the Warang Citi script, an abugida created in the 1950s for the Ho language of eastern India. Follows the standard Noto single-weight, neutral approach to underrepresented scripts.
summary: A faithful Noto rendering of Warang Citi, a mid-20th-century script designed for the Ho language of India.
description: Noto Sans Warang Citi supports a script invented by Lako Bodra specifically to write the Ho language, spoken by the Ho people of Jharkhand and Odisha. Its single-weight design aligns with the broader Noto philosophy of universal coverage without tofu. The typeface is most applicable in educational and governmental contexts serving Ho-language communities.

name: Noto Sans Yi
classification_notes: Renders the Yi syllabary, a script used by the Yi people of southwestern China, with the clean neutrality characteristic of the Noto family. The Yi script's distinctive geometric character gives this face an unexpectedly structured visual rhythm.
summary: A clean Noto typeface supporting the Yi syllabary, whose angular geometric forms give it a distinctive visual character.
description: Noto Sans Yi covers the standardised Yi syllabary used in China's Sichuan, Yunnan, Guizhou, and Guangxi provinces. The Yi script's inherently geometric, stroke-based construction translates naturally into the Noto sans-serif framework, producing a face with more visual presence than many of its script-support siblings. It serves publishers, educational institutions, and software platforms needing accurate Yi script rendering.

name: Noto Sans Zanabazar Square
classification_notes: Covers the Zanabazar Square script, a Mongolian Buddhist script from the 17th century, with the single-weight neutrality standard in Noto's historical script series. Highly specialised, serving primarily academic and religious digitisation projects.
summary: A niche but essential Noto typeface preserving the 17th-century Zanabazar Square script for digital scholarship.
description: Noto Sans Zanabazar Square supports a script created by the Mongolian monk and scholar Zanabazar for transcribing Sanskrit and Tibetan Buddhist texts. Added to Unicode 10.0, it remains among the most historically specialised scripts in the Noto portfolio. Its primary audience is academics, archivists, and digital humanities projects working with Mongolian Buddhist manuscripts.

name: Noto Serif
classification_notes: A comprehensive Noto serif covering Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek scripts with a broad nine-weight range. Designed for harmonious multilingual typesetting, drawing on classical serif conventions with sturdy, screen-optimised proportions.
summary: A robust, wide-ranging serif workhorse from the Noto family, built for reliable multilingual body text across Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts.
description: Noto Serif offers an impressive nine-weight range from Thin to Black, making it one of the more typographically complete members of the Noto family. Its transitional serif design provides comfortable, legible text across long-form editorial, academic, and digital publishing contexts. The support for Cyrillic Extended and Greek alongside Latin makes it a genuine choice for European multilingual documents requiring consistent visual harmony.

name: Noto Serif Ahom
classification_notes: Designed specifically to support the Ahom script, a historic abugida used in Assam, India. The single-weight design reflects the limited scope of the script's contemporary use and the preservation mission of the Noto project.
summary: A utilitarian serif designed solely to bring the endangered Ahom script into digital typesetting.
description: Noto Serif Ahom exists as part of Google's mission to eliminate typographic tofu — the blank boxes that appear when a font lacks a required character. With only a Regular weight, its application is narrow, serving scholars, archivists, and cultural preservation projects working with the historical Ahom language of northeast India.

name: Noto Serif Armenian
classification_notes: Covers the Armenian script with a full nine-weight range, offering refined stroke contrast and proportions sympathetic to Armenian letterform traditions. Latin support is included, making it viable for bilingual Armenian–Latin typography.
summary: A well-resourced serif spanning nine weights that gives Armenian-script typography serious typographic range.
description: Noto Serif Armenian is one of the more fully developed entries in the Noto serif family, offering everything from Thin to Black weights for sustained editorial and publishing work in Armenian. The design respects the visual rhythm and stroke characteristics of traditional Armenian letterforms while harmonising with Latin text. It suits bilingual books, academic publishing, and long-form digital reading.

name: Noto Serif Balinese
classification_notes: Supports the Balinese script, a traditional abugida used in Bali, Indonesia, in a single Regular weight. The design follows the curved, flowing characteristics inherent to Balinese letterforms while maintaining legibility on screen.
summary: A single-weight serif providing essential digital support for the Balinese script within the Noto project.
description: Noto Serif Balinese addresses the need for standardised digital rendering of the Balinese script, which remains in active use for religious, ceremonial, and cultural texts in Bali. Its single-weight offering limits versatility, but it serves its core purpose reliably for web content, educational material, and cultural documentation. The design integrates with other Noto family members for multilingual documents.

name: Noto Serif Bengali
classification_notes: Provides comprehensive coverage of the Bengali script across nine weights, with stroke modulation and proportions rooted in Bengali typographic tradition. Latin characters are harmonised to work alongside Bengali in bilingual settings.
summary: A thorough nine-weight serif that brings genuine typographic depth to Bengali-script publishing.
description: Noto Serif Bengali is among the most practically useful of the Noto script-specific serifs, with a weight range broad enough to support editorial hierarchies in Bengali-language books, newspapers, and digital platforms. The design honours the distinctive horizontal headline stroke of Bengali while achieving consistency with the wider Noto serif system. It is a dependable choice for any serious Bengali-language typographic project.

name: Noto Serif Devanagari
classification_notes: Offers a full nine-weight range for Devanagari, the most widely used script in South Asia, with careful attention to the characteristic shirorekha headline and balanced stroke contrast. Designed to harmonise with Latin serif companions in bilingual layouts.
summary: A thorough and well-executed serif for Devanagari that covers the full weight spectrum needed for professional publishing.
description: Noto Serif Devanagari is one of the most important entries in the Noto family given Devanagari's role as the script for Hindi, Marathi, Sanskrit, and dozens of other languages. The nine-weight range makes it suitable for everything from body text to headlines in books, newspapers, and digital products across South Asia. Its careful design makes it a reliable workhorse for any multilingual project requiring Devanagari and Latin in close proximity.

name: Noto Serif Display
classification_notes: A dedicated display variant of the Noto Serif family with enhanced contrast and refined detailing suited to large sizes, covering Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek scripts. The higher stroke contrast and more pronounced serifs distinguish it clearly from the text-optimised Noto Serif siblings.
summary: Noto Serif Display elevates the Noto serif system into headline territory with sharper contrast and more refined detailing for Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Where the standard Noto Serif is engineered for sustained reading, Noto Serif Display is optimised for impact at large sizes, making it appropriate for editorial headlines, poster work, and display applications in Cyrillic and Greek scripts. The increased stroke contrast gives it a more classical, high-quality feel that suits cultural, editorial, and publishing contexts. Its nine-weight range provides substantial flexibility for typographic hierarchies.

name: Noto Serif Dives Akuru
classification_notes: Supports the Dives Akuru script, a rare historical script from the Maldives, in a single Regular weight. As one of the more obscure scripts in the Noto project, it fulfils a preservation role rather than a broad publishing function.
summary: A rare single-weight serif preserving the endangered Dives Akuru script of the Maldives in digital form.
description: Noto Serif Dives Akuru is a highly specialised typeface serving linguists, historians, and cultural archivists working with a script that was historically used in the Maldives but has largely fallen out of everyday use. Its single weight reflects the limited scope of contemporary demand rather than any deficiency in the design. Like all Noto fonts, it is built to integrate with the broader family for multilingual documents.

name: Noto Serif Dogra
classification_notes: Provides digital support for Dogra, a script historically used in the Jammu region of South Asia, in a single Regular weight. The design follows the angular and distinctive stroke patterns of Dogra while maintaining readability at standard text sizes.
summary: A utilitarian single-weight serif bringing the historical Dogra script of Jammu into the digital Noto ecosystem.
description: Noto Serif Dogra supports a script once widely used for the Dogri language in the Jammu region, now included in Unicode as part of broader efforts to preserve South Asian scripts digitally. Its single-weight design serves the needs of cultural documentation, academic research, and regional identity projects rather than mainstream publishing. It functions reliably within mixed-script Noto documents wherever Dogra characters are required.

name: Noto Serif Ethiopic
classification_notes: Designed to harmonise Ethiopic script with Latin text, featuring upright, regularised letterforms suited to extended reading. The wide weight range makes it versatile across editorial and body text applications.
summary: A carefully constructed serif covering Ethiopic and Latin scripts with a broad weight range for serious text work.
description: Noto Serif Ethiopic is part of Google's Noto project, designed to eliminate tofu across the world's writing systems. Its regularised Ethiopic letterforms sit comfortably alongside Latin text, making it a reliable choice for multilingual publishing, academic work, and long-form reading. The nine-weight range gives designers meaningful flexibility from delicate display use to dense body copy.

name: Noto Serif Georgian
classification_notes: Covers the Georgian Mkhedruli script alongside Latin, with stroke contrast and proportions that echo classical serif conventions. The full nine-weight range supports both text and display settings.
summary: A well-considered serif typeface unifying Georgian and Latin scripts across a comprehensive weight range.
description: Noto Serif Georgian brings the Noto project's commitment to typographic harmony to the Georgian script, a writing system with distinctive rounded, looping forms. The design balances the organic character of Mkhedruli with the structured conventions of Latin serif typography. It suits multilingual publishing, government documents, and any project requiring reliable Georgian text support.

name: Noto Serif Grantha
classification_notes: Covers the rare and scholarly Grantha script used primarily in South Indian Sanskrit manuscripts, with a single Regular weight reflecting its specialised academic purpose. Letterforms follow traditional manuscript conventions with modest stroke contrast.
summary: A specialised serif typeface serving the rare Grantha script used in South Indian Sanskrit scholarship.
description: Noto Serif Grantha addresses one of the more obscure entries in the Noto collection, supporting a script historically used to write Sanskrit in southern India. Available only in Regular weight, it is purpose-built for academic, religious, and archival contexts rather than general design use. Its inclusion in the Noto family reflects the project's ambition to leave no writing system without digital typographic support.

name: Noto Serif Gujarati
classification_notes: Supports the Gujarati script with stroke modulation informed by traditional calligraphic practice, harmonised with Latin serif conventions across nine weights. The design handles the distinctive horizontal headline stroke of Gujarati with care.
summary: A thoughtfully unified serif covering Gujarati and Latin scripts with a full nine-weight range.
description: Noto Serif Gujarati applies the Noto project's cross-script harmonisation principles to Gujarati, one of India's major writing systems. The typeface respects the script's calligraphic origins while producing letterforms that function reliably in digital text settings. Its broad weight range makes it suitable for newspapers, books, and interface design serving Gujarati-speaking audiences.

name: Noto Serif Gurmukhi
classification_notes: Covers the Gurmukhi script used for Punjabi alongside Latin, with stroke contrast that reflects traditional pen-drawn letterforms. Nine weights provide strong versatility for both editorial and interface use.
summary: A reliable multiscript serif unifying Gurmukhi and Latin type across a wide spectrum of weights.
description: Noto Serif Gurmukhi supports the script of the Punjabi language, bringing consistent typographic quality to a writing system that has historically been underserved in digital environments. The letterforms balance fidelity to Gurmukhi calligraphic tradition with the regularisation needed for screen and print text. It serves publishing, education, and interface projects directed at Punjabi-reading audiences.

name: Noto Serif HK
classification_notes: A Traditional Chinese serif typeface covering Hong Kong character standards, with stroke contrast and structure following classical Ming-style conventions. Includes Cyrillic and Latin support for multilingual publishing.
summary: A Ming-style serif typeface optimised for Traditional Chinese text as used in Hong Kong, with broad weight support.
description: Noto Serif HK covers the Hong Kong SCS character set with the structured stroke contrast of classical Ming-style Chinese typography. Its eight weights from ExtraLight to Black make it adaptable across editorial, branding, and interface work in Hong Kong and broader Traditional Chinese contexts. The inclusion of Cyrillic and Latin extends its use to multilingual documents and international publishing.

name: Noto Serif Hebrew
classification_notes: A right-to-left serif covering Hebrew alongside Latin, with proportions and weight distribution that honour Hebrew typographic tradition while harmonising with Noto's broader design language. Nine weights serve a wide range of text and display applications.
summary: A considered Hebrew serif typeface offering broad weight coverage and strong harmony with Latin companions.
description: Noto Serif Hebrew tackles the distinct challenges of right-to-left typography, producing Hebrew letterforms with appropriate stroke contrast and rhythm rooted in traditional models. The nine-weight range supports everything from light display settings to dense body copy, and the Latin pairing makes it practical for bilingual Israeli publishing, academic work, and digital products. It is one of the more typographically mature entries in the Noto Serif family.

name: Noto Serif Hentaigana
classification_notes: Covers Hentaigana, a set of historical Japanese kana variants derived from cursive man'yōgana and largely obsolete since the Meiji era standardisation, with eight weights suggesting archival and scholarly display applications. The forms are fluid and calligraphic by nature.
summary: A specialised typeface digitising Hentaigana, the historical pre-modern Japanese kana variants, for scholarly and archival use.
description: Noto Serif Hentaigana addresses a highly specialised need, providing digital access to the variant kana forms used in Japanese texts before the Meiji-era script standardisation of the late nineteenth century. Its calligraphic, cursive letterforms make it unsuitable for general design work but invaluable for historians, archivists, and researchers engaging with pre-modern Japanese manuscripts. The eight-weight range is unusual for such a narrow-use script and hints at potential decorative or expressive applications.

name: Noto Serif JP
classification_notes: Designed to harmonise Japanese kanji, kana, and Latin characters within a unified serif framework, with stroke contrast and terminal details that respect traditional Japanese typographic conventions. Part of Google's Noto project aimed at eliminating tofu across all scripts.
summary: A comprehensive Japanese serif that unifies CJK and Latin characters within a single, harmonious type system.
description: Noto Serif JP is built for long-form Japanese text, offering eight weights that give designers flexibility across editorial, publishing, and digital contexts. Its design carefully balances traditional calligraphic stroke conventions with the demands of screen rendering. As part of the broader Noto family, it ensures visual consistency when mixing Japanese with Latin or Cyrillic text.

name: Noto Serif KR
classification_notes: Adapts serif typographic conventions to Korean Hangul alongside Latin and Cyrillic scripts, maintaining stroke contrast and structural consistency across writing systems. Part of the Noto project, designed to provide universal script coverage without visual discord.
summary: A Korean serif typeface that integrates Hangul with Latin and Cyrillic in a coherent, multi-script type system.
description: Noto Serif KR brings consistent typographic tone to Korean text, making it well suited to publishing, editorial design, and any project requiring reliable multilingual composition. Its eight weights offer meaningful range for establishing typographic hierarchy in Korean-language documents. The design respects Hangul's structural geometry while aligning with the broader Noto serif aesthetic.

name: Noto Serif Kannada
classification_notes: Applies serif conventions to the Kannada abugida script, with modulated stroke contrast and carefully drawn headline and vowel markers that reflect traditional Kannada letterform construction. Supports an extensive weight range for versatile typographic use.
summary: A dedicated Kannada serif offering broad weight coverage and faithful adherence to the script's traditional letterform conventions.
description: Noto Serif Kannada addresses the need for quality, screen-optimised typography in the Kannada script, which is widely used across Karnataka and the Kannada-speaking diaspora. Its nine weights make it practical for both body text and display use in publishing, web, and app contexts. The typeface integrates cleanly with Latin and Latin Extended characters for multilingual documents.

name: Noto Serif Khitan Small Script
classification_notes: A scholarly typeface designed specifically to represent the Khitan Small Script, a largely undeciphered historical writing system used by the Khitan people of northeast Asia. Available only in Regular weight, reflecting its specialist, archival purpose.
summary: A rare and highly specialist typeface encoding the historical Khitan Small Script for scholarly and archival use.
description: Noto Serif Khitan Small Script exists primarily to support academic, linguistic, and historical research involving this ancient writing system. With a single Regular weight and limited practical application outside specialist contexts, it is an archival tool rather than a general-purpose typeface. Its inclusion in the Noto project reflects Google's commitment to encoding every documented human script.

name: Noto Serif Khmer
classification_notes: Translates serif typographic principles into the rounded, loop-heavy structure of the Khmer script, with modulated stroke contrast adapted to Khmer's distinctive above-baseline vowel markers and subscript consonants. Offers a wide weight range for both text and display use.
summary: A well-considered Khmer serif that applies modulated contrast and broad weight coverage to one of Southeast Asia's most visually complex scripts.
description: Noto Serif Khmer provides reliable typographic quality for Cambodian-language publishing, digital media, and government communications. The Khmer script's intricate stacking vowels and subscripts demand careful spacing and vertical rhythm decisions, all of which the typeface handles with consistency. Nine weights make it adaptable across headline and body text applications.

name: Noto Serif Khojki
classification_notes: Designed to support the Khojki script, a historical mercantile script used by the Khoja community of South Asia, with serif detailing that aligns with Noto's broader typographic conventions. Limited to four weights, reflecting the script's specialist audience.
summary: A focused serif typeface for the Khojki script, serving a historically significant but specialist South Asian writing system.
description: Noto Serif Khojki was developed to digitally preserve and support the Khojki script, traditionally used in religious and community manuscripts of the Khoja people. Its four weights provide modest flexibility for both text and display use within this niche context. The typeface contributes to the broader mission of the Noto project to ensure no script community is left without quality digital typography.

name: Noto Serif Lao
classification_notes: Brings serif typographic structure to the Lao script, balancing the script's characteristically curved, closed letterforms with modulated stroke contrast drawn from traditional Lao manuscript conventions. A full nine-weight range supports diverse typographic applications.
summary: A capable Lao serif with a comprehensive weight range, suited to both long-form text and display use in Lao-language contexts.
description: Noto Serif Lao provides Lao-script typography with the consistency and quality needed for publishing, web, and official documentation. The Lao script shares structural similarities with Thai but has its own distinct letterform conventions, which this typeface respects throughout its design. Integration with Latin and Latin Extended characters supports bilingual Lao–English typesetting.

name: Noto Serif Makasar
classification_notes: A scholarly typeface encoding the Makasar script, a traditional script from South Sulawesi, Indonesia, with a single Regular weight reflecting its archival and preservation purpose. Supports Latin and Latin Extended characters for contextual use alongside scholarly annotations.
summary: A preservation-focused typeface encoding the endangered Makasar script from South Sulawesi for scholarly and cultural documentation.
description: Noto Serif Makasar was created to digitally support the traditional Lontara-derived Makasar script, which has limited surviving manuscript usage and is considered at risk of further decline. Like other single-weight Noto scripts, its primary audience is academic researchers, cultural institutions, and communities working to preserve the script's heritage. Its availability within the Noto ecosystem ensures it meets Unicode standards and can be used in modern digital environments.

name: Noto Serif Malayalam
classification_notes: Designed to harmonise with the Noto type system, this face renders Malayalam script with carefully balanced stroke contrast and traditional letterform proportions. Latin glyphs complement the Malayalam characters in weight and rhythm across the full nine-weight range.
summary: A multi-weight serif designed to render Malayalam script faithfully alongside Latin, forming part of Google's universal Noto type system.
description: Noto Serif Malayalam covers the full range of Malayalam characters with thoughtful stroke contrast suited to extended reading. Its nine weights make it versatile for editorial and publishing work targeting Malayalam-speaking audiences. As part of the Noto project, it is engineered to reduce tofu — the blank rectangles that appear when a system lacks a required glyph.

name: Noto Serif Myanmar
classification_notes: Built to support the complex Myanmar script with appropriate stroke modulation and traditional letterform structure, this face follows the broader Noto design philosophy of neutral, legible serifs. Its nine weights offer considerable flexibility for both display and text use.
summary: A dedicated serif for Myanmar script, offering nine weights as part of the Noto project's mission to support every written language.
description: Noto Serif Myanmar addresses the typographic needs of Burmese and other Myanmar script users with carefully drawn letterforms that respect the script's curvilinear character. The nine-weight range supports everything from fine editorial text to bold headline use. Its inclusion in the Noto system ensures consistent cross-script typesetting when combined with other Noto families.

name: Noto Serif NP Hmong
classification_notes: This face supports Nyiakeng Puachue Hmong, a script with a very limited digital typeface ecosystem, alongside standard Latin. The design follows the restrained, functional Noto serif approach with moderate stroke contrast suited to text setting.
summary: One of very few digital typefaces to support Nyiakeng Puachue Hmong script, making it an essential resource for communities using this writing system.
description: Noto Serif NP Hmong fills a critical gap in Unicode coverage by providing a readable serif face for the Nyiakeng Puachue Hmong script. With four weights from Regular to Bold, it offers enough range for basic editorial hierarchy. It is a functional rather than expressive design, prioritising legibility and faithful script representation above stylistic personality.

name: Noto Serif Old Uyghur
classification_notes: Old Uyghur is a historical script written vertically from right to left, and this single-weight Noto face is among the only contemporary digital typefaces to support it. The design necessarily follows historical manuscript forms with minimal embellishment.
summary: An extraordinarily rare digital typeface supporting Old Uyghur, a historical script used across Central Asia, making ancient texts digitally accessible.
description: Noto Serif Old Uyghur is a specialist tool for scholars, historians, and linguists working with Old Uyghur manuscripts and inscriptions. Available in a single weight, it prioritises accurate glyph representation over typographic range. Its inclusion in the Noto project reflects Google's commitment to encoding and rendering every living and historical script in Unicode.

name: Noto Serif Oriya
classification_notes: Designed to render Odia (Oriya) script with appropriate stroke contrast and rounded characteristic letterforms of that writing system. Latin and Latin Extended support allows bilingual typesetting, and four weights provide basic typographic hierarchy.
summary: A reliable serif covering Odia script with Latin support, providing one of the more refined digital type solutions for this Indian script.
description: Noto Serif Oriya meets the needs of publishers, educators, and developers working with the Odia language, which is spoken by tens of millions of people in eastern India. The four available weights — Regular through Bold — cover the essentials for book and editorial typography. Its design respects the rounded, looped forms characteristic of Odia script while maintaining the visual consistency of the wider Noto system.

name: Noto Serif Ottoman Siyaq
classification_notes: Ottoman Siyaq Numbers is a highly specialised historical numeral system used in Ottoman administrative documents, and this single-weight face is effectively the only contemporary digital option for rendering them. It is a scholarly and archival tool rather than a general-purpose typeface.
summary: An exceptionally niche typeface supporting Ottoman Siyaq numeral script, serving historians and archivists working with Ottoman-era documents.
description: Noto Serif Ottoman Siyaq exists almost entirely to fill a Unicode block that would otherwise go unsupported in digital environments. It enables the faithful reproduction of Ottoman administrative notation for scholarly publications, archival databases, and digital humanities projects. With a single weight and no stylistic variation, it is purely a utility typeface for a very specific historical purpose.

name: Noto Serif SC
classification_notes: Noto Serif SC is a refined serif for Simplified Chinese with carefully considered stroke contrast that bridges Chinese calligraphic tradition and contemporary print typography. Latin and Cyrillic coverage is designed to harmonise optically with the Chinese glyphs, and the eight-weight range is unusually generous for a CJK serif.
summary: A high-quality serif for Simplified Chinese that combines a broad weight range with strong Latin and Cyrillic support, making it one of the most capable CJK serif typefaces available for free.
description: Noto Serif SC draws on the Song typeface tradition — the dominant model for Chinese serif typography — while applying the Noto system's commitment to cross-script harmony. Its eight weights, from ExtraLight to Black, make it suitable for everything from long-form editorial text to bold display headlines in Chinese-language publishing. The quality of its Latin companion glyphs means it performs well in bilingual documents without requiring a separate Latin typeface.

name: Noto Serif Sinhala
classification_notes: Sinhala script has distinctive rounded, looping letterforms, and this face renders them with appropriate stroke modulation and careful spacing across nine weights. Latin and Latin Extended coverage is included, enabling bilingual Sri Lankan typesetting.
summary: A well-developed nine-weight serif for Sinhala script, offering one of the most complete typographic solutions available for Sri Lanka's primary written language.
description: Noto Serif Sinhala covers the distinctive curvilinear forms of Sinhala script with the stroke contrast and spacing required for comfortable extended reading. Its nine weights — from Thin to Black — provide the full range needed for editorial, advertising, and digital publishing in Sinhala. Combined with other Noto fonts, it enables consistent multilingual typesetting across Sinhala, Latin, and other scripts without visual discord.

name: Noto Serif TC
classification_notes: Part of Google's Noto project, designed to harmonise Traditional Chinese characters with Latin and Cyrillic scripts within a unified serif framework. Offers an extensive weight range to support editorial and body text use across multilingual documents.
summary: A comprehensive Traditional Chinese serif designed to sit harmoniously alongside Latin and Cyrillic within a single, unified type family.
description: Noto Serif TC is built to eliminate tofu — the blank rectangles that appear when a system lacks the required glyphs — by providing thorough coverage of Traditional Chinese alongside Latin and Cyrillic scripts. Its extensive weight range from ExtraLight to Black makes it equally suited to long-form body text and display headlines. It is an essential workhorse for multilingual publishing, digital products, and any project requiring consistent typographic treatment across Chinese and Latin scripts.

name: Noto Serif Tamil
classification_notes: Designed as part of the Noto project to provide a harmonised serif typeface for the Tamil script alongside Latin, featuring letterforms that respect Tamil calligraphic traditions while maintaining optical consistency across weights. The broad weight range supports both text and display applications.
summary: A carefully considered Tamil serif that unifies the Tamil script with Latin in a single cohesive family spanning nine weights.
description: Noto Serif Tamil addresses the longstanding challenge of typographic consistency in Tamil-language publishing by providing letterforms that honour the script's curved, flowing character while aligning optically with its Latin companion. Nine weights from Thin to Black give designers considerable flexibility for both body text and headline use. It is particularly well suited to bilingual publishing, educational materials, and digital interfaces serving Tamil-speaking audiences.

name: Noto Serif Tangut
classification_notes: A specialised typeface within the Noto project covering the Tangut script, a complex logographic writing system used by the Tangut people of medieval China. Available only in Regular weight, reflecting the scholarly and archival focus of this release rather than broad typographic application.
summary: A rare and highly specialised serif covering the medieval Tangut logographic script, designed primarily for scholarly and archival use.
description: Noto Serif Tangut exists to serve linguists, historians, and publishers working with the Tangut script, a logographic system with thousands of characters used by the Tangut people between the 11th and 14th centuries. Its single Regular weight reflects its niche, preservation-focused remit rather than any ambition for broad editorial deployment. For anyone requiring digital typesetting of Tangut texts, it is effectively the only viable option.

name: Noto Serif Telugu
classification_notes: Part of the Noto serif series, providing a unified serif treatment for the Telugu abugida script alongside Latin, with letterforms informed by the script's characteristic rounded strokes and complex conjunct consonants. Nine weights ensure versatility across text and display contexts.
summary: A harmonised Telugu serif that brings nine weights of consistent, script-sensitive design to one of South India's major writing systems.
description: Noto Serif Telugu balances the distinctive rounded curves and intricate conjunct structures of the Telugu script with the visual rhythm expected of a professional serif text face. Its nine-weight range from Thin to Black makes it adaptable to everything from newspaper body text to display headlines in Telugu-language media. It pairs naturally with its Latin Noto Serif counterpart, making it a strong choice for bilingual South Indian publishing and digital products.

name: Noto Serif Thai
classification_notes: A Noto serif family designed to bring typographic coherence to Thai script alongside Latin, respecting the looped letterforms and vowel mark conventions of traditional Thai typography within a contemporary serif framework. The wide weight range accommodates diverse typographic contexts.
summary: A refined Thai serif that respects the script's looped tradition while providing a full nine-weight range for multilingual typographic consistency.
description: Noto Serif Thai is engineered to give Thai-language typography the same level of craft and weight flexibility typically associated with quality Latin serif families. Its letterforms preserve the looped style that defines traditional Thai type while achieving optical harmony with the accompanying Latin glyphs. The result is a dependable workhorse for Thai-language publishing, digital interfaces, and any bilingual project pairing Thai with Latin content.

name: Noto Serif Tibetan
classification_notes: Designed for the Tibetan script as part of the Noto project, this family reflects the distinctive horizontal headline stroke and stacked character structures of Tibetan calligraphy within a regularised serif framework. Nine weights provide uncommon flexibility for a script with limited prior digital type development.
summary: A disciplined Tibetan serif that brings rare weight diversity and typographic rigour to one of Asia's most visually distinctive scripts.
description: Noto Serif Tibetan translates the calligraphic conventions of the Tibetan script — including its characteristic headline strokes and complex stacking — into a consistent, screen-optimised serif family. The nine-weight range from Thin to Black is exceptional for Tibetan type, where even a single quality digital face has historically been difficult to find. It serves religious publishing, academic research, and digital platforms serving Tibetan-speaking communities.

name: Noto Serif Todhri
classification_notes: A highly specialised Noto typeface covering Todhri, an obscure historical script with very limited contemporary use, available only in a single Regular weight. Its inclusion in the Noto project reflects the initiative's commitment to universal script coverage rather than practical design versatility.
summary: An extremely specialised serif covering the Todhri script, existing primarily to fulfil the Noto project's mission of universal Unicode coverage.
description: Noto Serif Todhri is one of the more esoteric entries in the Noto family, providing digital type support for a script with very few users or contemporary applications. Its single Regular weight is sufficient for its archival and scholarly purpose, serving linguists and historians who require accurate digital representation of Todhri texts. Beyond this narrow specialism, its practical typographic application is minimal.

name: Noto Serif Toto
classification_notes: A Noto serif family supporting the Toto script, a relatively newly encoded writing system used by the Toto people of West Bengal, available in four weights. Its inclusion reflects the Noto project's commitment to covering endangered and minority scripts alongside Latin.
summary: A conscientious serif supporting the endangered Toto script alongside Latin, offering four weights for a writing system with very few prior digital resources.
description: Noto Serif Toto provides digital type infrastructure for the Toto script, a minority writing system used by a small indigenous community in the India–Bhutan border region. Four weights from Regular to Bold give it modest but meaningful typographic range for a script that has only recently received Unicode encoding. Its primary value lies in cultural preservation and enabling digital communication for Toto-language communities.

name: Noto Serif Vithkuqi
classification_notes: Designed to support the Vithkuqi script, an indigenous Albanian alphabet invented in the 19th century, alongside Latin. The serif forms are clean and legible, suited to scholarly and archival use.
summary: A Noto serif supporting the rare Vithkuqi script alongside Latin, built for scholarly preservation of an obscure Albanian writing system.
description: Noto Serif Vithkuqi is part of Google's Noto project, which aims to achieve visual harmony across all the world's scripts. Its primary purpose is to support the Vithkuqi alphabet, used historically to write Albanian, making it a specialist tool for linguists, historians, and digital archivists. The Latin accompaniment follows standard Noto serif conventions, keeping the overall feel neutral and readable.

name: Noto Serif Yezidi
classification_notes: Supports the Yezidi script, used by the Yazidi religious minority, with clean upright serif letterforms that balance cultural specificity with broad legibility. Part of the wider Noto harmonisation effort.
summary: A Noto serif covering the Yezidi script, serving a historically underrepresented writing system with careful, legible letterforms.
description: Noto Serif Yezidi brings the Yazidi script into the Noto family, providing digital typographic support for a community whose writing system has rarely appeared in modern typefaces. The design prioritises clarity and fidelity to the script's conventions, making it valuable for cultural documentation, religious texts, and academic publishing. Latin coverage follows Noto's consistent, neutral approach.

name: Noto Traditional Nushu
classification_notes: Supports Nüshu, the only known writing system used exclusively by women, historically practised in Hunan province, China. The forms are elongated and flowing, reflecting the script's brushwork origins.
summary: A specialist Noto face supporting Nüshu, the world's only known women-exclusive script, preserving a rare Chinese literary tradition in digital form.
description: Noto Traditional Nüshu addresses one of the most culturally distinctive writing systems in existence — Nüshu was passed secretly among women in Jiangyong County and remained largely unknown outside China until the late 20th century. The typeface captures the script's characteristically narrow, elongated strokes that echo brush calligraphy, while the multi-weight range from Light to Bold gives it genuine typographic utility. It is an essential resource for scholars, cultural institutions, and publishers working to preserve this heritage.

name: Noto Znamenny Musical Notation
classification_notes: A single-weight face designed solely to render Znamenny musical notation, a system of neume-like symbols used in Russian Orthodox chant manuscripts. Highly specialised with no practical general-purpose application.
summary: A uniquely specialised Noto face encoding Znamenny neumes, the ancient notation system of Russian Orthodox chant, with no counterpart in mainstream typography.
description: Noto Znamenny Musical Notation exists for a single precise purpose: enabling digital reproduction of Znamenny chant scores, a tradition stretching back to medieval Russia. The symbols bear no resemblance to Western staff notation and require dedicated encoding and rendering support. Its audience is almost entirely musicologists, Orthodox Church publishers, and digital humanities researchers, making it one of the most niche typefaces in the entire Noto collection.

name: Nova Cut
classification_notes: Nova Cut features letterforms that simulate engraved or carved lettering, with inline cuts giving each character a three-dimensional chiselled quality. A purely decorative single-weight display face.
summary: Nova Cut simulates chiselled stone engraving with its inline cut detailing, lending headlines a classical, monumental quality.
description: The face belongs to the Nova family, a set of related decorative display designs sharing broadly geometric underpinnings. Nova Cut's defining feature is the fine incised line running through the strokes, evoking the look of letterforms carved into marble or metal. It suits ceremonial headings, logotypes, and poster work where a sense of permanence or classical prestige is desired, though its single weight limits versatility.

name: Nova Flat
classification_notes: Nova Flat shares the Nova family's geometric skeleton but removes contrast and detail, producing uniformly stroked, flat letterforms with a clean, almost stencil-like simplicity. Single weight only.
summary: Nova Flat strips the Nova family down to bare geometric bones, offering a clean, flat-stroked display face with a quietly industrial character.
description: Where other Nova variants add ornamentation, Nova Flat takes the opposite approach, relying entirely on the underlying geometric structure for its appeal. The result is a face that sits somewhere between a display sans and a stencil design, with open, even strokes that reproduce well at large sizes. It works best in contexts demanding bold, unadorned geometry — packaging, posters, and short-form brand headlines — though the single weight restricts its range significantly.

name: Nova Mono
classification_notes: Nova Mono is a monospaced face built on geometric principles, with circular bowls and consistent stroke widths that give it a clean, technical appearance. Supports both Latin and Greek scripts.
summary: Nova Mono is a geometric monospace with Greek support, offering a clean, consistent rhythm suited to code and technical typesetting.
description: The typeface applies the Nova family's geometric sensibility to a fixed-width grid, producing letterforms that feel orderly and precise. Greek script coverage makes it a useful choice for scientific and mathematical typesetting where Greek characters frequently appear alongside Latin text. As a coding font it is functional rather than exceptional, but its geometric clarity gives it a slightly more characterful feel than many utilitarian monospaces.

name: Nova Oval
classification_notes: Nova Oval introduces rounded, almost bubble-like terminals and joints to the Nova family's geometric structure, softening the forms considerably and lending them a playful, retro quality. Single weight only.
summary: Nova Oval rounds off the Nova family's geometry into soft, bubble-like letterforms with a distinctly playful, retro personality.
description: The face takes the same structural skeleton shared across the Nova series and inflates the terminals and stroke junctions into smooth oval forms, creating a look that recalls 1970s display typography and friendly consumer branding. It functions best at large display sizes where the rounded detailing is clearly visible and can carry a headline or logotype with warmth and character. The single-weight limitation makes it unsuitable for running text or any application requiring typographic hierarchy.

name: Nova Round
classification_notes: Nova Round features softly rounded terminals and a geometric structure, giving it a friendly, approachable character. It is part of the Nova family designed by Wojciech Kalinowski.
summary: A softly rounded geometric display face with a warm, approachable personality.
description: Nova Round's uniformly rounded stroke endings lend it a gentle, almost toy-like quality that suits playful branding, children's publishing, and informal display contexts. Its geometric underpinning keeps it structured enough for short headline use. Part of the broader Nova family, it works best at larger sizes where its rounded details are clearly visible.

name: Nova Script
classification_notes: Nova Script is an ornate, flowing script face with calligraphic flourishes and high contrast strokes, clearly designed for decorative display use. It shares the Nova family's experimental spirit.
summary: An ornate calligraphic script built for decorative headlines and display use.
description: Nova Script draws on classic penmanship traditions, featuring sweeping curves, high contrast between thick and thin strokes, and elaborate letterforms. It is suited to invitations, luxury packaging, and decorative poster work where a handcrafted, formal feel is required. Its complexity makes it unsuitable for body text or small sizes.

name: Nova Slim
classification_notes: Nova Slim is a narrow, upright geometric sans with a clean, architectural quality derived from its consistent stroke weight and condensed proportions. It is designed for display use at larger sizes.
summary: A slender geometric display sans with a cool, architectural economy of form.
description: Nova Slim's tight proportions and even stroke weight give it a modernist, structured appearance suited to headlines, signage, and editorial display contexts. Its narrow footprint makes it efficient for constrained layouts while retaining visual impact. Like its Nova siblings, it is limited to a single weight, restricting typographic flexibility.

name: Nova Square
classification_notes: Nova Square is built on strictly rectilinear, squared-off forms with minimal curves, giving it a distinctly technical and modular character. It reads as a digitally inspired geometric sans.
summary: A rigidly squared geometric sans that channels a digital, modular aesthetic.
description: Nova Square replaces organic curves with near-right-angle constructions, producing a typeface that feels engineered rather than drawn. This makes it well suited to technology branding, sci-fi-themed design, and industrial signage. Its single-weight offering and niche personality limit its broader application.

name: Numans
classification_notes: Numans is a clean, low-contrast humanist sans-serif with open apertures and a quiet, readable character. It is available only in Regular weight, limiting its versatility.
summary: A quiet, clean humanist sans-serif that prioritises readability over personality.
description: Numans offers open letterforms and balanced proportions that make it serviceable for screen interfaces and running text at modest sizes. Its neutral disposition means it rarely calls attention to itself, which suits functional design contexts. The single-weight restriction is a significant drawback for any serious typographic system.

name: Nunito
classification_notes: Nunito is a well-crafted rounded sans-serif with balanced, humanist proportions and consistently rounded terminals across a broad weight range. It has become one of Google Fonts' most popular typefaces for UI and branding work.
summary: One of the most popular rounded sans-serifs on Google Fonts, offering warmth and legibility across a wide weight range.
description: Nunito's uniformly rounded stroke endings give it an approachable, friendly tone without sacrificing readability at text sizes, making it a go-to choice for apps, dashboards, and consumer-facing interfaces. Its extensive weight range from ExtraLight to Black enables full typographic hierarchies within a single family. Cyrillic support broadens its usefulness for multilingual projects.

name: Nunito Sans
classification_notes: Nunito Sans is a companion to Nunito that retains rounded terminals but adopts squarer, more neutral proportions, bringing it closer to a conventional grotesque in feel. It similarly spans a broad weight range.
summary: A versatile rounded grotesque that balances friendliness with the neutrality needed for serious interface work.
description: Nunito Sans trades some of its sibling's overt warmth for a more structured, utilitarian character, making it better suited to corporate interfaces, editorial body text, and data-heavy screens. Its wide weight range and Cyrillic support make it a capable workhorse for multilingual digital products. It sits comfortably between expressive and functional, fitting a broad range of design briefs.

name: Nuosu SIL
classification_notes: Nuosu SIL is a specialised typeface designed specifically for the Yi script used in Sichuan, China, developed by SIL International. Its Latin characters are functional but secondary to its primary purpose of supporting the Yi writing system.
summary: A specialised typeface created by SIL International to support the Yi script of Sichuan, China.
description: Nuosu SIL fills a critical gap in Unicode coverage by providing a clean, readable implementation of the Yi syllabic script alongside Latin characters. It is intended primarily for linguistic, educational, and governmental use rather than decorative or branding purposes. Its inclusion on Google Fonts is a valuable contribution to digital language preservation and multilingual accessibility.

name: Odibee Sans
classification_notes: A bold, condensed display typeface with a retro video game aesthetic, featuring stark geometric forms and minimal stroke variation. Designed to evoke pixel-era digital lettering in a vector format.
summary: A retro-styled display face with a distinct video game character, rendered in clean geometric forms.
description: Odibee Sans channels the spirit of early digital and arcade typography through angular, condensed letterforms. Its single weight limits versatility but makes it immediately striking for headlines, game interfaces, and nostalgic branding projects. Best deployed sparingly where a punchy, retro-tech personality is the goal.

name: Odor Mean Chey
classification_notes: A traditional Khmer typeface with serif-like decorative terminals suited to formal and classical Cambodian script usage. The Latin companion is minimal and secondary in character.
summary: A traditional Khmer serif designed for readable, dignified typesetting in the Cambodian script.
description: Odor Mean Chey is one of relatively few Khmer typefaces available on Google Fonts, making it a practical choice for multilingual documents requiring Cambodian script. Its classical forms suit editorial and governmental contexts, though its single weight restricts typographic hierarchy. The Latin glyphs serve a supporting role rather than standing independently.

name: Offside
classification_notes: A stylised display typeface with distinctive angled cuts and geometric construction, giving letterforms an athletic or futuristic slant. Stroke terminals are sharp and purposeful.
summary: An angular, athletic display face built on slanted geometric forms that project energy and modernity.
description: Offside takes its visual cues from sports typography and dynamic branding environments, with sheared terminals and a forward-leaning rhythm throughout. Its single weight makes it a one-trick performer, best reserved for headlines, sport-related identities, and event graphics. The extended Latin character set adds modest utility for broader European language coverage.

name: Oi
classification_notes: An elaborately decorative display typeface with heavy Victorian-inspired ornamentation, inline details, and highly expressive letterforms. The script supports Arabic and Cyrillic in addition to Latin, which is unusual for a face of this style.
summary: A lavishly ornamental Victorian display face with unexpected multilingual reach across Arabic, Cyrillic, and Latin scripts.
description: Oi revels in nineteenth-century decorative excess, with inline strokes, floral accents, and theatrical proportions that make it unmistakable at large sizes. Its support for Arabic and Cyrillic scripts is a remarkable achievement for a face of this character, broadening its appeal considerably. It suits poster work, luxury packaging, heritage branding, and any context calling for unapologetic decorative drama.

name: Ojuju
