There is little in the way of distinctive design personality, and its value is essentially utilitarian — filling a gap in multilingual web typography. It is best deployed where Khmer script rendering is the primary requirement rather than aesthetic expression.

name: Sigmar
classification_notes: Sigmar is a heavy, high-impact slab serif with chunky serifs, strong stroke contrast, and an outlined or inlined decorative construction that gives it a bold, retro-inflected character. It builds on the Sigmar One legacy with additional script support.
summary: A thick, punchy slab serif display face with retro energy, designed for headlines and posters that need immediate visual weight.
description: Sigmar delivers robust, no-nonsense impact through its heavy stroke weights, squared slab serifs, and slightly condensed proportions. The typeface carries a vintage American wood-type sensibility, making it well suited to poster design, display advertising, and brand identities that lean into bold, retro aesthetics. Its added support for Vietnamese extends its usefulness across Southeast Asian markets.

name: Sigmar One
classification_notes: Sigmar One is a single-weight, heavy display slab serif with rounded terminals and a solid, upright construction reminiscent of early advertising types. It has an accessible, friendly boldness rather than aggressive sharpness.
summary: A single-weight heavy slab serif with rounded terminals that delivers bold, friendly impact without feeling aggressive.
description: Sigmar One occupies the space between brash display slab and approachable rounded face, its solid strokes and gently rounded details giving it warmth alongside its visual weight. It is a reliable workhorse for web headlines, app landing pages, and logo treatments that need emphatic scale without sharp severity. Its single-weight limitation makes it a pure display tool rather than a text family.

name: Signika
classification_notes: Signika is a signage-inspired humanist sans-serif designed for legibility at small sizes and from a distance, with low stroke contrast, open apertures, and slightly quirky letterforms. It was conceived specifically to address legibility challenges in signage and UI contexts.
summary: A humanist sans designed from the ground up for signage and screen legibility, with open forms and a subtle warmth that works across a wide range of sizes.
description: Signika was developed with wayfinding and signage in mind, resulting in a typeface with unusually open apertures, careful spacing, and forms that resist confusion at both small and large scales. It performs equally well in interface design, long-form text, and heading hierarchies, offering a more characterful alternative to neutral grotesques. The family's range of weights makes it a practical, flexible workhorse for editorial and digital product work.

name: Signika Negative
classification_notes: Signika Negative is a companion to Signika, adapted for reversed-out (light on dark) display conditions through adjusted spacing and slightly modified forms that compensate for optical spread on dark backgrounds. It shares Signika's humanist construction and open apertures.
summary: The reversed-out sibling of Signika, engineered specifically for white-on-dark legibility with optically compensated forms and spacing.
description: Signika Negative addresses the well-known problem of stroke spread in reversed-out typography, where white letterforms on dark backgrounds appear heavier and tighter than their positive counterparts. Its adjusted metrics and subtle form modifications ensure that text remains equally legible whether light or dark backgrounds are used, making it a thoughtful choice for interfaces that switch between modes. It is best used in tandem with Signika for consistent cross-background typographic systems.

name: Silkscreen
classification_notes: Silkscreen is a bitmap-inspired pixel font rendered in vector format, with a rigid grid-based construction that references early LCD and video game screen typography. It is intentionally low-resolution in aesthetic.
summary: A crisp pixel-grid font that channels early LCD and video game aesthetics with deliberate, nostalgic charm.
description: Silkscreen was among the earliest pixel fonts designed for web use, capturing the look of low-resolution screen typography from the era of early computing and arcade games. Its strict grid-based construction gives it a technical, retro personality that suits gaming interfaces, tech branding, and creative projects with a digital-nostalgia theme. Outside of those specific aesthetic contexts it has limited versatility, functioning as a one-note novelty rather than a general-purpose typeface.

name: Simonetta
classification_notes: Simonetta is an elegant, high-contrast display serif with delicate hairline serifs and a refined, Renaissance-inflected character. Its extreme stroke contrast makes it suitable only for large display sizes, where its fragile details can be appreciated.
summary: A high-contrast Renaissance-flavoured display serif with delicate hairlines and an air of refined elegance suited to fashion and editorial contexts.
description: Simonetta's extreme contrast between thick and thin strokes gives it a fragile, luxurious quality that sits well in fashion, beauty, and high-end editorial settings. Available in Regular and Black weights, it offers range within a display context, though neither weight is appropriate for body text given the hairline detail that vanishes at smaller sizes. It is best deployed in large-scale headline and logo applications where its calligraphic refinement can be seen clearly.

name: Single Day
classification_notes: Single Day is a Korean display typeface with a casual, hand-drawn quality suited to expressive headlines. Its letterforms have a lively, informal character that bridges Korean and Latin scripts.
summary: A casual Korean display face with a hand-drawn energy that brings warmth and personality to headlines.
description: Single Day suits informal branding, posters, and editorial contexts where a friendly, expressive Korean typeface is needed. Its hand-drawn quality makes it feel approachable and lively rather than corporate. Best reserved for large display sizes where its character can read clearly.

name: Sintony
classification_notes: Sintony is a clean humanist sans-serif with open apertures and balanced proportions designed with screen legibility in mind. Its modest weight range and restrained detailing make it a serviceable text face for digital interfaces.
summary: A clean, readable humanist sans-serif built for comfortable on-screen text use.
description: Sintony's open letterforms and even spacing make it a dependable choice for body copy in web and app contexts. It lacks the personality of more distinctive humanist faces but performs reliably across paragraphs and UI labels alike. A sensible workhorse for straightforward digital projects.

name: Sirin Stencil
classification_notes: Sirin Stencil is a single-weight stencil typeface with prominent cuts and angular letterforms that give it a utilitarian, military-influenced aesthetic. Its construction is simple and direct, suited to short display text.
summary: A bold stencil display face with a utilitarian, industrial character drawn from military and signage traditions.
description: Sirin Stencil works well for poster headlines, packaging, and any context requiring a raw, stencilled aesthetic. Its single weight limits flexibility, but the strong visual identity compensates at large sizes. Best deployed sparingly for maximum graphic impact.

name: Sirivennela
classification_notes: Sirivennela is a sans-serif typeface designed to support the Telugu script alongside Latin, with clean, legible letterforms suited to body text use. Its design prioritises readability over stylistic distinction.
summary: A functional sans-serif designed to serve Telugu and Latin scripts with consistent, readable letterforms.
description: Sirivennela fills an important gap by providing a clean, modern sans-serif option for Telugu-language content on screen and in print. Its Latin companion is straightforward and unpretentious, keeping the two scripts visually harmonious. A practical choice for multilingual South Indian publishing and web projects.

name: Six Caps
classification_notes: Six Caps is an extremely condensed, single-weight all-caps sans-serif with minimal stroke contrast and tight proportions. It is designed purely for large-scale display use where maximum vertical height and tight column widths are required.
summary: An ultra-condensed all-caps sans-serif built for dramatic, space-efficient headlines at large scale.
description: Six Caps excels in poster design, editorial headers, and any scenario where tall, tightly packed lettering is needed. Its extreme compression makes it unsuitable for body text but highly effective as a graphic element. The single weight and all-caps design keep it firmly in one-trick territory, but it executes that trick convincingly.

name: Sixtyfour
classification_notes: Sixtyfour is a variable monospace typeface with a retro digital aesthetic evoking early LED and dot-matrix displays, offering axes that allow adjustment of its segmented character. Its mathematical symbol support extends its technical utility.
summary: A variable monospace face with a retro LED display aesthetic, offering expressive control over its segmented letterforms.
description: Sixtyfour draws directly from the visual language of dot-matrix and seven-segment displays, making it ideal for tech-themed branding, gaming interfaces, and nostalgic design projects. Its variable axes allow designers to push or pull the segmented aesthetic, giving it more range than a simple novelty face. Mathematical character support broadens its application into technical and scientific contexts.

name: Sixtyfour Convergence
classification_notes: Sixtyfour Convergence is a companion to Sixtyfour, sharing the same retro segmented display concept but with a design focus on converging stroke terminals that add a distinct geometric tension. It supports Latin Extended and mathematical symbols.
summary: A geometric, segmented monospace variant that pushes the retro display aesthetic with converging stroke terminals.
description: Sixtyfour Convergence offers a more angular and tense interpretation of the LED display concept compared to its sibling, making it a stronger choice when a sharper, more futuristic edge is needed. Like Sixtyfour, it suits tech branding, gaming, and scientific editorial contexts. The two fonts work naturally as a family when varied weights or moods are required within the same project.

name: Skranji
classification_notes: Skranji is a bold display typeface with angular, blackletter-influenced forms and a rugged, tribal character. Its sharp strokes and aggressive construction give it a distinctly primal, fantasy-inspired feel.
summary: An angular, blackletter-inflected display face with a rugged, tribal energy suited to fantasy and heavy metal aesthetics.
description: Skranji draws on blackletter traditions but pushes them toward a more aggressive, almost runic visual territory, making it well suited to gaming, heavy metal, fantasy branding, and event posters. Its two weights offer some flexibility, though both sit firmly in display territory and are unsuitable for running text. A polarising choice that commits fully to its dramatic character.

name: Slabo 13px
classification_notes: Slabo 13px is a serif typeface engineered specifically for rendering at 13 pixels on screen, with carefully tuned proportions and spacing to ensure legibility at that precise size. Its design sacrifices conventional optical harmony in favour of pixel-perfect clarity.
summary: A serif typeface designed with singular purpose: to render cleanly and legibly at exactly 13 pixels on screen.
description: Slabo 13px is an unusual specialism in type design — a face built entirely around a single pixel size rather than broad optical flexibility. Designed by John Hudson of Tiro Typeworks, it excels in small UI text, footnotes, and interface labels where screen rendering at 13px is fixed. It is not intended for print or large display settings.

name: Slabo 27px
classification_notes: Slabo 27px follows the same philosophy as its sibling, but tuned for rendering at 27 pixels, giving it slightly more expressive letterforms that remain tightly controlled for on-screen use. It shares the same design logic of pixel-size specificity.
summary: A companion serif to Slabo 13px, optimised specifically for rendering at 27 pixels on screen with assured legibility.
description: Slabo 27px occupies a slightly larger niche than its sibling, working well as a screen subheading or medium-sized body text at its target size. Designed by John Hudson, it applies the same rigorous pixel-targeted logic to produce a typeface that performs reliably in digital contexts without generalising across sizes. It suits editorial web design and UI components where consistent 27px sizing is part of a design system.

name: Slackey
classification_notes: Slackey is a chunky, rounded display typeface with a casual, inflated quality reminiscent of bubble lettering and fun consumer packaging. Its letterforms are soft, bulbous, and highly informal.
summary: A bubbly, rounded display face with a lighthearted character suited to children's content and casual branding.
description: Slackey leans into the aesthetic of inflated, rounded lettering with an unabashedly cheerful personality. It works well for children's media, food packaging, event posters, and anywhere a sense of fun and approachability is required. Its limited weight range and novelty character mean it functions best in short headlines rather than sustained text.

name: Slackside One
classification_notes: Slackside One is a casual brush script designed to support both Japanese and Latin scripts, making it a notable multilingual offering in the script category. The brushstroke quality is relaxed and energetic, with a handwritten informality.
summary: A casual brush script with genuine Japanese and Latin support, bringing handwritten energy to multilingual settings.
description: Slackside One is distinctive for its cross-script ambition, harmonising a loose, brush-drawn aesthetic across Japanese and Latin character sets. This makes it genuinely useful for bilingual branding, packaging, or editorial work where tonal consistency across scripts matters. The informal, energetic stroke quality suits food and lifestyle brands with an East Asian cultural dimension.

name: Smokum
classification_notes: Smokum is a decorative display typeface with a strong Western or frontier aesthetic, featuring rough, irregular letterforms evoking hand-painted signage and vintage Americana. Its texture and character are deliberately crude.
summary: A rough-edged display face with unmistakable Western Americana character, recalling hand-painted frontier signage.
description: Smokum trades on nostalgia and texture, channelling the visual language of old saloon signs, circus bills, and frontier typography. It suits themed restaurant branding, retro event posters, and novelty packaging where period authenticity is the goal. Its single weight and highly specific personality limit its versatility considerably.

name: Smooch
classification_notes: Smooch is an expressive, high-energy display script with exaggerated ascenders and descenders and a strongly calligraphic baseline, suggesting confident hand lettering. It has a romantic, editorial quality with considerable flair.
summary: A bold, flowing display script with dramatic letterforms that command attention in headline and branding contexts.
description: Smooch brings genuine panache to the display script category, with sweeping strokes and pronounced contrast that make it well-suited to luxury branding, fashion editorial, and event invitations. Its Vietnamese support broadens its appeal across Southeast Asian markets. It is strictly a display face and should be set large, where its calligraphic details can be properly appreciated.

name: Smooch Sans
classification_notes: Smooch Sans is a wide-ranging humanist sans-serif with an extensive weight range from Thin to Black, drawing on warm, friendly proportions with good legibility across sizes. Its name reflects a kinship with the Smooch script family.
summary: A versatile humanist sans-serif spanning nine weights with warm proportions and strong multilingual coverage including Vietnamese.
description: Smooch Sans offers genuine flexibility across a broad weight spectrum, making it useful for everything from delicate body text at Thin weights to punchy headlines in Black. Its humanist construction gives it approachable, friendly character without sacrificing clarity, and its Vietnamese support makes it a practical choice for Southeast Asian digital projects. It works well in branding systems and editorial design that require typographic range from a single family.

name: Smythe
classification_notes: Smythe is a decorative display typeface with a distinctly medieval or gothic calligraphic influence, featuring elaborate strokes and a highly ornamental quality reminiscent of illuminated manuscript lettering rendered with a somewhat naive charm.
summary: A whimsical display face with gothic calligraphic roots, offering decorative drama for themed and novelty applications.
description: Smythe occupies a curious niche between blackletter tradition and illustrated novelty lettering, with an approachable quirkiness that softens its medieval references. It suits pub signage, fantasy game branding, Halloween materials, and any context where gothic atmosphere is wanted without strict historical rigour. Its expressive character is its entire selling point, and it should be used sparingly and at large sizes.

name: Sniglet
classification_notes: Sniglet features pronounced rounded terminals and open apertures, giving it a soft, approachable character suited to children's media and casual interfaces. Its chunky strokes and friendly curves make it immediately legible at display sizes.
summary: A bubbly, rounded display face with a distinctly cheerful personality best suited to playful, informal contexts.
description: Sniglet's generous rounded terminals and bold stroke weight give it an inviting, child-friendly warmth that works well for game interfaces, children's publishing, and casual branding. It performs best at headline sizes where its personality can shine without sacrificing legibility. The ExtraBold weight pushes the playfulness further for poster and packaging use.

name: Snippet
classification_notes: Snippet is a minimal, single-weight geometric sans with clean, unadorned letterforms and uniform stroke widths. Its limited weight range restricts versatility, making it a narrow-use display option.
summary: A bare-bones single-weight geometric sans with little to distinguish it beyond its simplicity.
description: Snippet offers clean, neutral letterforms drawn on geometric principles, but its single Regular weight severely limits typographic flexibility. It may serve adequately for lightweight UI labels or short display text, though it lacks the refinement or personality to stand out in competitive branding or editorial contexts. Its restricted character set further narrows its usefulness.

name: Snowburst One
classification_notes: Snowburst One is a decorative display typeface featuring inline contrast effects that simulate a snow or frost aesthetic, with chunky letterforms and ornamental detailing. It is firmly a one-trick novelty face designed for seasonal or thematic use.
summary: A seasonal novelty display face with built-in inline snow effects that limits it to highly specific thematic applications.
description: Snowburst One leans entirely on its decorative inline treatment to create the impression of frost or snow accumulation on bold letterforms. This makes it immediately eye-catching for winter campaigns, festive packaging, or holiday branding, but essentially useless outside those narrow contexts. At a single weight with no stylistic flexibility, its appeal is as short-lived as its thematic relevance.

name: Sofadi One
classification_notes: Sofadi One presents bold, slightly quirky letterforms with subtle geometric underpinnings and rounded qualities that give it a casual, handcrafted feel. It occupies the space between a workhorse sans and a display novelty without excelling at either.
summary: A single-weight decorative display sans with casual, rounded qualities that suits informal headline use.
description: Sofadi One blends geometric structure with a looseness that keeps it from feeling rigidly mechanical, lending it some charm in informal branding and display contexts. Its single weight restricts typographic hierarchy, making it best suited to short headline treatments or logo work where only one size and weight is required. It lacks the craft or distinctiveness to justify use in demanding design work.

name: Sofia
classification_notes: Sofia is a loose, upright casual script with informal letterforms that suggest handwriting without strong calligraphic formality. Its single weight and limited Latin-only coverage make it a restricted but accessible script option.
summary: A casual, upright script with a friendly handwritten feel suited to informal branding and display work.
description: Sofia treads a middle ground between formal scripts and rough brush lettering, offering approachable letterforms that work for café branding, packaging, or editorial pull quotes. Its upright posture makes it more versatile than heavily slanted scripts, though the single weight limits its range. It reads clearly at moderate sizes but lacks the refinement expected in premium typographic work.

name: Sofia Sans
classification_notes: Sofia Sans is a versatile geometric sans-serif with a broad nine-weight range and extensive multilingual support covering Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek scripts. Its clean, rational letterforms draw on geometric tradition while maintaining good legibility across sizes.
summary: A well-rounded geometric sans with strong multilingual coverage and a broad weight range that makes it a dependable workhorse for screen and print.
description: Sofia Sans covers an impressive range of weights from Thin to Black, making it genuinely flexible for typographic systems that need contrast and hierarchy. Its Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek support elevates it above many Google Fonts alternatives for international projects. The geometric construction keeps it feeling modern and rational, suitable for branding, UI design, and editorial work alike.

name: Sofia Sans Condensed
classification_notes: Sofia Sans Condensed offers the same geometric rational design as Sofia Sans but in a condensed width, retaining the full nine-weight range and multilingual script support. The narrower proportions make it particularly effective for space-efficient headlines and data-heavy interfaces.
summary: The condensed companion to Sofia Sans, offering the same extensive weight range and multilingual support in a space-saving width.
description: Sofia Sans Condensed retains all the characteristics of its parent family while offering significantly narrower letterforms ideal for headlines, tables, and interfaces where horizontal space is at a premium. The full weight range from Thin to Black ensures it can support complex typographic hierarchies without switching families. Cyrillic and Greek support makes it a strong choice for multilingual European design projects.

name: Sofia Sans Extra Condensed
classification_notes: Sofia Sans Extra Condensed takes the family's geometric foundations to their most compressed extreme, producing very narrow letterforms across nine weights with full Cyrillic, Cyrillic Extended, and Greek script support. At extreme compression it excels in poster headlines and space-constrained environments.
summary: The most compressed member of the Sofia Sans family, built for maximum typographic impact in tight horizontal spaces.
description: Sofia Sans Extra Condensed pushes the family's proportions to their narrowest, producing a typeface well suited to newspaper-style headlines, poster design, and any context where vertical rhythm must be prioritised over horizontal spread. The nine-weight range still provides meaningful hierarchy, and the multilingual coverage remains intact. At lighter weights it can serve informational text in tight layouts, whilst heavier weights deliver punchy display impact.

name: Sofia Sans Semi Condensed
classification_notes: A semi-condensed variant of the Sofia Sans family, offering a wide weight range with a neutral grotesque character. The condensed proportions make it space-efficient while retaining legibility across Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin scripts.
summary: A versatile semi-condensed grotesque with exceptional script coverage and a broad weight range.
description: Sofia Sans Semi Condensed sits comfortably between a neutral grotesque and a workhorse display face, its condensed proportions lending themselves well to headlines, editorial layouts, and space-constrained UI contexts. The extensive weight range from Thin to Black gives designers considerable flexibility. Strong Cyrillic and Greek support makes it a practical choice for multilingual projects.

name: Solitreo
classification_notes: Solitreo is based on the traditional Sephardic Jewish cursive script used for writing Ladino, making it a rare and culturally specific typeface. Its informal, looping letterforms reflect historical handwriting traditions rather than modern calligraphic convention.
summary: A culturally significant typeface reviving the traditional Sephardic cursive script used for writing Ladino.
description: Solitreo occupies a unique niche in type design, drawing directly from historical Sephardic handwriting traditions to serve a script that had no modern digital representation. Its looping, connected letterforms are informal and organic, suited to heritage, cultural, and editorial contexts. Practical applications are necessarily narrow, but within those contexts it is irreplaceable.

name: Solway
classification_notes: Solway is a sturdy, contemporary serif with moderate contrast and robust stroke weights that give it a dependable, bookish character. The design balances traditional serif conventions with slightly informal details that keep it approachable.
summary: A dependable contemporary serif with a warm, bookish character well suited to extended reading.
description: Solway's moderate contrast and solid construction make it a reliable choice for body text in both print and screen contexts, particularly where a traditional serif feel is desired without stiffness. Its range from Light to ExtraBold gives it enough flexibility to handle headline duties alongside continuous text. It works well in editorial, publishing, and branding contexts that call for a trustworthy, unpretentious serif.

name: Sometype Mono
classification_notes: Sometype Mono is a clean, geometric monospace with open apertures and consistent stroke weights that prioritise legibility. Its design leans modern rather than retro, avoiding the quirky idiosyncrasies common in coding-focused monospaces.
summary: A clean, modern geometric monospace designed equally well for coding environments and typographic display.
description: Sometype Mono avoids the retro affectations of many monospace fonts, instead offering a straightforward geometric approach with open letterforms and consistent rhythm. It performs reliably in code editors and terminal environments while remaining composed enough for tabular data and UI contexts. The four-weight range covers most practical needs without overcomplicating the family.

name: Song Myung
classification_notes: Song Myung is a Korean-Latin serif typeface with a light, refined stroke structure reminiscent of traditional East Asian brush calligraphy adapted for digital use. The Latin companion follows the weight and spirit of the Korean glyphs.
summary: A refined Korean serif with calligraphic roots, designed for comfortable reading in Korean-language editorial contexts.
description: Song Myung draws on the traditions of East Asian brush lettering, translating those qualities into a digitally functional serif suitable for body text and editorial use. Its single weight limits its versatility but focuses it clearly on sustained reading applications. The accompanying Latin characters are harmonious, making it useful for mixed Korean-English documents.

name: Sono
classification_notes: Sono is a variable sans-serif that transitions between proportional and monospaced spacing, offering a distinctive and practical design that serves both running text and technical display. Its clean, geometric-leaning forms are friendly without being overtly playful.
summary: An inventive variable sans that spans proportional and monospaced spacing in a single, cohesive family.
description: Sono's defining feature is its variable spacing axis, allowing it to shift from conventional proportional text to fully monospaced output within the same typeface. This makes it exceptionally practical for interfaces that mix prose and code, or for designers who want typographic consistency across varied content types. Its clean, open letterforms hold up well at small sizes on screen, and the Vietnamese script support broadens its international utility.

name: Sonsie One
classification_notes: Sonsie One is an exuberant display face with heavy, rounded serifs and thick strokes that give it a retro, almost novelty character. It is emphatically single-use, built for large-scale decorative headlines rather than any functional text role.
summary: A bold, rounded display serif with an unmistakably retro personality suited to eye-catching headlines.
description: Sonsie One makes no pretence of subtlety, its thick rounded strokes and heavy serifs calling back to mid-century display lettering and fairground signage. It works best at large sizes where its distinctive weight and curves can make a confident visual statement. Practical applications are limited to posters, packaging, and branding contexts that actively want a retro, exuberant tone.

name: Sora
classification_notes: Sora is a clean, humanist-leaning geometric sans with even stroke weights and open apertures that give it a contemporary, digital-native feel. Its balanced proportions and generous x-height make it well suited to screen interfaces and product design.
summary: A refined geometric sans with a digital-native sensibility that works cleanly across UI, branding, and headline contexts.
description: Sora strikes a balance between geometric precision and humanist warmth, avoiding the coldness of strict geometric sans designs while remaining modern and unobtrusive. Its generous x-height and open apertures ensure strong legibility at small sizes on screen, making it a solid choice for product interfaces and app design. The eight-weight range from Thin to ExtraBold gives it the versatility to carry an entire typographic system on its own.

name: Sorts Mill Goudy
classification_notes: A revival of Frederic Goudy's classic Kennerley and Forum typefaces, featuring warm oldstyle proportions, bracketed serifs, and calligraphic stroke contrast. The design retains an organic, hand-cut quality that distinguishes it from more mechanical revivals.
summary: A faithful and characterful revival of Goudy's oldstyle tradition, warm in texture and well-suited to long-form setting.
description: Sorts Mill Goudy draws on the humanist oldstyle lineage of Frederic Goudy, delivering a typeface with genuine personality and historical grounding. Its bracketed serifs and calligraphic stroke contrast make it an excellent choice for book typography, literary publishing, and editorial work. The single Regular weight limits its versatility, but within that constraint it performs with considerable grace.

name: Sour Gummy
classification_notes: A playful, heavily rounded sans-serif with a wide weight range and exaggerated, bubbly terminals that give it a distinctly friendly and informal character. The generous x-height and soft geometry lean strongly toward display and casual use.
summary: A bubbly, rounded sans with maximum friendliness and a wide weight range built for playful display work.
description: Sour Gummy leans hard into rounded, soft geometry, making it immediately approachable and well-suited to children's content, casual branding, and lighthearted digital interfaces. Its broad weight range from Thin to Black gives designers real flexibility within its cheerful register. It is not a workhorse for body text but excels wherever warmth and personality are the primary brief.

name: Source Code Pro
classification_notes: Designed as part of Adobe's Source family, Source Code Pro brings humanist warmth to the monospace format, with carefully adjusted letterforms that reduce the mechanical rigidity typical of coding fonts. Its large x-height and clear differentiation between similar characters make it highly legible in code editors.
summary: Adobe's benchmark coding monospace, combining humanist clarity with the precision demands of the programming environment.
description: Source Code Pro was designed specifically for use in programming environments, addressing the legibility challenges that arise when distinguishing between similar glyphs such as zero and capital O or one and lowercase l. Its humanist underpinning prevents it from feeling cold or mechanical, making extended coding sessions more comfortable. Broad script support and multiple weights extend its usefulness beyond coding into technical documentation and terminal interfaces.

name: Source Sans 3
classification_notes: The open-source companion to Adobe's Source family, Source Sans 3 is a clean, highly legible humanist sans-serif designed with user interface and body text legibility as primary goals. Its neutral yet warm character, broad weight range, and extensive script coverage make it a reliable workhorse.
summary: Adobe's open-source humanist sans, built for screen legibility and long-form reading across a wide range of weights and scripts.
description: Source Sans 3 was Adobe's first open-source typeface family, and it remains one of the most dependable choices for interface design and editorial work on screen. Its humanist proportions keep it warm enough for body text while its neutral clarity suits UI environments without friction. Cyrillic, Greek, and extended Latin coverage make it a strong candidate for multilingual projects.

name: Source Serif 4
classification_notes: Source Serif 4 sits between oldstyle and modern traditions, with moderate stroke contrast, bracketed serifs, and a large x-height that prioritises screen legibility. Its wide weight range and optical size awareness give it unusual versatility across display and text settings.
summary: A versatile, screen-optimised transitional serif from Adobe with a wide weight range and serious multilingual credentials.
description: Source Serif 4 is designed to complement the Source Sans and Source Code families, providing a serif option of equal rigour and range. Its moderate contrast and generous x-height ensure readability at small sizes on screen, while heavier weights perform confidently as display type. Extended Cyrillic, Greek, and Latin coverage makes it one of the more capable multilingual serif options available on Google Fonts.

name: Space Grotesk
classification_notes: Space Grotesk occupies a distinctive middle ground between geometric and grotesque sans traditions, with deliberate quirks in letter construction — most notably the angular terminals and idiosyncratic details — that give it personality without sacrificing legibility. Its proportions and weight range suit both display and UI work.
summary: A characterful geometric grotesque with deliberate quirks that set it apart from neutrality without compromising usability.
description: Space Grotesk was developed from the proportions of Space Mono but reworked as a proportional typeface, retaining some of the mono's distinctive angularity in its terminals. Those idiosyncratic details make it a natural fit for technology branding, editorial headlines, and digital product design where a touch of personality is welcome. Its five weights cover most practical needs, and it holds up well at both display and interface sizes.

name: Space Mono
classification_notes: Space Mono is a fixed-width typeface with strong geometric construction and deliberately retro character, evoking the aesthetics of early digital and typewriter output. Its idiosyncratic letterforms, including the double-storey a and circular terminals, give it a recognisable personality.
summary: A geometric monospace with a strong retro-digital personality, as at home in headlines as it is in code editors.
description: Space Mono was commissioned by Google and designed by Colophon Foundry to serve both functional coding environments and expressive display contexts. Its geometric construction and retro flavour — reminiscent of early computer terminals — make it particularly popular in technology editorial and speculative design aesthetics. The limited two-weight range keeps it focused, and its personality is strong enough that overuse quickly becomes apparent.

name: Special Elite
classification_notes: Special Elite mimics the uneven, ink-heavy impression of a worn typewriter typeface with mechanical damage, producing a deliberately imperfect texture rich in nostalgic character. Each letterform carries irregular edges and ink traps that reinforce its vintage, analogue quality.
summary: A distressed typewriter-style display face with deliberate imperfection that evokes worn mechanical type.
description: Special Elite replicates the aesthetic of a typewriter font whose keys have seen considerable use, complete with uneven ink distribution and irregular letterforms. It is a single-trick typeface, but within its niche — vintage editorial, noir-inflected design, pulp fiction aesthetics, and retro branding — it delivers convincingly. Its single weight and narrow application range make it a specialist tool rather than a workhorse.

name: Special Gothic
classification_notes: A contemporary take on American gothic/grotesque letterforms with upright, even-stroked construction. Offers a solid weight range suitable for both headline and text use.
summary: A sturdy American gothic sans with clean, no-nonsense construction across a useful weight range.
description: Special Gothic draws on the tradition of condensed American gothic typefaces, delivering solid, even strokes with minimal stylistic flourish. The weight range from Regular to Bold makes it versatile for editorial headlines, branding, and UI work. Its straightforward character suits projects that need strength without ostentation.

name: Special Gothic Condensed One
classification_notes: A single-weight condensed gothic sans with tight proportions and strong vertical rhythm, clearly designed for maximum impact in tight horizontal spaces.
summary: A tightly condensed gothic sans built for high-impact headlines and poster work.
description: Special Gothic Condensed One compresses the gothic sans structure into narrow, upright letterforms that pack a visual punch at large sizes. Its single Regular weight is best deployed in headlines, posters, and signage where horizontal space is limited. The stripped-back offering limits versatility but suits its purpose well.

name: Special Gothic Expanded One
classification_notes: A wide, expanded counterpart to the Special Gothic family, with broad letterforms that command attention across display contexts.
summary: An expanded gothic sans that trades compactness for commanding width, ideal for short display text.
description: Special Gothic Expanded One stretches gothic sans proportions horizontally, creating letterforms that fill space and assert presence at large sizes. Like its condensed sibling, it is available in a single weight, which limits its scope to display and headline applications. It pairs naturally with the condensed and standard cuts for a complete typographic system.

name: Spectral
classification_notes: A screen-optimised serif with high contrast and sharp detailing, designed specifically for digital reading environments across an extensive weight range. Features strong optical considerations for on-screen performance.
summary: A screen-first high-contrast serif with exceptional range, built for demanding editorial and publishing work.
description: Spectral was commissioned by Google and designed by Production Type as a text serif optimised for digital reading, offering seven weights with accompanying italics for thorough typographic control. Its high contrast and refined detailing give it elegance without sacrificing legibility at body sizes on screen. It is an excellent choice for long-form digital publishing, news platforms, and editorial interfaces requiring Cyrillic support.

name: Spectral SC
classification_notes: The small capitals companion to Spectral, offering true-drawn small caps across the full weight range for typographically refined settings.
summary: The dedicated small caps cut of Spectral, providing true-drawn capitals for refined typographic hierarchy.
description: Spectral SC delivers properly proportioned small capitals rather than algorithmically scaled versions, maintaining the optical consistency of the parent family. It is intended for use alongside the standard Spectral cuts to achieve sophisticated heading and subheading treatments in editorial and publishing contexts. The full seven-weight range ensures it can serve any level of typographic hierarchy.

name: Spicy Rice
classification_notes: A bold decorative display face with chunky, highly stylised letterforms that carry an ethnic or folk-inspired character. Very limited in application due to its singular ornamental quality.
summary: A heavy decorative display face with folk and ethnic styling suited to very specific thematic projects.
description: Spicy Rice features thick, exaggerated letterforms with distinctive stylistic detailing that evokes folk art and world typography influences. Its novelty character makes it best suited to packaging, event branding, and display contexts where that specific cultural aesthetic is intentional. It is a single-weight, single-purpose face with little room for broader application.

name: Spinnaker
classification_notes: A light, humanist sans-serif with open letterforms and gentle proportions, offering a friendly and approachable character at a single weight.
summary: A gentle, open humanist sans with a friendly disposition, available in a single regular weight.
description: Spinnaker takes a humanist approach to the sans-serif, with open apertures and warmly proportioned letterforms that feel approachable rather than clinical. Its single weight limits its versatility, but it performs capably in body text, UI copy, and lightweight editorial contexts. It is a clean, unassuming choice where a hard-edged grotesque would feel too cold.

name: Spirax
classification_notes: A highly stylised upright display script with decorative flourishes and calligraphic influences, designed purely for headline and ornamental use at large sizes.
summary: An ornate upright display script with pronounced calligraphic flourishes, built strictly for decorative headline use.
description: Spirax presents an elaborate, upright script character with sweeping strokes and detailed ornamental features that make it a strong choice for boutique branding, invitations, and decorative headings. Its Latin-only coverage and single weight confirm its role as a purely expressive display face with no ambition towards text use. At large sizes it makes a distinctive statement, though its very specific personality demands careful contextual matching.

name: Splash
classification_notes: Splash is a casual, loose handwritten script with a relaxed, informal character. Its irregular letterforms and natural pen variation give it an authentic hand-drawn quality.
summary: A loose, casual handwritten script with natural irregularity and a genuinely hand-drawn feel.
description: Splash suits informal branding, greeting cards, and social media graphics where a personal, approachable tone is needed. Its extended Latin and Vietnamese support broadens its utility across multilingual projects. Best used at larger display sizes where its hand-drawn character can be fully appreciated.

name: Spline Sans
classification_notes: Spline Sans is a clean geometric sans-serif with softened construction and a contemporary, neutral disposition. Its even stroke weights and open apertures make it highly legible across digital interfaces.
summary: A clean, contemporary geometric sans-serif built with digital interfaces and extended reading in mind.
description: Spline Sans offers a solid range of weights from Light to Bold, making it adaptable for both body copy and headline use. Its neutral character keeps it versatile for UI design, editorial layouts, and branding work. The accompanying monospace sibling makes it particularly well-suited to technical documentation and design systems.

name: Spline Sans Mono
classification_notes: Spline Sans Mono mirrors the geometric construction of its sans-serif companion while adhering to fixed-width spacing conventions. It maintains strong legibility across weights, balancing technical precision with approachable forms.
summary: A geometric monospace that pairs seamlessly with Spline Sans, offering consistent design language for technical and code-facing contexts.
description: Spline Sans Mono is purpose-built for code editors, terminal interfaces, and technical documentation. Its five weights give designers more expressive range than most monospace fonts allow. As a matched companion to Spline Sans, it enables cohesive multi-format design systems without visual jarring.

name: Squada One
classification_notes: Squada One is a bold, condensed display face with sharp geometric construction and a strong industrial character. Its single Regular weight is designed purely for impactful headline and poster use.
summary: A sharp, condensed geometric display face with a commanding industrial presence suited to headlines and posters.
description: Squada One's tight proportions and bold geometry make it highly effective for headlines, banners, and any context demanding visual authority in limited space. Its single weight limits flexibility, but within that constraint it performs with confidence. It works well in sports, gaming, and technology-adjacent branding.

name: Square Peg
classification_notes: Square Peg is a whimsical, bouncy casual script with exaggerated letterform variation and a playful rhythm. Its loose construction evokes informal handwriting with a distinctly expressive personality.
summary: A bouncy, exuberant casual script with a strong playful personality and lively rhythm.
description: Square Peg is best deployed in display contexts where personality and warmth are the priority — packaging, children's content, event branding, and social graphics all suit it well. Its extended Latin and Vietnamese coverage adds useful multilingual reach. It is strictly a headline face and should not be used for body text.

name: Sree Krushnadevaraya
classification_notes: Sree Krushnadevaraya is a traditional serif typeface designed primarily for the Telugu script, with Latin support as a secondary consideration. It reflects classical Telugu letterform conventions with careful attention to stroke modulation.
summary: A traditional Telugu serif designed for reliable text-setting in one of India's major scripts, with supporting Latin coverage.
description: Sree Krushnadevaraya serves Telugu-language publishing, educational materials, and web content where a respectful, conventional approach to the script is required. Its single Regular weight keeps it simple but limits its versatility across typographic hierarchies. The Latin companion is functional rather than distinctive.

name: Sriracha
classification_notes: Sriracha is a friendly, rounded brush script with a distinctly casual Thai and Latin character. Its strokes convey a hand-lettered quality with smooth, flowing connections between letterforms.
summary: A warm, flowing brush script with genuine Thai character and a friendly, approachable personality across both Thai and Latin glyphs.
description: Sriracha bridges Thai and Latin scripts with a consistent casual brush aesthetic, making it valuable for bilingual branding and signage in Thai-speaking markets. Its rounded, hand-drawn forms suit food and beverage packaging, informal branding, and editorial display use. It is a display face and loses its charm at small sizes.

name: Srisakdi
classification_notes: Srisakdi is an expressive display typeface designed for Thai script with supporting Latin glyphs, offering a decorative, characterful style in Regular and Bold. Its forms lean towards the playful and attention-grabbing rather than the neutral.
summary: A lively Thai display typeface with decorative flair, suited to headlines and promotional material in Thai-language contexts.
description: Srisakdi delivers expressive impact for Thai-language advertising, packaging, and event graphics where visual energy is essential. Its two weights provide a basic degree of typographic flexibility for headline hierarchies. The Latin glyphs are competent but secondary — the real strength lies in its Thai character design.

name: Staatliches
classification_notes: Extremely tall and narrow all-caps letterforms with uniform stroke width and tight spacing. Designed specifically for maximum visual impact at large sizes with minimal horizontal footprint.
summary: Staatliches is an aggressively condensed all-caps display face that packs enormous typographic punch into a narrow column.
description: Its extreme vertical proportions make it ideal for posters, banners, and editorial headlines where space is limited but impact is essential. The uniform stroke weight and tight letter spacing give it a mechanical, almost industrial quality. Best used at large sizes; it loses legibility quickly at text sizes.

name: Stack Sans Headline
classification_notes: A headline-optimised grotesque sans with optical adjustments for large-scale use, featuring slightly wider proportions and open apertures suited to display settings. Part of the Stack Sans superfamily.
summary: Stack Sans Headline is the display-tuned member of the Stack Sans family, refined for large-scale typographic settings.
description: Its letterforms are optimised for headlines and titles, with proportions and spacing calibrated to read cleanly at elevated sizes. As part of a cohesive superfamily, it pairs naturally with Stack Sans Text for a disciplined editorial system. The range of weights from ExtraLight to Bold offers considerable expressive flexibility.

name: Stack Sans Notch
classification_notes: A variant of the Stack Sans family featuring a distinctive notched or cut detail within letterforms, adding a decorative or technical character while retaining the grotesque sans skeleton. Designed as an expressive display companion.
summary: Stack Sans Notch introduces a signature notched detail into the Stack Sans framework, giving it a distinctive technical-decorative character.
description: The notch treatment creates visual interest at large sizes without abandoning the structural logic of the broader family. It suits branding and editorial contexts where a touch of individuality is needed within an otherwise systematic type approach. Best reserved for headlines and display use rather than body copy.

name: Stack Sans Text
classification_notes: The text-optimised member of the Stack Sans superfamily, with spacing and proportions tuned for comfortable reading at smaller sizes. Shares the grotesque DNA of the family while prioritising legibility and neutrality.
summary: Stack Sans Text is the workhorse member of the Stack Sans family, designed for sustained reading at body and UI scales.
description: Its adjusted spacing and proportions make it comfortable for paragraphs, interfaces, and editorial body copy alike. When used alongside Stack Sans Headline and Notch, it completes a versatile typographic system suited to publications, apps, and branding. The six-weight range ensures it can handle a wide variety of typographic hierarchies.

name: Stalemate
classification_notes: A flowing, high-contrast formal script with tall ascenders and elegant swash-like letterforms reminiscent of traditional copperplate or Spencerian penmanship. Designed primarily for display and decorative purposes.
summary: Stalemate is a flowing formal script that channels copperplate elegance for use in decorative headlines and luxury branding.
description: Its tall, sweeping letterforms and hairline-to-thick stroke contrast give it a classical, ceremonial quality well suited to wedding stationery, packaging, and premium brand identities. Available in a single weight, it is unambiguously a display face and should not be used for body text. Pairs well with clean, minimal serifs or sans-serifs that let the script take centre stage.

name: Stalinist One
classification_notes: A bold, politically charged display typeface mimicking the monumental Soviet-era constructivist lettering style, with heavy slab-like serifs and robust stroke weights evoking propaganda posters and public signage. Supports Cyrillic.
summary: Stalinist One is a deliberately heavy, propaganda-inflected display face that recreates the imposing typographic language of Soviet-era monumental lettering.
description: Its thick strokes, slab-like serifs, and assertive proportions make it unmistakable in poster, headline, and signage contexts. The Cyrillic support is historically appropriate and broadens its utility for multilingual Eastern European applications. It is strictly a novelty display face — effective in small doses but exhausting at body sizes.

name: Stardos Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil-style display typeface featuring interrupted strokes and cut-out bridges characteristic of physical stencil lettering, with a robust, utilitarian quality. Available in Regular and Bold weights.
summary: Stardos Stencil is a clean, functional stencil typeface that brings the utilitarian aesthetic of physical stencil lettering to digital type.
description: The interrupted strokes are well-judged, avoiding the clumsiness that plagues many stencil fonts while maintaining clear stencil character. It works well for headlines, posters, and packaging with a military, industrial, or raw urban flavour. Two weights give it modest range, though it remains firmly a display-only proposition.

name: Stick
classification_notes: A sans-serif typeface designed to harmonise Latin and Japanese (CJK) scripts in a single face, with simplified, stroke-based letterforms that complement the visual rhythm of Japanese type. Supports Cyrillic, Japanese, and Latin scripts.
summary: Stick is a unified Latin and Japanese sans-serif that bridges both scripts with a consistent, stroke-driven visual logic.
description: Its simplified letterforms draw on the structural conventions of Japanese gothic type, resulting in a typeface that reads cohesively whether rendering Latin, Cyrillic, or Japanese glyphs. This makes it particularly valuable for multilingual web and interface projects spanning East Asian and European languages. The single weight limits its typographic range, but its cross-script consistency is genuinely useful.

name: Stick No Bills
classification_notes: An ultra-condensed sans-serif with a strong vertical emphasis and clean strokes, supporting Latin and Sinhala scripts. The extreme condensed proportions make it suited for dramatic headline use.
summary: An aggressively condensed sans-serif that commands attention in tight headline settings.
description: Stick No Bills packs considerable typographic impact into a narrow footprint, making it ideal for posters, banners, and editorial headlines where vertical space is at a premium. Its Sinhala script support is a notable inclusion, broadening its utility across multilingual contexts. The range of weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold gives designers flexibility in controlling visual weight without sacrificing its condensed character.

name: Stint Ultra Condensed
classification_notes: A single-weight serif pushed to extreme condensed proportions, with tall x-height and compact letterforms designed for maximum text density. The serifs remain visible but are subordinated to the overall compressed structure.
summary: An extremely narrow serif designed for situations where horizontal space is almost non-existent.
description: Stint Ultra Condensed takes condensed typography to its logical extreme, squeezing letterforms into remarkably slim columns while retaining legibility at larger sizes. It suits newspaper mastheads, pull quotes, and decorative headline applications where a vintage or editorial flavour is desired. The single Regular weight limits versatility, making it a specialist tool rather than an everyday workhorse.

name: Stint Ultra Expanded
classification_notes: The counterpart to Stint Ultra Condensed, this face stretches letterforms to pronounced wide proportions while retaining serif detailing. Designed as a display curiosity with a single weight.
summary: A dramatically expanded serif that stretches letterforms to theatrical widths for display use.
description: Stint Ultra Expanded occupies the opposite end of the width spectrum from its condensed sibling, spreading each character across an unusually wide baseline. This makes it best suited to short words or single-line treatments on posters and headings where the expansive rhythm can be appreciated. Its single weight restricts practical application, and it functions primarily as a novelty display face.

name: Stoke
classification_notes: A serif face with pronounced stroke contrast and refined detailing, available in Light and Regular weights. The design has an editorial quality with elegant proportions suited to display rather than continuous text.
summary: A refined display serif with noticeable stroke contrast and an understated elegance.
description: Stoke carries the hallmarks of a fashion-adjacent editorial serif — clean lines, moderate contrast, and letterforms that hold up well at headline sizes. Its Light and Regular weights are both appropriate for magazine headers, branding, and editorial layouts where sophistication is the priority. It is less suited to extended body text, where the limited weight range would become a constraint.

name: Story Script
classification_notes: A sans-serif-classified casual hand-lettered script with relaxed, informal letterforms and natural connecting strokes. The design mimics spontaneous handwriting with a friendly, approachable character.
summary: A loose, hand-drawn script that brings a warm and personal feel to display settings.
description: Story Script captures the spontaneity of casual handwriting, with natural letterform variation and flowing connections that feel uncontrived. It suits packaging, greeting card design, social media graphics, and any branding context requiring a human, approachable tone. The single Regular weight keeps it in novelty territory, best deployed sparingly as an accent rather than a sustained text face.

name: Strait
classification_notes: A plain, single-weight grotesque sans-serif with minimal distinguishing features and neutral construction. Functional rather than characterful, it prioritises basic readability over typographic personality.
summary: A no-frills grotesque sans that prioritises straightforward legibility over stylistic distinction.
description: Strait is a functional single-weight grotesque that aims for neutrality, making it serviceable for web body copy and UI text at modest sizes. Its lack of weights or variants significantly limits its usefulness in any design system requiring hierarchy or contrast. It is a workable utility face but unlikely to be a first choice where more developed grotesque options are available.

name: Strichpunkt Sans
classification_notes: A clean geometric sans-serif with a wide weight range from Regular through to Black, featuring consistent stroke construction and open apertures. The design leans toward modern utility with a structured, considered appearance.
summary: A well-weighted geometric sans with a broad range of weights suited to contemporary branding and editorial work.
description: Strichpunkt Sans offers a confident geometric construction with enough weight variation — from Regular to Black — to build meaningful typographic hierarchy within a single family. Its clean forms and open apertures translate well across both screen and print contexts, from UI design to headline typography. The family has a modern Swiss quality that makes it adaptable to branding, editorial, and packaging without feeling generic.

name: Style Script
classification_notes: A flowing formal script with high contrast strokes, elegant loops, and classical copperplate-influenced construction. Designed for decorative display applications with refined, traditional letterforms.
summary: An elegant formal script drawing on copperplate traditions for refined display applications.
description: Style Script delivers the sweeping flourishes and high stroke contrast associated with classic copperplate penmanship, lending it an air of ceremony and sophistication. It is well-suited to wedding stationery, luxury branding, fashion editorial, and any context where a formal hand-lettered quality is required. As a single-weight display face it works best at larger sizes where its calligraphic detailing can be fully appreciated.

name: Stylish
classification_notes: A clean, upright sans-serif designed primarily for Korean Hangul script with a modern, unadorned aesthetic. The letterforms are simple and functional, reflecting contemporary Korean type design conventions.
summary: A minimal, functional sans-serif built for Korean text with a clean, contemporary character.
description: Stylish offers straightforward Hangul letterforms suited to digital interfaces and body text applications. Its unaffected design makes it adaptable across UI, editorial, and general web use. The lack of decorative flourish keeps it firmly in utilitarian territory.

name: Sue Ellen Francisco
classification_notes: A loose, hand-lettered script with an informal, schoolgirl handwriting quality. Letterforms are bouncy and uneven, giving it a distinctly personal and approachable character.
summary: A cheerful, hand-drawn script that mimics casual everyday handwriting with a light, bouncy touch.
description: Sue Ellen Francisco captures the feel of a pen scrawled quickly across paper, with irregular baseline and informal letterforms. It suits greeting cards, children's content, and personal branding where warmth and approachability matter. Its single weight limits versatility but the character is immediately engaging.

name: Suez One
classification_notes: A sturdy display slab serif with strong, chunky serifs and relatively high contrast, designed to support both Hebrew and Latin scripts harmoniously. The robust construction gives it a confident, editorial presence.
summary: A confident slab serif designed for bilingual Hebrew and Latin typesetting, with a bold, editorial character.
description: Suez One's thick strokes and prominent serifs give it strong visual presence at display sizes, making it well suited to headlines and masthead use. Its careful balancing of Hebrew and Latin letterforms is a notable technical achievement, ensuring neither script feels like an afterthought. It works particularly well in editorial and cultural contexts where both scripts appear together.

name: Sulphur Point
classification_notes: A light, airy geometric sans-serif with an almost architectural quality, featuring thin strokes and open apertures that give it a futuristic, slightly cold character. The design leans toward display use despite its text weight range.
summary: A slender, architectural geometric sans with a futuristic edge that suits modern branding and display work.
description: Sulphur Point's spare, minimal construction draws on geometric traditions but with a distinctly contemporary, almost technical sensibility. The light weight in particular creates an elegant, refined impression suited to fashion, technology, and design-forward branding. At smaller sizes the thin strokes can compromise legibility, so it performs best in headline and display contexts.

name: Sumana
classification_notes: A bilingual serif typeface supporting Devanagari and Latin scripts, with warm, humanist proportions and moderate contrast. The design prioritises readability and harmony across both script systems.
summary: A warm, readable bilingual serif designed to serve Devanagari and Latin scripts with equal care.
description: Sumana addresses the genuine typographic need for a harmonious serif that handles both Devanagari and Latin text without either script appearing compromised. Its moderate contrast and humanist forms make it well suited to extended body text in books, editorial, and web publishing. The pairing of scripts is thoughtful, resulting in a cohesive reading experience across both writing systems.

name: Sunflower
classification_notes: A contemporary Korean sans-serif with clean, modern proportions and a slightly warm character that distinguishes it from purely mechanical gothic designs. The three-weight range provides practical flexibility for Korean typesetting.
summary: A modern, approachable Korean sans-serif with enough warmth to soften the utilitarian gothic tradition.
description: Sunflower sits within the broad Korean gothic sans tradition but introduces subtle humanist touches that make it feel less rigid than many contemporaries. Its three-weight family — light, medium, and bold — covers most practical typesetting needs for web and app interfaces. It performs reliably across body text and headline use in Korean digital publishing.

name: Sunshiney
classification_notes: A light, informal handwriting script with a sunny, carefree quality and uneven, natural letterforms. The design evokes quick, cheerful pen lettering with minimal connecting strokes.
summary: A light-hearted, sunny handwriting script with an informal, carefree personality.
description: Sunshiney mimics the effortless feel of casual handwriting, with loose, airy letterforms that convey optimism and approachability. It is best deployed at large sizes for short display text — invitations, social graphics, and informal branding benefit most from its cheerful character. The single weight and limited script support restrict its versatility considerably.

name: Supermercado One
classification_notes: A quirky, rounded display face with a retro supermarket-signage aesthetic, featuring chunky, bubble-like letterforms with strong personality. The design is unapologetically decorative and niche in application.
summary: A bold, retro-flavoured rounded display face inspired by mid-century supermarket signage, full of personality.
description: Supermercado One leans heavily into a nostalgic, commercial vernacular aesthetic that makes it instantly evocative of a specific era in American retail and advertising. Its chunky rounded forms and bold weight make it effective for poster work, packaging, and any project that calls for a fun, retro sensibility. The novelty nature of the design limits it to short, punchy display use only.

name: Sura
classification_notes: Sura is a dual-script serif designed to harmonise Devanagari and Latin letterforms within a single cohesive typeface. It features moderate contrast and sturdy construction suited to extended reading.
summary: A dependable dual-script serif that brings Devanagari and Latin together with consistent weight and rhythm.
description: Sura is built for publications and digital content that must serve both Devanagari and Latin readers without visual inconsistency. Its moderate stroke contrast and sturdy serifs make it comfortable at body sizes. It suits editorial layouts, long-form web content, and multilingual documents where typographic cohesion is a priority.

name: Suranna
classification_notes: Suranna is a single-weight serif designed primarily for the Telugu script, with a companion Latin. The design draws on traditional Telugu typographic conventions with clean, well-proportioned letterforms.
summary: A clean single-weight Telugu serif that pairs respectfully with Latin for bilingual text settings.
description: Suranna follows traditional Telugu calligraphic structure while being optimised for screen and print readability. Its single Regular weight limits versatility but makes it a reliable choice for body text in Telugu-language publishing. It is a practical option for news sites, educational materials, and digital documents serving Telugu readers.

name: Suravaram
classification_notes: Suravaram is a single-weight Telugu serif with a companion Latin, designed with attention to traditional Telugu script proportions and modest stroke contrast. It is closely related in spirit to Suranna but represents a distinct design.
summary: A single-weight Telugu serif offering a traditional, readable option for Telugu-language text settings.
description: Suravaram provides a dependable typographic solution for Telugu script content alongside Latin, maintaining cultural authenticity in its letterform construction. Its single weight makes it best suited to running text rather than complex hierarchical layouts. It is appropriate for editorial, educational, and documentary uses in Telugu.

name: Suwannaphum
classification_notes: Suwannaphum is a multi-weight Khmer serif with a broad weight range from Thin to Black, designed to bring typographic range to Khmer-script publishing. The Latin companion shares the same overall weight distribution.
summary: A rare multi-weight Khmer serif that gives designers genuine typographic hierarchy for Khmer-language projects.
description: Suwannaphum is notable for offering five weights across the Khmer script, a level of typographic range that is uncommon in this script category. This makes it genuinely useful for newspapers, magazines, and websites that need to establish clear visual hierarchy in Khmer. The Thin and Black extremes also open it up to display and headline applications.

name: Swanky and Moo Moo
classification_notes: Swanky and Moo Moo is a casual, bouncy hand-lettered script with exaggerated loops and an overtly playful character. It operates firmly in the novelty display category with limited typographic utility.
summary: An exuberantly bouncy hand-lettered script best deployed sparingly for fun, informal display use.
description: Swanky and Moo Moo leans hard into a whimsical, childlike aesthetic with oversized loops and irregular baseline movement. It is suited to greeting cards, party invitations, children's branding, and any context demanding an unashamedly lighthearted tone. It has little utility beyond short display settings and should be used with restraint.

name: Syncopate
classification_notes: Syncopate is a stark, wide geometric sans-serif with uppercase-dominant construction, open spacing, and a strong architectural quality. It is built for impact at large sizes rather than text legibility.
summary: A wide, uppercase-driven geometric sans with a bold architectural presence designed purely for display impact.
description: Syncopate's generous letter spacing and near-monolinear strokes give it a cold, modernist quality reminiscent of titling faces used in science fiction and technical branding. It works well for short headlines, logotypes, posters, and motion graphics where typographic authority is more important than warmth. Its limited weight range and poor small-size performance make it a one-trick specialist.

name: Syne
classification_notes: Syne is a contemporary geometric sans-serif with a distinctive ExtraBold weight that features highly experimental, almost deconstructed letterforms, while the lighter weights remain usable for editorial text. Designed for the arts and culture sector.
summary: A culture-sector geometric sans whose ExtraBold weight pushes into genuinely experimental display territory.
description: Syne was originally created for the Synchrotron SOLEIL arts programme, and its design reflects that cultural context — clean and functional in lighter weights, daringly idiosyncratic at the heavy end. The ExtraBold features manipulated, partially deconstructed letterforms that make it stand out in headline and poster use. It suits arts organisations, editorial projects, and progressive branding that wants geometric rigour with a creative edge.

name: Syne Mono
classification_notes: Syne Mono is the fixed-width companion to the Syne family, carrying the same geometric construction into a monospace format. It retains the family's clean, contemporary character while conforming to coding and typographic grid requirements.
summary: The monospace companion to Syne, bringing its geometric personality to code and technical display contexts.
description: Syne Mono translates the Syne family's clean geometric aesthetic into a fixed-width format suitable for code editors, terminal interfaces, and design-forward technical documentation. Its single Regular weight limits it to contexts where hierarchy is achieved through other means. It is an appealing option for developers and designers who want a code font with more character than a purely utilitarian monospace.

name: Syne Tactile
classification_notes: Syne Tactile is a highly experimental display face from the Syne family, featuring letterforms that appear to be constructed from raised tactile dots, mimicking braille-like surface texture. The design prioritises visual spectacle over legibility, making it unsuitable for running text.
summary: A wildly experimental display typeface whose letterforms are built entirely from raised dot patterns, giving the impression of tactile printed surfaces.
description: Syne Tactile is part of the broader Syne superfamily but stands apart as a pure novelty display face, its characters composed of circular dot elements that evoke tactile printing or braille aesthetics. It is best reserved for large-scale headline use, poster work, or art direction where texture and visual drama take precedence. Practical readability at small sizes is negligible, so it functions purely as a statement piece.

name: TASA Explorer
classification_notes: TASA Explorer is a clean geometric sans-serif with an exploratory, forward-looking character, featuring open apertures and balanced stroke contrast that keeps it highly legible across sizes. Its weight range and structured geometry give it a technical yet approachable quality.
summary: A confident geometric sans-serif with open forms and a crisp technical character, well suited to brand and editorial work.
description: TASA Explorer is produced by TASA Type, a Taiwanese type foundry known for thoughtful Latin and CJK design work. Its broad weight range from Regular to ExtraBold makes it versatile across headlines, UI interfaces, and editorial layouts. The open apertures and stable geometry ensure strong legibility on screen and in print alike.

name: TASA Orbiter
classification_notes: TASA Orbiter shares the TASA Type design language but adopts a rounder, more circular geometric construction, giving it a softer and more friendly tone compared to Explorer. Stroke terminals are clean and the overall rhythm is even and measured.
summary: A rounded geometric sans from TASA Type with a warm, approachable character built on circular proportions and even strokes.
description: TASA Orbiter sits alongside TASA Explorer in the foundry's Latin type offering but leans into softer, more rounded geometry that makes it feel distinctly friendlier. The five-weight range provides enough flexibility for interface design, product branding, and editorial use where a less severe geometric voice is preferred. It performs well at both display and text sizes on screen.

name: Tac One
classification_notes: Tac One is a single-weight geometric sans-serif with a distinctive wide, flat construction and strong horizontal emphasis in its letterforms. Its mathematical script support alongside Latin makes it a functional choice for technical and scientific publishing.
summary: A wide, flat geometric sans-serif with mathematical script support that gives it a practical edge in technical and scientific contexts.
description: Tac One's single-weight offering limits its typographic flexibility, but its inclusion of mathematical characters expands its utility beyond typical display use into STEM publishing and technical documentation. The wide proportions and flat geometric construction give headlines a stable, authoritative presence. It is a workmanlike choice for projects where scientific readability and clean aesthetics must coexist.

name: Tagesschrift
classification_notes: Tagesschrift is a display typeface that emulates the visual quality of hand-drawn newspaper or broadsheet lettering, with a rough, spontaneous texture that recalls physical print production. It is a single-weight novelty face with strong retro and editorial character.
summary: A textured display face that convincingly evokes hand-drawn newspaper headline lettering with a rough, ink-worn quality.
description: Tagesschrift — German for 'daily paper' — is designed explicitly to reference the visual language of traditional printed newspapers and broadsheets. Its irregular strokes and worn textures give it an immediate retro character well suited to editorial design, poster work, and branding projects that want an authentic vintage press feel. With only a single weight available, its application is narrow but effective within that niche.

name: Tai Heritage Pro
classification_notes: Tai Heritage Pro is a specialist typeface designed primarily to support the Tai Viet script used by Tai Dam, Tai Dón, and related communities in Southeast Asia, with accompanying Latin glyphs. Its serif construction is conservative and legibility-focused, prioritising cultural preservation over stylistic expression.
summary: A humanist serif created by SIL International primarily to provide robust support for the Tai Viet script used by minority communities in Southeast Asia.
description: Tai Heritage Pro is a culturally significant typeface rather than a fashionable one, developed by SIL International to preserve and support Tai Viet script communities in Vietnam, Laos, and diaspora populations. The Latin component is functional and conservative, designed to complement rather than compete with the Tai Viet glyphs. It is an essential tool for linguists, publishers, and NGOs working with these communities, and its open-source licensing reflects its mission-driven origins.

name: Tajawal
classification_notes: Tajawal is a dual-script humanist sans-serif supporting Arabic and Latin, designed with an emphasis on clean, modern proportions that work harmoniously across both scripts. Its broad weight range from ExtraLight to Black makes it highly versatile for contemporary Arabic-Latin design projects.
summary: A well-crafted dual-script humanist sans-serif balancing Arabic and Latin in a generous weight range, ideal for modern multilingual design.
description: Tajawal addresses the persistent challenge of harmonious Arabic-Latin type pairing by designing both scripts within a single coherent visual system, avoiding the mismatched feel of separately sourced typefaces.
